Claude Crushes SaaS, NVDA & GOOGL Near $5 Trillion, Elon Musk vs Sam Altman, Bitcoin Back to $80K

> "One was a $300 million company, one was a billion dollar company...
TL;DR: Mike Novogratz predicts an "AI productivity miracle" will drive the S&P 500 and Bitcoin significantly higher by year-end, even as generative AI tools like Claude threaten to dismantle the traditional SaaS and VC business models by allowing small teams to replicate...

TL;DR: Mike Novogratz predicts an "AI productivity miracle" will drive the S&P 500 and Bitcoin significantly higher by year-end, even as generative AI tools like Claude threaten to dismantle the traditional SaaS and VC business models by allowing small teams to replicate billion-dollar companies.

AI and the Deconstruction of Software

  • SaaS Under Attack: Novogratz warns that legacy software companies (e.g., Salesforce, Adobe, ServiceNow) are highly vulnerable. He cites an example of a "big AI player" who realized they could duplicate the functionality of a $1 billion company in one month using only four or five employees and Claude.
  • The "Capital Light" Pivot: The barrier to entry for new competitors has shifted from massive VC funding rounds to "how many tokens you need." This makes the sector extremely capital-light but puts established moats at risk.
  • The Infrastructure Trade: There is a clear market divergence: long Nvidia (chips), data centers, and power; short legacy software.
      • Power Scarcity: As token usage skyrockets, the demand for power and data centers is becoming the primary bottleneck and a major investment opportunity.

Crypto and Regulatory Outlook

  • Bitcoin Momentum: Bitcoin is currently testing the $80,000–$84,000 range. Novogratz believes if it breaks $84,000, it will quickly head toward $100,000.
  • The Clarity Act: Novogratz places a 70–75% probability on the Clarity Act passing. He notes that Republicans feel pressured to deliver on campaign promises, while some Democrats are warming to the idea of US leadership in tokenization.
  • Tokenization of Everything: The next frontier is the tokenization of mortgages, equities, and private assets. This will provide billions of people globally—who currently cannot access a broker—the ability to own US brands like Apple through neo-banks on their mobile devices.
  • Institutional Shift: The "Consensus" conference in Miami has transitioned from a retail-focused "brouhaha" to an institutional event, signaling deep professional interest.

Macroeconomic "Wall of Worry"

  • Ignoring Inflation: Despite PCE inflation at 4.5% (well above the Fed's 2% target) and a fractured Fed, the market remains resilient. Novogratz attributes this to the AI story driving bottom-line profits through aggressive cost-cutting.
  • Oil and Stagflation: Crude oil sustained above $100 is viewed as a major bearish signal, threatening "demand destruction" in Asia and Europe and potentially leading to stagflation.
  • The Dollar-China Trade: Novogratz’s highest conviction trade for the next 12 months is shorting the Dollar against the Chinese Yuan, betting on Chinese deflation and the eventual appreciation of the currency.

The 2026 IPO Super-Cycle

  • Major Listings: A "blow-off top" for the current market cycle could be triggered by a cluster of massive IPOs around 2026, including OpenAI, SpaceX, and Anthropic.
  • SpaceX Valuation: Novogratz expects SpaceX to price over $1.75 trillion. He notes that while institutional demand is infinite, early investors (some up 100x) are eager to "ring the cash register," creating massive liquidity events.
  • AI Bubble Timing: The current "AI chapter" could last anywhere from 3 to 24 months. Novogratz suggests we are currently in a "mania" phase similar to 1998 or 1999.

Notable Quotes

"One was a $300 million company, one was a billion dollar company... they realized they could pretty much duplicate those companies with four or five employees and Claude. He thinks they'll have both of those companies built internally within a month." — Mike Novogratz
"Warren Buffett says that we've got a church attached to a casino and he says there's more people in the casino than the church right now." — Anthony Scaramucci
"We're going to go through a productivity miracle rally... Companies are going to cut cost and cut cost and cut cost and that just drives things to the bottom line." — Mike Novogratz
"I've had this ballet in my mind that SpaceX IPO, Anthropic, OpenAI... that's like four years of buybacks. It is so much supply coming into the market and I can see us rally into that and that being the top." — Mike Novogratz

But we're seeing mania starting and so I kind of keep saying hold onto your seats. Alright my friend, we'll see you next week. Alright? It's good shit. Good stuff. Peace.

Starcloud's Philip Johnston: Why the Cheapest Compute Will Be in Space

Sequoia Capital(180.0K) ·11:52 ·3.2K views · 2026-05-06
Philip Johnston (co-founder and CEO, Starcloud)
Starcloud is moving AI compute to orbit to leverage 24/7 solar energy and 8x higher power density, predicting that space-based data centers will become cheaper than terrestrial ones once launch costs drop below $500/kg.
TL;DR Starcloud is moving AI compute to orbit to leverage 24/7 solar energy and 8x higher power density, predicting that space-based data centers will become cheaper than terrestrial ones once launch costs drop below $500/kg. Cost Advantage: Building solar-powered data...

TL;DR

Starcloud is moving AI compute to orbit to leverage 24/7 solar energy and 8x higher power density, predicting that space-based data centers will become cheaper than terrestrial ones once launch costs drop below $500/kg.

The Economic Case for Space-Based Compute

  • Cost Advantage: Building solar-powered data centers on Earth requires expensive land permits and massive battery storage for night-time operation. Space-based arrays eliminate both costs due to 24/7 sun exposure.
  • Energy Density: One square meter of solar panel in space generates eight times the energy of the same panel on Earth.
  • The Launch "Break-Even":
      • $500/kg Threshold: Philip Johnston identifies $500/kg as the point where space compute becomes cheaper than terrestrial.
      • The Starship Factor: While current costs are higher, SpaceX’s Starship is designed to reach $10–$20/kg, well below the break-even point.
  • Capital Efficiency: Starcloud estimates a 20-gigawatt constellation will require $100 billion in CAPEX, which Johnston argues is significantly lower than the cost of building equivalent capacity on Earth.

Proven Technical Feasibility

  • Starcloud 1 Milestone: The company successfully deployed the Starcloud 1 satellite equipped with NVIDIA H100 GPUs, proving that state-of-the-art terrestrial chips can survive the vacuum of space.
  • Validation of Workloads: Starcloud 1 was the first to:
      • Train a model in space (nanoGPT).
      • Run a version of Google’s Gemini in orbit.
      • Perform high-powered inference on Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data.
  • Radiation Resilience: Despite fears of frequent "bit flips," extensive ground testing at cyclotrons (Knoxville and Brookhaven National Lab) has allowed Starcloud to develop effective shielding and software-level protections.

Infrastructure and Constellation Roadmap

  • Scale of Ambition: Starcloud has filed with the FCC for a constellation of 88,000 satellites.
  • Technical Specifications:
      • Capacity: 20 gigawatts of total inference capacity.
      • Connectivity: All-optical links between satellites.
      • Latency: Sub-50 millisecond latency to any point on Earth.
  • Focus on Inference: The constellation is designed for inference workloads (e.g., 3D video generation, AI agents, code generation), which Johnston predicts will comprise 99% of the AI market.
  • Future "Mega-Structures": For massive training clusters, Johnston envisions 5-gigawatt structures (4km wide) docked together in space, though this is estimated to be 15 years away.

Overcoming Orbital Challenges

  • Thermal Management:
      • The Vacuum Problem: Because space is a vacuum, heat must be dissipated via infrared radiation rather than convection.
      • Space Ruben-1 Chip: Starcloud is collaborating with NVIDIA on a specialized "Space Ruben-1" chip designed to run at higher temperatures. This reduces the mass of the required radiators by leveraging the Stefan-Boltzmann law (dissipation is proportional to the fourth power of temperature).
  • Orbital Congestion and Safety:
      • Natural De-orbiting: By operating at low altitudes (approx. 400km), satellites will naturally de-orbit within months if they fail, preventing long-term debris.
      • Collision Avoidance: Johnston points to SpaceX’s fleet of 10,000 satellites as proof that sophisticated collision avoidance software can manage large constellations safely.

Notable Quotes

"In space, you don't need to pay for permitted land, so your biggest cost is gone. You don't need to pay for battery storage... and you need eight times less solar cells. The only additional cost is the launch cost." — Philip Johnston
"This really is the start of the largest infrastructure project ever... it’s also the start of a Kardashev type 2 Dyson sphere type civilization." — Philip Johnston
"We see that break-even cost [for space compute] to be around five hundred dollars a kilo... Starship is designed to produce launch costs of around ten to twenty dollars a kilo." — Philip Johnston
Thanks so much for having me. My name is Philip Johnston and I'm the co-founder and CEO of Starcloud, and just like the previous company, we have also been abusing GPUs in ways they were not designed for. So yeah, we're building data centers in space, mainly for the energy that we can draw. I will spend the next five minutes explaining why it will soon make much more sense to build data centers in space than it does to build them on Earth. And then I'll take five minutes for questions, so please start thinking of some questions. Before I do that though, I want to show a quick video, which is actually the deployment of Starcloud 1. This was... it had five NVIDIA GPUs on it, but the most significant was the NVIDIA H100 chip, and I'll just quickly play the video first. Starcloud 1 separation confirmed. So we don't normally get as great a deployment video, by the way. Half the time it like deploys into the shadow. So the reason this was so significant is until this point many people thought you actually couldn't run state-of-the-art terrestrial data center grade GPUs in space for two main reasons. One is the thermal dissipation, so they produce... they're very power dense, they produce a lot of heat. And the second is the radiation tolerance. So people thought that you would have bit flips at too high a rate. And so by... with this chip, we were the first to train a model in space. We actually trained nano GPT from Andrej Karpathy, and then we also were the first to run a version of Gemini, the first to do high-powered inference on SAR data, so other satellite data. And so it was a very significant step in proving that we can actually run the state-of-the-art terrestrially. But the... yeah, I think maybe to make the case for why it will soon make more sense in terms of energy cost, I'd like to quickly draw a comparison with... with a solar project on Earth since solar is the cheapest form of energy that we have on Earth. So if you want to build a solar project to power a new data center, you have three main costs. So the first is the cost of permitted land and in fact, in North America, that's actually the largest cost, or can be for most new solar projects. The second is the cost of battery storage and backup power because we're only... you know, we only have peak power for about four hours of the day, so we need to charge those batteries to use at night. And then the last is the cost of the solar cells themselves. So how does that compare to building a similarly sized solar project in space? Well, in space, number one, we don't need to pay for permitted land, so your biggest cost is gone. You don't need to pay for battery storage and backup power because we're 24/7 in the sun, so your second biggest cost is gone. And then you need eight times less solar cells because one square meter of solar panel in space produces eight times the energy of one square meter of solar panel on Earth. So the only additional cost, or the main additional cost we have in space, is the launch cost. And so you can clearly see there's a break-even point where the launch cost comes below the cost of permitted land, batteries, and solar. And we see that break-even cost to be around five hundred dollars a kilo, so about a ten-x reduction from where we are today. But that's well within range of the launch vehicles that are coming online. So for comparison, Starship is designed to produce launch costs of around ten to twenty dollars a kilo. And so I think I'll just finish by playing you a... one final concept video, and this shows a constellation that we're building now. So we've just filed with the FCC for a constellation of 88,000 satellites. Each one's about 200 kilowatts. It will enable us to deploy on the order of 20 gigawatts of new compute capacity. Really just scratching the surface with this new constellation. And it will enable... it's basically for all inference workloads. And so this could be... and yeah, maybe I'll start the video and you can get a sense of it. So in this case it's to generate a 3D video, but it could also be for back-office business processing agents, code generation agents. They'll come up via optical link to this constellation in this dawn-dusk sun-synchronous orbit, which means it's always in the sun. 24/7 power, sub-50 millisecond latency to anywhere on Earth, all optically linked. And this really is the start of the largest infrastructure project ever. I mean we're talking about, just for this constellation of 88,000, we're talking about a hundred billion dollars of capex, which is actually much lower than it would cost to do... to do terrestrially. And not only is it the start of the largest infrastructure project, it's also, in my opinion, the start of a Kardashev type 2 Dyson sphere type civilization, and potentially Kardashev type 3. I will finish there and we have about four minutes for questions, so any questions? Yeah, we'll start at the front. The intuition on the availability of solar is obvious. Can you just give us the napkin math on the radiator equation again, so like dissipating heat for anyone that's thought about it, it always feels hard, and then I'll also please say something about the availability of dawn-dusk, that orbit is finite, right? Yes, yeah. It's a great question. So because space is a vacuum, it's actually much harder... you know, space is only 3 degrees Kelvin, so very low ambient temperature, but because it's a vacuum, as you so rightly point out, it's actually quite difficult to dissipate that heat, and what it requires is a large surface area so that you can emit that in infrared. So everything that's warm is glowing in infrared all the time. If you had an infrared camera on my face, you'd see that I'm glowing. And so the rough math on the surface area is, your solar panels generate around 200 watts per square meter, and the radiator, if you keep it around 50 degrees C, will dissipate around 800 watts per square meter. So what that means is if you've got a... you need about a quarter again the surface area in radiator than you have on... on solar panel. So if you had a 400 square meter solar panel, you'd need an additional 100 square meters of radiator to dissipate that heat. There's a very nice equation called the Stefan-Boltzmann equation, which basically says that the... that the thermal dissipation is proportional to the fourth power of the temperature. So if you can jack up that temperature instead of being 50 degrees to 80 degrees, which is like a ten percent increase in Kelvin, then you actually... you can actually half the surface area of your radiator, or close to half the surface area of your radiator. And so that's what we're working on with NVIDIA now. If anybody was at GTC, you'll have seen Jensen walk out to this... to the deployment video of Starcloud 1, and then he spent five minutes talking about the... the new Space Ruben-1 chip that we're working on and it's designed to run at a hotter temperature without having a higher failure rate, and the reason you want it to run at a hotter temperature is so that you can lower the mass on the radiator. Great question though, yeah. What about Kessler syndrome, the favorite thing, right? Now launch yet more satellites, yet more in limited orbits as everyone will want. What happens then? Yeah, it's a great question and it's also related to the question asked about the space in space. I think... so it's something we take incredibly seriously. You know, everybody needs to be a responsible user of space, we do and everybody else we're, you know, keen to make sure that space is usable forever. For the first few satellites, so you can solve it in a few ways. If you fly at a relatively low altitude, the chance of a Kessler type effect is... is extremely low. So our first satellite we're flying around 400 kilometers altitude. That means that it will naturally de-orbit within a few months. And so if you were to have a collision at that altitude, by the time it gets round to the next orbit, you're already a few hundred meters below where you had the collision and the chance, yeah, chance of Kessler is very, very low. As you fly higher, it's actually extremely unpopulated, those high orbits, because then you start to edge into the... the Van Allen radiation belt. But I mean we actually have a pretty good case study for this, and that is SpaceX is now operating around 10,000 satellites without ever having a single collision in low Earth orbit. And the way that you do that is by having pretty sophisticated collision avoidance. The other reason I think people think this is more of an issue than it... than it actually is, and the reason that space is so much larger than it looks, is when you see a map of all of those satellites, each dot on those maps is about the width of California, and you're representing something that might be this wide by something the width of California. And so people can often think that space is very congested. It's actually, we can easily fit on the order of terawatts of compute in just this dawn-dusk sun-synchronous orbit without having, you know, huge problems with collision avoidance. Any other questions? Yeah. Is radiation like bit flipping, is that something you actually have to think about or consider? How does that impact stuff? Yes, it is something we have to think about. So the way that we're solving it is just an enormous amount of ground testing. So we've done four rounds of testing at the cyclotron down in Knoxville, it's a high-velocity proton particle accelerator, and we take all of that telemetry and then that informs our choice on shielding. And then for heavy ions, we have to go to the Brookhaven National Lab and we basically run all the chips through... through the space environment. So over a 24-hour period, you can put it through five years' worth of radiation dose and then we take all that data and we then use that to inform shielding, but also software development choices for it. Yeah. Do you do continuous compute, or do you mention different something? This is almost exclusively... I mean actually for the foreseeable future, we'll just be for inference. And the reason... the reason it's for inference is number one, inference is going to be like 99 percent of the compute market very soon anyway, so even if we, you know, we wouldn't want to be running a large training set... oh well, running large training sets would be a very small percentage of the total in five to ten years' time of AI workloads. But secondly, it's very hard. We would need a... yeah, we would need to dock together a large five-gigawatt kind of structure. I actually have a video of that here, but I won't waste everyone's time with it. Unless anybody wants to see a video of a five-gigawatt data center in space? Do you guys want to see that? All right. We made this video because we didn't want people to be like, "Oh you could never train a model in space." So this is what a five-gigawatt, four-kilometer-by-four-kilometer structure in space would look like. So this would be a Starship launch vehicle with a 40-megawatt is what you can fit per Starship launch vehicle, which will connect to a central spine, which is connected to this enormous solar panel. On the back there we have a one-kilometer-by-four-kilometer radiator. Yeah, that... that's how you would train a large model, but as I say, probably be at least 15 years before we get to anything like that. One more... I think we're 40 seconds... or did we... okay. By when do you think the majority of the data center will be in the space? Oh, that's a great question, and I actually wanted to ask all of you. Let's run a poll. So the... the question I want to ask is, when do you think it will be cheaper to run compute in space for anybody? It could be for SpaceX or for us. And the four answers will be the next five years it will be cheaper, in five to ten years it will be cheaper, sometime after ten years, or never. Okay, so who thinks within five years it will be cheaper to run compute in space than terrestrially? Hmm, interesting. Who thinks five to ten years? Who thinks beyond ten years? And who thinks never? Brave. Okay, that's... that's interesting for me to see. I don't... yeah, I think that's... we are out of time so I will leave it there. Thank you very much for your time.

AI That Designs Its Own Chips: Ricursive's Anna Goldie and Azalia Mirhoseini

Sequoia Capital(180.0K) ·10:38 ·4.6K views · 2026-05-06
Anna Goldie (co-founder, Ricursive Intelligence) Azalia Mirhoseini (co-founder, Ricursive Intelligence)
Ricursive Intelligence, founded by the creators of Google's AlphaChip, is building an AI-driven platform to automate and optimize semiconductor design, aiming to accelerate the industry from the "fabless" era to a "design-less" era where AI custom-en...
TL;DR Ricursive Intelligence, founded by the creators of Google's AlphaChip, is building an AI-driven platform to automate and optimize semiconductor design, aiming to accelerate the industry from the "fabless" era to a "design-less" era where AI custom-engineers its own...

TL;DR

Ricursive Intelligence, founded by the creators of Google's AlphaChip, is building an AI-driven platform to automate and optimize semiconductor design, aiming to accelerate the industry from the "fabless" era to a "design-less" era where AI custom-engineers its own hardware at 1,000x the speed of traditional tools.

The Foundation: From AlphaChip to Ricursive

Co-founders Anna Goldie and Azalia Mirhoseini have spent a decade at Google Brain, DeepMind, Anthropic, and Stanford developing the intersection of RL (reinforcement learning) and hardware.

  • AlphaChip Legacy: Their previous work at Google, AlphaChip, used deep RL to generate superhuman chip layouts. It has been used in the production of the last four generations of Google TPUs, Axion CPUs, Pixel phone chips, and autonomous vehicle hardware.
  • The Recursive Thesis: The company’s core mission is to close the self-improving loop between AI software and its physical substrate, using AI to optimize the very chips that train and run it.
  • Interdisciplinary Team: The company bridges the gap between frontier model experts (from Gemini, Claude, and Grok) and traditional chip design veterans.

The Three-Phase Strategic Roadmap

Ricursive is moving toward total vertical integration of hardware and software through three distinct stages:

  • Phase I: Acceleration: Developing AI tools for physical design and verification that run 100x to 1,000x faster than current commercial software.
      • Current "long poles" in design (physical placement and verification) can take a year and thousands of experts; Ricursive aims to slash this timeline.
      • Financial Impact: One day of delay for a major chip like Nvidia’s Blackwell is estimated to cost ~$225 million in lost opportunity.
  • Phase II: The "Design-less" Platform: Becoming a service where customers provide a specific workload (e.g., a specific LLM architecture) and the platform automatically generates the GDSII-clean design for manufacturing.
  • Phase III: Vertical Integration: Designing and building proprietary Ricursive chips and training in-house models simultaneously to achieve performance levels impossible with off-the-shelf hardware.

Technical Innovation: Speed as an Enabler

The team is redesigning traditional chip design tools from the ground up to be compatible with AI's need for fast iteration.

  • High-Fidelity Speed: They have built tools like a Static Timing Analysis (STA) engine that correlates with commercial tools but operates 1,000x faster.
  • Reinforcement Learning Loops: By increasing tool speed, Ricursive enables AI agents to run millions of iterations to co-optimize across a massive design space that human engineers could never explore.
  • Organic Architectures: Unlike the rigid, grid-based layouts created by human experts, AI-generated placements often feature curved, organic shapes. These "shocking" designs minimize wire length and improve performance beyond human capability.

Market Impact: The "Design-less" Era

Ricursive aims to democratize custom silicon, moving the industry beyond the limitations of general-purpose chips.

  • Customization over Generalization: As AI workloads scale, even a 1% improvement in chip performance for a frontier model translates into massive financial and energy gains.
  • The Cambrian Explosion: Ricursive intends to enable a "Cambrian explosion" of chips tailored to specific needs—ranging from low-power edge devices to high-throughput data center accelerators.
  • Industry Paradigm Shift: Just as TSMC enabled the "fabless" era (where Apple and Nvidia could exist without owning factories), Ricursive wants to enable the "design-less" era, where companies focus on applications while AI handles the complex hardware engineering.

Notable Quotes

"Our thesis in this company is that chips are the fuel for AI and that we should be using AI to design, to optimize, and automate the chip design process and close this recursive self-improving loop between AI and its physical substrate." — Anna Goldie
"We want to be the platform for chip design so companies can focus on the application, modeling, and other layers, and we can be the compute and hardware that enable those applications." — Azalia Mirhoseini
"Human experts, they tend to make these very aligned regular-looking placements, but the AI-generated ones look more like organic, curved [shapes]... which minimizes wire length [and] improves performance." — Anna Goldie
One of the themes that we've heard throughout the day is that neural nets are replacing a lot of traditional tools. And I think one of the most exciting application categories where we've actually seen that come to life is within chip design, where neural nets are now becoming superhuman at certain parts of the semiconductor design process. And so, I'm thrilled to introduce Anna and Azalia. They were the co-creators of AlphaChip, which did exactly this, at Google, and was used on multiple generations of TPU, and have now started a company to build this. Thank you, and welcome, Anna and Azalia. Hi everyone. Azalia and I are so excited to be here today to talk about our new company, Ricursive Intelligence, where we're doing AI for chip design and chip design for AI. We've been working together closely for the last 10 years across Google Brain, Anthropic, DeepMind, and then because it wasn't enough to work at one institution in parallel, I started my PhD while continuing to work full-time and Azalia joined the Stanford faculty. So, you know, we have been working in many different places together for a long time. But our thesis in this company is that chips are the fuel for AI and that we should be using AI to design, to optimize, and automate the chip design process and close this recursive self-improving loop between AI and its physical substrate. We started this direction in 2018 with our work on AlphaChip, where we developed a deep reinforcement learning agent that was capable of generating superhuman chip layouts. This work was published in Nature, but the interesting part about it, in our opinion, was that it was actually used in the tape-out of real chips. So the last four generations now of Google's AI accelerator chips, TPU, data center CPUs called Axion, Pixel phones, and also autonomous vehicle chips, and in addition to adoption by external companies like MediaTek. And so we decided to start this company to take this work to the next level and take on all of the chip design workflow. We see the company in three phases. Currently we're in Phase I, where we want to accelerate the chip design process. Today there are two long poles. One is physical design, which is placing the billions of standard cells or billions of transistors and routing billions of these components onto a chip canvas. And design verification, which is verifying the correctness of the logic of that chip. Each of these can take up to a year and involves hundreds or thousands of human experts, and the stakes are extremely high. So we've heard estimates like one day of delay of an NVIDIA chip like a Blackwell costs the company something like $225 million in lost opportunity cost. So we want to help existing chip makers get to market faster, build faster, cheaper, and more environmentally friendly chips. But I think in Phase II of the company, we want to democratize chip design. So we want to become a platform for designing new hardware where we can take as input a workload, say like the next Claude model, design an architecture that massively accelerates that workload, and then do the entire design process all the way to GDSII-clean, which is the format that we send to the fabs for manufacturing. In that case, we can massively unlock the number of customers that we could serve. Like any company that has a workload that they serve at sufficient scale could benefit from custom chips even if they don't have teams of hundreds or thousands of human experts. And then Phase III of the company would be vertical integration. So if we have this capability to quickly design highly performant chips, why not build our own chips? Why not train our own models and co-evolve them and serve intelligence at a price or capability that would be impossible to match. So Azalia's going to talk a bit more about our approach in this company. Yes, if I can stop on this slide. So on the right side, we are showing you the flow, the traditional flow for chip design. It starts with architecture design and it goes all the way to sign-off, and that's what you send to the fabs. And as you can see, there are many components here, and the way these phases are done today with human experts in the loop working with commercial tools that sometimes take days to run for a single iteration of an optimization. So our approach here at Ricursive is to first redesign the way these tools perform, make them 100x to 1,000x faster, and then they're primed to be used with AI because as you know, our AIs really like fast iteration loops and they can just exponentially learn more and co-optimize across a very, very large space if we enable them to do so. So by co-designing across the stack, what this enables is unlocking massive performance improvements and time-to-market, which comes from both the co-design and the automation. To just show you a glimpse into what we're building, here is an STA, a static timing analysis engine. This is one of the very challenging components of physical design. And what we are showing here is that we have built a tool that correlates with the commercial tools with very high fidelity, and what we can do here is do so 1,000x faster. Now imagine if you're doing an AI tool-use or an RL loop, we can now have this kind of feedback signal that we can use in the optimization and we can do a lot more with it. Here is an example of how our outer loop, our AI, works with this tool. Like here early on is what we get with a single iteration of our inner loop, but as the AI optimizes around the recipes that are possible to use these tools with, we can get significantly more performance. So taking a step back, what Ricursive is enabling is a new era which we call designless. Just like fabless enabled by companies like TSMC made it such that we can have NVIDIA, Apple, and other companies focus on designing chips and send off the designs for fabrication elsewhere, we want to be the platform for chip design so companies can focus on the application, modeling, and other layers, and we can be the compute and hardware that enable those applications. And the impact would be that we can democratize chip design and enable a lot more variety and performance types of chips possible. So right now there are a few mainstream chips for AI inference, but as you can imagine and as a lot of talks and conversations today allude to, we are going to need a whole lot more performance in the coming years. And one way to unlock a lot of performance is through customization. So when we build chips that are truly customized to the workloads that we are serving, and we at Ricursive want to be the platform that enables this Cambrian explosion of chips so we can build a lot more variety of chips that are really custom to the types of workloads that companies and the users care about. And you can imagine these chips can enable a very large workload like a frontier model or they can enable like a very low power or high throughput or other kind of variations of performance that we would need. And finally, we have an amazing team. Our company is a little unusual in that we have this subset of people who are very expert in LLMs. They have worked on Claude, Gemini, Grok, and such in the past and now we have put them together with these experts in chip design. And so it's a very great mix and we are very glad that we get to build together. Yeah, and with that we can conclude the talk. Happy to answer questions. Say a word on the shape of the chip placements that you end up seeing out of these models. Oh shoot, we should have shown some of the layouts. So I think just like in AlphaChip, we're seeing these kind of curved organic-looking shapes to our placements. Human experts, they tend to make these very aligned regular-looking placements, but the AI-generated ones look more like yeah, organic, curved, which minimizes wire length, improves performance, but was kind of shocking to physical design engineers when they first saw them. A question on the cost of these specialized chips. I totally understand that you could make a better chip with AI and have better placements and stuff, but I think Azalia was also making the point that you could make specialized chips. How does the scale work out? I don't know the first principles of chip design. Can you make thousands of different chips and make them as cheaply as one Hopper architecture? Yes, there's definitely what we are doing here is like we are going into a new regime where we can work compute to our advantage. So by scaling compute, we can reduce the runtimes to design the chip and also to enable more performant chips. So we are basically introducing a knob. Now through customization, given the scale at which AI workloads are going to be run, the economy of scale is working itself, right? Even a 1% improvement in a chip that serves a frontier model is a massive gain and success if we could enable that. But at different scales, we have different performance gains, and again what we are talking here is that we are using compute to bring automations and better performance and that's a knob that we can play with.

"Il faut un discours politique européen comme l’a eu Mario Draghi" (Jean-Pierre Petit)

BFM Business(243.0K) ·26:21 ·1.5K views · 2026-05-06
Jean-Pierre Petit (Economist and President, Cahiers Verts de l'Économie)
The global market rally is being driven almost exclusively by a high-performing U.S.
TL;DR The global market rally is being driven almost exclusively by a high-performing U.S. tech sector and the AI revolution, creating a massive prosperity gap between a stagnant Europe and the rest of the world. As France prepares for a new Governor at the Banque de France,...

TL;DR

The global market rally is being driven almost exclusively by a high-performing U.S. tech sector and the AI revolution, creating a massive prosperity gap between a stagnant Europe and the rest of the world. As France prepares for a new Governor at the Banque de France, experts warn of "gerontocracy" and the need for a strong, "Draghi-style" political voice to challenge ECB orthodoxy.

The Tech-Driven Market Reality

  • Tech Dominance: Tech now accounts for 42–43% of the S&P 500 index.
      • Growth Engine: The S&P 500's recent 9% gain is nearly entirely attributable to tech; the non-tech portion of the index is hovering near 0%.
      • Earnings Expectations: Projected Earnings Per Share (EPS) growth for the tech sector is 42% this year, surpassing last year's performance.
  • AI as the New "Defensive" Play: André Loesekrug-Pietri argues that tech has shifted from a "dynamic" portfolio asset to a "defensive" one.
      • Strategic Importance: Tech is now synonymous with national sovereignty (chips), energy efficiency, and defense (drones and satellites).
      • Market Cap vs. GDP: The combined market capitalization of Nvidia, TSMC, and Samsung now exceeds the total GDP of Germany.
  • Bubble Analysis: Jean-Pierre Petit notes that while valuations are high, we have not yet reached a "bubble peak."
      • According to his 10-criteria model, the market is currently at 73% of historical bubble peaks (like 1929 or 2000).

Macroeconomic Divergence: US vs. Europe

  • Growth Disparity: The U.S. economy is growing at a 2% annualized rate, while the Eurozone remains stagnant at 0.6%.
  • Central Bank Pragmatism: Panelists criticized the European Central Bank (ECB) for being overly "excited" to raise or maintain high rates despite being closer to a recession than the U.S.
      • The "Hawkish" Threat: Concerns were raised regarding ECB board members (specifically Isabel Schnabel) and the need for a French governor who can provide a political counterweight.
  • Interest Rate Handicap: The 10-year German Bund is around 3%, while the U.S. 10-year Treasury is near 4.5–5%, creating different pressures on growth and investment.

Leadership Transition at the Banque de France

  • The Nominee: Emmanuel Moulin (former Secretary General of the Élysée) has been designated to succeed François Villeroy de Galhau.
  • The "Elite" Critique: Jean-Pierre Petit criticized the appointment process as a self-promoting cycle within the "politico-administrative sphere," where positions are granted based on rank (Inspection des Finances/Civil Administrators) rather than a proven track record of success.
  • Key Challenges for the New Governor:
      • Debt Sustainability: Addressing a 5%+ deficit and an increasingly unsustainable debt burden.
      • Strategic Vision: The experts called for the governor to present a clear "road map" for the energy, digital, and demographic transitions rather than just a "polite" administrative discourse.
      • Leadership: The need for a "political" voice at the European level, similar to the influence once held by Mario Draghi.

The "Gerontocracy" and Generational Debt

  • Demographic Shift: François Villeroy de Galhau’s parting letter labeled France a "gerontocracy."
      • Electorate Aging: By 2025, voters over 50 will be the absolute majority. This shifts political focus toward short-term interests rather than long-term "grand bets" (like the AI or EV bets made by the US and China).
  • Pension Systems: Roland Gillet highlighted that capitalization-based pension systems (like in the US or Netherlands) allow citizens to benefit directly from market growth, whereas France’s "pay-as-you-go" (répartition) system creates intergenerational friction during economic stagnation.
  • Employment Crisis: Jean-Pierre Petit argued that the only way to solve the generational debt crisis is to aggressively increase employment rates among seniors.
"The market capitalization of NVIDIA plus TSMC plus Samsung is today more than the German GDP... the disconnect between a sluggish European economy and this tech is increasing the prosperity gap in a prodigious way."

— André Loesekrug-Pietri

"We are not at a bubble peak. I have a model based on ten criteria... and today we are not at 100%. We are at 73%, to be precise."

— Jean-Pierre Petit

"We need a European political discourse like Mario Draghi had... If the new governor does not confront the board at Frankfurt and just gives the usual polite speech, nothing will change."

— Jean-Pierre Petit

"France is a grand country, but it has discredited itself regarding its public finances. The challenge is how to restore an action that shows we aren't just saying 'we'll do better next year' while the deficit slips again."

— Roland Gillet

Allez, on est de retour pour la deuxième partie de l'émission. Il est 10h passé d'une minute. Vous êtes toujours en direct sur BFM Business. C'est Les Experts, nouvel horaire : 9h30, 10h30 tous les jours sur l'antenne en direct bien évidemment. On va parler des marchés. Des marchés qui s'envolent de record en record. Le monde s'embrase et les marchés regardent ailleurs. Jean-Pierre Petit, les puces notamment poussent les indices ce matin. Intel et Samsung discuteraient avec Apple. Ça fait flamber Samsung là, je vois. On est à plus de 14 % ce matin. Samsung a franchi la barre des 1 000 milliards de dollars de capitalisation. Les marchés sont-ils aveugles à la grande marche du monde, Jean-Pierre Petit ? Non, je ne pense pas. Ils regardent la réalité. La réalité, alors il faut quand même savoir que plus de 40 % du S&P 500, qui est l'indice phare mondial, est composé de valeurs tech au sens large du terme. Et ça se décidera je suis d'accord entre ces deux-là, ça se décidera par un vote avec le nombre de pays que chacun y est et c'est celui qui aura la majorité. Et il y en a un qui va rentrer frustré dans son pays en disant "moi j'aurais voulu des taux plus bas" et moi j'aurais voulu des taux plus hauts. Et c'est ça aussi qui ne va pas. Parce que même à imaginer que ce que vous dites est juste, et c'est vrai que c'est correct par le côté "il faut bien entendu pouvoir avoir quelqu'un qui porte le message", il n'en reste pas moins que vu les disparités entre les pays, qu'elles soient au niveau fiscal ou autre... même si vous vouliez vous accorder aujourd'hui à dire voilà le meilleur taux optimal, il y en a qui vont sortir gagnants, il y en a qui vont sortir perdants. Parce que la situation économique n'est pas du tout la même et n'impose pas les mêmes taux d'intérêt réels et ça la coordination est importante. Oui mais il faut quand même un discours politique européen comme Mario Draghi l'a eu. Et ce que j'aimerais, puisque Raphaël a demandé quel est le principal défi d'Emmanuel Moulin, c'est de dire ça et de porter la voix de Jean-Pierre Petit à Francfort et de se confronter à Madame Schnabel. Parce que s'il se confronte pas et qu'il y a un discours policé comme d'habitude... Il y a un aspect leadership essentiel. Et c'est ça que les parlementaires doivent tester pendant ces auditions. Pardon de revenir au sujet de la discussion qui est celle de la gérontocratie en France. Roland je voudrais votre avis. Il y a deux points pour moi qui sont importants et André l'a dit : c'est on se rend compte que ça augmente de trois mois chaque année. Mais qu'est-ce que c'est finalement ? On a un arbre avec effectivement l'âge des gens on sait, c'est d'ailleurs au niveau des retraites un des enjeux qui est le plus prévisible si on excepte les gens qui nous rejoignent ou les gens qui nous quittent, on sait où on va. Donc forcément on sait qu'on va nécessairement dans beaucoup de pays vers un vieillissement de la population. Un deuxième élément c'est qu'il existe des zones géographiques qui n'ont pas le même problème pour une seule et unique raison. Et regardez un peu ce que ça soulage alors j'ai pas dit que cétait bien je vous dis juste que ça soulage le gouvernement américain ou le gouvernement hollandais : c'est quand vous avez un système de pension qui fonctionne par capitalisation, vous n'avez pas ce problème sur le dos. Et au contraire si la bourse se passe bien vous donnez du pouvoir d'achat aux gens parce que leur argent a été bien placé et a généré des profits qu'ils vont pouvoir aujourd'hui utiliser. Et beaucoup de gens ne comprennent pas aux États-Unis à tort ou à raison, on parle pas de Donald Trump on parle de façon générale, pourquoi des gens sont si sensibles parfois au milieu d'une petite ferme au milieu des États-Unis à l'activité économique. Ils sont agriculteurs parce qu'ils voient qu'ils gagnent parfois plus avec l'action qui est menée dans la croissance de leur économie parce qu'ils en récoltent une bonne partie aussi dans leurs fonds de pension. Et donc clairement ici on a d'autant plus un débat important au niveau européen qu'on est essentiellement sur des systèmes de répartition avec des assurances complémentaires éventuellement mais en tous les cas sur les régimes traditionnels que n'ont pas d'autres pays. Et un dernier point que je voudrais souligner qui moi me paraît important et de nouveau vous l'avez dit tous les deux au niveau de la France et au niveau des autres pays : dans la politique industrielle on est quand même capables quand on fait les choses ensemble d'avoir un Airbus aujourd'hui qui a reçu moins d'argent public qu'un Boeing dans le temps et qui est un vrai concurrent. Donc on a quand même des capacités si on se met ensemble. Je pense que c'est très important de le rappeler. Ce sera le mot de la fin. Merci à tous les trois Roland Gillet, André Loesekrug-Pietri et Jean-Pierre Petit pour cette émission passionnante disponible en podcast et replay sur toutes les plateformes, sur l'application BFM Business bien sûr. Tout de suite c'est BFM Entreprises Spécial Outils RH animée par Sandra Gandoin. Quant à nous on se retrouve demain même heure nouvel horaire 9h30-10h30, même endroit par contre toujours l'antenne de BFM Business. Très bonne journée à toutes et à tous, à demain.

MAGA Dominates the Indiana Primaries; Ken Griffin "Doubles Down" on NYC Exodus, & More | RCPod

RealClearPolitics(26.9K) ·49:24 ·5.9K views · 2026-05-06
Ken Griffin (Founder and CEO, Citadel)
MAGA-backed challengers dominated the Indiana GOP primaries, signaling Donald Trump’s continued intra-party influence, while billionaire Ken Griffin’s pivot to Miami highlights a growing "capital flight" from progressive-led cities facing safety and tax concerns.
TL;DR MAGA-backed challengers dominated the Indiana GOP primaries, signaling Donald Trump’s continued intra-party influence, while billionaire Ken Griffin’s pivot to Miami highlights a growing "capital flight" from progressive-led cities facing safety and tax concerns. ...

TL;DR

MAGA-backed challengers dominated the Indiana GOP primaries, signaling Donald Trump’s continued intra-party influence, while billionaire Ken Griffin’s pivot to Miami highlights a growing "capital flight" from progressive-led cities facing safety and tax concerns.

Indiana Primaries: The "Trump Revenge" Tour

  • Primary Sweep: Donald Trump successfully targeted seven Republican state senators in Indiana who had opposed redistricting efforts.
      • The Results: Five incumbents lost their primaries to Trump-backed challengers, while two survived.
      • Political Capital: Speakers debated whether this was a "Pyrrhic victory." While it solidifies Trump's control over the GOP base, Tom Bevan (RCP President) noted that while Trump’s endorsement is decisive in primaries, its impact in general elections remains less certain.
  • Financial Stakes: Approximately $12 million was spent on these local races, highlighting the high cost of intra-party loyalty tests.

The Business Exodus: Griffin vs. New York City

  • Ken Griffin’s Strategic Pivot: Billionaire Ken Griffin (Citadel) confirmed he is "doubling down" on Miami over New York and Chicago.
      • Safety and Sentiment: Griffin cited the recent murder of a United Healthcare executive in Manhattan—blocks from his home—as a primary safety concern. He criticized NYC Mayor Mamdani for "demonizing" high earners.
      • Business Climate: Griffin argued that Florida embraces "personal freedom, liberty, and the American dream," contrasting it with the "Marxist" rhetoric of socialist mayors in New York and Chicago.
  • Policy Implications: Carl Cannon (Washington Bureau Chief) and Tom Bevan discussed how progressive cities are increasingly "hating employers" rather than just seeking tax revenue, leading to a self-defeating erosion of the tax base as billionaires relocate to Florida and Texas.

California Politics and the Kamala Harris Mystery

  • Harris’s Future: James Freeman (Wall Street Journal) noted that Vice President Kamala Harris has conspicuously avoided endorsing a candidate in the California governor's race.
      • The Malibu Move: Harris recently purchased an $8 million home in Malibu (Point Dume), leading to speculation about whether she is preparing for a "downshift" or keeping a base for a future gubernatorial run.
      • Gubernatorial Contenders: Xavier Becerra is emerging as a frontrunner following the exit of Eric Swalwell. Meanwhile, billionaire Tom Steyer is struggling with "likability" issues and progressive "purity tests" regarding his past investments.

Free Speech and Civil Liberties

  • Doxxing Controversy: An Arlington, VA prosecutor declined to charge an activist who distributed flyers containing Stephen Miller’s home address.
      • Legal Debate: The prosecutor claimed the move protected "peaceful political protest." Carl Cannon criticized this as "disgraceful," arguing that doxxing is a clear attempt at intimidation and harassment rather than protected petitioning of the government.
  • Global Media Shift: Sarah Rogers, the nominee to lead the U.S. Agency for Global Media, is being branded a "Free Speech Warrior."
      • Countering Censorship: Rogers is pushing a strategic plan to combat digital censorship, specifically targeting European allies (like the UK and Germany) who have jailed citizens for social media posts.
      • Targeting the "Disinformation Industry": The panel noted Rogers’ intent to push back against groups like the Global Disinformation Index, which have previously targeted American outlets like RealClearPolitics.

Notable Quotes

"I’ve got a bunch of flags on my boat, but they ain’t no white flags. I don’t surrender. That’s the story of my life." — Ted Turner (quoted by Andrew Walworth)
"It’s unquestionably true that we made the right choice [moving to Miami]... we want to be in a state that embraces business, embraces education, and embraces personal freedom." — Ken Griffin
"What has a chilling effect is Republicans wanting to live in Northern Virginia... This is not petitioning government. It was an intimidation, it was an attempt to drive this family out." — Carl Cannon
"This is what happens. This is democracy. When you piss off voters... there are consequences for those things." — Tom Bevan (on the Indiana primary results)
Welcome to RealClearPolitics on SiriusXM for Wednesday, May 6th. I'm Andrew Walworth and joining me are RCP President and Co-Founder Tom Bevan and Washington Bureau Chief Carl Cannon. And we're going to talk about politics in a moment but first some breaking news. Ted Turner has passed away at the age of 87. He was, of course, the founder of CNN. He reshaped the television business entirely. Carl, what are your thoughts? Well, you know, I remember when I first got to Washington, CNN was relatively new and they had a White House correspondent named Dean Reynolds. It's hard to imagine back then, Andy; it seems like forever. But in other ways it seems like yesterday. You had CBS, NBC, ABC. Fox News wasn't a thing yet. And there was this new idea that you had this cable. Everybody knew what cable was; we thought it was for watching movies. And Ted Turner was this, you know, he was out of Atlanta and he was this brash Southerner. You know, he figured you could watch the Cubs game, he figured you'd watch the Cubs games and then later the Braves games anywhere in the country. And then he started this news service that was going to be 24-hour news. And I don't think any... I certainly didn't realize how much it would change things. I remember thinking about Dean Reynolds—you remember Frank Reynolds, Andy? His father was a famous broadcaster and I thought Dean Reynolds was maybe trying to stick it to his dad, but no, he wasn't. And then he hired these people along... some of them are still with us. Wolf Blitzer was a, you know, early adopter. And you know, cable... cable changed everything. Not just the technology, but this idea that news never ends. And until then, you know, whether you were a network correspondent, a magazine reporter or a newspaperman, if you had a deadline, when your deadline was over you could go to Class Reunion where the Boston Globe guys went and drink. You were done. This... this changed everything. You were never done. And it's... it's in some ways warped, you know, the news business and it hasn't all been positive, but he deserves credit. He saw around the corner and he gave us instant communications. And in the early days when they especially, remember was it James Earl Jones? That voice from God, "most trusted name in broadcasting," whatever it was, they covered hard news. That was their thing. It was very little opinion. It was all news and he deserves credit for that. Yeah, it was called Chicken Noodle News when it started, Tom. I remember that. It was considered sort of low budget and cheap and went under really... Now referred to as the Communist News Network. By its critics of course. Well, before in between then, it was the Clinton News Network, remember? Right, exactly. Yeah, no look, Ted Turner was a, you know, love him or hate him, he was absolutely an iconic figure in the media business. And because he was this sort of brash—I mean they called him the "Mouth of the South"—he had opinions about a lot of things and he was an activist and was married to Jane Fonda for a time. I don't know, and so... he was always sort of in the limelight. He was kind of a story in and of himself. But what he did, I mean he did revolutionize the news business and I think, you know, I'm not sure what he thought of CNN today in today's iteration, but it certainly is not and has not been for some time the company that he founded and based on the principles that he founded. And so... Yeah, and he did other things too. He bought a big ranch in Montana and raised bison and had bison burgers. I... Montana Ted's, is that the name of that chain that's still around? I think it might be. He was always dabbling; he was always interested in how the world worked. Dabbling? He was not a dabbler. He was a... he was an all-in kind of guy. He spent... he gave a billion dollars to the UN back when a billion dollars was real money. Yeah, that's right. You know, I will tell you this about CNN. My wife was... was a producer at CNN for a couple of years. She was actually Wolf Blitzer's producer when Wolf was hosting one of the Sunday shows. And we would go out to... we would try to go out on a Saturday night... and back then you had to wear a pager. And we would be sitting there in the movie theater and she would get a page and inevitably something... it was always Saturday night, something would happen in the Middle East. I don't know why it was always the Middle East. And they'd have to blow up the entire show and then she'd be up all night rebooking and you know... It's still the Middle East. Yeah, it's still... but Saturday night was always a big night in the Middle East back then. And but I remember that, it was really this 24-hour sort of around-the-clock experience to be a producer at CNN. I once went... Tom mentioned he was married to Jane Fonda, my wife informs me that she saw an interview once and Ted Turner called her the love of his life. I think they were married about 10 years. But I was... I was going out to Montana to go fly fishing, my friend Ray Strother picked me up. He had a Jeep Wagoneer or something and sandwiches in the back and beer, enough really comfortably for 12 people for three days, and it was just he and I going fishing for two days. And to tease him because there were like 20 sandwiches in the cooler, I say, "Hey let's stop at this steakhouse and get a steak." And he looked out the window and Ray did and he said, "You'd like that place Cannon." I said, "How's that?" He said, "Well, Sandy and I," that's his wife, "we were going there, we were meeting Ted Turner and his wife and two other couples a couple weeks ago. And we got there and Turner I saw and some seemed to be some problem with the reservation." "We had eight people and finally he heard the guy, the host, the guy owner of the bar said, 'Well, Mr. Turner, you and your party are welcome, but the bitch stays in the car.'" And Turner had this ferocious temper and Ray thought there was going to be a set-to. Instead the guy... he had his Purple Heart from Vietnam on the wall and he walked around with his... with his leg, his prosthesis. Turner just... even Ted Turner says, "Well, I understand," he said. But and of course they didn't leave Jane Fonda in the car, they all went out and went to another restaurant but... Okay on that note, wow. Well let's move on. I'll just give you one more thing on him just because he was a big sailor. Well, wait a minute, I thought you were going to add to before you get to the... the sailing. That wasn't the end of the story, there's more to it? No, I thought you were going to add, well Jane Fon... or Tom was going to pipe in about her anti-war... do people know that already? No, not touching that. No, you're just going to give the context? She was... people know about Hanoi Jane, yeah. They... Okay. All right, okay. Well, go ahead, talk about sailing then. This... this is the sailing. Anyway, he... maybe a more uplifting story but... he won the America's Cup in 1977, which was a big deal. And he said this about it, he said, "I've got a bunch of flags on my boat, but they ain't no white flags. I don't surrender. That's the story of my life." Pre- Pretty good. Okay, so dabble... dabbling was the wrong word. I... dabbling is not the word. Anyway, let's talk about some politics here Tom. We previewed yesterday the Indiana Republican primaries where Donald Trump had been engaged in this six-month effort to defeat seven lawmakers there who opposed redistricting. The redistricting would give the state one more Republican district. The results are in. Five of those state senators lost their primaries to challengers backed by Trump. One race I think is still being counted, one survived. This is in some ways a Pyrrhic victory in that it won't change anything in Indy when it comes to the map for this fall. But the president won five out of seven. It's payback. What do you make of it Tom? Well, as I recall from yesterday's show, you guys were both boo-hooing about what this... how terrible this was for democracy or something. Especially me, yeah. Exactly. This is what happened. I mean this... this is democracy. When you piss off voters, and you... you know you can say you're taking a principled stand and that's fine, good for you. But there are consequences for those things in a democracy and obviously the voters, the Republican voters in the state of Indiana, felt differently. And so the majority of these, yeah I think four of the seven lost, there's one race that has not been called yet. It's... it's really, really close, like five votes, three votes, something like that. And then two survived; two incumbents survived and they survived fairly easily, double-digit wins in both cases. So it wasn't a clean sweep, but it was I think as it has been dubbed in the media this morning, you know, "Trump's revenge." Well, I think you know when we're having when we're waging war against a country of 100 million people, the size of Alaska with, you know, an implacable foe of the United States. This is exactly what I want the President of the United States, the Commander-in-Chief to do. Rip up Washington's golf courses, redesign... get involved in state legislative races in Indiana, you know, build an arch. Yeah, this is great Tom. He's not allowed... he's not allowed to do anything else except for focus on Iran according to... Well, you know, when after Pearl Harbor Franklin Roosevelt turned to his aide, I think it was Sam Rosenman and said, "Doctor New Deal's just become Doctor Win the War." Yeah, I kind of would like that single-minded focus Tom. Okay. All right, but well Tom, you're right. I mean the headlines are all over at least Washington. This shows how powerful Trump is still within the Republican party and you cross him at your own peril. But you know, is it possible that we're making too much of this? I mean if you think about this, is 12 million dollars—that's the final tally according to NBC—spent on these races. Yes, he won and and he won them easily, but you know it's kind of a bazooka versus a pea-shooter kind of story in some extent. And you know I'm again my question from yesterday was, was I think to Carl's point, is the effort worth it? I mean it was to Indiana voters I guess. I mean you guys like you can you can frown on this all you want but at the end of the day like they they had an election and the votes were counted. Look I... you know Trump, this idea that Trump, yes he still has some juice. Is it overstated? A little bit. I mean obviously we've seen in a lot of these special elections, you know, candidates that he's endorsed have lost. Yeah, what we found is that he can beat Republicans. Well yes. His his endorsement still matters mostly in Republican primaries, for sure. In a general election not as much. And and so... so yeah, I mean I think there's there's a little bit of overstating, you know, what this means as far as like you know Trump having staying power and not being a lame duck or having having juice. Tom do you think he'll be feeling emboldened and come out and endorse Ken Paxton now in this Texas Senate primary? Well we do have a poll out this morning from I think it's the University of Houston on that race showing Paxton leading Corbin by 48 to 45. So I don't know that Ken Paxton actually needs his help, but he might get it anyway. Yeah. All right, well let's talk about billionaire Ken Griffin. He was kind of the... he's the featured poster boy in these recent social media posts by Mayor Mamdani of New York. The mayor announced his tax on second homes in New York City and he taped that piece—we played it a couple weeks ago—right outside of Griffin's apartment, name-checked him. Griffin is not taking any of this lying down. Here he is at the Milken Institute talking about what may be coming next: "You know one of the when when we moved from Chicago there was a debate between New York and Miami. It's unquestionably true that we made the right choice. I'll leave it at that. It's unquestionably true that we made the right choice." "And now, and now what the Mayor of New York has made clear to my partners and principally my New York partners, my New York partners is that we need to double down on our bet in Miami because we want to be in a state that embraces that embraces business, that embraces education, that embraces personal freedom and liberty and that embraces people having an opportunity to live the American dream." Tom, we talk about this all the time on this show, that money is mobile. Do you think Griffin is going to actually leave New York and do you think Mamdani wants him to stay? So Mamdani has been doing basically cleanup ever since he released this video which he thought was so cool where he goes up and, you know, taps on the camera lens and is like, "Hey," and called out Ken Griffin by name. And in that clip that you just played of Ken Griffin at the Milken Institute, there was another portion where he talked about the fact that that video... I mean it it did personally offend him and one of the reasons it did he said is that the United Healthcare executive who was gunned down in broad daylight in Manhattan—that happened a couple blocks from where he lives. And so he was concerned for his safety, as as I think many of these folks are as the left demonizes them for being these evil monsters that are, you know, warping capitalism and all that. And so I I do think, I mean he went out of his way to say "my New York partners, my New York partners," he's he's made it clear to them. And I thought that was interesting because now even even his folks who live in New York are telling him, "Yeah you know what? It's probably better that we we expand the Miami office and not really do much here." So I don't know what's going to happen with 350 Park and what they're going to end up doing there, whether they're going to pull the plug completely or just downshift, but... And I'm sure Ron DeSantis is probably playing that clip all over the place because it it is an advertisement for for Florida in general and the kind of the kind of environment that somebody like Ken Griffin, who brings a lot of jobs. I mean we suffered here in Chicago when he left and and he got run out of town by you know basically the mayor Brandon Johnson who just was again going to tax and put the clamps on and we had you know crime and all this stuff. It wasn't a hard decision for Ken Griffin to pull the plug here. Yeah. Carl what do you make of it? Well, you know these these socialist mayors who who hate billionaires and and they say this themselves. They they don't often say the word hate but they they just they say hateful things about billionaires. It's like they you know before they wanted they liked employment but they didn't like employers. They've gone further than this. You know when Jeff Bezos—another billionaire who was pilloried in New York this week—when he he wanted to open a a big facility in in New York in an area that needs the jobs, and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez came out against it, so did Bernie Sanders. It wasn't even in her district, it was in the adjoining district. But now these these socialist mayors have gone from hating employers to seems to be hating jobs. And you wonder how... maybe that's just how they they don't if they don't believe in private enterprise, they they don't have the baseline assumptions that we've always had in this country that you're entitled that you have opportunity and you you create wealth for yourself and for others. They don't look at it that way and and you know and have some they seem to have a Marxist outlook on it and... but the voters voted them in. They are Marxists. Why are you surprised that they would have a Marxist outlook on...? Yeah but I but I wonder what Tom... Tom likes to invoke the wisdom of the voters. Let's let's revisit that for a second. They voted this guy in Tom. What did they think they were...? They voted Brandon Johnson in, I mean... Yeah and then we've got Mayor Katie Wilson out in Seattle. Well you're about to get you're about to get either Karen Bass re-elected or someone to the left of her. Right. So you know, this is what voters in these big cities continue are continuing to to vote for. And it's not going to end well. Yeah and as as Dick Tuck said when when he ran in California and lost a minor race, "The voters have spoken, the bastards." Well so Wilson, Mayor Wilson she's this new mayor out there. She said this the other day. She was on a panel and she said, "I think the claims that millionaires are going to leave our state are like super overblown. And if the ones that leave like," and then she like waves and says "bye." It's remarkable sort of cavalier attitude about... You didn't do the proper... Getting into your tax base, yeah I... She she also... she's also cavalier about... she talks like she's in college. I mean bye-bye. Yeah, right. Look we have look on a more serious note and and you're right. We have you know example after example of of these socialist mayors that are pursuing policies that are I think destructive, destructive to the cities. They could be arguing for and implementing tax increases and talking about generating revenue in sort of the more traditional way that that appeals to liberals and maybe even independents and some centrists without talking the way they do about billionaires and how evil they are and all these things. So we have a piece on the front page from Sarah Pequeno of USA Today talking about "Well it's we're finally taxing the rich in California and the billionaires in California they hate this tax and that's why we should do it." You know meanwhile billionaires are leaving California and and they're going to continue to leave California. And and so it's it's a self-defeating policy that they have particularly the way they they're talking about this policy. I mean it's just not... and eventually they're going to figure it out I guess but it's going... they haven't gotten there yet. Hey everyone, I'm Tom Bevan, co-host of the RealClearPolitics podcast and I want to talk to you about DeleteMe. DeleteMe makes it easy, quick and safe to remove your personal data online at a time when surveillance and data breaches are common enough to make everyone vulnerable. DeleteMe knows your privacy is worth protecting. Sign on and provide DeleteMe with the information that you want deleted and let their experts take it from there. It isn't just a one-time service. DeleteMe is always working for you, constantly monitoring and removing personal information you don't want on the internet. The New York Times Wirecutter has called DeleteMe their top pick for data removal services. As someone with an online presence myself, I started using DeleteMe and it is a great service. Take control of your data and keep your private life private by signing up for DeleteMe. Now at a special discount for our listeners, get 20% off your DeleteMe plan when you go to joindeleteme.com/clear and use promo code clear at checkout. The only way to get 20% off is go to joindeleteme.com/clear and enter promo code clear at checkout. Remember, joined now by James Freeman of the Wall Street Journal's editorial page. He also writes their Best of the Web column. He joins us here from time to time. James, your latest Best of the Web column is on our front page today. It deals with former Vice President and Democratic presidential candidate Kamala Harris and in particular you look at the fact that she hasn't made an endorsement in the California governor's race as well as her recent real estate acquisition. So what is going on and what's the connection between the two? Yeah thanks for having me. Thanks for the plug on your homepage, appreciate it. The... I go there all the time so it's nice to see it there. The... the Kamala Harris mystery I guess the plot is thickening perhaps. She can still be a write-in candidate if she wants to be in the California governor's race and a lot of Democrats are wondering why she hasn't joined that race. It makes a lot of sense, allows her to create a new story after the debacle of the 24 election and the end of the Biden administration. And although people recall her losing every swing state in 2024 to Donald Trump, she still won big in California. So this is an electorate that that likes her a lot, has voted for her many times. Seems... seems like the thing to do. Now the other issue is she has just bought an $8 million home in Malibu overlooking the ocean. It's gorgeous. It's a place called Point Dume. Now that's not D-O-O-M but D-U-M-E and it's known as kind of an idyllic beautiful place where people like to get away from it all and maybe maybe downshift. So I think some of her new neighbors are thinking perhaps this signals the end of her political career. But just the one more strand is the presidential polling in your RealClear average she was maintaining a lead over Gavin Newsom as the 2028 frontrunner for the Democrats and then this Harvard Harris poll this week has her with a huge lead, 50 to 22 and then a bunch of also-rans. So it's an interesting mix for her but some signals that maybe she's not going to run for anything. The other thing though though she hasn't endorsed, right? I mean does that mean she's trying to keep her door... That looks to me like that may mean that she's trying to keep the door open if she hasn't picked someone else. That seems reasonable. She did make a number of endorsements a few statewide offices, treasurer, controller. I'm not sure people were waiting with bated breath for the Harris endorsement in the controller race. She also endorsed Karen Bass for some reason running again as mayor of Los Angeles after a really disastrous tenure. But she has not endorsed in the governor's race and it's especially odd because it's a race where you have a number of Democrats running. No one really pulling away. Swalwell had been pulling away and now is out of the race after sexual assault allegations which he denies. But she could have been the kingmaker or the queenmaker here in the governor's race and for some reason she has not decided to endorse. And I I think that does it certainly does leave the door open for her if she wants to join. Ballots have started to go out but she could still present herself as a write-in candidate and I wouldn't rule out a victory in the primary if she does that even though it's only a few weeks until early June. Carl, did you... you were going to ask a question earlier? Yeah you know, Jim, she's never really shown any interest in the governorship. You know this this there was some question you know eight years ago, was she going to run or Gavin was going to run, which which one was going to run for the Senate? And and she's never shown any aptitude for executive management. But but she's just never even just even talked about wanting to be governor. So I I wonder if it's just a human thing, that's not the job she wants. The job she wants is the one she thinks she almost got. I wonder if it's that simple in her mind, I don't know. The other thing I wanted to bring up to you, Jim, we... you know she knows these candidates and that she hasn't endorsed is of interest to me. Now Tom Steyer ran for president, she ran. And I was watching the debate last night and you know he along with Sheriff Bianco, he was on that stage the you know those he was the least likable candidate. You know that's a subjective judgment but... but Becerra was the one who got the most barbs. So the other Democrats Porter, Steyer, Villarrosa, Matt Mahan, they seem to think that Becerra is the guy who's emerging, Tom, and that and that and they want to be that person. They want to face Steve Hilton themselves. So they attacked him and he was funny about it. He was asked about it at some point by a moderator and said, "You know your name keeps, you know they keep going after you," and he said, "Yeah, it's nice to hear your name mentioned." He he kind of smiled and said it. And you forget you know he wasn't just in Biden's cabinet, you know he was acting attorney general of the state, he sued Trump 140 times. He was a popular congressman, he's from Sacramento, his parents were Mexican and he was... Becerra if I sound like I like him, he went to my high school, CK McClatchy High School. And and we did a we moderated I we did an event with him at RealClearPolitics and he came, he was very gracious. He seems to be the person... I think you are a soft touch, Carl. Yeah, yeah. Well I liked him. I think I'd vote for Matt Mahan if I had a choice but I'm registered here now. But my point is that they think it's Becerra. I think. Yeah Jim? Yeah and that was the polling upshot after Swalwell left as you mentioned. It seemed in that I think it was the Emerson poll where essentially almost all of the gain was to Becerra and really not for anyone else. So it's kind of strange, I don't know if Becerra has a big ego but knowing that you're the backup when people aren't able to vote for Eric Swalwell is probably not the greatest feeling in the world but it did make him a contender and I think that's what you were you were seeing with people taking shots at him on stage. But still no one really breaking through. Mahan is, you know I mentioned, among the most intriguing candidates because he's trying to chart a more somewhat moderate tone but it's in the context of California. He's pretty much down the line fairly hard left on most issues but a little where he departs from the orthodoxy maybe is on safer streets. He's taken what you might call a tough-love approach on on homelessness for people who refuse treatment, who refuse to get shelter, who will not accept the terms of even even charity to to show up at a reasonable hour and remain drug-free and stay in a shelter. He is not going to allow them to just take over public spaces. And this is even in California becoming increasingly the an acknowledged bit of common sense. But he's still is portrayed as sort of not sufficiently progressive enough in the context of this race. But not a lot of time left but I I still think it's possible where he is able to grow his vote with people who who think they don't want to go for the full raging leftist howl that Steyer and some others are offering. Yeah well early voting started on Monday. The mailing ballots have been sent out. The primary is June 2nd actually is the actual date of the primary. But let's talk about Tom Steyer for a second, Jim. You know we've talked about billionaires from time to time on this show. He's he's kind of this self-hating billionaire and I just wonder he's spent all this money on ads and stuff and he's and it's you know Carl's right, he's sort of the least likable guy in the race. And I'm just wondering if it's just the mere fact that he's a billionaire? This is just not a year if you're running as a billionaire in the Democratic Party because they're just they you know they just really hate billionaires out there. Well I don't know. I mean this is kind of intriguing. I'm wondering if maybe Bernie Sanders has got more financial ambitions than we may have realized. You guys probably remember that Bernie used to rail about millionaires and billionaires. And then once he became a millionaire, he now he just rants about billionaires. But now you have the organization he founded, which is literally a an organization against billionaires, endorsing Tom Steyer. So I don't know, is this a signal that maybe Bernie is hoping that he too will someday become a billionaire? I don't know but I think I think maybe we have to realize that if you're willing to say what they want to hear, the the hard left may find you very charming despite what others may think about watching his debate performance. I I think the problem with Tom Steyer is that he is not... he is seen as like too opportunistic and he's not a full-throated progressive.
