You know and then the knock on him, oh he's invested in, you know, coal companies and private prisons and all this stuff, ICE detention facilities and he's just he's seen as greedy as opposed to, as the aforementioned JB Pritzker, who's also a billionaire and everybody likes him and he inherited his money the old-fashioned way, right? So and he's fully committed and has been fully committed, you know, Alex Soros... Democrats can't seem to stop taking pictures with him, they love him. So it's only certain billionaires that are unacceptable. Anybody with an R next to their name, of course, but there are some billionaires in the Democratic Party that are that are not as acceptable as others and Tom Steyer certainly falls seems to fall into that category. There was this decision that came down yesterday in Arlington, Virginia from the county prosecutor decided that the woman who distributed these leaflets disclosing the home address of White House aide Stephen Miller will not face criminal charges. The prosecutor says that the proceeding would violate her free speech rights and "risk having a chilling effect on others wishing to engage in peaceful political protest." So Carl, we're all pretty much Pro-First Amendment rights people here on this podcast, but this morning meeting this decision seemed to bother you. Why isn't this a victory for free speech? Well, the prosecutor, Parisa Dehghani-Tafti, who was in her second term, she won re-election recently. She was one of these socialist prosecutors nobody ever heard of who was promoted who was basically given a million dollars in a race that you know normally you'd spend $60,000, a local DA's race. George Soros's foundation. Tom, but but Carl, Tom would say this is just the way elections run. This is what we learned in Indiana, you know. The voters have a say. Yeah but voters voters have a say but you know he ran outspent 10 to 1 by a billionaire who as far as I know has never set foot in Northern Virginia, George Soros. And and she... there was a Democratic prosecutor there, it wasn't like you had a right-wing prosecutor locking up minorities. You had this very... anyway now we know why she's here. Here she didn't just disclose the house of Stephen Miller. She this woman, Barbara Wien, who is like a a former like peace studies activist—that doesn't sound very peaceful to me. Sounds like if you're on the receiving end of it it must seem like domestic terrorism. That's how it seemed to Stephen Miller's wife. She was on her front porch. This woman was passing out flyers saying "No Nazis in Northern Virginia." And then when she stood on her porch, she looked at her and did this thing with her eyes, like "I'm watching you." The quote "peace activist." And this was one day after Charlie Kirk was shot. This is not... this was an intimidation; it was an attempt to drive this family out of Northern Virginia. It was an attempt by a Democratic Party activist to say "You can't live here because I don't agree with your policies." And the prosecutor wrote in her court filings, "Petitioning government is a clear and quintessentially protected activity." She wasn't petitioning government; she was harassing a family and frightening them. And these are... that's a crime in Virginia. It's on the books. Stephen Miller's wife Katie found the statute and this prosecutor essentially refused to you know prosecute, to to follow the law, to enforce the statute, much the way Merrick Garland refused to reinforce the statute by prohibiting that kind of thing at judges' houses, Supreme Court judges. We live in an atmosphere where people are being killed for their political views. We were at the White House Correspondents Association dinner where a guy is accused who reads this kind of stuff, you know, "You're Nazis if I disagree with you." And so I don't think this... I think this was a political decision. I don't think it was she followed the law, I don't think she followed the Constitution. This is not petitioning government. It would not have a chilling effect on free speech. What it would have what has a chilling effect is Republicans wanting to live in Northern Virginia. That's the chilling effect. And I just in... and this for this for this family, oh we never intended to threaten his children or having them flee Arlington, but what else would be the result of that? So I I think this is disgraceful, actually. Stephen Miller did move his family onto a military base after this Tom. So... I emphasize this, it was one day after Charlie Kirk was shot and killed. Yeah. Think about that, man. Yeah. And a lot of Trump administration officials have had to do something similar. And I would just add to Carl because I agree with him, you know how would this prosecutor feel if if a Republican had been out there on you know in the street while she was on the front porch handing out flyers and trying to intimidate her? Would she be oh this is just clearly a free speech issue, even though you know a Democrat was just murdered in cold blood publicly yesterday? Like I don't think that she would have the same viewpoint and mentality on the issue if it were happening to her or somebody she knew or one of her allies. I think it's... But but so Tom, there so there is a First Amendment concern here. What you would like is a prosecutor who would say, "I I don't..." say something more tempered, "I don't think I could win this case. I think the statute may run afoul the First Amendment. Obviously I don't condone this kind of behavior." But she didn't do that. She basically championed it. She basically oh this is this is free speech, it's great. That's that was kind of an offensive... So the stat... so Miller's wife, like to your point, she she went and found the state law. And what the state law says it is a misdemeanor to to quote "publish a person's name or photograph along with identifying information quote with the intent to coerce, imitate, intimidate or harass." Sounds to me like pretty clearly... just that that's exactly what was going on. It was clearly an attempt to intimidate and harass. Correct. And that's that's what doxxing is, right? You don't you don't publish people's personal information, their home address, their phone numbers unless you're trying to incite the mob against them. Yeah, what other purpose is there to do that? Well... we'll follow the case I guess I guess I wonder if there's a federal statute that could be applied as well, but we'll see. Anyway, the Free Press has a piece, Carl. It says "Meet the Free Speech Warrior of the Trump administration" and they're talking about Sarah Rogers. She is this 43-year-old attorney. She is now the Undersecretary for Public Diplomacy at the State Department. She is nominated to be the CEO of the US Agency for Global Media, which runs Voice of America, an institution that we talk about from time to time here. And according to the Free Press, there is now a new strategic plan for public diplomacy and "digital freedom," in quotes, is at the center of it. The idea is to push back I guess on European allies especially who censor free speech. This is a pretty interesting initiative and she's a she's a pretty interesting person. What do you make of it? She she's the right person for the job. She went to Europe and said some of the things JD Vance said. And some twit German legislator with who's on the European Union whatever it is, Parliament, I don't even know what they call it... he like tweeted to her, like tweeted publicly, said "Oh there's no censorship in Germany" and then adding because he's German, "And you have to abide by but and you have to obey our laws." Well which is it? So there and she tweeted back at this clown and said, "Oh really? There was a 15-year-old girl was gang-raped in Germany and another a German woman called one of the rapists a name and she did more time in jail than the rapist. So I think there is censorship in your country, pal." We haven't heard from him lately. She has gone after the Global Disinformation Index, Tom, which is a a group that actually tried to censor stuff here in the United States. It tried to censor RealClearPolitics. Yeah. So what do you make of all this? Yeah no, I love it. I mean look there has been I think well, in the I think it's the sort of push for censorship started when Donald Trump first took office. That's when it first sort of these organizations, this sort of cottage industry of of censorship and trying to monitor and and suppress misinformation and disinformation and all of these things. And the GDI was part of that. The GDI is this group out of London that actually received I think it was a $400,000 grant from our very own State Department to censor organizations, including us, American media companies. And so I you know and obviously there's censorship in Europe. I mean we just gave the Samisdat Prize a couple of months ago to Graham Linehan, the the Scottish comedian who was famously, you know tweeted something, tweeted a joke about transgender women being actually men and... "Kick her in the balls." It was a funny... Yeah it was "kick her in the balls" was his joke. But he when he landed at Heathrow Airport there were five British MPs there I guess to to arrest him. And he's had his life and his livelihood, I mean ruined, it's taken a real toll on him and we heard him tell his story and it's it's shocking. And it's not just happening in Britain, it's happening all across Europe. And so... But but in Britain there have been several hundred people who have tweeted something, Facebook posts and you've got cops knocking on your door in the middle of the night. It's insane. Or or even or private text messages that have somehow gotten out and then yeah then the cops are at their door. So Sarah Rogers to the rescue! Yeah exactly, go get 'em. It's a target-rich environment. I hope she she goes to town. Tom, I have a British friend and I I told him, you know he was asking me about this and do we have a difference of opinion. I said about this I said, "We always have. Remember we fought a war with you over this and we won. And we'd do it again." We didn't like censorship. It was one of the things the—can I call them colonists? That has a bad ring to it now. It's why the First Amendment was first. Anyway go ahead Andy. Yeah no, well we'll see what happens. Anyway you know she and she has this job as the I guess as the assistant secretary for public diplomacy and if they can... Wait isn't she getting the new job at the at the head of the whole thing that Carrie Lake was supposed to run and got... The head of the whole thing that Carrie Lake was supposed to run. And she's got to be confirmed by the Senate. I think Jim Glassman was the last person who held both of these positions simultaneously and that... Well he was good. That's a good example to follow. Yeah, so I think it's a step in the right direction. Well and by the way we should say I mean her boss Marco Rubio has been very good on these issues too, as has, you know, JD Vance in terms of... I mean the Europeans haven't liked it, they haven't liked what they've heard from him, but he has been sort of calling out and telling the truth about this stuff. Yeah and let's get a special pleading in for for the head of Voice of America, you know. We've had him on our show, we we love him. All right, all right, with all that pleading we're going to have to stop the show right there. I'm Andrew Walworth, I've got Tom and Carl with me. Thank you for joining us. We'll be back here tomorrow. You're listening to RealClearPolitics. Have a great day.

Newsmax’s Greg Kelly Declares “Immature Hegseth ‘Unqualified’ for Job”

Dan Abrams(21.3K) ·45:03 ·6.7K views · 2026-05-06
Dan Abrams (Host) Greg Kelly (Host, Newsmax) Pete Hegseth (Subject of debate)
Newsmax anchor and former Marine pilot Greg Kelly stands by his public characterization of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth as "immature" and "unqualified," yet he refuses to explicitly call for Hegseth's firing, citing the complications of the ongoing Iran war and MAGA political optics.
TL;DR Newsmax anchor and former Marine pilot Greg Kelly stands by his public characterization of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth as "immature" and "unqualified," yet he refuses to explicitly call for Hegseth's firing, citing the complications of the ongoing Iran war and MAGA...

TL;DR

Newsmax anchor and former Marine pilot Greg Kelly stands by his public characterization of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth as "immature" and "unqualified," yet he refuses to explicitly call for Hegseth's firing, citing the complications of the ongoing Iran war and MAGA political optics.

Kelly’s Critique of Pete Hegseth

  • Professional Unfitness: Greg Kelly (Newsmax anchor and former Marine Lieutenant Colonel) reiterates that he views Hegseth as "unworthy" and "unqualified" for the role of Secretary of Defense.
      • Rhetorical Risks: Kelly previously criticized Hegseth’s "trash talk" regarding Iran, noting that such taunting puts active-duty pilots at risk of torture if captured.
      • Character Concerns: Kelly stands by social media posts calling Hegseth "Paranoid Pete" and "immature," alleging that Hegseth is "drunk on power" and has fired high-ranking officials, such as the Secretary of the Navy, out of personal insecurity.
      • Security Liability: Kelly previously stated that Hegseth’s personal life and alleged lies to the Trump administration regarding personal issues made him "black-mailable."

The "MAGA World" Political Pressure

  • The "Joni Ernst" Effect: Host Dan Abrams highlights a clip from Senator Tammy Duckworth, who argued that Republicans (like Senator Joni Ernst) have been bullied by the "MAGA far-right wing" into withdrawing their concerns about Hegseth.
  • The "Tap-Dance": Abrams accuses Kelly of being "scared of the backlash." While Kelly admits Hegseth "doesn't belong in the job," he refuses to say Hegseth should be removed, citing "limitations" in his perspective and the need to maintain House control in the midterms.
  • The "Facilitator" Philosophy: Kelly argues that a Secretary of Defense should not be a "roadblock" to the President. He contends that Hegseth, unlike previous "Deep State" secretaries, is there to actualize the President’s policies without traditional "guardrails."

Legality and Military Conduct

  • War Crimes and Illegal Orders: The discussion touches on the legality of attacking drug boats.
      • The Conflict of Duty: Kelly argues that rank-and-file officers should only disobey orders that "shock the conscience" (e.g., My Lai), asserting that many orders currently labeled as "war crimes" by critics have classified legal justifications.
      • Civilian Subordination: Kelly asserts that the military is subordinate to civilian leadership and that military service is not a prerequisite for the public to criticize military leaders.
  • Strategic Timing: Despite his personal distaste for Hegseth’s conduct, Kelly suggests that firing a Secretary of Defense in the middle of an active war with Iran might be a strategic mistake.

Public and Host Reaction

  • Caller Criticism: Several callers, including a retired Marine Gunnery Sergeant, expressed disappointment in Kelly’s refusal to take a firm stance on Hegseth's ouster, calling his hesitation an "embarrassment" and a "tap-dance."
  • Systemic Inevitability: Abrams concludes that even if Hegseth were fired, the administration would likely replace him with someone similarly "subservient" to Donald Trump, making the specific individual in the role less relevant than the administration's broader policy.

Notable Quotes

"Pete Hegseth should save his trash talk for his podcast bros. The reason professional warriors avoid unnecessary taunting of the enemy: it can come back and bite hard." — Greg Kelly
"Hegseth is immature. He doesn't belong in the job... Immaturity, inexperience, unworthiness, overall jerkiness, he's totally 'unqualified' to be the Secretary of Defense." — Greg Kelly
"It felt to me like he was scared of the backlash, of what would happen if he completely stood by everything he said... this is instructive as to the price that people can pay within MAGA world if you stray in any way from the party line." — Dan Abrams
"I think that Joni Ernst, when she came out and said initially that she had some concerns about Pete Hegseth, immediately was flooded with attacks from the MAGA far-right wing crowd... I think this is all about people buckling under to Donald Trump." — Senator Tammy Duckworth
So bottom line, you think Hegseth should be out? Look, it's not my call. Bottom line, I— I'm asking your opinion. I am at times outraged by things he does, but it, it is not my call. And Dan, that's not a cop-out. It's not. I am thinking about— Sure, it is. It's not my call to say anything that I say on the radio. It's not my call. You know, I, I talk about things all the time that aren't my call, but— you have a, you have a huge platform, and people wanna know what Greg Kelly thinks, right? Hi, and welcome to the show. So, when I planned today's show, I had expected that we were going to do a show on Pete Hegseth. Pete Hegseth has been under fire for a variety of reasons. I have been critical of Hegseth for a number of reasons. And I invited Greg Kelly on the show, who is an anchor at the very conservative network Newsmax. He's also a host on WABC, and he does conservative talk radio. Greg is an old friend; I've known Greg for a long time. And he has been incredibly critical of Hegseth. He also served and flew dangerous missions, etc. So he seemed kind of like the ultimate person to talk to. And as we had the conversation, and I taped it with him about two hours ago, it was clear that he didn't really want to criticize Hegseth even though he'd been incredibly critical of him on X. And before I play that interview, I think this goes to a point that Tammy Duckworth, Senator, Democrat, made back in January. And this was about Hegseth, right? Remember, Senator Joni Ernst had expressed real reservations and concerns about Hegseth because of issues related to women and the drinking, etc. And it took her a while to come around on supporting Hegseth. And there was enormous backlash against Joni Ernst. So this is what Duckworth said in January— oh, this is January, I think it's January 25 or January 26. I think this is— let's play this. I think that Joni Ernst, when she came out and said initially that she had some concerns about Pete Hegseth, immediately was flooded with attacks from the MAGA far-right wing crowd. So much so, I mean and even highest levels of the Trump administration, those closest to President Trump, said everything from, "You will never win reelection, you will be, you know, primaried." So much so that she not long after that, surprisingly came out and said, "Oh, I don't actually have any reservations anymore." I think this is all about people buckling under to Donald Trump. And it is really quite sad because this is about the operational readiness of our United States military. This is about the men and women who wear the uniform of this great nation and us having here in the Senate the responsibility to ensure that they are safeguarded and supported when we ask them to go out and do the most difficult job possible, which is defending our freedom and our liberties. Yeah, it has been a lot of pressure from Donald Trump and his allies. Senator— We pulled that sound after I did this interview with Greg Kelly because it made me realize how much pressure he must be under after having made these comments. And he only made them on X, right? And I know he's, you know, spoken about and I think he's been somewhat critical of Hegseth beyond that. But I don't think that he's ever gone as far on TV or the radio as he did in his tweets, or his Xs, or whatever you call them these days. And again, you guys can be the judge. It felt to me like he was scared of the backlash, of what would happen if he completely stood by everything he said. And as you'll hear here, he says he still stands by them, but when I pushed him, I wasn't as sure. And again, instead of this being a focus now on Pete Hegseth, which was my intention, I think this is instructive as to the price that people can pay within MAGA world if you stray in any way from the party line. Here now, my interview from a couple of hours ago with Newsmax host Greg Kelly. Joining me now is Greg Kelly. He is an anchor for Newsmax TV, he is a radio host on WABC, he's also writing for Substack now. But in his past life, he flew Harrier attack jets as a US Marine Corps officer, nine years on active duty, another dozen in the reserves, eventually achieved the rank of Lieutenant Colonel. Greg, thanks very much for joining us, appreciate it. Thank you Dan, great to see you, thanks. All right, so one of the easiest things to do when people criticize Pete Hegseth is to either say that they're liberal or that they never served, right? And neither of those apply to you. You are neither a liberal and you are someone who has served. And yet you have been very critical of Pete Hegseth. And I first noticed your criticism of Hegseth in something that I was very concerned about myself, when that airman had not been rescued yet, and Hegseth was talking about all the things that was gonna happen to the Iranians if the guy got captured. And you wrote the following, this is March 13, you said, "Pete Hegseth should save his trash talk for his podcast bros. The reason professional warriors avoid unnecessary taunting of the enemy: it can come back and bite hard. And it's the pilots who fly over enemy territory at risk. I served with four pilots who got shot down and tortured. Lose an engine, only one in the F-35, thanks a lot Lockheed Martin, and they'll be the ones to answer for Pete's rhetoric. Let General Kane talk more." I mean, was that the first instance where you said to yourself, you know, this guy is a potential liability? No, actually, my concerns about Pete Hegseth go back to the transition, when he was named. I had issues with Pete, I kind of rolled my eyes, but there were certain things I could get behind. Hegseth was the only guy I knew of, other than myself, who called for the ouster of the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. This is before he was named, this is before, you know, C.Q. Brown was a very bad guy, totally wrong for that job and was— needed to go. He was very political, just like Milley, and so I could get behind that. And then we heard things about his, his personal life, and he, in my opinion, was black-mailable. I said it publicly, I published pieces, I said it on television. And, you know, he gets the job. But that concern that I have, I learned about that not on cable TV but from my time in the Marine Corps. I went to SERE school, which is basically how to be a POW school. Much— much of the curriculum is classified so I won't get into it, but you're not supposed to mistreat our prisoners, and you're not supposed to because of how we could be treated if we're ultimately a prisoner of war. And you have to respect, to a certain degree, the enemy. One quick thing I'd like to point out, Dan, in your intro, like you're right, I did serve, and I happen to be MAGA. But what I have learned over the past ten years, really, is that the military works for us. The military is supposed to be subordinate to civilian leadership. And the idea that you have to have served in order to be in a moral safe place to criticize, those days are over. After what General Milley did, and I'm sorry to go off topic, but I mean, after what General Milley did and how he did it, you can't reserve criticism for just those who have served. These guys serve all of us. They serve the American people, and the American people and our representatives should hold them to account. Well, look, I completely agree with you. I've used the example in the past of, you know, just because I don't do drugs doesn't mean that I can't have very strong opinions about drug policy in this country. Just— the military's a little bit different, but you can certainly have strong opinions and, as you point out, it is in the end civilian leadership. But when I read— there's just a lot of trash talk that comes from Hegseth, and I think there's this sense that like, that's what we want from our military, right? We do want our military to be tough, right? We do want them to be warriors. But that doesn't necessarily translate the way that Hegseth has been doing it. And, you know, again, I'm now reading from another one of your comments on X where you said, "Hegseth weirdo drama somehow follows this guy everywhere he goes." "The word is another general looked at him funny so Pete drunk on power, hopefully just power, fired this guy. From Fox & Friends birthday parties to the situation room, it's always a problem with this guy. He lied to the Trump administration about his extensive personal issues and now he's blowing up Army careers of good men because of his personal insecurity. Paranoid Pete." I mean, look, putting aside particular people who you agreed with Hegseth on who you thought— you know, there's a lot of very senior people who are losing their jobs in this administration. Some of them deserve to go. But with Randy George and the Secretary of the Navy, he went too far. I will point out, look, I stand by my tweets. There is a language of Twitter, I enjoy it, I enjoy the give and take, I enjoy the— You're not gonna pull back on this, come on. You're not gonna tell me you didn't mean this stuff, come on. Oh no, no, no, I love it. Twitter is an art. Twitter is like, I just want to— You got me, you got me. I've been following these tweets and then finally when, you know, I'm not gonna— I'll get to the— the more recent ones, but I've been following your tweets about this and I'm like, wow, Greg Kelly is going after Hegseth again and again and again. Well, I'm doing it from a place of principle. And I'm doing it in a way Twitter, you know, that's— that's how we speak on Twitter. And I'm proud of it, I don't take anything back. There's always truth in my tweets, and that's what makes them, I think, powerful and why they land. So look, Hegseth is immature. He doesn't belong in the job. Now, I will say this, and I have been watching closely, there is something to be said for a Hegseth-like person in that job. Hegseth-like, I mean, right, somebody who's never worked in the swamp, somebody who's never worked for the Deep State, and I do believe that those things exist in their negative. You had Esper and you had Mattis under Trump one, who had careers and lives outside of President Trump. And some people will say well that's a good thing. Our Constitution does not put up guardrails for a president within the executive branch, for the most part. These people are there to actualize President Trump's policies, and we voted for him, he won. And the idea that the Secretary of Defense is supposed to be a roadblock or a speed bump to the president, I don't subscribe to that. That's actually not what our founding fathers had in mind. Well let me ask you this, let's— let's veer off for a minute, right? Because one of— one of the questions that Hegseth was asked in one of the hearings was about if Trump were to, you know, issue some blatantly unconstitutional or dangerous order, should he just do it, right? And your position is that there should be no pushback— well forget that's not your position. Let me rephrase that. Your position is that he should not serve as a roadblock even if that's the case? Well listen, this— I— that question and this conversation's not happening in a vacuum. I mean, the term war crime has been totally kind of misunderstood, thrown around rather casually— Which is why I didn't use that word in my question to you. Fair enough. But that is the context in which he was asked that question. And the term has like lost all of its meaning. And I've written and I've done extensive presentations about this. Interestingly, the Democrats who came forward and said you must obey— disobey illegal orders, you know it sounds great, right? It sounds interesting and it sounds— of course you must disobey illegal orders. The problem is it's truly not the job of rank and file officers and enlisted and senior even policy advisors to question orders unless they shock the conscience. Illegal orders, war crimes, are so apparently obvious and there is a body of work behind this that they must shock the conscience of the average person. And I went into detail about how the— I'm sorry, but the boats that we were taking out from Venezuela, CNN used those as teasers, right? With the music in the background. By definition they don't shock the conscience. They were there right in front of us. My Lai shocks the conscience. Women and children napalmed to death, free-fire zones. There is a world of difference between— Well, but there's a difference between the Secretary of Defense and some— and someone who is ordered to take this out, right? Ordered to execute on this. And I made this point recently, right? When someone said to me, "Well, you know, this is war crime," and I said look— they asked me, do you think that the people on the drug boats cases, right? The ones who actually went forward and did this are going to be charged with war crimes? And I said absolutely not. And I said— and I don't think that they should be. I think that they are unconstitutional, I think they are indefensible as a legal matter, but that's a very different thing from saying do I think that the, you know, the person who's flying the plane who's dropping the bomb should be charged with a war crime? And the answer is no, because of the point you're making, which is there's some ambiguity as to what the definition of a war crime is. But, but, shouldn't the Secretary of Defense be the one who is serving as that roadblock? Not the guy who's got the order who's on the plane, but the Secretary of Defense be saying, and again, whether this is a war crime or just, you know, indefensible legally, shouldn't the Secretary of Defense be the one to say we shouldn't be doing this? Well, the question is in a way it reminds me of "Will you condemn white supremacy?" You know, we— they badgered President Trump about why won't you condemn white supremacy? Why won't you say war crimes are wrong and you will disobey an unlawful order? That is so far from what President Trump is doing and from where we are in this moment. So I find the question insulting. I think anybody would, you know, are you a white supremacist? But you're changing— but white supremacist is not— I mean, look again, there is no legal justification for attacking these drug boats, period. There's no legal justification. Whether it is a, you know, a war crime becomes definitions of international law and look, I think it probably is, but that becomes an academic to some degree discussion, which is why I don't use the word war crimes and don't sort of throw them around as, you know, hysterically oh— you know. But there is no justification. And putting even that aside, your point about the Secretary of Defense, who you've been very critical of, should just sort of be following orders, doesn't— I mean, shouldn't he be doing more than that? Well, he is doing more than that. I mean, he is an advisor, they talk about things in these situations. I mean, they are obviously advising. But the Secretary of War is replaceable. At the end of the day, he has to actualize what the president wants. And I'm glad you're not using the term war crime. Now, I differ, we have a difference of opinion here. I believe they are legally justified. There is that memorandum, granted it is classified and we have not read it or most of it, in the Justice Department that grants what they argue is a legal justification. But this issue of war crimes and illegal— look, we don't want 10 million people running around, or 3 million, 1.9, with the Constitution saying "I believe this violates the Constitution." This is not up to them. I'm coming back to your point about the Secretary of Defense. The Secretary of Defense is an advisor as well. He's also in the chain of command. And I would think that if he felt uncomfortable with something or so— it was so outlandish, resignation is a— You know Hegseth would never resign over anything. I mean that's the problem with a lot of the folks I think in many of these administrations is they're so, you know, they love the power. And look, you've made this point about Hegseth in a lot of your comments. I mean the fact that he is sort of drunk with power. And one of the things, and you say that that manifests itself in terms of some of the firings, right? I mean again, coming back to your comments on X, April 22nd: "Wow, Secretary of the Navy fired. You know I have my differences with Pete, but given the timing," and you're being sarcastic, "I bet Hegseth had a very good reason to jettison that guy. Disloyal, rooting for the enemy." And then you go on on the 24th to say what a despicable guy Hegseth is, frustrated that he can't— do you know, are you laughing because you don't remember saying this or—? Oh no, no, no, I'm laughing because quite frankly these are so beautifully crafted. All right, good, good, good. Okay, I stand by— okay, all right, good, good, okay. Well let me just finish. So you say what a despicable guy Hegseth is, frustrated that he can't remove his real nemesis, the Army Secretary. He fires the Navy Secretary, has him walk out of the building by security, but this was not an urgent matter. And you go on from there. I mean, look, you read your comments and your point is this guy was wildly unqualified and he is showing himself to be someone who can't be trusted in this role. Well listen, I'm making the case out loud as you— as you see. I will point out some people have said there's a discrepancy, I found out more information. I really wasn't being sarcastic in that initial tweet. There must have been a good reason. There were rumors going around that John Phalen had been fired for cause. I later found out that was not the case. So, he is— look, he's taking up space, I don't think he has that much to offer the president. But the president has larger concerns, and also in the political concerns and midterms, you know, is it wise to fire the Secretary of Defense in the middle of war when he arguably, look, the war is going well, contrary to overwhelming negative coverage. It's very hard— I don't know if that's the move. And he has somewhat, you could argue, settled in the role. He has kind of figured some things out. I am at times outraged by things he does, but it is not my call. And Dan, that's not a cop-out. It's not. I am thinking about— Sure, it is. It's not my call to say anything that I say on the radio. It's not my call. You know, I, I talk about things all the time that aren't my call, but— you have a, you have a huge platform, and people wanna know what Greg Kelly thinks, right? Well, I am telling you right now, I may think something, but I am also aware of my limitations and my qualifications. I am not one of these guys who covers the midterms and, you know, all these districts and what's up, what's down. I think it would be great if we were able to maintain control of the House. I know that doesn't seem very likely. Does getting rid of Pete in this environment help or harm? I have never sat in the situation room with— with Pete either. I have, you know— I'll change the question for you to make it easier, all right? After the midterms, should Trump get rid of Hegseth? You know, it's interesting to me, now Dan, I— like this has become your amuse that I won't take a position. Well you would do the same thing Greg, if you were in my position, you know, and you were asking someone questions. I mean, well, look at what I have said. It's strong, right? Now, he doesn't work for me. I mean he works for all of us, right? Right. That's the point, and I do think he's unworthy. But there are things happening that I don't have access to, that you don't have access to. And I have to tell you, I have seen the generals be wildly wrong before. We've seen that throughout history. This whole idea and even Dan Kane, you know, okay Dan Kane's great, Dan— I don't know. I don't know. The generals were wrong in Vietnam. The Secretary of Defense was— you know. I mean— so I am aware of how far removed— I am only evaluating things I see in public. Yes, I talk to some people, I have certain insight, but I have to respect that there are others who have I guess more sensitive concerns or other concerns and I'm limited in my perspective. I am talking about what I see. Am I pulling the trigger? I'm not necessarily going to do that. Immaturity, inexperience, unworthiness, overall jerkiness, he's totally "unqualified" to be the Secretary of Defense or ever whatever the hell we're calling him. Well I have the right and actually everybody does to question everything. Those are the words of Greg Kelly on X. And those are the words right now, I totally stand by them. Now, it is not my position to fire him or keep him in office. You know what I mean? And I— I would imagine if I— But you think he's— you think he's unqualified. Well certainly. However, Dan, we're in the middle of a war, right? We are also in the middle of a very competitive election season. President Trump has concerns beyond that, and I think he has to factor everything in. He knows how I feel, and if he can— if there is some merit in having Pete stay, and there very well might be, there very well might be. I don't see it, I'm not privy to it. I do appreciate that— that Pete has a certain flexibility independent of the swamp that Esper and Panetta and let's see who else, Mattis and all the others, all the other typical secretaries— he is not so burdened. And there are things to be appreciated there. But ultimately it's not my call. Yes, we all have the— we all have the right to sound off, but it's President Trump's decision. And he has to make a decision, it's not just based on what Greg Kelly says. Of course, of course. Yeah, except that I want to know what Greg Kelly says and Greg Kelly doesn't seem to want to say what Greg Kelly says so— No, I mean I'm being honest with you. Look, you're in a tough spot. You're in a tough spot. You are a MAGA guy, your brand— it's not a tough political spot, it's not a tough political spot. It's a spot where I respect President Trump, I respect the other things. I can— I do not know what he knows, right? Look to me he's this, that and the other thing. You know at this point about a thousand things about Hegseth that I don't know. You know what it's like to be in the situation room with him in a moment of crisis. I don't know what that's like. Pete may be doing spectacular things behind the scenes. I tend to doubt that, but I can't rule it out. I can't rule it out, Dan. It's— it's— maybe he has risen to the moment. But I tend to be pretty skeptical. Fair enough. Greg Kelly, thank you so much for taking the time, fun conversation. By the way, next time we can do a debate about the January 6ers etc. I didn't want to take us off on a tangent, but we can do another debate about some of the other issues that you raised. But as always, I appreciate you coming on the show for a really interesting conversation and appreciate your time. Dan Abrams, thank you very much, so appreciate you. All right, so you know, as I listen to that back, I have to say I don't think I gave Kelly enough credit at the outset. Because as I was doing that interview, I thought he was being intellectually disconnected, let's say. But he wasn't. I now— whether you agree or disagree, he wasn't running from his criticism as much as I had thought he was. It just seems that on the ultimate question of should he be gone, he was nuanced. Because he did say, and I had forgotten he said this as I listened to it, he said he's immature and doesn't belong in the job. So he didn't change his position. Let's go to Texas and Ike. Ike, how you doing? What you got? Good. Well, the biggest issue here is— is we've got bullies in these positions. Hegseth is clearly unqualified for the job, you know, and he just— he just rattles off the bullyness that his boss does. And you can argue that his boss is not qualified for his job. People don't respond well to being bullied. I have a business, you come bully me, you won't ever do business with me again. And this host, you know, the only people that seem to like this bully are the people that want to stay in favor. So if he goes and fires him, he's gonna appoint another bully 'cause all he's gonna be is a bullhorn for his bullying. And he's got a Congress that won't— that's going to give him what he wants. So that— that's my view of the world. Thank you Ike, appreciate it. Let's go to Iowa and Ron. Ron, how you doing? What's going on? Hey Dan, I'm glad you took my call before I have to go drive the bus again. Pete Hegseth is on there for one reason: the— the Department of Defense, the Secretary has to sign off on using troops inside the US. Been getting rid of generals and admirals, I'm pretty sure it's probably by if they find out they won't follow an order that he gives, he gets rid of 'em. Probably threatens their pension if they speak out, or even all the way up to, you know, charging them with treason 'cause you give away information. And that's how they— they're controlling the situation. And when they have to, if something happens in the midterms or whatever, that's when they'll be using troops in the United States and Hegseth has to be there for that. Just out of curiosity, how do you think they're going to go about using troops in the United States? What exactly are they going to do do you think? Oh, I think you're going to see both ICE agents and troops at polling places that are heavily Democratic to try to intimidate the vote— just as an intimidation tactic. And you look at— you can talk about Trump all you want, but you look at him, he does the illegal thing first and then, you know, he doesn't ask permission. You have to take him into court, he's been doing that the whole administration. So you— you know, think about it, he's going to do whatever he wants and the courts aren't going to be able to act fast enough to stop something like that. So that's where we are. Thank you Ron, appreciate it. Let's go to Nicole in California. Hi Nicole, what you got? Yeah, my— my reaction to Mr. Kelly is that he first of all said that the war was going really well and then of course discussed all the negative defects of— of Hegseth. And I guess my main point is none of the callers and myself, you, and Mr. Kelly are not in the situation room, you don't know what's going on. Yes, he's probably immature and everything else he says, but he can also be effective. And I've been impressed by he and— and General Kane's, you know, when they go over all the details of what's going on. I think he's very bright, I think he's obviously intelligent, and you know, despite his defects he may be doing a good job. He may be doing a good job in certain areas, but I do think his defects are too significant to overlook. I mean, you start with the fact that early on in his tenure he's talking about, you know, top secret missions on a non-secure platform. Anyone else would have been out just as a result of that. He's firing people, he's firing people that he's intimidated by or he doesn't like on a personal level, no matter how much service they have to this country. The Iran war, look you can say it's going really well, we'll see, I'm still— I'm not one of these people saying it's a disaster, we shall see what happens. I am dubious that it's going to end up well, but that's not a judgment point yet. The attack on the drug boats and his celebrations about these, the fact that they ended up killing a guy who had survived one of the attacks— I mean these are all things that would have led anyone else to have lost their job. Well I don't think anyone micro-managed anybody in Biden's administration, and it seems like you've taken each person one by one and micro-managed every move they made. I don't— I don't applaud anything that he did what you mentioned. But I still think that he's effective, and I think in general America is— is weak and we don't put up with discomfort. And you know, you call him a bully or you can call him strength. I just— I just see a hippie mentality versus people that want to be strong. I get it Nicole, and I appreciate— look, this is— your position is your opinion, and you are allowed to have it and I welcome those opinions on this show. That is why I appreciate you calling in, thank you. Let's go to— let's go to Rick in Texas. Rick, how you doing? What's going on? Dan, I'm a retired Marine Corps Gunnery Sergeant and that Lieutenant Colonel you just had on sounded like a private that lost his weapon on the rifle range. He really embarrassed himself in the Marine Corps. I mean you gave him a dozen or more times. What do you think about the guy and should he be fired? And he tap-danced like a private in boot camp. He's an embarrassment, I— I appreciate the fact that he served as a Marine Lieutenant Colonel, but I can tell you, I'm glad he was a reservist and not active duty. All right, Rick, thank you. John's in Kentucky. John, what you got? Hey Dan. What I would say is that I really appreciate you sharing that Greg Kelly interview simply because it reinforces why people are so frustrated with politics, politicians, and sometimes those that cover politics and politicians. You gave him every opportunity to say "Yeah, Pete Hegseth needs to go" and he avoided it. He said that he was immature, he said he's irresponsible, all the— all the list of things. And you know, why can't the guy just man up and just say "Yeah, I think he needs to go." The second thing that I would say is for all of Pete Hegseth's complaints about what the media is doing in their coverage of this war in Iran, the DOD and the White House are doing a pitiful job of promoting the positives from it. Where's the Norman Schwarzkopf moment where they're talking about what we've done, why we've done it and how successful we've been. So, you know, the guy has no one to blame but himself. I don't know, I think that they've done a little bit of that, I just don't think they've done it very effectively. But John thank you. Let's go to Ohio and Bill. Bill, what you got? Hi. Well, pretty much everyone's touched on what I was going to say, but you know, he didn't really make a solid criticism at all. And for him to say he wanted to be in the war room, like you said, all he had to do was get on— what is it, Signal chat or whatever it is. I mean the incompetence is just at a level that's just astounding. I just— I'm bewildered. Bill thank you. Let's do this, let's take a break, come back with more of your calls on my interview with Greg Kelly, who has surprisingly criticized Pete Hegseth and coming from a guy who was flying jets as a US Marine Corps officer, nine years on active duty, and also a host at Newsmax. So you know, this doesn't benefit him politically or professionally to issue this criticism. John is in Virginia. John, what you got? I agree with you and some of the callers that he could have easily said "It's the President's choice to keep Hegseth because he knows quite a bit, but I think he's disqualified and probably should resign" or something like that. That would have been easy but like you said he's probably under a little pressure. About the— about the war crimes, I am sorry, some of the stuff they have done comes really close and that incident in September as far as I'm concerned went over the edge. Not only did they kill those guys on that boat that were hanging on to a boat that was not really seaworthy, but they tried to hide it, they wouldn't release the video and a bunch of other things. I mean it had all the makings of a war crime. Look, I said in my interview with Greg that I think it was likely a war crime, particularly that incident, right? But what I think that we ought not do when talking about these things is, you know, get— get sidetracked. Meaning as I said to Greg, it is clearly unlawful, it is clearly unconstitutional in my view. Those are nobodies— no-brainers. The war crimes defense of the, you know, what the administration is claiming was a non-international armed conflict, right? Against these drug cartels, that's not a winning argument to me, but it's an argument. And so what happens, and this is why I think that Greg kept trying to bring it back to the war crimes, because there's an argument to be made about that. And people just— some people just sort of state it as a fact that well of course we're committing war crimes, of course we're committing war crimes, and the answer is that it's a bit more nuanced than that. And it provides ammunition for people who want to defend the administration to say "See, you just immediately go to war crimes" and I will say that I do think it— I think in an analysis it would be one. Albert is in New Mexico. Albert, what you got? Hey, thanks for taking my call Dan, you're great. I was disgusted when I heard Kelly lead off his reason for not overtly saying that Hegseth should be fired by saying "Well, you know, the President's got to consider the House of Representatives." I want to know, how many people would he be willing to see die from the inadequacy of this Secretary of Defense in pursuit of a couple of seats in the House of Representatives? All right, well I'm going to give you the honest answer, I'm gonna give you the honest response to it. I hear you. Here's the honest response. If Hegseth were to go, someone else would be put in his place who would do exactly what Hegseth is doing, just in a different way. So you're not going to suddenly get someone who's fundamentally different in terms of his outlook and willingness to be subservient to Donald Trump. So you don't— so you don't want the administration to say "We've seen how this guy operates, he's not good enough for the job, we're going to put somebody better in there" when everybody, almost everyone outside of MAGA thinks that he's inadequate and unqualified. And even Kelly thought those things and he still says "Let's be transactional and keep him in"? Well, look, but— but again, it's a little unfair because I think any administration would think twice about firing the Secretary of Defense if they thought it could be a major political liability. Now, you could argue he should have been fired immediately after the Signal event, but it's the firing him today, right? I don't think it's been something that's precipitated it today. The Iran war, Kelly's got a point. In the middle of the Iran war probably not the best time. So in that one I would quasi defend him. Thanks for listening, see tomorrow.

Full speech: VP Vance speaks at Iowa manufacturing facility

KWQC News(13.3K) ·31:14 ·889 views · 2026-05-06
JD Vance (Vice President) Zach Nunn (U.S. Representative, Iowa)
Vice President JD Vance’s address in Iowa frames the upcoming election as a choice between a government that prioritizes foreign interests and "fraudsters" versus one that protects American workers through tariffs, domestic tax cuts, and aggressive oversight of federal spending.
TL;DR Vice President JD Vance’s address in Iowa frames the upcoming election as a choice between a government that prioritizes foreign interests and "fraudsters" versus one that protects American workers through tariffs, domestic tax cuts, and aggressive oversight of federal...

TL;DR

Vice President JD Vance’s address in Iowa frames the upcoming election as a choice between a government that prioritizes foreign interests and "fraudsters" versus one that protects American workers through tariffs, domestic tax cuts, and aggressive oversight of federal spending.

Economic Protectionism and Manufacturing

  • Combatting Foreign Dumping: Vance highlighted the history of the Iowa manufacturing facility, noting that it nearly collapsed due to the People's Republic of China "dumping" goods produced by cheap or slave labor to undercut American wages.
  • Tariff and Tax Strategy:
      • Advocated for a "big fat tariff" on foreign goods to protect local jobs.
      • Promoted tax policies that reward domestic production: "If you're building right here in Iowa... you deserve a tax cut."
  • Housing and Rent:
      • Claimed that rent costs have decreased for eight consecutive months.
      • Policy Principle: Argued that American homes and farmland should belong to American families rather than foreign investors or illegal immigrants.

Federal Oversight and the "Fraud Task Force"

  • Targeting Systemic Abuse: Vance, serving as the leader of a federal fraud task force, described federal fraud as "fishing in a barrel with dynamite," citing rampant mismanagement of taxpayer dollars.
  • SNAP Benefit Reform:
      • Reported that Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins identified 355,000 people receiving double SNAP benefits and 186,000 deceased individuals still on the rolls.
      • Criticized the "Lamborghini-owning" wealthy who remain on food assistance.
  • Accountability in Minnesota: Cited a case in suburban Minneapolis where federal funds intended for autistic children were allegedly diverted to "Somalian fraudsters" through systemic application fraud, leading the administration to demand state-level audits.

Iowa Agriculture and Trade

  • Expanding Export Markets: Claimed the administration doubled the export market for Iowa farmers in 18 months by using trade authorities to force foreign countries to accept American products.
  • Energy and Input Costs:
      • Reiterated support for E15 fuel to expand market access for corn farmers.
      • Acknowledged the burden of high fertilizer prices and pledged continued work with Rep. Zach Nunn to lower agricultural input costs.

Political Contrast and "Common Sense"

  • Opposition Critique: Vance criticized Rep. Nunn’s opponent, Sarah Trone Garriott, for focusing on "far-left" social issues—such as gender transition policies for minors—rather than the economic concerns of the district.
  • Voter Realignment: Vance, noting his own blue-collar Democratic upbringing, argued that the modern Democratic Party in Washington D.C. has abandoned working people in favor of illegal immigration and insider stock trading.
  • The Burden of Sacrifice:
      • Highlighted the heavy toll Iowa has taken in recent Middle East operations, noting the state has borne a significant percentage of recent casualties.
      • The "Earn This" Standard: Closed with a call to make American politics "worthy" of the sacrifice made by Gold Star families and service members.
"If you're building right here in Iowa, if you're making something right here in Iowa... you deserve a tax cut. But if you're a foreign country dumping that garbage into our country... you're going to pay a big fat tariff." — Vice President JD Vance
"Finding fraud in the federal government is kind of like fishing in a barrel with dynamite. Every week I get a report from the task force and I am shocked every single day by the things that we find." — Vice President JD Vance
"We have to meet their sacrifice by carrying something forward that they can't carry forward. We have to make this country worthy of that sacrifice." — Vice President JD Vance
Look, I'm here today to talk a little bit about the stakes in November of 2026. I think that we have done a lot in Washington, DC to be proud of, and we're going to talk about it. But the fundamental question for us in this election is actually not any specific question of public policy; it's not any particular issue. It's fundamentally: do you want people in Washington, DC who fight for you, who fight for the people of this district, or who fight for corruption and fraud? Because I'm 41 years old, and for the first time in my entire life, I think we have seen a major political party in the United States of America completely go off the rails. They're not just talking about different views on taxes, or on regulation, or on the Clean Water Act, as important as all those things are. They're talking about who does the government actually stand and fight for. When the President of the United States asks the entire Congress to stand for the simple principle that the people's government ought to fight for the American people first, what did we see? We saw every single Congressional Democrat sit on their hands. They didn't care about you, they didn't care about the people of this district, and they didn't care about the farmers or the factory workers or the people who actually make this country run. Because now we have in Washington, DC a Democrat party that is so focused on illegal immigration, that is so focused on people who do not have the legal right to be here, and that is so focused on fraud because so many of their friends get rich from fraud, that they forgot to look after you. And whatever your political views, whether you're a sensible Republican, Independent, or even a sensible Democrat—because I know we got a lot of those in Iowa, even if not in Washington, DC—I think that you deserve to have a President of the United States and a Congressman like Zach Nunn who fights for you every single day. That's what he's been doing. Now let me just give you a very concrete example of what fighting for you looks like. This is not just abstract; this is not words written on a paper. This is real people's lives and this is real dollars and cents. A few years ago, I talked to the owners of this factory who told me about the great products that they were making. They were paying their workers a good wage, and they were building a great business right here in Iowa. You know, this company was started in 2009, the year that I graduated from college. Sorry for anybody that makes me look kind of young. But here's the thing: to start a business in 2009 was a tough thing to do. It was the middle of the global financial crisis, and you had to take some real risk. And what did this business find after building a great business and after paying hundreds of people a solid wage? What did this business find? They found that foreign countries, in particular the People's Republic of China, were dumping goods, undercutting the wages of the workers of this factory, and driving this place out of business. They were using cheap labor, sometimes slave labor, to try to steal jobs from the hardworking people of this district and this factory floor. And for my entire life, we had Presidents and politicians of both parties who didn't do a single thing to stop it. They said, "Oh, by the way, absolutely. Bring those cheap goods into our country. Bring those things that were built by the hands of slaves rather than the hands of our own workers." And of course, we knew that when they drove those factories out of business, then they'd come in and jack up the price right after. So what happened? For 41 years, we all know the story. The factories closed down. My hometown, this is the story of my hometown where I grew up in a community that was proud, where people built things, where new homes were being made. And by the time I graduated from high school, the manufacturers were laying people off. Those big beautiful factories were shuttering their doors. We never had a political movement that said "enough is enough" until Donald J. Trump and Zach Nunn came along. And you know what we said? The Democrats, every day they will tell you they will fight against our policies to try to bring back American manufacturing. They will fight against our policies that try to fight back against those people who would dump that stuff into our communities. And you know what we say? We say that our tax policy ought to be pretty simple, and that's exactly what it has been. If you're building right here in Iowa, if you're making something right here in Iowa, if you're working every single day right here in Iowa, you deserve a tax cut, and that's exactly what we delivered with Congressman Nunn. But if you're a foreign country dumping that garbage into our country, if you're trying to undercut the wages of Iowa workers, if you're trying to steal the jobs from Iowa farmers and Iowa manufacturers, you know what you're going to do? You're going to pay a big fat tariff before you bring anything into our country because we're protecting the jobs of our workers and our people for a change. And that's all this is about. When you hear the Democrats attack the Trump administration's tax policies, that's what they're attacking. They're attacking the policies that make it harder for people to steal your jobs, and they're attacking the policies that give you more money to keep in your own pocket. Now, I talked to the factory owners just a couple of minutes ago, I should say. And you know what they told me? They told me that the working families' tax cuts, the tax cuts made possible by Congressman Nunn, meant that their business had a lot more money that they could give to workers in higher bonuses and higher salaries. They could hire more people; they could expand and create new jobs that didn't exist before. Isn't it nice to have a government that finally is fighting for you instead of fighting against you? And that's what we're trying to have in Washington, DC. Now there is so much I'm proud of, and so much that is moving in the right direction. Do you know, when I was going around the campaign trail in 2024, one of the number one things that young people said to me—young Americans in particular—is that they couldn't afford to buy a home? They couldn't afford their rent if that's what they wanted to do. And you know, for the first time we've seen in over five years, for eight months running, we have seen the costs of rent come down in the United States of America. Our young people are starting to get close. They're making progress, and they're starting to get to the point where they can finally afford their slice of the American dream. And you know why we did that and you know how we did it? It's actually not that complicated. It is a simple principle: American homes ought to belong to American families, not foreign investors, not foreign owners, and not Chinese investors who are buying up our farmland. Again, it's not rocket science; it's a simple principle that if you have a government that fights for you, it can make your life easier. It can make it easier for you to start a business or to work in an amazing job. We didn't do anything that was particularly difficult. We did something that was simple and common sense. And I think that's what defines the Republican party in Washington right now: it's simply about common sense. You want to lower the cost of homes for American families? Here's a simple thing: why don't you stop giving 30 million homes to illegal aliens? Why don't you seal the border and make sure those homes go to American families for a change? That's how you lower the cost of housing. If you want to make it easier for young Americans to start a family or to get a good job, stop giving jobs to foreign companies and stop giving jobs to foreign countries. Protect the good, well-paying jobs that exist right here in the United States of America, and that's what we did. And here is the final thing. When we talk about standing for you—again, whatever your views on a specific policy—there is maybe no more important issue than how you pay your taxes to the federal government. We just had tax day just a couple of weeks ago. How about you have a government that actually takes those resources as what they are? Your sweat, your tears, your time away from your family. And we protect every single one of those dollars. That's what we're doing by going after fraud finally in the federal government. Let me just give you a couple of examples. We had let fraud become so rampant in this country that people were able to get rich not by creating something amazing, not by employing someone, not by building something beautiful with their hands—they were able to get rich by defrauding every single person in this room. And they were taking money that should go to America's low-income families, to America's elderly, to people who are struggling in our communities. They were stealing it out of their pocket and stealing it out of your pocket so they could get rich. Let me give you just a couple of examples. There was a program in suburban Minneapolis. It's actually a national program but it was taken advantage of in suburban Minneapolis. This program makes it possible—the American people in their generosity make it possible—for the parents of young children who are autistic to get access to after-school services. I think that's a cool thing because, as Americans, we take care of one another. But you know what happened? That money wasn't going to the parents of autistic children; that money was going to Somalian fraudsters. Because somebody figured out in the illegal immigrant community that if they went around and said that all of the children in a particular household had that particular problem, then they could collect a lot of money from the federal government. And so what happened? You paid your taxes, those people got rich, and the families who needed those services never got to access those services. They were stolen from, and you were stolen from, and your government didn't do a single thing about it. Until, until Donald J. Trump and Congressman Nunn came along. What we did is very simple. We went to the State of Minnesota and said, "We want you to audit the people who are getting access to these funds. And if you're not willing to confirm that the people who are getting these benefits actually deserve these benefits, then you're not getting another dime of federal money. We cut them off." And that's exactly what we got to do. And ladies and gentlemen, people have asked me—you know, I'm the leader of the fraud task force in Washington—and people have asked me, is that a hard job? And I say, well, let me think. Finding fraud in the federal government is kind of like fishing in a barrel with dynamite. Every week I get a report from the task force—you know we have our weekly meeting—and I am shocked every single day by the things that we find. Just one of the all-stars on our fraud task force is our great Agriculture Secretary, Brooke Rollins. And Brooke has found out that we've got 355,000 people on SNAP benefits receiving double benefits. That we've got 186,000 dead people getting SNAP benefits. 186,000 dead Americans getting food stamps right now. Now I know the fake news media is going to say that the big headline from this speech is "JD Vance proposes that we take away food stamps." That's what they're going to say. I actually think that we should take food stamps away from dead people. I am guilty of that. I think that's a reasonable thing. I also was talking to Brooke backstage and she said, she said, "You know that there are people who have Lamborghinis..."—this is not a joke—"there are people who have Lamborghinis who currently receive SNAP benefits." I said, "Huh. That's interesting." You know, I'm a pretty conservative guy but I would think that like 95% of the United States of America would agree that if you are wealthy enough to afford a Lamborghini, then you are wealthy enough to not receive SNAP benefits from the American people. Is that common sense? And I could go down and down the list. We just served 22 investigatory procedures at the Department of Justice. We went from discovering what happened in Minneapolis to serving 22 subpoenas so that we could go after the fraud that's happening in Minneapolis. And we did that in just three months. And yes, ladies and gentlemen, when we find crime, those people are going to go to prison for defrauding you because that's exactly how it should be. Now the last thing I want to talk about, my friends, is we know that under the Biden administration Iowa farmers and farmers all over the United States of America were hit, and they were hit hard. What we've done using the President's trade authorities and using his incredible trade policies is that we have doubled the export market for Iowa farmers in just 18 months, and that is an amazing thing. Because what happened for years is that people would dump their agricultural products into the United States of America but said, "You're not allowed to sell great Iowa product overseas." Well, we flipped that on its head. We told all those countries, "You're done dumping that crap into the United States of America, and if you don't want to pay a higher tariff rate, you better let Iowa farmers sell in your country." And every single one of them have let Iowa farmers sell into their country. Again, it's amazing what happens when you got a President and a Congressman who's fighting for you instead of fighting against you. And that is the record that we've had over the past 18 months. USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! USA! So, let me just say a couple more things here. So one, and I was flipping around in my notes here because I've been trying to find the name of the person that Zach is running against. Because we're going to make fun of her a little bit now. Now here's the problem. You know, I'm sure—well, I don't know anything about her. What I do know is her voting record. I know what she has chosen to prioritize, and it's not you, and it's not your families, and it's not this district. Because here's the thing. There are problems and there are challenges. You know, one of the things I'm proudest of is that under the President's leadership, we have had American families about $1,300 richer from the day that he took office until today. That's between the working families' tax cuts, that's lowering the cost of rent—that's all that stuff combined. Now that's the good news. Let me give you the bad news. The honest piece of this is that under the Biden administration, the average American family lost about $3,000. So on the one hand, we got a great record to brag on. On the other hand, because of the hole that the Biden administration and the Democrats put us in, we got a lot more work to do. Okay? But we recognize that work, and we're excited about that work. That's why you sent us to Washington, DC. That's why you sent Zach to Washington, DC—was to work on these problems. We know right now that we've got a lot of Iowa farmers that would love to sell more E15 into the market, and we're fighting every day to help them. Now I said that, and I did not even see the E15 crew we got over here. I need one of those T-shirts. But the Congressman has got legislation that's working right now, and we're working on it every single day because, number one, we want Iowa farmers to have access to more markets, but number two, we want everybody—all of our fellow citizens across our 50 great states—to benefit from what's made right here in Iowa. That's why we're fighting for E15. We also know that a lot of our farmers are struggling with high fertilizer prices. I'm aware of that. As the President of the United States said, we got a little blip in the Middle East, we got to take care of some business on the foreign policy side, but you know what we're doing simultaneously? We're working with the Congressman and we're finding ways every single day to make sure that you guys and the farmers of this great state get access to the products that they need. We're working on it; we're working on it every single day. Now when I see that, when I see Iowa farmers who need to get that E15 to market, what is this...? Zach, you're going to have to help me out with her name here. I lost my page here. Okay, there we go. Sarah Trone Garriott. There it is. I'm on the wrong page here. So while Zach is fighting to make sure—for the benefit of Iowa, but frankly for the benefit of the whole country—that we get more E15 into the market all over the United States of America, his opponent voted to push biological males in girls' sports as early as kindergarten. While he fought very hard for the working families' tax cut to make sure that you got to keep more of your hard-earned money—and why you guys in fact are going to have the biggest tax refund season in American history—he was fighting for more money in your pocket. She was voting against banning gender transition surgeries for minors. She was voting against schools giving parental notification if students start changing their pronouns. Now, we all hear that and we all think that's pretty crazy. But recognize that not everybody agrees with us. Here's the thing: even if you don't think that's crazy, why is that the priority of a woman who wants to represent you in Washington, DC? Why isn't she working to increase your take-home pay? Why isn't she fighting for the farmers to get more of that E15 all over the United States of America? Why isn't she fighting to lower housing costs for the people of this district, the people of the state, and the people of the United States of America? And it goes back to the question that I asked at the very beginning of this discussion. We have two parties in Washington, DC. And I hate to say this because I was raised by blue-collar union Democrats. My Mamaw and Pappaw, they said that back when they were voting in the 80s and 90s, the man and woman who raised me—Mamaw and Pappaw—said "we're for Democrats because Democrats are for working people." And I don't think that's true anymore, my friends. And it's a heartbreaking thing. It's heartbreaking for a kid who came from a union Democrat family to realize that Democrats these days seem to care more about gender transition than they do about you keeping more of your hard-earned money. And I really do believe this: that there are Democrats like my Mamaw and Pappaw all over the great state of Iowa, but I hate to say it and I hate to break it to you, but there are none left in Washington, DC. The Democrats in Washington, DC—they want to trade stocks, insider trading for money, just like Nancy Pelosi did. The best part of the State of the Union address is when the President of the United States called out Nancy Pelosi for her stock trading. And I look over at her and she has got the sourest look I have ever seen on a human being's face. Turns out Nancy Pelosi likes nothing less than being called out for her insider stock trading. You know, we're just talking about that State of the Union. You know, this beautiful six-year-old girl—you may remember this, it was a few months ago—it was a very revealing moment about who our respective political parties stand for. Because this little girl had been shot by an illegal alien, but she had survived. Miraculously, she was in amazing condition; she was as sweet, just a really cute kid. And you know, I'm the father of a six-year-old boy. He's actually here with me; he's backstage. And this girl was so proud—she was proud to be there in the chamber and she gives her dad a big hug. And the Republicans all stand and cheer and the Democrats all sit on their hands. And I remember thinking to myself, "What is wrong with you that you cannot stand up and cheer for a six-year-old girl who survived an assassin's bullet?" What is wrong with you that you cannot vote to give more money in the pockets of Iowa farmers and Iowa factory workers? What is wrong with you when you cannot stand up for the Americans—some of whom literally have taken bullets in the United States of America—but you fight every single day for illegal immigrants who have no right to be in the United States in the first place? And what does it say about a political party that they have fought more against our anti-fraud efforts than they ever did to protect your dollars and to protect the benefits of the people who use those benefits? My friends, this is not a normal election. This is not a normal political environment. This is a contest between a party that wants to take all of your money and give it to illegal aliens and a contest between gentlemen like Zach Nunn who fight every single day for you. And I have a simple question here, which is: do you want somebody who votes to give your money to illegal aliens? Do you want somebody who votes to raise your taxes? Do you want somebody who supports defrauding you and taking money away from poor kids so they can give it to the fraudsters? Then do we want crazy far-left Democrats in Washington, DC? Here's what we do want. Do we want somebody who fights for you every single day? Do we want somebody who has put more money in your pocket? Do we want somebody who gives E15 all over the United States of America so Iowa farmers can profit from it? So what do we want? We want Congressman Zach Nunn and you got to get him to Washington, DC. So let me just say one more thing, my friends, and then I will hit the road. You know, like I said, I've got my six-year-old son back there. And the backstory of why, by the way, I started in Washington, DC—we flew from Washington to Ohio, I voted in Ohio's primary today. From Ohio, that's right. The media asked me who I voted for. None of your business. Went from Ohio to Oklahoma City to do a fundraiser for the Republican National Committee and then I came here to be with all of you this evening. And you know, I can't wait to tell my wife when we get home that we've watched about nine episodes of X-Men and my son has eaten four chocolate chip cookies. She's going to be very pleased; she's going to be very pleased with the Vice President. But here's the thing. It kind of just worked out—I wasn't planning this—but I've got my six-year-old because last week I took his little sister on a trip and he was feeling a little jealous. And you know, when he's a kindergartner, it's not like missing school—no offense, but one day of kindergarten's not going to cause him too many problems. So he came out with me today and we've had such a good day and it's just been an amazing thing. I always enjoy coming out and doing events like this, but to do it with your six-year-old son by your side is really an amazing thing. But as it happened, when we landed here in Des Moines at the airport, we met a couple of Gold Star families from Iowa. A couple of you probably know this, maybe all of you know this: that in what's going on in the Middle East, this operation in Iran, Iowa has borne a heavier burden maybe than any other state in the Union. This great state has lost about half of our KIA, and I know that you all will join me in saying that we love them and we're proud of them and we're praying for them every single day. Also on the tarmac, a couple of Gold Star families met me. A couple of families who've lost people just in the last nine or so months—one in Iran and then one in Syria about nine or so months ago. And I was talking to them and one of them told me—I had no idea, I didn't plan to see them but they were here and of course I was honored to see them. It's an amazing thing as a Vice President of the United States to meet the parents and the families of the people who put on a uniform and sacrifice their life for this country. So I'm always happy to do it, always honored to do it. But one family—and I won't use their name, I don't know how political they are if they're political at all—but they told me that their son's birthday was today. And so I get in the car with my little six-year-old, you know, and I look into those big eyes.
