I probably visit only 40 or 50 of them over the last few years. I actually love learning about business, I read a lot, and I try to learn from everybody I meet. I'll say that. How would you define the corporate culture now? I think that we are half unlocked. I think half the people now embrace risk. I think the growth side has been embraced now. People accept that we can grow, we will grow, we have the right to grow. I think that people have accepted that we want to be very productive and very lean, so the amount of overhead is decreasing and that seems to be a muscle memory that is there. I would say on the risk side, we're only halfway there. I think I would like to unlock people's ability to take risk a bit more. In the past, you did everything yourself. Now you partner with some of the other great companies. Just what kind of change in mentality did that take? So, if you think that it's a fixed pie, then you're going to always fight for the largest slice for yourself. I think that's just natural. If you think that by partnering you change the shape and size of the pie—and so you've got to convince yourself of that—then you say well I'll partner and I'll share, but I'm sharing from a bigger pie. That's kind of my mindset. I kind of put it this way: if you partner with somebody, are you going to increase your chances of winning? And the answer is like, usually yes. If that's the case, you're increasing the size of the pie. You have something called getting fired mentality. What what what does that mean? Fifteen years ago, I walked into the office of a mentor of mine in the morning and I was kind of frustrated. I said, like, "look, you know, I really believe I need to say these things, but I'm afraid that if I say them somebody will want to try to fire me." And this person turned around and told me, "Arvind, you should live in the pleasure of being fired." And I looked at him and he said, "that doesn't mean that you always pick a fight, it doesn't mean that you just make disruptive things for the sake of it, but if you are living in the pleasure of being fired, that means you're not afraid of being fired." That means you'll do the right thing. And if you have confidence in your abilities, why does it matter? And that is truly freeing. And I encourage people to think like that and to be like that, and I think it really has been very powerful. Have you been close to being fired? Couple of times. When? the last time was probably in 2014. What happened? I was trying to make a decision that was popular with some people and was extremely unpopular with some others. So they decided that they were going to try and get me fired. They almost succeeded. Yeah, not quite. No, not quite. Now you've been in one company for a long time, right? Why have you stayed for 35 years? I've done so many different things. Has that been, has that been a deliberate policy from the company to move you around, or is it you yourself? No. I think it was, I always just put it like this: If you put blinders on you'll keep doing one thing for a long time. If you keep yourself open to opportunity, those opportunities come. I spent my first 10 years—I'll use probably a more extreme example—I was an introverted researcher for 10 years. Then somebody asked me, "Do you want to come run a business?" You ask the average researcher if they want to come run a business and they're like, "I know nothing about it." I ran to it. I did that for five years. Then somebody came and said, "Do you want to run the database business?" And I said, "I know many areas of software; the one area I know nothing about is database, so yes." Then they said, "Do you want to run our semiconductor business?" I said yes. So I think that that allowed me to just do so many different things. In some sense, I've had seven different careers even though it's been in one company. Your father was a major general in the Indian Army. Is that what you, where you got your lack of fear from? A big part of it. He definitely was fearless in his personal approach to life, in how he lived his life. Part of it was, I mean, if you spend weeks in the jungles at 18,000 feet, you tend to be like that. But part of it was probably his natural personality. But you observe that and then you observe him and his friends; they were very similar. It wasn't that that dissimilar. And you get, not so much a lack of fear, but there is also the fact that any military professional also prepares a lot. And so you say the phrase that I think is common in business: "Luck favors the prepared." So you put the two together. How do you relax? I like to read. What do you read? Almost anything. I was reading, I was reading a book on the technology trap, which is by an Oxford economic historian on how technology can evolve. I read books on how and what is happening on geopolitics. You love to read about why do different cultures behave in certain ways. I love to read biographies, whether it's of Alexander Hamilton or Benjamin Franklin. Do you read poetry? I do not read poetry. I've read a little bit of poetry, but I do not read. What kind of music, what kind of music do you listen to? I mostly listen to the music I listened to in college, which is classic rock from the '70s and '80s. Such as? The Talking Heads was the last concert I went to. I love David Byrne. I've actually listened to him maybe a half dozen times live. Eric Clapton many times, Queen, Jethro Tull, Pink Floyd—but that's a little bit... you can... there's a few of their people around but not the whole group; they are just some recent examples. All my kind of music as well. Arvind, finally, what is your advice to young people? Number one, do something you have a passion for and an interest in. It's really important to wake up enthused about the day. Yep. Do it with people that you respect and can learn from. I don't say—so that's not necessarily your friends and popular—but do it with people that you like working with. Do not ever focus on the title or the compensation. I believe if you have the first two, those will come. I'm not saying don't focus on that at all, but don't make it your criteria for doing something. Good advice. Arvind, thank you. Thank you, Nicolai. Good to run this.

President Trump and the First Lady Participate in a Military Mother's Day Event

The White House(3.1M) ·37:24 ·58.1K views · 2026-05-06
Donald Trump (President) Melania Trump (First Lady)
President Donald J.
TL;DR President Donald J. Trump and First Lady Melania Trump hosted a White House event to honor the "quiet power" and sacrifices of military mothers while providing updates on a $1.5 trillion military investment and record-breaking recruitment numbers. The Moral Foundation:...

TL;DR

President Donald J. Trump and First Lady Melania Trump hosted a White House event to honor the "quiet power" and sacrifices of military mothers while providing updates on a $1.5 trillion military investment and record-breaking recruitment numbers.

Honoring the Sacrifice of Military Families

  • The Moral Foundation: First Lady Melania Trump emphasized that military mothers build the "moral foundation" of families and the nation, noting their resilience during deployments and the unique emotional bond between mother and child.
  • Recognition of Personal Sacrifice: The President and First Lady highlighted the specific challenges faced by military families, including:
      • Frequent Relocations: Recognition of families like Britney Stevenson’s, who moved 10 times across six duty stations in 15 years.
      • Missed Milestones: Acknowledgment of mothers who miss birthdays, holidays, and first words due to service obligations.
  • Gold Star and Healing Families: The event highlighted the recovery of Specialist Andrew Wolf, who survived a terrorist ambush in D.C. The President credited his mother, Melody Wolf, with "willing" his recovery through faith and positive thinking.

Military Readiness and Technological Advancement

  • Historic Investment: President Trump announced a $1.5 trillion upgrade to the U.S. military, emphasizing that all equipment is "Built in the USA."
  • Technological Superiority: The President cited a recent defensive success where 111 high-speed missiles aimed at a U.S. aircraft carrier were all successfully intercepted.
      • Missile Defense: Highlighted the Patriot missile system and newer technologies as being "100% accurate" in recent skirmishes.
      • Space Force: Claimed the U.S. is now leading Russia and China in space capabilities by an insurmountable margin.
  • Recruitment Surge: After experiencing what he described as the "worst recruiting in history," the President reported that every branch of the military is currently setting new records for recruitment and applicant quality.

Geopolitical and Domestic Security Updates

  • Iran and Venezuela: The President described current operations in Iran as a "wall of steel" blockade, stating the Iranian Navy is effectively "out of business" and predicting they will soon negotiate a deal regarding nuclear weapons.
  • Domestic Crime Reduction: The briefing noted significant drops in crime in cities where federal or National Guard resources were deployed:
      • Washington, D.C.: Reported a 92% decrease in "bad numbers" (violent crime).
      • New Orleans/Memphis: Reported crime reductions of 79-80% following specific interventions.
  • Maritime Drug Interdiction: The President claimed a 97% reduction in drugs entering the country by sea due to enhanced naval and Coast Guard activity.

White House Infrastructure and Personnel

  • New Presidential Ballroom: Construction is currently underway on a new, secure White House ballroom designed to hold over 1,000 guests.
      • Security Specs: The building features "knockout panels" and glass six inches thick to repel various weapons.
  • Cabinet and Staff Acknowledgments:
      • Secretary Linda McMahon: Praised for her work in Education.
      • Pete Hegseth: Referred to as "Secretary of War," he was commended for his leadership and synergy with military generals.
      • John Ratcliffe: Recognized for his role leading the CIA.

***

"At the very heart of America's strength lies the boundless love and quiet power of mothers. We are the most devoted teachers, gently nurturing empathy, inspiring dreams, and guiding our children towards goodness." — Melania Trump, First Lady of the United States
"We have the greatest military that we've ever had. We have the greatest military in the world, by far. There's nobody even close. And I think we've displayed that." — Donald J. Trump, President of the United States
"I have no doubt that [my son] will be 100% back to Andrew within a few years... independent, driving, being a young bachelor again. We have a strong faith in God, and God is what brought our son through this." — Melody Wolf, Military Mother
Ladies, the President of the United States, Donald J. Trump, and First Lady Melania Trump. Good afternoon and welcome to the White House. I would like to extend a warm welcome to our Vice President, J.D. Vance, and his wife, Usha. Welcome. It is our pleasure to be joined today by Secretary McMahon and Mrs. Hegseth, and I am also honored to welcome Mrs. Melody Wolf and Lorraine Hock. To my chief of staff, Haley Harrison, who is preparing to welcome her second child into the world: Haley, you are a wonderful mother, and I wish you and your growing family all great things. I often think about the brave people who make the ultimate sacrifice for our freedom. When I went to honor the fallen service members from the 103rd Sustainment Command at Dover Air Force Base last March, I realized that words cannot comfort the all-consuming grief that family members, and mothers in particular, experience. The loss is overwhelming. Although I understand the deep pride these families feel in their loved one's service to our great Republic, I cannot fathom the depth of their sorrow. A mother is awestruck when she welcomes her child into the world. After nine months, the miracle of life creates a fascinating bond between mother and child. With each breath, day by day, this bond deepens, bringing forth emotions never felt before. Quite frankly, these are emotions understood only by women. Only a mother can carry this type of powerful connection with her child, which is also too profound for words. This is a time of military conflict for America, and whereby we honor our selfless and valiant service members regularly. Today is the day we hold their mothers in the highest regard. It is these military mothers who find themselves alone, awake in their thoughts in the darkest hours of the night. Of course, we must also remember the mothers with a spouse overseas, praying for a safe return so their family can become whole once again. At the very heart of America's strength lies the boundless love and quiet power of mothers. We are the most devoted teachers, gently nurturing empathy, inspiring dreams, and guiding our children towards goodness. We help them rise with courage when life grows difficult. In every hug, every story read at bedtime, and every sacrifice made without complaint, mothers build the moral foundation of our families. In doing so, America's mothers help build the soul of our nation. I pray you find enduring strength as your loved one serves in defense of our freedom. Most know my husband as the strong Commander-in-Chief, but his empathy transcends the role and shapes a caring leader who constantly remembers each and every American soldier is someone's child. Distinguished guests, please join me with a warm welcome for our President, Donald J. Trump. Thank you, honey. Great job. So I want to thank everybody for being here. We have so many things to talk about, but there's nothing more important than military moms, that I can tell you. Because without... without you, I don't think we'd be in this house. I don't think this house probably ever would have been built a long time ago. So important. I want to start off by saying we were in our great Vice President's right here, and we're in a... I call it a skirmish, because that's what it is, it's a skirmish. And we're doing unbelievably well, as we did in Venezuela, where it was rapid, over in one day. And we're doing pretty much equally as well, I would say, larger, but we're doing very well in Iran. And it's going very smoothly, and we'll see what happens. They want to make a deal, they want to negotiate. And I think that military mothers would want to hear that. Do you agree? I think that's appropriate. Somebody would say, "Oh, that's not appropriate." I think military mothers want to hear about this stuff. And we have the greatest... we have the greatest... we have the greatest military that we've ever had. We have the greatest military in the world, by far. There's nobody even close. And I think we've displayed that. I built the military in my first term, much of it. It was when I inherited the military, it was like when I inherited the border. The border was a mess, and the military was a mess. A lot of you know that because you were... you were there to witness it. And now we have the strongest military that we've ever had. And we're just getting an approval for 1.5 trillion dollars to upgrade our military still further. But when I was spending all that money in my first term—it was a great term, very unbelievable term—we had the best economic numbers that the country's ever had. Now we're going to blow them away. We're blowing them away right now. But I built the military in my first term with the help of a lot of great admirals, generals, and everybody else. And I didn't know we'd be using it quite so much, but that's okay. And whatever it is, because in the case of Iran, we're not going to let Iran have a nuclear weapon. We're not going to let that happen. And we won't let that happen. And so we're... we're dealing with people that want to make a deal very much, and we'll see whether or not they can make a deal that's satisfactory to us. We have it very much under control. The blockade is unbelievable. The Navy's been incredible. The job they did, it's like a... it's like a wall of steel. Nobody goes through. In particular, the Iranians, they're not getting anything through, one way or the other. So they're out of business. And we'll see whether or not they are agreeing, and if they don't agree, they'll end up agreeing shortly thereafter. So that's the way it is. And I thought you might want to have a little rundown. Now the fake news is all happy because they can hear what we had to say. And I have to say, J.D.'s doing a fantastic job. He's working with us, with him and all of the team. Marco and we have Steve Wittkoff, Jared. We have a whole group. The generals and General Kane has been fantastic. Pete Hegseth, incredible. We have just a great group of people. CIA, John Ratcliffe, he's great. I think Mrs. Ratcliffe is even here someplace. Are you here, Mrs. Ratcliffe? I think somebody said yes. But we have amazing people, we have an amazing military, and I think it's on display. It's... sometimes you'd rather not have it be on display, but they're there for a purpose. And the greatest military anywhere in the world. And the world is watching us as we do our thing. And we have the respect of the world, which we didn't have two years ago. We were a laughingstock all over the world. Now we have the hottest country anywhere in the world, in every way. So I just want to congratulate you all, because you're a big part of it. And I want to thank you, Melania. Amazing the job you've done. She did a little movie and it became the number one movie. Now, you know... and I can't exaggerate, because if I did, they would be all over. It wasn't number one, it was number two, it was number five. So I have to be very, very careful with it. And then it went streaming. It's a different world. I didn't even know what they were talking about. They said, "Oh, and it became number one streaming too." So congratulations. That's an amazing achievement. And most importantly, and most importantly to her, she's done an incredible job as First Lady. And she's an incredible mom. She has a little boy who's quite tall. He's a little boy to us, but he's quite tall, right? And he's great, Barron. And she takes great care of him. And thank you. Great mom. Today we're honored to welcome to the White House a group of strong and resilient women who represent the best of our country, the amazing military mothers. So important. And we want to thank you all for being here. So many of you I know or recognize and I want to thank you very much because there's nothing like the White House, right? Is it the coolest? And by the way, right behind me, see that? You call it a window or a drape. I call it a knockout panel. Because behind that, in fact, if you want to see it, I think we could work it out, but behind that window is a... and drape. In fact, the drapes are so nice, I think I'm going to leave them. How am I going to get a nicer something than that? But that's the entrance to the new ballroom that's being built. Which will be... which everybody... which everybody likes, especially since last Saturday night they like it even more because it'll have a thing called security. It'll be nice, but right back there is where we're building. So if you hear any pounding noises, the pile drivers are out. It's already up to the first floor, but we're... we're a little bit ahead of schedule, right on budget. But it's going to be something great. It'll be one of the most beautiful buildings of its kind anywhere in the world. And you know, for 150 years, presidents have wanted a room. This is the ballroom right here. This is... and it's too small. We had the King and Queen of a lovely country, United Kingdom, and I had... we would have had at least a thousand, everybody wanted to be here, and we had 112 people because this is a lovely room, but it's not big enough. It's got one... and it's been that way for many, many years. So every president has been calling, but I was in a position being a builder and having built many ballrooms and many other things, I'm... I'm good at ballrooms. And we're giving you the best... I believe it's the best... it'll be the best ever built. And it will have windows that are thicker than any ballroom I've ever built. I used to... I usually have glass that's about a quarter of an inch thick. This is about six inches thick. This can repel a lot of different... a lot of different weapons. But it's going to be incredible. It's going to be, aside from being secure, it's going to be the most... I think it's going to be the most beautiful ballroom ever built. And it's something that the White House has needed, the presidents have needed. And it's an honor to be involved, and it really has become very popular. When people see the design, they really... they really like it. Been getting some great reviews. Also with us is Secretary of Education Linda McMahon, my friend, who's done such a fantastic job, wherever you are, Linda. Hi, Linda. She's great, what a job. Along with Jennifer Hegseth. Jennifer. Hi, Jennifer. He's doing some job, I'll tell you. He's doing a job. You know he stood up, he was the only one. He... he was standing up in the ballroom the other day in the event, the White House... we call... they call it the White House Dinner. You know they were saying, "Can you use..." well, when you use that term, I guess you probably can use it, but we had a little dinner the other night and your husband was standing on the top of a table. Everyone else was under the table. So that's what we want of our Secretary of War, right? We want somebody... but he was standing on top of the table. Everyone else was under the damn table. Anyway, he's spent... he's doing a fantastic job. He and the General, those are great news conferences. They work very well together, I can tell you. The wife of Pete Hegseth, and say hello to Pete. Michelle Ratcliffe. Michelle. Where is Michelle? I think she's here someplace. Michelle. And the wife of CIA Director John Ratcliffe, and Jackie Emmer. Jackie. You are so great, Jackie. I know Jackie well because her husband and I, we didn't know each other, we... we fought. You know, in politics, you can fight. And then when we got to know each other, we loved each other, right? But she always liked Trump, and she convinced her husband, who's a big power in the House, she convinced her husband that Trump was much better than he thought. And I remember you, I like you. Now I really like Em, he's an incredible... but I liked you more and I'm always... because you were there long before him, right? But Jackie, you're great. I appreciate it. Thank you very much. And as you know, Tom Emmer is the House Majority Whip and he's a fantastic person. Real leader. Comes from an interesting place, a place that's under investigation by our great Vice President. They're finding a lot and Tom Emmer was one of the biggest complainers about what was going on in Minnesota and lots of other places. I also want to say a special thank you to the many moms among our fantastic military aides and social aides who support us here at the White House. They've been incredible, all of you have been so incredible, and I want to thank you very much for being with us today, it's beautiful. The women in this room have sacrificed for our country in countless ways. Among you are veterans and active duty service members, proud spouses, and the parents of those who serve in uniform. Amazing people. The most amazing people. Many of you have missed cherished moments, and you really have. You've spent so much time taking care of the ones you love. You've missed holidays, birthday parties, graduations, or even a child's first words. Others have moved more times than anybody can even count. And I don't know if you love doing it or not, but you get very good at it. You have the way, you know the way, you know the way to do it. But you do it all because you love America, you love our country. And this week as we prepare to celebrate Mother's Day, we take this opportunity on behalf of all of us to say thank you very much for the incredible job. You do it with love, so it's not... I don't think there's no such thing as a job if you love it. But we'll call it a job nevertheless. The job you do is incredible and everybody respects you so much. You have no idea. So I just want to thank you all and thank you all for being here. Joining us is someone who knows the incredible sacrifice of service, Britney Stevenson, from Camp Lejeune. That's a hell of a camp. That's beyond a camp. The word "camp," I don't know, it doesn't look like a camp to me. Her parents were both Marines and in 1983 her dad survived the Marine barracks bombing in Beirut. We're talking about that even to this day, that was something. Britney loved the Marines so much that she married a Marine herself, and the love of her life, Taylor. Over 15 years, her family has moved 10 homes, 10 different homes, and been assigned to six duty stations from all over the world. And now her daughter Ava is part of the Marine Corps Junior ROTC program and wants to become a Marine as well. So Britney, your family is the definition of Semper Fi and always faithful. And I want to thank you. Where are you? Where are you? Please stand up. Would you like to come up and say... come here, Britney. Come on. "Thank you, not really sure what to say, but this is such an honor and a privilege to be here. I'm truly touched to represent my family, the Marine Corps, Camp Lejeune, and military moms and spouses like you all. So thank you." Good job. She had no idea she was going to be doing that, but I... I looked at her, I said she looks so great, I'm going to get her up here, right? That was beautiful. Thank you very much. As Melania mentioned, we're also honored to be joined by a military mother who sacrificed far more than most, that's Melody Wolf. And you know, I love the name Melody because for a long time, you know, they have spell-correct and word-correct on these crazy machines that we use to put out Truths, or they used to be called tweets. And every time I wrote "Melania," it would correct to "Melody." So I'd do things and I work very fast, very fast. Blah-blah-blah. And I talk about... and I say, "And Melania is fantastic and Happy Mother's Day Melania, our great First Lady Melania." But it would spell correct and word correct to "Melody." And sometimes I wouldn't proofread it and I'd say, "Hmm." And I would get just absolutely decimated, these people would decimate me. They said he didn't know the name of his wife, he keeps calling her... and I said what the hell is wrong with this machine? I didn't know about that little feature. But I got that corrected eventually. You know who corrected it? The military. I said come here, you got to correct this, you're killing me. I took more abuse. She's been called Melody a lot. Anyway, I had to explain it to you this way. I stood up here to explain it to you. I apologize. Last year, Melody's 24-year-old son, Andrew Wolf, of the West Virginia National Guard, was on patrol in Washington, D.C., helping to restore law and order to our capital city. And I spoke to her, this woman is amazing. Along with her comrade, specialist Sarah Backstrom, who was just a fantastic person. He was ambushed by a terrorist and shot in the head. A violent, horrible person did this. Nobody thought Andrew was going to make it. But Melody told me, and I called her that evening in the hospital. Sarah passed away also from a shot to the head by these people, these horrible, horrible people. But Melody was the one person... In fact, Melody's husband, who's great, he didn't feel the same way. But she said, "I have no doubt, sir, I'm telling you, he's going to be fine. Don't worry about it. He's going to be fine." I never saw anything like it, it was really pretty amazing. And she was right. A week went by, another week went by, I'd call. I'd say, "How is he doing?" "Sir, he's doing really well." And the doctors would say, "I think he maybe will make it." And she turned out to be right. It was sort of a miracle, actually. And I've never seen—you know, they talk about the power of positive thinking—I've never seen anything like this one. J.D., you wouldn't believe it. He had no chance. I'm telling you, and she willed it. And now... it's really something very special that I think I hear he's just doing fantastic. It was a Bible verse from the book of Isaiah, "Do not fear, for I am with you." With God's help Andrew has made a really incredible recovery. It's hard to believe. And during my State of the Union address, we were privileged to award him the Purple Heart. This was a real Purple Heart. This was a... a terrible thing that happened. And Melody, where are you Melody? There you are. Hi. I haven't seen you in a little while, but it's true the story, right? You were the only one. You know that you were the only one. She was so positive, I don't think she heard anybody else. And your husband's doing okay? He feels much better about it. He was... he was not feeling too good. I think you should... you want to say something about your boy and how... come on up here, Melody. No, it's an amazing... it's such an amazing story. Come. We went through a couple of bad nights together, right? "Yes. I first want to say that we had the nation behind us at a time when nobody thought our boy would make it. He was 24 years old, he was a young man on the streets of D.C., a shot to the left part of his brain. We have a strong faith in God, and God is what brought our son through this. He gave us amazing backing. He gave us amazing doctors, he gave us amazing nurses, a great... and our community, our nation, our community, our just all of the support and the support of the White House medical staff. Anything that our son needed was met. We are just truly thankful and he—we're a little over five months—he came home from the hospital April 2nd, and he is now enjoying being home. He's getting back into playing golf. He loves cornhole, as you know. Yes, uh-huh. He loves doing so many activities and so we're truly blessed that we're able to get him into some outpatient care closer to home. We do have another surgery coming up in September and just recovery is my understanding that will happen for quite some time, but I have no doubt that he will be 100% back to Andrew within a few years, independent, driving, being a young bachelor again. So thank you so much." Incredible. I'm so proud of you. That's incredible. Nobody really can understand it, but I was with the General who was fantastic, right? And he said, "Not much of a chance, maybe no chance." I mean I hate to say it even, but no chance. They all said the same thing and she said, "No, God is with him. I'm telling you, President, God is with him, he's going to be okay." And I get reports that he's amazing, right? So congratulations, thank you very much. You taught us all something. Appreciate it. Also joining us is an outstanding active duty Air Force mom, Lieutenant Colonel Amanda Colazo. She's an MQ-9 Reaper drone pilot, whoa, that's pretty good. An evaluator, a weapons officer, and desk officer at the Defense Threat Reduction Agency. She has traveled to 20 countries, flown over 7,000 hours, and eliminated many high-value targets. She's a great... great talent, they say. Our military is strong again and as Amanda says to herself, and we are as a nation, we are really respected again. She said that, and it's true. So Amanda, thank you very much. Where are you Amanda? Where's... come on up here Amanda, I got to do this. You know, they're not... they're not expected to, but they're doing very well. Come on up. That's great. "Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you, Melania, for hosting this event. Thank you, Cindy, for inviting me out here. I just want to say I'm proud to be an American, I am proud to serve our nation in the world's greatest Air Force. Thank you for all of the moms out here and for your spouses, your kids, yourselves. So thank you all and Happy Mother's Day." Thank you. She is very sharp, isn't she? She'll be running for office someday, huh? Great. Thank you very much, appreciate it. As Commander-in-Chief, I will always support our great service members and their families. We've invested a record-setting trillion, and now it's up to 1.5 trillion dollars, all built in the USA, by the way. All right, so it's a lot of jobs too, you know. Some people said I did it for that, actually I didn't. I did it for the military, but it's a byproduct. We have tremendous numbers of very talented people building all of the things that we do. And we make the best equipment anywhere in the world, by far. They're all ordering it for us. In fact, we've... I've given a special edict that the defense companies who have the best equipment, they're not making it fast enough. And now they're all building plants all over the place. I said, "You're not allowed to buy back stock anymore." I told some of these people that are making millions and millions of dollars, "You got to reduce your salary, I'm going to reduce your salary, you're going to put it into weapons." And... it's been incredible the spirit we have. And they're building, some of them are building six, seven plants right now to make the Patriots and the Tomahawks. Tomahawks are unbelievable. Patriots are unbelievable. We had... recently, we had 111 missiles shot at one of our very valuable objects, something called an aircraft carrier. Think of this: 111 missiles, high range, high quality, speed missiles, very high, very fast, very top of the line. 111 out of 111 missiles shot at, you know... Well, it used to be you'd like to say a billion dollar boat, now they're about 18 billion dollars, they're very expensive. But 111 coming at them at 3,500 miles an hour, all 111 were shot down and didn't even get close to the ship. And they're all laying at the bottom of the ocean along with the Iranian Navy. They're all laying very nicely under... under the water. But think of that. Five years ago a thing like that would have been impossible. Ten years ago it's not even thinkable. Ronald Reagan actually thought of the concept, they called it Star Wars, but it wasn't a concept that anybody thought would ever work. But you know, you have these things coming at you and we have the Patriots, the Patriots are the best of them. And we have other things also, but it's when you think you hit 100% of the missiles coming at you. It's not like oh, let's let a couple get through, it's not that bad. One gets through, it's problems. So we can do things today and no other country has anything like that. They don't have anything with the power, with the strength and with the—you know it's accuracy really, it's accuracy. Got to hit, it's like... it's like hitting a needle in a haystack, they were saying. And yet they do it every time, it's pretty amazing. And we have the most talented people operating that equipment. For that you have to be high IQ. And you have to have no choke, because you have about 12 seconds to make a decision and take the aim and you have to be very good mathematically, you have to know what you're doing, you have to be very smart. You have to see the education, the school they go to, the best schools in the world, some of them come out of... I know I called up a couple, I said, "Where did you go to school?" "I went to MIT." Oh, good, that's good. So it's you know we have—it's a very different, frankly it's a very different military in many ways. But we have invested a record-setting amount of dollars and the US Armed Forces is now stronger than it's ever been and we're getting stronger and getting stronger and it's so important. Every branch is setting records in recruitment and I'm very proud of the Space Force because that was a branch that—a new one that came up—and we were losing to Russia and to China in space. And we had it as part of the Air Force, but it wasn't really thought of, you know once it's part of something. And we put a great general in charge and the Space Force is now leading the other countries, China, Russia, and there is no fourth, really. And leading them by—we saw numbers the other day—leading, we're leading in space by so much it's we're never going to be caught. So I'm very proud of Space Force. First time that that's taken place, a new force. The last one was the Air Force and this is—that was 78 years ago, so this is brand new and we're very proud of it. It's turned out to be I think it's going to be one of our most important as time goes by. A lot of the things that you see, a lot of the accuracy of the weapons is caused because of what we created with Space Force, so we're very proud of that. But every branch is setting records in recruitment when last year, if you remember, before I came into office—actually after the election it started picking up rapidly to be honest with you. November 5th we had a little election, we did quite well. We won every swing state, we won the popular vote, we won everything. We won 87% of the counties. We won it all. What I will tell you, right after that election it started, but before that we had the worst recruiting in the history of our country. People didn't want to be in the armed services. They also—they didn't want to be police, they didn't want to be firemen, they didn't want to be anything with a uniform representing our country. And it was the lowest number we've ever had. And I can say very confidently that at this moment we have the highest recruitment, the most successful recruitment for the military: Army, Navy, the Navy, Air Force, Marines, Coast Guard, Space Force, all of it. We have the highest recruitment, the best recruitment that we've ever had. We have lines of people wanting to get in, and we're taking people based on their fitness and their quality, we're taking—you wouldn't believe what the difference it is. And Washington, D.C. is an example, and that's why it's so sad when Melody comes up and talks about that one event. Because you know the numbers in Washington, D.C. are so incredible. The numbers have gone down from—we had a place that was so so unsafe, our great capital, and now it's considered one of the safest cities anywhere in the United States. We went to Memphis, and Memphis was a... was a killing field practically, and now it's down, the numbers are down, crime 80%. In New Orleans, Louisiana, the governor called up, Jeff Landry, he said, "Sir we have the Mardi Gras coming up and we have a lot of crime in the city, can you do something?" And we put our people there, mostly National Guard, but whatever was necessary. And we have crime down 79% after two and a half, three months. And by the way, if they had the brains in Chicago they'd ask us to go there, we'll solve the problem for them very quickly. We'd go to San Francisco very quickly, San Francisco would be so easy for us. We'd go to New York and help them out, we'd love to help them out. And we'd get crime numbers down 70%, 80%, 90% in a matter of months. We took it in in D.C. was really bad—well I don't want to go into the numbers in terms of using the word murder but it was really bad and it's gone from that to being down 92%. And actually I'm trying to figure you know the the 8% who they are. One of the things we done—we did—and some people like it, but the numbers are—the numbers speak for themselves. If you really get down you see drug traffic coming into our country is way down and by sea. By sea, by ocean, by the water, you know a lot of people say, "What do you mean by sea? You said 'see' like vision?" No it's the S-E-A. But it's down 97%. Think of it, 97%. For years they'd grab a boat and they'd bring them in and they'd see a judge and they'd let them out and the next day he's back in his country bringing more drugs in and nothing worked. We're down nothing, get worse, worse, worse. Down 97%. And we're trying to figure who the 3% are because we consider them to be maybe the bravest people anywhere in the world. You look at those boats, pew, pew. And you feel terribly except for one thing. Those boats—25,000 people die from just a couple of those boats, think of it, from drugs, ruined families, ruined everything. And when you see that you say thank you very much. But drugs coming in by sea are down 97%. And now we've started the land force, which is much easier and you'll hear some complaints from some people in like representatives from Mexico and other places. But if they're not going to do the job, we're going to do the job, and they understand that. So that's sort of the easy one by comparison to the sea. We had to do the sea first, you had no idea how much came in by water. And very little, almost nothing is coming in right now. They don't want to do that anymore. But no one should ever doubt the resolve of America's military moms. You're incredible from the start of our Republic down to today. Our nation's been strengthened by the love and devotion and the commitment of incredible women. These are really incredible—you are really incredible women, who've served our country, supported our troops, and poured out their care and compassion for this nation that we all love. So I just want to thank everybody, and Happy Mother's Day, and God bless you all. Thank you very much, thank you.

FFmpeg: The Incredible Technology Behind Video on the Internet | Lex Fridman Podcast #496

Lex Fridman (Host, Lex Fridman Podcast) Jean-Baptiste Kempf (Lead developer of VLC and President of VideoLAN) Kieran Kunhya (FFmpeg contributor and Codec engineer)
FFmpeg and VLC serve as the invisible open-source backbone of modern digital media, sustained by a tiny cohort of volunteer developers prioritizing low-level assembly optimization and ethical code quality over corporate monetization.
TL;DR FFmpeg and VLC serve as the invisible open-source backbone of modern digital media, sustained by a tiny cohort of volunteer developers prioritizing low-level assembly optimization and ethical code quality over corporate monetization. Despite facing severe developer...

TL;DR

FFmpeg and VLC serve as the invisible open-source backbone of modern digital media, sustained by a tiny cohort of volunteer developers prioritizing low-level assembly optimization and ethical code quality over corporate monetization. Despite facing severe developer burnout, security alarmism from tech giants, and immense scale, these projects are actively expanding to preserve human history and power the next generation of ultra-low-latency spatial and robotic computing.

The Technical Magic of Modern Video Codecs

  • Asymmetric Compression Demands: Video compression targets a 100x to 1000x reduction in file size to make streaming viable. The process is mathematically complex and asymmetric, requiring significantly more CPU power to encode once than to decode millions of times on consumer devices.
  • Human Perception Optimization: Codecs leverage human biology to discard imperceptible data.
      • YUV Color Space: Instead of standard RGB, codecs compress video using YUV, which isolates luminance (brightness) from chrominance (color). Because human eyes are less sensitive to color resolution changes, chrominance data can be heavily scaled down without a perceived loss in quality.
      • Psychovisual Rate Distortion: Championed by the *x264* encoder team, this method abandons pure mathematical metrics (like peak signal-to-noise ratio) to optimize for what actually looks pleasing and sharp on a human eye, preventing the blurring common in older corporate codecs.
  • The Frame Prediction Pipeline: Streamed video does not send consecutive full images. It relies on a delicate balance of frame types:
      • I-Frames (Intra-coded): Complete, independent images akin to JPEGs.
      • P-Frames (Predicted): Images that only encode the differences/movements from a previous frame.
      • B-Frames (Bi-directional predicted): Highly complex frames that interpolate data by looking at frames both in the past and the future, requiring deep memory buffering.
      • Intra-Refresh: A method common in low-latency streams that dynamically refreshes vertical columns of pixels across frames, entirely eliminating the need to send large, bandwidth-heavy keyframes.
  • The Critical Role of Assembly: To run these intensive mathematical predictions in real time on billions of devices, compilers are not enough.
      • Handwritten Code: Top-tier decoders like *dav1d* (AV1) utilize up to 79.9% handwritten assembly code to bypass the inefficiencies of auto-vectorization.
      • Blacksmithing the Craft: Kieran Kunhya developed an open-source repository (*asm-lessons*) to teach high-performance vector register optimization, maintaining that understanding assembly makes developers fundamentally better programmers.

Ethical Open Source and the VLC Origin Story

  • The Student Project Origins: VLC was born in the 1990s at France's *École Centrale Paris* as "Network 2000." Students wanted to play *Doom* and stream satellite television over a token-ring local network. They bypassed university bureaucracy to eventually open-source the "VideoLAN Client" (VLC) in 2001.
  • The "Cone Player" Identity: The orange construction cone remains one of the most recognized software icons globally. Jean-Baptiste Kempf noted that 25% of VideoLAN web traffic comes from users searching "cone player" because they do not even know the software's official name.
  • Rejecting Multi-Million Dollar Shady Buyouts: As the lead developer of VLC, Jean-Baptiste Kempf repeatedly rejected tens of millions of dollars to monetize VLC with spyware-laden search toolbars, bloatware, or sneaky in-app advertisements. He did so out of a belief in ethical software development and respect for the hundreds of volunteers who built the player.
  • The Exhausting Process of Relicensing: To integrate VLC's core engine (*libVLC*) into proprietary platforms (like commercial video games) without forcing those companies to open-source their entire product under the GPL license, Kempf manually transitioned the code to the more permissive LGPL.
      • This required tracking down and getting written consent from over 350 global contributors over several years—a process that included contacting the grieving family of a deceased teenage contributor to respectfully explain the licensing transition.

Corporate Exploitation, Security Alarmism, and Volunteer Burnout

  • The "DDOS" of AI-Generated Bug Reports: Kieran Kunhya highlighted a rising issue where security teams at major corporations use automated AI tools to scan open-source software, filing generic, highly verbose, "critical-priority" bug reports for obscure 1990s game codecs.
      • This creates an unsustainable dynamic where heavily funded corporations claim public glory for "discovering" bugs while dumping the actual labor of writing patches onto a small group of unpaid volunteers.
  • Corporate Entitlement to Free Support: Unpaid volunteers maintain the infrastructure that powers trillion-dollar corporations, yet companies regularly demand SLA-style urgent assistance.
      • The Microsoft Teams Incident: Microsoft engineers posted a "high priority" issue on the public FFmpeg tracker. When volunteers politely requested a long-term maintenance contract to fund the work, Microsoft declined and offered a nominal, one-time payment of a few thousand dollars.
  • Severe Developer Burnout and Safety: The mental health of open-source maintainers is under constant threat.
      • The *xz* Backdoor Precedent: The infamous *xz* utility compromise occurred because a single, burnt-out maintainer was targeted by coordinated online actors until he surrendered commit access to an attacker.
      • Extreme Hostility: Jean-Baptiste Kempf revealed he received physical death threats containing suspicious white powder (which turned out to be flour) in 2009 after deciding to drop VLC support for the obsolete PowerPC computer architecture.

Digital Preservation and the Future of Low-Latency Multimedia

  • Preserving Human History: Digital archiving communities utilize FFmpeg and the lossless *FFV1* codec as a digital Rosetta Stone. These open-source standards ensure that digitized historical footage from the 20th century remains readable a thousand years from now, long after proprietary corporate formats die out.
  • Kyber and the 4-Millisecond Latency Frontier: Jean-Baptiste Kempf’s new open-source startup, *Kyber*, uses the UDP-based QUIC protocol to target an ultra-low, 4-millisecond "glass-to-glass" latency.
      • This technology is critical for teleoperating rovers, remote drones, surgical equipment, and humanoid robots, ensuring that multiple video streams, sensors, and physical commands remain perfectly synchronized over the internet despite network clock drifts.
  • Defining the Future of "Multimedia": Both guests view multimedia not merely as flat audio and video, but as the digital representation of human sensory input over time.
      • VLC and FFmpeg are actively integrating support for spatial audio, 3D volumetric point clouds, haptics (for motion seats in theaters), and potentially olfactory (smell) data and direct neural signals (via brain-computer interfaces like Neuralink) in the future.

Notable Quotes

"The important is, is your code good? We care about excellent code. We don't care who you are. Like, maybe you're a dog. I don't care, right? I don't care where you come from... We care about the quality of your code, because this is what defines our community." — *Jean-Baptiste Kempf*
"This is what peak video codec should look like: 79.9% assembly, 19.6% C, and 0.5% other." — *Kieran Kunhya (referencing the low-level efficiency of modern decoders)*
"I believe there is right and wrong, right? And this was the right decision at the time, it still is. I want to be proud of what I've been doing, and like, if I had sold out, I would have betrayed so many other people's work." — *Jean-Baptiste Kempf (on turning down millions to keep VLC ad-free)*
"Don't regret anything. No, it's because regrets are a tax on your mind. Learn from your mistake, sure, but don't regret." — *Jean-Baptiste Kempf & Kieran Kunhya*

I don't care where you come from. I need to look at your code. "Oh yeah, but I'm an engineer at this very large company in Italy, in Germany, in the US." We don't care. We care about the quality of your code, because this is what defines our community. And we've tried that forever, right? For two years, and two years later, showing hundreds of examples of handwritten assembly, they still say, "No, no, no, you're doing it wrong, the compiler can do this." Did intelligence agencies try to, like, say, "Can you put a backdoor in VLC?" Yes, two of them. What did you say? No. I was a lot less polite, basically saying hell no. Like, if we had to compromise our software, we would shut it down. This is clear. Any tweets, Kieran, you regret? Tweets of regret? Or is it like that, how does the French song go, "regret nothing"? Don't regret anything. No, it's because regrets are a tax on your mind. Lex Fridman Podcast. The following is a conversation all about FFmpeg and VLC with Jean-Baptiste Kempf and Kieran Kunhya. FFmpeg is an open-source software system that is the invisible backbone behind YouTube, Netflix, Chrome, VLC, Discord, and basically every platform that touches video or audio on the internet. It can decode, encode, transcode, stream, and play almost any video or audio format ever created. To me, it is one of the most incredible software systems ever developed, and it's all done by volunteers. VLC is also a legendary piece of software. It is an open-source media player that plays basically anything you throw at it: any format, any platform, no ads, no tracking. It has been downloaded over 6 billion times. And again, for me, it has been one of my favorite pieces of software ever, with the most legendary logo, which I, of course, had to honor in this conversation by wearing the VLC traffic cone hat the whole time. So again, above all else, thank you to the incredible volunteer engineers who put their heart and soul into this code that has been used and loved by billions of people. Thank you. And about the two great engineers and human beings I'm talking to in this episode: Jean-Baptiste is the president of VideoLAN and is a key figure behind VLC and FFmpeg. Kieran is a long-time codec engineer, FFmpeg contributor, and the man behind the now infamous FFmpeg account on Twitter/X, that I recommend everybody follow for the memes and for the unapologetic celebration of open source and great low-level software engineering. Let me also say that it's inspiring and humbling that so much of modern civilization rests on software built by people who are not chasing fame or money, but are obsessed with the craft of engineering. We live in a world where billions of people consume video every day without ever thinking about the invisible machinery underneath it. But that machinery matters. Open-source infrastructure matters. It is one of the great examples of human beings quietly collaborating across borders to build something useful, durable, and elegant for the rest of us. And so, this conversation is not just about codecs and media pipelines. It is also about the deeper spirit of engineering and generosity that makes projects like FFmpeg possible. Again, I can never say it enough: thank you. This is the Lex Fridman Podcast. By the way, for people who are not familiar, I am wearing a hat. Would it be fair to say this is the best worst logo of all time, the cone? Yeah, by far, right? The logo of VLC is so iconic. Like, we are a team with a small number of people and the icon is known everywhere. I go to the middle of nowhere in India or in China, and people know the cone, right? And 25% of the website traffic that comes to our main website is search terms for "cone player", right? So so many people don't know VLC, right? They know the cone player. That's definitely what they google for, "cone player". Yeah, they go on Google, and they put "cone player" and they download VLC, right? So, that's iconic. Yeah, and we'll talk about the mission of FFmpeg being kind of the archival aspect of it, so you can think about a thousand years from now we'll have all these videos that only VLC can open. Human civilization has already destroyed itself multiple times, and the only thing that will remain is this like, you know, the cockroaches will be crawling around, and it'll be the VLC logo with some of our archival footage that VLC can open. And the aliens will show up, and they'll press play, and they'll get to see... We really, we really hope so, right? But there are also so many memes where people say, "Well, I'm sure I can put a pancake inside my DVD drive and VLC will play it." Can it? No, we tried, it doesn't. But we actually have a video of us trying that. It didn't work. A codec for physical reality—I don't know what that would even look like. There was a guy who did that, right? He printed a small cone, like the ones we distribute as goodies, and inside he put an RFID chip, which was his way of playing a movie, right? So, JB, you're the lead developer behind the legendary VLC player. Kieran, amongst many other things, you're the lead developer behind the legendary FFmpeg handle on Twitter... And both of you have spicy opinions, I would say. So today, I want to talk about FFmpeg and VLC. For context, for people who are not aware—and I'm sure basically everybody listening to this has used these two technologies probably regularly without knowing it: FFmpeg underlies basically most video on the internet, including YouTube, Netflix, Chrome, Firefox, of course VLC, and countless other video platforms. It is estimated that over 90% of video processing workflows online and offline involve FFmpeg. VLC has been downloaded at least 6.5 billion times, but likely that number, because it's impossible to really count the number, is much higher than that. Virtually any operating system supports virtually any media format, the limitation being it can't open pancakes. So, can we just lay out some of the basics to help people understand what's involved in all of this? So, when we press play on a video player like VLC, what happens? How does it go from the file or the stream to the pixels on the screen and the sound on the speaker? What are the big stages to be aware of? So, there are several stages, right? The first stage is to get from an address, which is like a type of URL, to give you a stream of bytes, right? So, this would be, for example, HTTP, a file, or a DVD. You give the path to the media and it gives you a stream of data. The stream needs to be cut up by what's known as the container, the demultiplexer, or demux—we'll try and keep the jargon light throughout this, but it needs to go and start demarcating video and audio frames, so it just gets data from the operating system blocks at a time, and needs to start cutting these frames up into compressed data. It then needs to start doing simple parsing of the video frames, mainly to figure out whether that codec is GPU decodable or needs to fall back to software. We're very used to assuming the GPU will play all of these things, there'll be hardware acceleration, but I think it's up to 45% of files that are not GPU decodable, so these need to be probed; they need to be detected. There can be variants of a given codec, some of which are decodable on the GPU. Different vendors of GPU might have different capabilities, so those need to be detected. If it's GPU capable, you pass it through to the GPU black box. So, now if there's a software fallback, that means, in the beginning, the first step is to do de-entropy coding, so removing the mathematical coding of the bitstream. This uses capabilities such as Huffman coding or arithmetic coding to actually decompress the mathematical layer of the bitstream. We then need to start reading the syntax elements for intra-prediction, so intra-prediction is like still images of the video, your I-frames. This works and operates in the spatial domain. So, you do your intra-prediction spatial domain, and you have a residual because your prediction isn't quite matching that of reality. You've made a prediction, but then there's a little bit left, and that's what's known as the residual. This is stored in the frequency domain, and these are quantized to compress their space. We then need to do the inverse transform to bring them back to the spatial domain and apply these residuals. So, a lot of the process of decoding is this thing is compressed, yes, yes... and you have to predict the highest quality thing that's supposed to go there. The I-frame is the best representation you have spatially, and then there's a lot of temporal compression that can happen depending on the codec, and then you're predicting the reality that was captured in this lossy form. Yeah, because what people don't realize is that the compression on video and audio is 100 times, right? Like, people don't realize how much we compress. For audio, when you go from normal audio to MP3, you compress by ten times, right? When you move to video, you need 100 times, 200 times, right? So, you need to remove all the details that you don't care about, because all the compressions that we do—and that's very important, people forget about that—are designed to be viewed by humans, right? So, all the codecs, either for audio, mimic basically how your ear works, and a lot of things about the response on the ear, and the same for your eyes, right? And so, for example, on video, we don't work on RGB, right? Everyone expects us to work in RGB, but we don't. We move to YUV, which is basically, one is luminance, brightness, and the others are colors. And this matches your eyes, where inside your eyes, you have the cones and the buttons [rods], where some of them look on brightness and the others on colors, right? So, we need to compress a lot, and so we need to degrade, but in order to degrade, we need to match the human perception. And this is why it's so difficult. And then we need to use the maximum mathematical power, using very complex technologies. We move to the frequency domain, as Kieran said, and we do a ton of de-quantizing in order to get the best compression while it still looks good. You're trying to compress in order to maximize the highest quality thing for human perception. That is correct. That is correct. And that is very important, right? Compression is not like a ZIP. A ZIP has data in, you get data out, and you try with all the ZIP compression to arrive at the limit. Here, we are degrading the signal, right? And so, we need to degrade both the audio and the video signal in the best way possible. And we can do that, but it involves, first, a lot of theoretical knowledge about how the eye works, and a lot of mathematical changes and tricks, right? For example, when you move to RGB, and you go to YUV, what we do very often is that we scale down the resolution of the color compared to the brightness. And most of the time, just doing this, even without compression, divides the size by two. But most people don't see it, and so on, and so on, right? And then, you go to very complex mathematical changes—so of course, Fourier transforms, which de facto are not Fourier transforms, they are discrete cosine transforms, but that's the same idea, the frequency domain. We split the video by blocks, right? So, that's why when it's wrongly decoded or badly encoded, you see those blocks. We do this to arrive at compression states that are insanely high, right? And each generation of the codec is like 30% less bitrate for the same quality, right? And this requires an amount of computational power that is huge. No, no, but you should elaborate: it's 30% better, but it takes an order of magnitude, perhaps even two orders of magnitude, more compression power. That's the big difference. What do you mean by compression power? Sorry, CPU power to achieve that level of compression. Oh yeah. So, you have to be able to leverage the CPU and sometimes the GPU, like you mentioned, and then we should mention that a lot of this programming is done at the lowest possible stack, whether it's C and of course, as the legendary Twitter handle re-emphasizes over and over, a lot of assembly. So what happens globally is that you have an address which, through the operating system, gives you a stream of bytes, so a stream of data, right? And this is the first step, and the second step is demuxing, where you're going to separate audio, video, and subtitles into different tracks. And then, on each of those tracks, you're going to decompress them and decode them: audio with an audio codec, video with a video codec, and subtitles with a subtitle codec. Once you've decompressed those things, you have raw images, and you're going to talk with your graphics card and your screen to display that. And same for the audio, you're going to talk to your audio card, which then is going to go in analog to your speakers. And everything we've just said in the past couple of minutes, every sentence is someone's lifetime work. There are books written about every sentence, so the level of complexity, in many cases, is inordinate. Every sentence has thousands of people working on this in the industry as a whole, with books written about it, so there's a lot of detail, there's a lot of subtleties, there's a lot of both academic and practical realities, both of which matter. We mentioned codecs, but I don't think you mentioned containers. So, what are the actual containers for some of the stuff we're talking about? People are familiar with MP4, MOV, MKV, so anyway, what are containers versus the things that go inside? The container is what we also call the muxer. When we say demuxing, it means decontainerizing. Actually, if you look, "mux" means multiplexer and "demux" is demultiplexer. In the same way, a codec is actually a coder-decoder, right? And so containers are this collection of multiple tracks, right? So, it's what normal people call the file format, but it's a bit more subtle than that. The most known one, of course, is MP4, but when I started, it was AVI—which was the video format from Microsoft—and MOV, which became MP4, the format from Apple. In the open-source community, one of the people who is still active on VideoLAN is Steve Lhomme, who started the Matroska format, which is a bit more complex and more future-proof. And there are so many others. So, I mean, there's a pretty common thing—and maybe it'll even happen in this conversation—that people confuse the container and the codec, right? So, they confuse MP4 and H.264, for example. Is that a horrible violation? No, it's not, because technically the name of H.264 is MPEG-4 Part 10. MPEG-4 is actually a meta-specification which has several things in it, right? There is Part 2, there are audio codecs like AAC, which de facto is MPEG-4 Audio, and there are actually several video codecs inside the MPEG-4 specification. One of them is MPEG-4 Part 10, also called AVC, also called H.264, right? So, it's completely the fault of the industry for making things difficult to understand. It's very difficult, so people then don't understand why sometimes you talk about MPEG-4 Part 10 when you mean H.264, and why it's not MP4. You can technically shove all kinds of different codecs inside containers, and horribly so... But broadly speaking, though, MP4 is understood to generally be H.264 plus AAC audio. 99% of the time, that's what it is, and the rest are de minimis—small edge effects compared to that. So, it's not the end of the world, though there are people who do get annoyed by that. But also, in reality, for something like VLC, the file may say .mp4, but it may be something completely different. And that's one of the challenges both FFmpeg and VLC have: the real world is a completely different place to a three-letter file extension. And this is very important to say, right? For example, in VLC and FFmpeg, we discard the file format. We look into the file to understand what's in it, because so many people say, "Oh, it's a video, it must be MP4," but technically, it's an MOV, or maybe it's an MKV, right? So, we analyze in real time everything that we have, and we don't trust the format. So, what information does the fact that it's .mp4 give you? It helps; it gives you a hint. It's just like, "Oh, it ends with .mp4, I will start first by opening and probing it with the MP4 container demuxer to see if it matches." But I don't trust it, and if I'm lost, I say, "Okay, maybe I'm going to try something else." It just bumps the priority of that module. To take a bit of a tangent there: the dumb thing is, if you try the MP4, but it turns out to be a different codec than you would have expected, most players will just break there. Yes. Yes. And so, how do you not break? Philosophically, I'm sure there's a bunch of stumbling blocks along the way where it's easy to just break and stop, freak out, and that's it. How does VLC avoid that? This is why VLC is popular. The reason is because VLC was actually just the client of a streaming solution called VideoLAN from a very long time ago, the late '90s. And when you're playing video over UDP on a network, packets might be damaged, right? So, you don't trust your inputs. This is very important in today's security: you don't trust your inputs. So, everything in VLC is prepared to work with broken files. It's a philosophical idea from the beginning, and everything is engineered into that. It's a culture, right? VLC became very popular because, a long time ago, when people were pirating content—which they do a lot less today... And none of us ever have. No, of course not. The metadata to play some files, like AVI, is at the end of the file. When you're still downloading, you don't have that, right? So, VLC was just like, "Hey, this file is broken, but I'm still going to try to interpret it." And this was very useful. We hinted at the awesomeness of the various different stages, we hinted at the awesomeness of codecs, and the depth and the richness and the complexity of everything involved there. Let's just try to define: what is a video codec? What's involved there, and what does it mean to compress something? You already started to hint at it, but can we elaborate a little bit more? So, there's a huge amount of redundancy in any video, both spatial and temporal, and the point of any video codec is to remove this redundant data and use mathematical properties as part of this reduction process. This often uses several orders of magnitude more compute to compress, because that's more costly—both financially and in CPU resources—versus decompression. So, it's asymmetric in that respect. This is often the case because compression is done once, but there could be lots of viewers of that resulting file. So, you take that information and compress it by 100x or 200x, removing redundant information, and using mathematical properties to make that small. But codecs also need properties such as error resilience. As JB suggested, VLC in the beginning was used to play UDP network feeds, and UDP network feeds lose packets. So, one of the design goals of a codec is also to be recoverable. You need to actually be able to join a stream—it's not necessarily a file—and get on the decoding process, and start decoding. And to give a clearer picture to people who are not familiar: when you watch any type of movie, you're going to see the camera pan and travel. You realize that, for example, the entire background is the same for a minute, or 30 seconds. So, you can reuse the cloud that you see in the background; you can reuse that from one frame to another. The more memory you have, the more power, and the more comparisons you can make, the more compressed it can be. And most modern codecs are basically doing that. So, just to make it even more explicit, what is video? Video is a bunch of RGB pixels, with three values, on a grid of pixels, and you have, let's say, 24, 30, or 60 frames a second, repeating and showing different stuff 30 times a second. And so, the philosophical and technical question is: how can I compress all of that, and store all of that, at 100x... Or 1000x, right? 1000x, mhm. The target is 1000x, right? And when you say redundancy, what does redundant mean? At best, it's stuff that humans wouldn't notice if it was missing. For example, you have a picture of a cloud, right? In the next frame, there's still going to be the same cloud, so it's redundant. You could just transmit it once, and not repeat it, right? Or you have a black background behind me, for example, where the black is the same across the whole picture. You can say, "Well, in this picture, take the pixels that you have on the top left, and for the ones on the top right, I'm not going to give the values; I'm just going to tell you they are the same as the top left." And then you can say for frame ten, reuse something from the previous frame or the frame before that, and so on. So, it's basically unlimited, but in reality, it's limited in terms of memory or compute power. For example, if you need to compare pixels across 200 past frames at 4K resolution, it's a huge amount of compute. And then, when you're showing it, you have to decompress all of that. So, is the codec—meaning the encoding and the decoding—a coupled process that you're developing? Yes, exactly, right? And those involve two different trade-offs. Are you going to compress more, even if it might be more difficult to decode? Are you going to make a codec that is more complex to encode and easier to decode? Or are you going to make a codec that is easier to encode, because you need to be fast, but then the client-side player is going to spend more time? That's why you have so many different types of codecs; it's not always easy. And to make it even more complex, modern codecs like AV1, AV2, or VVC are actually not single codecs. They are a collection of tools, right? There are multiple tools and multiple codecs within the same specification to get more compression depending on the image. Just to elaborate, codecs like AV1 and VVC have a very wide audience. It could be screen-share content, video, or animation—all of which require different coding tools. What happens these days is a collection of tools are put together and called AV1, AV2, or VVC to allow for different use cases. So, you may be on Zoom sharing your PowerPoint, and then you need to show the audience a video. That codec needs to start changing its toolset depending on the content to compress in a different way. And like you said, there's a bunch of incredible engineers behind each part of those tools that make up AV1, for example. Sure. So, we've kind of danced around it. We talked about VLC, the logo, and the hat. Let's talk about FFmpeg. What is FFmpeg, exactly? FFmpeg is basically the low-level libraries for codecs—so compression and decompression, muxers and demuxers, and filters. That is the core of it, and then you have several tools which allow you to create a pipeline to process any type of video file. And it's used as a library inside almost everything: from VLC, to Chrome, to your smart TV. Basically any video you see online usually uses FFmpeg. FFmpeg has all those types of tools inside it, and sometimes depends on other libraries like x264, libvpx, and others. So, it is really the de facto tool to process images. From a philosophical level, I think it's incredible that your home videos, your grandmother's home videos, and trillion-dollar corporations effectively are on a level playing field using the same technology stack. It wouldn't be a surprise if these big companies just have 3,000-line FFmpeg commands. Oh, wow. There are some that use the API, but there are some that just have long command lines. Yeah, there's a bunch of tools, like literally the command line tool FFmpeg, and of course FFprobe. There are libraries: libavcodec, libavformat, libavfilter. But FFmpeg on the command line is legendary because you can cut, customize, and configure so many parameters. You can customize everything to hell. It's a language, an actual language. It's an actual—oh yeah, you could think of it as a programming language. Of course, I'm sure. Most people are going to take FFmpeg, put file in, file out, and specify the format, right? But you can write commands with thousands of characters, and we've seen people doing programmatic generation of command lines to drive FFmpeg. There is a ton of people who are using AI to generate command lines for FFmpeg because it's so complex. You can specify so many filters on the command line. So, FFmpeg is this collection of toolboxes for multimedia processing that everyone uses. And everyone who is watching your videos is also using it, right? If you are on YouTube, well, they use FFmpeg on the server side, and the client side is probably Chrome, which also uses FFmpeg. If you're using OBS to record, well, it's FFmpeg. If you're using a ton of professional broadcast boxes, it's very possible that somewhere inside them, FFmpeg is running. There's so many use cases. Just to give people an idea: I use FFmpeg a lot on everything. Just trivial stuff like taking a video, adding an intro and an outro, and fading one into the other—like a dip-to-black where it dips and then shows the next video, and doing the same thing with audio where there's a cross-dissolve that quiets the audio and makes it loud again. And there's a bunch of stuff like showing captions on-screen, baking the captions in where you can customize the font, or doing all kinds of layering of audio and video. There's a million things and, of course, all of that works magically with basically any codec. Anything you can shove in on the audio and video side, it just works. But it's like, for example, you can do things that you would normally do with Adobe After Effects... Oh, wow. ...in the command line on FFmpeg. It's very interesting because for images, there is no such tool. There are a few tools, but not with the breadth of FFmpeg. So, ImageMagick has a similar kind of... Yes, but you will not do complex filters on it. You don't have the equivalent of Photoshop in the command line, but for video, you have FFmpeg. Yeah, it's incredible. It's an example of what happens when a bunch of great people get together, get a vision, and stick by that vision for many years. The vision behind both VLC and FFmpeg is to make everything that is very complex easy to use for normal people—for everyone. Our goal is to take something that is insanely complex technically and make it easy to use. People use VLC, they drop a file, they don't realize how complex it is, but it plays. Or they put any type of input inside FFmpeg with complex filters, and it works magically. This is our mission: to make very complex things simple. We wouldn't be here, and the podcast wouldn't be here, if this still required a traditional television studio setup. It's tools like FFmpeg that democratized this. The podcast and streaming revolution, the YouTube revolution—FFmpeg was a big player in that because it democratized technology that once, in the '90s, required equipment costing hundreds of thousands of dollars and was the size of a car. Now, everybody has that on almost an exact level playing field. That is remarkable. It gave a voice to a lot of people. And just to clarify, when you say, "You wouldn't be here," you mean the podcast, not me on a biological level. The podcast, sorry! Yes, you as a podcast host. I was like, "Wow, VLC did not have anything to do on a biological level with creating me as a human!" But you realize also that everything moved from text to images, and images to video, right? Look at social networks. Video is everywhere; it's the most powerful medium there is. When you see Shorts, Reels, and TikTok, it's amazingly powerful. Video is amazing for that, but the underlying complexity is massive. This is what people don't realize. It gave power to the individual all across the world. That's real freedom. I can't believe it, but we still haven't explicitly mentioned the most obvious thing for people who are not familiar: that it is open-source, and there's an open-source community of users and developers behind it. It's really a movement. We'll talk a bunch of different ways about the community behind it, but can you speak to the open-source element? When we say "What is FFmpeg?", it's an open-source project. Yeah, so FFmpeg, VLC, x264, VideoLAN—everything we do is fully open-source. For people who don't understand what open-source is, my usual analogy is about a chocolate cheesecake, right? Usually, when you want to buy a cake, you go to a bakery, and they give you the cheesecake. The other way of having a cheesecake is having your grandma give you the recipe to make it. When we do open-source, we give you the chocolate cake and the recipe to remake it, but we also tell you how to build the oven, and we allow you to modify the recipe and resell it to someone else. This is because software is just a very long recipe of small instructions. Computers are not very clever, but they go very, very fast. A normal program has tens of billions of instructions, instead of the tens of steps you have in a chocolate recipe. A lot of the software industry was about selling software where you just get the final cheesecake. In open-source, we give you everything, and that manages to get a lot of people to work together.
