You decide that you're going to make the best program, the best recipe for video, and you create communities. In FFmpeg, since the beginning of the project, probably... Two thousand to three thousand people have contributed. ...two thousand to three thousand people have contributed from the beginning, right? And it's exactly like the Linux kernel. The Linux kernel has probably 10,000 people contributing from everywhere. They get together—well, mostly online, so they virtually get together—to create the best tool for the job. On FFmpeg and VLC, it's just like, "Well, this codec doesn't work, so I'm going to work on it, and I'm going to add support for this file inside FFmpeg so it will be beneficial to everyone." Again, we work for the greater good; we work for everyone. That is what open-source is. And we should mention that depending on the licensing, you can probably build a billion-dollar, maybe even a trillion-dollar company around it as a wrapper. Yes, people do. Well, yeah, mhm. People do, right? There were a lot of problems with, mostly, cloud providers who are basically running open-source tools in the cloud and just giving you an API to access them. There were a lot of databases, like MongoDB or Elasticsearch, that changed their licenses in order to avoid those types of scenarios. This is a question we get a lot in FFmpeg: "Why don't you do that?" But you can't. We have thousands of contributors, some of whom aren't even alive anymore. You would need all of their agreements to do that. JB will talk a bit later about how challenging that process was in VLC to do the relicensing. The license is a social contract, in the terms of Rousseau, of the community. The community does not agree on much besides the license. People discuss things because of the license, and it also allows for license forks, right? Sometimes the community splits, but because of the license, it's possible to merge back later. We've seen that so many times—GCC and EGCS in the past, or, for example, web browsers. They started as KHTML, which became WebKit, and then became Blink. So, the open-source license is the core of the community. People come from all around the world, with very different backgrounds, religions, and political borders, and they work in the same way on a project to solve a specific problem. The specific problem we're working on is making multimedia easy for everyone. Looking it up on Perplexity here, looking at the different open-source licenses, most major open-source licenses fall into two buckets: permissive (very few conditions) and copyleft (share-alike requirements for derivatives). Below is a brief, practical summary of the main ones you'll see in the wild: MIT License, BSD, ISC, Apache, GNU GPL, GNU AGPL... where's LGPL? There's LGPL, let's see, there's the Mozilla Public License, there's the Eclipse Public License. It goes on; there's a lot of variety. I think the really popular ones are MIT, GPL, LGPL, and BSD. And BSD, Apache... Sometimes you see Apache, mhm. Unlicense—that's an option. It attempts to dedicate code to the public domain with a fallback permissive license. There are many licenses for many different things. What people don't understand is that public domain is something that doesn't exist worldwide. All open-source licensing uses copyright law, international copyright law, in order to grant rights on how you use the software or how you modify it. It's, de facto, a copyright license contract that you give to the end-user or to the developer. So, you have the first category, which are basically very permissive: MIT, BSD. You give the code, and basically they can do whatever they want. You take it, you modify it, you do what you want. This is popular for JavaScript and BSD operating systems. So, for some of them, one of the parameters is whether they require attribution, meaning if you use the code, you have to say so. Yes, in those types of permissive licenses, for some, you need to say if you're using it, which is called attribution, and for some, you don't. And then there is the other class of license, which is copyleft, where you need to give back your modifications to the community. There are different strings attached, from weaker copyleft licenses like the Mozilla Public License, to some which are a bit stronger like GPL, or even very strong like AGPL. All of those are different types of licensing that depend on what your goals are and how you want to structure your community, which is why I spoke about a social contract. This is very important to understand. FFmpeg and VLC are mostly GPL or LGPL; the Linux kernel is GPL, but Android is Apache. A ton of the JavaScript frameworks that are used are mostly MIT, and all the BSD kernels—OpenBSD, NetBSD—are, of course, BSD. So, it's a philosophical choice on how you want people to contribute back, basically. At one point, you moved from GPL to LGPL on certain parts of the project. Can you describe the difference between the two, and what does it take to move to a more permissive license? LGPL is more permissive than GPL. Yeah. You have to realize that you can always go from more permissive to less permissive, because those licenses are statements of rights. If you restrict, you can always restrict more. In a GPL project, you can take MIT code, but you cannot do the opposite because there are more constraints to match. Indeed, in fact, I changed the core of libVLC, which is the engine of VLC, from GPL to LGPL. There were two reasons to do that. The first one is so people can use the VLC engine, libVLC, inside third-party applications. A lot of applications playing video on your phone or tablet actually use the VLC engine inside, which in turn calls FFmpeg. That was one of the ways to build a company I created, which does consulting and integration of those third-party applications, where you integrate VLC into commercial solutions, like inside game engines or things like that. With GPL, you couldn't do that, because it meant you needed to open-source your entire application, which commercial companies don't want to do. With LGPL, you can build a commercial product around it without being forced to open-source your proprietary code. That's a big leap. It means you can play video inside your proprietary game. Exactly. "I'm a game developer, and I want to play some videos, but I don't want to be forced to open-source my entire game just to do that." That's where libVLC under the LGPL allows you to do that. The LGPL—the Lesser GPL, or Library GPL as it used to be known—allows you to do that. FFmpeg is basically the same. The LGPL forces you to give back what you changed on that specific component or library, which is why it's the Library GPL. So, you can use FFmpeg under LGPL inside any type of application, even closed-source, but you need to publish any modifications you made to FFmpeg itself. The same goes for libVLC. Is it limiting from an open-source perspective to go GPL? Because if your code is GPL, you are basically discouraging companies from building a business around it, right? Is that a fair thing to say? It depends on the company. If a company's business model requires their application to be closed-source, then yes, it is limiting. That's why, for example, I moved to LGPL. The second reason is a bit more obscure: the terms and conditions of the Apple App Store for iOS make it very complex to distribute a GPL application, while it is easier to have LGPL applications on there. So, while VLC on Windows, Mac, and Linux is GPL, the core is LGPL. On iOS, the iPhone and Apple TV versions are under a different license called the MPL. And yes, I went and changed the license, and it was a long story. To change the license, you basically have to contact all the contributors, right? Yes. It's very important to understand that open-source projects are what US copyright law calls "joint works" (or in civil law, "collective works" or "collaborative works"). You work together toward the same goal, and it creates one software release, but the copyright is retained by all the individual contributors. Some open-source projects force copyright assignment, but we don't do that; we are communities. Everyone keeps the copyright on what they changed. That copyright stays even if, eventually, your contribution is deleted because a newer contribution was based on your previous one. So, if you want to properly relicense, you need to find all the contributors. At that time, I had to contact more than 350 people. Sometimes they are just an old email address, so you actually have to track them down. I actually traveled to places that I had found online, going to people's workplaces to say, "Well, you licensed this code, do you want to change it from GPL to LGPL?" Most of the time they didn't even care; they just wanted to help VLC. But it also brought me into very complex situations. I once arrived at the workplace of a factory worker, and I said, "I need you to sign this." It turned out his son had died, and he was the one who actually wrote the code. I had to explain the meaning of open-source, and reassure him that I was not a company trying to rip off the few lines of code his son had written, but that it was useful and the whole community agreed on the change. He had no idea, being a factory worker. I was a lot younger—this was 14 years ago—and I was almost in tears. It is very difficult because we are talking about people's lives. He explained things, and we looked at a photo of his son. It's important to do it right and to do it correctly, but yes, that means tracking down everything because every contribution matters. There are some projects that don't respect that and do relicensing aggressively, but as I said, that destroys the heart of the community. We only agree on the license, so that is crucial. I would emphasize that the community is such a wide-ranging group of people. There are people in a Syrian warzone with only part-time electricity; there are people from all walks of life—rich, poor, young, old. It's quite remarkable to get a group of people aligned on something. That's an achievement in itself. Yeah, it's incredible. And a lot of them are introverts, so finding them and getting them to answer an email can be quite difficult. Most of us are introverts, right? You need to be more precise: you have extreme introverts, extremely extreme introverts, and regular introverts. It's a whole spectrum of different people. But it doesn't matter. The important thing is: is your code good? Is your code great? Is your technology great? We care about excellent code; we don't care who you are. We have no way to check, and we cannot check. Maybe you're a dog—I don't care, right? I don't care where you're from. I just need to look at your code. This is important because people sometimes don't understand that. They come to the community and send some patches, and they get rejected, and they don't like it. We have to say, "Sorry, it's just not up to our standards." "Oh yeah, but I'm an engineer at this very large company in Italy, in Germany, in the US." We don't care. We care about the quality of your code, because this is what defines our community. This means we have a lot of contributors from very different backgrounds who are very introverted, sure, but that's okay, right? One of the legends of the community is, of course, Linus Torvalds, who created Linux and is the long-time maintainer of the Linux kernel. As the legend goes, he can be pretty harsh in this meritocratic process of reviewing code and saying it's not good enough. Can you speak to the legend of Linus Torvalds? Linus is one of a kind, right? I would even go so far as to say that what he did with Git is more interesting than what he did with the Linux kernel. He can be very harsh, but what people don't see is that usually when he is harsh, it is to people who are maintainers of part of the kernel, so they know him. He is not harsh like that to everyone. The thing is, what he created in his room is basically powering every server online, even on Microsoft's cloud, Azure. I'm quite sure 70% to 80% of those servers are running Linux. All of your Android phones are running Linux. What he did with the power of open-source is amazing. And yes, the quality of the Linux kernel is very high, and yes, it's difficult, but we cannot compromise on that. We cannot compromise on quality, because in the end—and you have to understand this—the core community of VLC is five people, and the core community of FFmpeg is 10 to 15. We are the ones who are going to maintain your code, right? Out of 1,000 contributors in the timeline, only about 10 stay. There is just a 1% chance that someone comes and stays, 1%. You will have a change of job, a change of wife, children, or accidents in life. You're going to change jobs, whatever, and you're not going to come back; it's most likely. So, we are the ones who are going to maintain your code. It needs to be maintainable, it needs to be excellent, and yes, sometimes that means that you need to rework your work because it was good, but it's not excellent. We need excellence because we are very few to maintain something that is critical for the whole world. But we should also mention that there is some spiciness, some harshness to the language that is sometimes used when you're keeping this high bar of excellence. Is there something to say to that? It's true, and it's also the fact that, for example, what we're doing is low-level, extremely technical. You get into this community and the tone gets very much like a subculture, right? So, people who arrive from the outside are basically not known to the subculture. Most of those people around FFmpeg and VLC, we do VideoLAN Dev Days, VDD, every year. They're so fun in real life, and they love it, but it's true that when you're online, sometimes you don't realize how the tone is. But that's okay, right? It's a culture. I mean, you get this in the gaming culture, where the way people communicate is pretty harsh and intense. As everyone understands, the way you show love and respect just looks different in different communities. Sometimes people—it depends. If it's a book club, usually people are going to be much sweeter. If it's an open source project that's very high stakes and used by millions of people, it's different. But you don't often see insults like you see in gaming, right? And so, Linus's tone is a bit unusual even for the open source community. It's more like it's harsh on the results, saying, "No, this is not good, this is crap," those types of things that you will see. We try not to make it about the person; we make it about the code. Yes, very, very matter-of-fact. And I think that you've got to look at it in terms of, you know, the famous fact that FFmpeg is developed almost entirely by volunteers, and that's true. You've got to imagine someone is on a hard day's work at their day job, they come home, and, you know, terseness might be a thing. That's not something to take personally. You're tired, you're busy, but you still care about this open source stuff. Yeah, but you may not be able to explain and hand-hold someone on every subtle detail. And also, you have to realize that most people don't speak English as a native language. This is especially true for open source projects like FFmpeg and VLC, which are mostly centered out of Europe. Sometimes people who are from the US or just are very not happy about the tone, but most of the time it's also like they don't know better, right? It's difficult. English is a difficult language; there are so many subtleties and tones and so on that you don't have, right? So, often it's also difficult in those types of communities with different cultures and languages. So, as the legend goes, JB, you have repeatedly turned down millions of dollars to keep VLC open source, free for everyone, without ads. So, take me through the reasoning behind that decision of leaving millions of dollars on the table. Yeah, that's like almost a meme, right? On Reddit. There literally is a meme on Reddit. Look at him looking like a wizard in the VLC hat on Reddit: "This is Jean-Baptiste Kempf, the creator of VLC media player. He refused tens of millions of dollars in order to keep VLC ads-free. Thanks, Jean!" You can even summon him on Reddit. Yeah, and usually, if you see, right? It's usually like people tag me, right? And then there is me, and I say, "Good morning." I got 24k upvotes, which is great, right? My karma on Reddit is amazing, at least on that account. So, the question needs to be answered first: what is the story about VLC, right? Because yes, this is true. I refused dozens of millions of dollars, yes, several times. Yes, I could be a multi-millionaire and be somewhere on a beach, but I did not do it because I thought it was not moral, and it was not the right thing to do. This is very important for myself, to be like, I work for the greater good, I work for people. And it's not just by myself, but the reason is also because I did not feel that I'm completely legitimate to do that, and let me explain to you why. VLC's story is a very weird story. In France, we have universities, and we have a type of top college. These top of excellence schools are engineering schools, business schools, and basically lawyers and medical schools, right? But they're outside of the university system. In order to enter those, you spend two years working like crazy on math and physics to enter those best engineering schools. One of the schools is called the École Centrale Paris—it has changed its name since, but it was called École Centrale Paris. And, because it was Centrale, they had to move it because it was too small after World War II. They moved it—they wanted to move it to the center of France in a place called Clermont-Ferrand, but the alumni decided that this was not okay, right? It is the school that Eiffel attended—the one who did the Eiffel Tower, right? So they said, "No, no, we are an amazing, great school, we cannot do that." And so they bought a piece of land south of Paris, very near Paris, and it was a campus managed by a non-profit of the alumni, okay? Because of that, everything on the campus was managed by students. The university did nothing, right? So, radio, TV, supermarket, library, defining who was going into which rooms—everything was managed by the students. That's amazing. That's an amazing experiment, and it didn't all go to hell quickly. It somehow flourished. It worked great, and I learned so much in my life doing those side activities, right? Because you're 22, and you need to run your campus, else you don't have electricity, right? So, you care about that, right? But anyway, in the 80s, they did a full experiment of deploying a network, mostly sponsored by IBM and 3Com, which was a token ring network. Token ring is something that probably almost no one knows about anymore. It's a networking technology where you don't have routers, right? Everyone is linked, it's like a ring, and when you want to send a message, you talk to your neighbor, who's going to put the message to the next one, who's going to put the things to the next one, in terms of a ring. The issue with token ring, of course, is that it's very slow because every computer on the network needs to open the message, see if it's okay, see if it is for me, and if not, send it back like a token traveling around the ring. In the 80s, when you're doing some telnet and sending mails at university, that's okay, right? But start the 90s, and the 90s start video games, and when you have high latency in video games, basically you die, right? So in 1994 or 1995, around Doom and Duke Nukem coming around, they wanted a faster network. So the students go and see the university and say, "You know what? We want a faster network. We need to work, but also play video games." And the university tells them basically, "Oh, I'm sorry, we cannot help you. Because you understand, the campus is not ours, you manage it, so do something, and you should see some basically partners of the university, and basically go away." And they go, and they actually go and see the CIO of Bouygues, which is a large French company that does some TV in France. He says, "Well, you know what? The future of video is satellite." Well, today we know it's not, but at least that was a good idea in 1995, the first of satellite dishes. He says, "Instead of having like one satellite dish and a big decoder for each of the students, which are 1,500, what if you build—you put an enormous dish and only one decoder, and you send the video directly on the network?" And that required a very fast network. Today, it's obvious, but at the time, it was like the first to do video streaming. So, they built this project, which was called Network 2000. Of course, we're in the 90s, right? Everything futuristic is called 2000. Like, 2000, yeah. And so they do the Network 2000 project. It's completely hacked; it crashes after 45 seconds. That's okay, the demo is 40 seconds. It leaks memory; that's okay, they put 64 megabytes of RAM instead of the 8 or 16 you normally have, and the demo should have stopped there. That was the Network 2000 project by the students. What was the format of the video that they had to work with? MPEG-2, because satellite is MPEG-2 TS for transport, MPEG-2 video, and MPEG-2 audio at that time. And the project should have stopped there. Everyone was happy, they had like an amazing ATM network at 155 megabits per second, they had probably one of the best networks in Europe at that time, and they stopped the project. Six months or a year later, two students arrive and say, "Well, you know what? Maybe other people care about video streamed on a local network." And they create the VideoLAN project. VideoLAN. One of them is called Christophe Massiot, who is a good friend of both Kieran and me, and they start the project. It's not even open source yet, and they spend around three years to get the school to agree to make it open source. Because the university, because of the IP and copyright of the students, wanted to basically monetize these MPEG-2 decoders. Just to be clear, what was the main application? Streaming on a local network. It was streaming on a local network. By the way, this is to state the obvious, this is 10 years before YouTube. You have a Pentium 60 or 75, right? The main machine was a 486 DX at 33 megahertz, right? Bear in mind, television was the main form of video at the time. You could get new channels; in the 90s, having even one new channel when you grew up with four channels, having a fifth or a sixth was a big deal. And so having this satellite service with, you know, dozens, even hundreds of channels was so groundbreaking. Especially because this is a university where you had a ton of different nationalities, right? So, there was a ton of people who wanted—so, in the end, they had like several dishes on different types of satellites, right? Because, for example, a lot of people were coming from the Maghreb or the Middle East, and they went to different types of satellites. Anyway, the solution worked great, and they started the VideoLAN project. The VideoLAN project had several—and some are completely crazy—solutions, like one on how to create multicast on a unicast network, but let's not come to that, it's too complex. But the VideoLAN client part is what became VLC. Yeah. Actually, they basically strong-armed the university to force it to open source, because the university did not understand that. In 2001, it's still early, but basically, yes, the university agreed in early 2001 to make it open source. I joined the project in 2003, because that's when I joined the university. So the first thing is, I'm not the one who created VLC, because actually, no one did, right? It just kind of naturally emerged from the VideoLAN project. We should mention that, like, again, you said it—just to make it clear—VideoLAN as what it became, what it was at the time, was a set of technologies around video. And VLC, what you call the client, that's the thing that most normies think of as the thing, which is the thing that pops up when you click on a video and it plays. So I arrived in 2003, and then I created the open source, non-profit organization called VideoLAN, and I took everything out of the university to create a non-profit project and something sustainable. Yes, it's true that I spent more time than anyone on VLC and VideoLAN, that is sure, but it's a continuity of a previous project, VideoLAN, the student project, which is a continuity of the Network 2000 project, which is a continuity of that and that. I'm sure there are moments along the way there where you were thinking about what the future of this is from an open source perspective. Because as the internet is blowing up, there are companies—I mean, for people who don't remember, there are companies making huge amounts of money. And I can tell you that in 2005, the project should have died, and I stepped in to continue the project. At some point, we were only two active developers, and I thought it was a great technology, it was useful, and it would be useful. I made that my life and my time. I made that grow from a few hundreds of thousands of users, to millions of users, to what we have now, which is probably billions of versions of VLC around the world, used everywhere. So, that's a bit of the story of VLC. There is a ton of very funny stories around that, with many people from around the world working on it, like you said, in Syria or the middle of nowhere in India. Along the way, I got several offers, which were either to bundle toolbars—right, you remember those horrible toolbars, which were basically spyware—or changing your web browser or your search engine, or even like advertisement inside VLC. And I didn't like that, right? I am—and people don't understand this—it's not that I'm against money, right? I'm very happy to make money. I created several startups, and one, I hope, is going to work very well. It's the fact that I believe you need to make money ethically. There is a right way of doing that, and doing sneaky advertisement or stealing data is not the correct way, right? For example, if Netflix arrived at some point and they said, "Well, we want to put Netflix inside VLC," probably the story would have been different, right? But they didn't. The only people who came to us were shady ad companies, and if I did that, right, I would have a ton of money, right? And then three years later, the project is gone, right? Someone forks it and something else happens. So it's not even necessarily ads or any of that; it's the shadiness, the dishonesty of it. So you had a good radar, you had a good threshold of, like, "No, this compromises the spirit of what this is supposed to represent." But also, it's for me, right? Very selfishly, I need to go to bed at night and be happy about what I've done, right? Maybe it's my upbringing, maybe it's my parents' fault, or whatever, right? But I believe there is right and wrong, right? And this was the right decision at the time, and it still is. I want to be proud of what I've been doing. And like, if I had sold out, I would have betrayed so many other people's work. Yeah. Well, I should say, me and most of the internet thank you for that decision. It's inspiring for others, I think, that are pushing the open source movement forward, that it's okay to make these kinds of huge sacrifices if you believe it's right. And I think in that case it was right, and it was the reason that VLC became as successful as it was, because it's an embodiment, it's a symbol of, you know, the freedom of what the open source community can create. Yeah, and being a service for so many people around the world, and this is important. We should emphasize that in the 2000s it was really normal to download a program and have it secretly install some spyware. Yes, it was. It was buried in very faint text or in the license text box that nobody reads at the bottom: "Oh, we will be installing this toolbar and changing all of these things." And it was very common to have to, you know, install a program to do something at the time of any sort. To put yourself in the mind of a developer at that time, I think it's very easy—for everybody listening to this, it's very easy at that time to convince yourself to take a few thousand dollars, a few thousand dollars to do it. To say no to much more money takes guts and takes vision. The last offer I had was obscene. And they said, "Yeah, but imagine with all that money, you could build something new, open source, right?" It was like the mind trick was difficult, but for me, it was just like, "No, this doesn't work like that, and this is not the right thing, so I don't do it." And again, right, it's not that I don't like money or whatever, it's just like, it wasn't right. Well, once again, thank you from me and from the rest of the internet. Let me talk a little bit more about the open source movement, about the fact that as you say, over and over and over and over, FFmpeg is—and many open source projects are—built by volunteers. So, there was a bit of drama recently, Kieran, on the interwebs on Twitter. You have a spicy style on Twitter that I think articulates and celebrates all the incredible developers and development and the code, especially assembly that's involved in building some of these codecs, building some of this incredible technology. But that brings us to a bit of a debacle that happened. Tell me the full saga of what happened with the Google security engineers. Just to be clear, Google is one of the biggest supporters of open source out there; they have been for a long time. It's just I think some things kind of went overboard this time. So, FFmpeg itself—and this is not like a secret, it's on the homepage—you know, it processes untrusted data, and there can be security issues when you parse untrusted data. That's very normal. But recently, what changed was Google started using AI to create security reports on an open source project, FFmpeg. Volunteers had to deal with that. They provided very limited funding, and they even went to the media first, announcing how good their AI was before the issues could be fixed. And this is in the public forum. Yeah, this is all public. So, reporting an issue using AI to find an issue in the code, which is a security vulnerability, and then reporting that publicly before you're able to fix it? Yeah, it's announcing how good their AI is, and they provided a standard 90-day industry deadline without potentially understanding the nature of volunteer-driven development. In addition, this vulnerability was on an obscure 1990s game codec. And let's look at it from their standpoint to begin with. Let's, you know... Yeah, can you steelman their case? Yeah, sure. They have substantial resources and work on the security of open source projects that, you know, are ubiquitous, and they've used a lot of compute to do that, and very expensive and very capable security researchers to do that. From their viewpoint, they are contributing by doing that, but I think that's where opinions differ. It opened up a lot of interesting issues, I would say. It does seem that there's a portion of the security community that looks at themselves a bit like building architects who never have to go to the site, you know. Going to the site is something that is a little bit beneath them, the actual day-to-day construction. They're there to do security things, and it's someone else's problem. The security industry also kind of has a very aggressive tone towards things. The language they use is extremely aggressive; they use very strong language like, "You will get popped." And to Joe Public, "get popped," you know, it means something quite bad. For them, it means to get hacked. The way I would look at it personally is a little bit like the padlock on your home. Not everyone—the padlock on your home, or, you know, the lock on your home is there to protect against the capabilities of what it's there to protect. It's not there to protect nuclear secrets; it's not there to protect Fort Knox. And it could be looked at that they're using AI at a level of scale to go and pick those locks, and then say, "Hey, your lock's not secure, you need to deal with this." Whereas actually, they're the ones with the resources to be able to fix this, but that seems to be not something they contribute to in terms of patches or in terms of financial support. And the scale of AI is kind of the issue. The bug reports are very wordy, and they are so much that it's almost a denial of service by AI-generated bug reports on very niche codecs. And the other issue the security community has is everything is marked high priority: "You're going to, you know, this is the most important thing in the world, and you need to deal with this high, high, high vulnerability, scary, scary, scary," on a game codec used on one disk in 1993. Yeah, and that's where the dichotomy lies. Going around telling everyone that their padlock's not safe—well, that's a hobby project of somebody. The safety of that codec is commensurate with what that person thinks; it's their hobby. It's good that they're doing security analysis on it, but it doesn't need a big scary warning saying, "This is a critical vulnerability." We also recently saw that there was another, quote-unquote, "vulnerability." It wasn't Google in this case, but a filter could overflow and have an integer overflow, and one of your pixels could be the wrong color, and this was marked high, 7.5 severity in red. And at some point, the security industry needs to realize you can't keep crying wolf like this, because this just leads people to do the equivalent of putting password stickers on their PC. You know, you can't just keep crying wolf every day. And I appreciate that is their modus operandi—to create as much scare and fear—but from the Google standpoint, at the end of the day, they need to contribute either financially or with patches. Google uses FFmpeg on a scale probably you or I couldn't even contemplate—millions of CPU cores—and yes, they contribute in areas mostly regarding their own products, so VP9, AV1, but in a wider sense there's a disproportionate level of contribution. Yes, they fund students, yes, they fund Summer of Code, and I think—so, Alex Strange is a former FFmpeg developer, I think posting in a personal capacity... So, he posted about security engineers on Hacker News. His post reads, "The problem with security reports in general is security people are rampant self-promoters. (In parentheses, Linus once called them something worse.) Imagine you're a humble volunteer OSS developer. If a security researcher finds a bug in your code, they're going to make up a cute name for it, start a website with a logo, Google is going to give them a million-dollar bounty, they're going to go to Defcon and get a prize, and I assume go to some kind of secret security people orgy where everyone is dressed like they're in the Matrix. Nobody is going to do any of this for you when you fix it." Basically, he is commenting on how the sort of incentives for the different people involved are misaligned. The problem here is a disproportion of means on discovery compared to patching it, right? And this is the biggest issue, right? And after that debacle, Google did make some changes. They are now starting to send patches, which is good. And they also now have reward programs for fixing issues, so it has changed a bit because of that debacle, which is good, right? But we've seen—and we talk about Google—but we have seen some other large companies saying, "Oh, you need to fix this bug because it's critical in our product." Can you explain the xz fiasco? The FFmpeg tweet reads: "The xz fiasco has shown how a dependence on unpaid volunteers can cause major problems. Trillion-dollar corporations expect free and urgent support from volunteers. Microsoft Teams posted on a bug tracker full of volunteers that their issue is high priority." "After politely requesting a support contract from Microsoft for long-term maintenance, they offered a one-time payment of a few thousand dollars instead. This is unacceptable. We didn't make it up; this is what Microsoft Teams actually did." And then you give the image and the details and all that kind of stuff, showing that these trillion-dollar companies are not giving much money, not giving much support. They think an open source project is a traditional vendor and they have an SLA. They think a public bug tracker is actually, you know, a third-party vendor's Jira where you can do all of these things. It's not. It's there to report bugs. I think the thing that made this particularly heinous was the name-dropping of Microsoft, the name-dropping that this is a visible product. If this was just a general bug report, I think that would have made it a lot better. Yes, so they literally said like, "This is a big deal because a lot of people are using it in Microsoft." I wonder what happens psychologically. So, I think what happens in these companies—maybe you can correct me—is they, you're right, they just think of FFmpeg as like a vendor that Microsoft surely is paying a huge amount of money to. They kind of assume that in their interaction, and nobody anywhere on the stack is going like, "Wait a minute, shouldn't we be giving like a million dollars to FFmpeg?" And this is a very big problem in large—like, we're talking about some companies, but it's the same everywhere, right? A lot of those companies, like, when we talk to that person, right? He was just like a manager on one project in Microsoft Teams, right? He had never really discussed with the open source community, and he had no idea, right? It was like that. But the problem is that usually there is what we call OSPOs, right? Open source program offices in those types of companies, and they are the ones who are supposed to discuss with open source vendors or open source communities. But they often don't explain that correctly internally, right? And here it's just like, "We are not your supplier. If you want me to be your supplier, I'm very happy, right? I will send you a contract and SLAs." Like, I created five companies who are doing that around open source projects, so that's okay. We should say that some of the spicy tweets that you're behind, Kieran, and some of the debacles produced results—positive results. Donations have increased substantially. They're still not enough to cover even a single full-time developer, but on both an awareness level and a technical level, there's substantially more technical awareness and sort of awareness of the importance of FFmpeg as a result of X and what's happened. I can say, you know, it's solved its purpose. People realize the level of importance FFmpeg has. And on VideoLAN, it's the same, right? Like, for example, a very simple example: for more than a year, we couldn't update VLC on Android because of a bug on the Play Store, on the Android Play Store, right? The only way we got someone to answer was to put a very spicy, as you say, tweet saying that we were going to stop distributing VLC for Android, right? And we have around 100 million people using that. And then someone from Android actually came and discussed it with us, right? We had the same issue with Microsoft, saying that we were going to stop distributing VLC on the Windows Store. And unfortunately, we are so small that the only very strong power we have to solve those issues is blaming them on social networks, because it snowballs and then they listen to us. But those large companies often have difficulty talking to us. Like, for example, VLC is probably one of the top 10 software programs used on Windows, yet I am not part of Microsoft's ISV programs, right? I don't have a point of contact at Microsoft, right? Where I'm sure any other software—Adobe, Spotify—has a point of contact, I don't have that, right? So raising awareness works. It's sometimes very spicy and there is a lot of drama, but X and Twitter are okay for that, and it's efficient. So everybody listening to this should go follow FFmpeg on Twitter, on X, and follow VideoLAN on Twitter, on X. Go donate. Donate to FFmpeg. And thank you, Lex, over the years—several years—you've been a supporter of, you know, FFmpeg and VideoLAN on X, giving us shoutouts and appreciating what we do. FFmpeg for life. And, for example, like, Tim Sweeney, Carmack, and a few others—like very high-level people—have raised awareness on our X accounts, and that helped a lot also. Karpathy. Karpathy as well, yes. Yeah, I mean, also, you know, outside of the fact that so many people use it and it's so impactful on the world, it's also a great representation of a great open source project, like the value of assembly and C, and making sure that you take programming seriously for real-world systems. It's not just that. We'll talk about assembly later, I'm sure, because that's its whole topic in itself. But it's also celebrating people like Andreas Rheinhardt who do maintenance, I believe unpaid, as a volunteer. He's doing massive refactoring. Andreas Rheinhardt and Anton Khirnov are rewriting ffmpeg.c with threading, celebrating those guys and celebrating the untold labor that's gone into this, which actually doesn't change anything from the user standpoint. The files are exactly the same, but wow, the airplane has been rebuilt whilst it's in the air. Kieran, on X, you said, "There's a teenager running this account," referring to the FFmpeg account, and you responded, "Teenagers have written more assembly in FFmpeg than Google engineers." But also, you were just pointing out that there are a lot of incredible contributors who are teenagers. Like JB said, we don't care who you are, where you're from, or what you do. Teenagers have written thousands of lines of assembly over the years. Give a shoutout back in the day to Daniel Kang. Also, highlight the work of people like Ruikai Peng; this is a 16-year-old. Some of his first contributions to FFmpeg actually did put some of these, quote-unquote, "security researchers" to shame by actually finding issues and fixing them, and being 16. There are no barriers. There are no barriers where you have to study at college under this person and understand these things. If you can learn C—and let's be honest, that is from the K&R book—if you learn C, you can learn assembly. We'll talk about that maybe a bit later, but you can contribute to world-class technologies. In VLC, one of the oldest contributors is called Felix. He's the one doing everything on Mac and iOS. He started working on VLC when he was 16. We had a guy called Edward Wang, who used to be a Google Summer of Code student who stayed for three years around VideoLAN. He was 14, right? And he was part of Google Summer of Code and Google Code-in, which were programs where basically we had students or high schoolers write a ton of assembly for x264, and for VLC, and for FFmpeg, right? So, everyone can contribute. And he also did a good job because he didn't play the alarmist, creating a CVE, which is like a public exposure of security, and doing these big scary red 7.5 high priorities. He just fixed an issue in Git after three days, and just fixed it. He didn't need to go and play a big security drama about it, and I think I posted, "The kids are all right." Whereas, you know, there is a portion of the security community, as Alex said, that likes to hype themselves up by creating drama. They would have happily raised, "This is a high priority, CVE 8.0 or whatever," on an issue that actually was in Git, wasn't even in a release—it was in development—and three days later, it was fixed. Well, I just want to put a little bit of love out there, even to the bigger community. Much love and respect to Google engineers. Like you said, they are some of the best software engineers in the world, and they do contribute a lot, even on the security front. And also, you know, I'm a big fan of Theo. Much love to Theo; he was part of this debacle and drama a little bit. I think when you just zoom out on the grand arc of human history, the drama contributed positively to everybody involved. Donations went up, it brought more attention to the topic, and it allowed everybody to bicker in a way that ultimately got them to figure out what FFmpeg is all about. So, the way we look at this is like it's a rap battle, at the end of the day. No, but it is. We say stuff—we say stuff, yeah, but we can leave it on. X is a perfect place for, you know, an international rap battle. You say stuff, I say stuff about your mama, but it doesn't mean, you know, I'm going to have an actual personal issue with her. And that's what it looks like. The Theo situation—you know, JB can maybe expand—went a little bit too far, and there was a... but you know, it's just a bit of fun. It's just a bit of a rap battle, a bit of WWE, you know? Everyone is having a bit of fun on X; it doesn't need to be taken seriously. You know, the teenagers thing... so, that guy was a Google employee saying, "Hey, you know, there are other ways to run an open source business, you know." Come on, just have a bit of fun, you know? That's the point of this account. And furthermore, if you can teach people about the ways of open source projects, assembly, etc. by doing that, I think there's a lot to be offered here. It's not dunking on people for dunking's sake; it's showing actually... the story that I think X learned is that these are not big corporate open source projects. This is not Kubernetes where there are hundreds, maybe thousands of people paid to develop this stuff. These are just people in their basements in their spare time. And if you can address that topic in a fun and entertaining way, I think that's a good thing. That's the value of X and the reach we have. And to be honest, right? Like, even at Google, Google is one entity, but with so many different people, right? And there are a ton of Google engineers we work with all the time, from YouTube to Chrome, to Chrome Media, to the rest of Google. Those are very different types of entities. But what we do is efficient. And for example, for Theo, right? It went a bit too far. I had him—like, I calmed everyone down. I had him on the phone, and we said, "Okay, this goes too far," and so on. But in the end, yeah, it's a rap battle, but it's positive for the project. The awareness we have on open source—and I mean true open source from communities, right, not corporate-driven—has increased dramatically in the last two years, and this is useful. What do you think motivates all the incredible contributors that we've been talking about? Like, what's the engine? It's so interesting to see. Like you said, they're sitting in the basement. What's the driver? What's the engine there? There are many drivers, but weirdly, the main one is that what we do in multimedia plays videos, and video is cool, right? And for example, we have so many people in the community who arrived because they loved watching anime, right? And this is like the advice when people ask me, "What should I work on in open source, how do I start?" and my answer is always the same: work on something you love. I am working on VLC because I love movies. CPU architecture, computer architecture is key, and this is also why it's handwritten. I don't know anyone—I've never heard of any other project than dav1d doing that. This is what Kieran calls an art, right? It is an art. I think in the mass world, there isn't something on billions of devices like this. I know there are some specialist industries—I know in high-frequency trading they take this really seriously where they're receiving feeds from a market and they need to react within x number of microseconds, and so the instructions matter—but that's not a mass-produced thing that's on a billion devices. That's hyper-specialized, running on hyper-specialized hardware. We're running on all hardware from... Sorry to linger on it, but that's a really counter-intuitive, almost like revolutionary idea here: that there is a huge amount of value to assembly. Like, what are we supposed to take away from that? There's a bunch of people listening to this, and they're basically—sorry, myself included—you know, I programmed for many, many years in C and C++, going up the standards of C++, fell in love with C++, even meta-programming and so on. And then I transitioned more and more, because of machine learning, about 15 years ago, to Python. And so, in this Python world, JavaScript world, now vibe coding where I'm just using natural language, sitting in my jacuzzi, drinking a drink, and just talking to the computer, like *record scratch*, why is there value? We go back all the way down to the low level? Like, what's the intuition? Because you can get more power per dollar invested, right? And sometimes, it's going to be a problem that is limited by your hardware. A good analogy is what you see in quantization in LLMs, right? People are doing, "Oh, I'm going to do that in FP8 or FP4," or some crazy things like Microsoft did with 1.58 bits, because you're constrained by memory, because you're constrained by the machine you can run. Because at some point, we are doing real-time, and I believe this is going to happen on AI inference also. At some point, you need to get faster, and you cannot always get more powerful hardware, right? So you need to analyze code and see where—like, where is the mission-critical part, where are the things that are called non-stop? And for example, dav1d is a good example. It's going to be run billions of hours per day. That makes sense. It doesn't make sense to be on the glue of the FFmpeg CLI; it makes sense over there. Yeah, and this has to do—we'll also talk about it more—with your new effort, your new company, Kyber, which is doing that kind of thing for ultra-low latency, with the slogan being "every millisecond counts." So when you are actually extremely highly constrained in some dimension... We are also arriving at a point where we've done so many great things, but the hardware is getting back to us, right? Because costs are increasing, because we need more power, and so you're limited by either your CPU, your RAM, or your networking, and you need to optimize. This is where the value is going to be, especially because doing AI is going to help do the programming of like, business logic, right? And so the core things that you will not be able to vibe code are optimizations for the hardware to be as fast as is possible. I'd love to talk to you about who and how should learn assembly. But first, I think we need a bathroom break. All right, and we're back. There's this nice repo with assembly lessons. First of all, do you think developers should learn how to program in assembly, and how would you go about learning it? And what is this "asm-lessons"? So, I personally wasn't happy with the way assembly is taught in books and online because it's very grammar-focused, and you don't, in general, learn a language from learning the grammar and the structure. You learn a language by asking someone what their name is, and you start from there. You go and solve real problems that you have when you want to communicate; you don't learn sentence structure first, like, "This is an interrogative and this is the adverb." And all the assembly books seem to be doing that, going through every instruction, even ones that aren't really relevant, explaining what they all do and how they... it actually doesn't really change much. And the other problem that we have in our community is assembly is taught sort of hand-to-hand, like person-to-person, like blacksmithing, one by one. That's the only logical sort of analogy, and that doesn't really scale online, and it doesn't do other things. So, I started a set of assembly lessons in the way it's done in FFmpeg, which is a little bit different to the way assembly is done in general. I'm trying to think of another good, big use case of assembly; it's in embedded devices, in really, really low-power, cheap devices. That's completely different to what we're doing here. I think it would be good if you could highlight the requirements, which are quite simple. It's high school mathematics and C, and actually, not even C—really, it's pointers. To emphasize, yes, we've talked about how brilliant this stuff is, but high schoolers, like Daniel Kang, have written assembly in FFmpeg. I think there have been contributions because of these lessons. So, it's really about trying to get this dying art to continue, because we've shown it's possible with dav1d to produce something amazing. There are still a lot of codecs in FFmpeg that are only partially assembly-optimized. And so, it really starts with the basics and continues, explaining a lot of the jargon and a lot of the syntax. It doesn't really try and explain to you, you know, interrupt handlers and interrupt instructions and all these different jump targets. It actually makes this really vector-focused, and describes all kinds of registers—general-purpose registers, vector registers, with really nice examples. This is cool. It's a classic—it's a classic example of FFmpeg. But some of this assembly language is really beautiful, and I think it's beautiful because it's kind of like flying a Spitfire. It's really aviation at its purest, but also pushing the aircraft beyond what the designer thought was possible. So, we're abusing, for example, sometimes cryptography instructions to do certain things. And there's a level of beauty and art where it's really you and the processor. There is nothing in between; it's you and the joystick of the cockpit, and you move that joystick, and it's physically connected to the ailerons. You can push that plane beyond what it can normally do. And there's a level of, yeah, beauty and amazingness to doing that. But I don't think the sort of person-by-person assembly that is—someone taught me, and I've taught multiple people—is going to work in the long run, just because of the particular flavor and the way that we do it. It's literally... now, I was going to say wizards handing it down. I realize I look like a wizard wearing this hat. But you're basically just like the sages, the wise sages handing down the craft. Can I ask about LLMs, like, can they help? They had more of an understanding than I expected, but they are still... I've asked them questions, and they still go and start hallucinating—not hallucinating, but making modifications. And then I go, "Is this exact? No, fix it." And then it just goes and does the same thing. There isn't the corpus of information like Stack Overflow to work on. There is not enough data to train on, and this is the biggest issue. I started my career actually doing some assembly for Itanium, right? So the Itanium is a dead processor type, right, which was done by Intel and HP a long time ago when they wanted to do 64 bits. Well, they lost, and then we got AMD who did AMD64, which became x86-64. But Itanium was extremely interesting in the sense that those were processors that had a ton of computing power to do floats, FMAs, which is similar to what we need now for LLMs, right? And you could pack three operations per line that could be loaded. So basically, you had an output of 6 billion operations per second, but the memory bus only allowed 1.5, right? So your CPU was four times faster. So you had to do crazy things to pack things in memory and reuse the registers. And those types of semantics, no language could do that, right? So, like, I have the Itanium programming book because Intel did amazing books, but that's exactly what Kieran says: if you don't know what you're going to do, it's impossible to read, right? It's a ton of jargon and so on, while those lessons are amazing because they are targeted to a real problem, and you can do it yourself. And people have had their patches, and they said, "Oh, I studied your lessons, and here are my first changes." That's amazing. And part of that, in the lessons, is a framework called x86inc, written by Loren Merritt when he was working on x264, and it allows you to do more things to avoid caring too much about the different calling conventions. We had a lot of students who gave code to x264 using that a long time ago, right? So, it's really doable. And I believe it's necessary to understand assembly language, even if you don't do it much, to understand what's going on inside your computer, and that will make you a better programmer. I assure you of that because, doing that, you will understand some of the architecture of the memory inside your computer, right? Understanding registers, L1, L2, L3, RAM, SSDs, disk, and so on, which are very important because then you have a good programming culture that will make you a better programmer. What do you think about the Rust programming language? Because that's a bit of a meme. We have very different opinions with Kieran on... I think it's valuable what they're doing in terms of memory safety as a concept. Can it achieve some of the speedup that assembly achieves? Oh, not assembly by hand, no. I think that's a given. C, potentially, but I see it as having a very big Esperanto vibe about it. It's like, "We're going to solve this, and we're doing this in a particular way." Meaning it's a bit too utopian? There's a lot of focus on self-importance rather than solving real-world problems. It reminds me of the Sinclair C5. Sir Clive Sinclair of Sinclair Computers built a car, and he said, "Oh, everyone will be traveling around in one of these electric cars." And it first reminds me of that, where I think the community doesn't quite understand that in order to get people to move, you have to build something that's as good as, if not better than, what you have now. Yes, people are doing Rust rewrites, but if they only do 85-90% of the feature set of what we need, like things like Coreutils—that last 1% takes 99% of the time, to use Elon's famous quote. "Prototypes are easy"—like, this kind of stuff is easy, but to get a real electric car, you have to make a car as good as, if not better than, what we have now, and Rust isn't at that stage yet. I think we—I don't think anyone would object to seeing Rust code in FFmpeg, but it needs to work as well and support the same unit testing as everything else, and it needs to be flawless. It can't just randomly break. They can't just randomly break ABI when they want to. It needs to have, I think, more... I think it still has only one compiler implementation. Yes. So, it's got to be as good as, if not better. And saying, "Hey, here's my utopia of memory safety," isn't enough, even though we probably all agree that that's the goal. So, I've done a ton of Rust, and the two major topics I had were adding Rust modules inside VLC. One of the reasons VLC got popular, which was one of the main architectural decisions, is that VLC has a very small core and a ton of modules, right? And so you can write modules in C, in C++, in Objective-C, and anything that is basically interoperable with C. And so we did some Rust modules, and so I have experience with that, and I wrote some of it. And also, my new startup, called Kyber, is an open-source project mainly done in Rust. What Rust is extremely good at is, in the sense that, it's a better C++ that cares about memory and allows you to do things about memory ownership that no one else can do so far. However, it's great when you start a new project from scratch and you do everything in Rust, but it's very not good when you interop with existing parts. And some parts of the Rust community believe that they need to rewrite everything, and everything will be better with Rust. And the answer is like, no. I'm almost always—in all my years of being an engineer, manager, and CTO of startups and so on—of the mind: don't rewrite, right? That's the initial instinct for a lot of people when they show up to a codebase, probably before LLMs, because they don't understand the wisdom of the way things have been done in the past. They say, "Well, we need to rewrite it," hence why there's a thousand JavaScript frameworks. But the reason is the following, and this is very important to understand: it is an order of magnitude easier to write code than to read code. Yeah. And you see that also with LLMs; they can vibe code, but analyzing is a lot more difficult. And so when you arrive to a very complex piece of code, you don't understand it, right? Because it's so much more effort to understand the code from someone else, because you don't have their thought process. Often I joke about some languages, mostly Perl, for example, which has very complex syntax. And imagine I am at my maximum intellectual efficiency in programming, right? And I write the best code ever. I will not be able to understand myself six months later, right, because reading code is more difficult. So very often you arrive, you don't understand all the wisdom, all the business logic, the reasons things were done, which is maybe not documented, and you say, "Well, I'm going to rewrite it." And the thing is, no, you don't, right? Because as Kieran said, "Oh, I'm going to rewrite Coreutils in Rust," and then, of course, you arrive very quickly at 80%, then 90% takes a bit more time, and then you've got the last ones, right? On the other side, for new projects, it's great. Everything related to parsing files and networking, because of the memory checker and borrow checker, it's amazing and there is nothing else. To answer a bit differently for us, imagine I take a piece of software like dav1d or x264, right, which has a ton of runtime in assembly. I rewrite the C part in Rust, so it's more secure. Yes, but then you arrive into the assembly, and you can jump anywhere in the memory because we're doing handwritten assembly. So even if I rewrite the C part in Rust for security reasons, you break all the security when you write handwritten assembly because we can jump anywhere. So, in my opinion, we need to do something that is secure assembly, right? Which means compile-time checking the assembly, similar to the check assembly project that we're doing on dav1d and x264 with VideoLAN. The goal is to start instrumenting your assembly at compile time to check that it's not jumping anywhere in the memory. Otherwise, you might rewrite a part of C in Rust, but if you want to have the same performance, you're going to have inline assembly, and so you destroy your whole security model. So that's a bit of what I think about Rust. And I would say, on a personal level, I'm so in awe of assembly. Actually, once in a while—it never gets old—seeing speed improvements of like 62x is amazing. Sometimes, on a personal level, I run our internal test suite at work and just see it, and I'm still in awe at the gains we have. Yes, there's a source of joy and happiness in programming for different reasons, but I think one of the greatest happinesses is in the optimization of code, and it sounds like you're right at the cutting edge of that. In the community, I want to speak about two people who are wizards of assembly, right? The two of them are actually working and living in the North of Europe, in Sweden and Finland. Henrik Gramner knows so much about Intel x86 assembly that when we ask questions at Intel about SIMDs, they tell us, "Why are you asking us, Intel? You have Henrik. Henrik knows better." He knows all the cycles of almost all the SIMD instructions for all the CPU generations, like, "Oh, yes, this is a P4, this is a Nehalem, this is a Core 2, etc." That person is like the best person on assembly in the world, and he's the nicest person that you've ever seen. He is very humble. He arrives, you don't see him, and he's amazing. And the other one is called Martin, Martin Storsjö, and he's doing mostly the same on ARM, right? So, NEON, right, and iPhones and Androids and so on. He codes in assembly on his phone, editing it with the crappy virtual keyboard you have, while watching his kids play in the playground, right? Like, this is just wizard level. So those two people are like—yeah, that's it. So when you're programming assembly at that high level, a part of that is knowing the architecture that you're programming on. So x86, ARM—ARM in particular, yes. ARM in particular. But x86... I mean, these are complicated architectures, right? Yeah, but ARM, in some ways, is more like x86 with out-of-order execution; it's not so bad. With ARM, you really need to understand all the different generations of ARM processors because they're all different. There's A72, etc., etc. And there are the Apple variants, there's this variant, there's that, and you need to write code that works efficiently on all of them. x86, broadly speaking, you have Intel, AMD, and you have sub-variants, but generally speaking, something fast is going to remain fast on all of the variants. Whereas in ARM, it's a completely different, much more complicated ballgame. We're taking a non-linear journey through history here, but we were talking about Michael Niedermayer, and I wanted to ask about this. For a time, there was a split in FFmpeg and libav? Yes, so in open-source projects, sometimes you disagree, right? That's a nice way of putting it, yeah. And the good thing is, because of the license, you're allowed to basically do your own fork, right? And this is normal, and this has happened all the time, right? An example was GCC, at the time of GCC2 and EGCS, which became then GCC3, right? There is what we call KHTML with WebKit and Blink; it is the same process. And also, like, when I want to do a new feature today in VLC, I fork, I do my thing on my own, and then I merge back to the community. So there was a split in the open-source community on FFmpeg, which became libav and FFmpeg, and after a few years, well, the community merged back and people moved on. It's a bit of drama that is normal in the open-source community, but forks are important because they change the status quo of a community. Not talking about FFmpeg and libav here, but the GCC fork made GCC a lot better because some people wanted to change the architecture fundamentally to make it faster. And of course, it's always a question of people and so on, but in the end, you realize that FFmpeg today is better than it was before the fork, and now, well, we're back all together, right? And I spent a lot of time—and Kieran can say—in the community. It's not often, to be honest, very well explained, because a ton of the reasons are not very public, but I think that that's normal, and that's good. Yeah, I mean you're making it sound really nice, but there are battles. There are pretty heated battles inside open-source projects. I mean, it is a very passionate community, and you kind of, in a distributed way, have to define the direction of things. So, looking here at Perplexity, "FFmpeg and libav split in 2011 mainly over project governance, leadership style, and development processes, not because of a fundamental technical disagreement. FFmpeg effectively absorbed libav's work, while libav withered and most distributions and developers moved back to FFmpeg." Yeah, that was a weird... from a user's perspective, that was a weird experience. Because, you know, I'm a Linux user. So, whether it's Ubuntu and so on, all of a sudden, I think for a little bit, Ubuntu—am I remembering correctly?—switched to libav? Around 12.04, 14.04, something like that, yes. And then they switched back to FFmpeg. I was like, "What is happening?" So you get to feel the ripple effects of the different internal debates that are happening. To be fair, on Apple, when you type `gcc`, you get `clang`—they did something like that as well, so... Yeah, to me, it's like the fork was heated drama, but most of the development from libav was merged back into FFmpeg, right? So, de facto, FFmpeg got a superset of libav, and that gave the users—because in the end, we work for the users—a larger set of features. And a ton of things that were discussed, for example, the debate on reviews and on how we push, are things that now are completely settled in FFmpeg, following mostly what everyone in the community agrees on, right? So, de facto, everyone who was active on libav came back to work on FFmpeg because the disagreements were fixed, and in the end, FFmpeg is stronger than it was before the fork. I know people love drama, but... Well, my main concern—I understand, and I think looking at the long history, it's all for the good—but I am concerned because there are so few humans that are critical to the success of open-source projects. I have seen it take a psychological toll on folks, and sometimes it leads to burnout. So, you have these incredible people that are at the core of open-source projects, and there is a moment that happens. Because, what is the motivation for doing it? Ultimately, it's because you're passionate about it and it makes you happy. At a certain point, you wake up and it's like, "This has been a bit too much heat from the drama." So, while at the project level, the project continues and often flourishes, sometimes there are these individual humans that are just like, "I've had enough." Yeah, but it's not just about forks, right? What you are referring to is one of the most challenging and most interesting parts of open-source today: maintainers' burnout, right? And AI is a problem because of that. Daniel Stenberg, the maintainer of curl, who is probably one of the best promoters of open-source in the world—he's, by the way, a member of the European Open Source Academy with me, so I'm very humble to be in the same community as him, right?—is against what he calls "AI slop," because it gives a ton of fake reports, bad reports, and bad patches. Then, a lot of maintainers have a heavy burden to maintain the software. This is straining the minds of open-source developers much more than forks. For example, the xz fiasco happened because there was one guy maintaining it, and he got basically hammered by two attackers who were asking him questions non-stop at weird times of night to block him. At some point he got fed up and said, "Okay, I can't do that," and gave commit access to the attacker. No, for sure. I wonder how we help that, because those people are so important. The human beings are so important to the core of these projects. So, for example, now I am maintaining a ton of multimedia and non-multimedia libraries as a maintainer, because the previous maintainers got fed up, right? Some inside VideoLAN, some outside VideoLAN, because sometimes you need a tough skin, right? You get things like, not really attacks, but, "Oh, this is not working, this is not working," and you feel it personally. This is also why resources, or the Google fiasco, was a problem, right? They don't realize that in the end, you have that meme graph where you see everything, and it's just one random open-source project that is maintaining the whole internet. You've seen that one, right? Yeah, this is the meme. I mean, it applies to a lot of open-source projects, but this is the entire modern digital multimedia infrastructure, and then that thing at the very bottom that everything relies on is FFmpeg. It's true. And then there's usually a handful of folks that are maintaining that. In FFmpeg or VLC, right, you have a community of 10 to 15 core developers, so they are not the worst open-source projects. xz, which is in even more installations, is one person, right? There is one guy. libxml2... Yeah, libxml2, right? There was a big stop. No one is maintaining libxml2 anymore, which is the only library that is able to parse XML everywhere. All the crazy edge cases of XML under ridiculous circumstances, and they get attacked by security researchers because there's some other crazy edge case that they haven't thought of. And it's like, yeah, but the body of knowledge to actually resolve that is massive. There is one guy maintaining all the time zones for everyone, who is in the middle of—I think it was Nebraska or South Dakota? The mental health of the open-source maintainers is something that large corporations don't care about or don't see, right? It's just like, "Oh, yeah, I'm just doing an open-source report," and so on. Some of it is financial, and people should definitely support open-source financially all across the board. But some of it is also spiritual, on a basic human level. There's something that happens, like with this image of FFmpeg and so much of the internet depending on it, where people almost talk down to the folks who are carrying these projects forward and maintaining them. In the security community, they certainly did. That was one of the things where I think that argument came out; there was a portion of the security community that was like, "No, these guys write crap code, they need to fix their crap code." I'm like, "No, no, no, this is a guy's hobby project. You have a security bot that's gone and found some AI-generated stuff. That guy didn't write crap code, it's just an edge case at the 99.99999% level that he didn't think about because it's his hobby project decoding Star Wars games." Forget the hobby project aspect of it, it's just hard work and it's beautiful. The right approach is to celebrate people for doing incredible, incredible work. It's just amazing that humans step up, not really getting paid at first or maybe ever, doing it out of the love of it. Human civilization runs on people like that; we need to celebrate them. To give you an idea, I received death threats on VideoLAN, right? And—you mentioned that to me—what was behind that? So, that must be, what, 2009, 2010, right? Apple was moving from PowerPC to Core Duo—that was probably in 2006—and by 2009 or 2010, I decided that we were not going to do new versions of VLC for PowerPC. Like, at that time, we were close to the version 1.0 release, and we were only four of us. It was just like, "No, this is not possible." So, I received a death threat with some powder in it, right? Remember, there were some anthrax threats at that time. It was because I had taken the decision to not maintain the PowerPC port anymore. And, of course, it wasn't anthrax. Of course, it was some type of flour, but I received that with a letter like, "You're a piece of shit, you should die, PowerPC forever," and so on. That was 2009 or 2010, right? I was young, and I was just like, "Why? What did I do?" Yeah, they can break your spirit. It's like, "Why?" My mother freaked out, right? We had to go see the police and so on. Now, I'm going to say that I'm quite happy that this happened at that time because it forged me a lot. I can take a lot of hate on me; I'm okay with it. But it sucks that that's part of reality. All the people that love VLC, all the people that love FFmpeg—like me, you know—I legitimately, probably thousands of times in my life, have had a smile on my face because FFmpeg made me happy, period. And how many times did I get a chance to say that? Zero, until I realized there's a Twitter account, and every once in a while I'm messaging it. One of the things I like on the Reddit meme about me—which I don't like this meme for a lot of reasons, but—someone says, "Oh, JB is on Reddit," which I am, right? And I say hello, right? And then I get so many people who say, "Oh, thank you for VLC." I take pictures, and then I share them to Signal, to IRC. Yes, we use IRC on VLC... I saw as a quick tangent, you mentioned IRC is like Slack for old people. So you still use IRC? Of course, yeah, I have it on my phone as well. Of course, every day. Works fine. Wow, it works fine. Works fine, yes. You don't have to power it with a crank... No, but there's no tracking, there's no nothing. The biggest issue, to be honest, compared to Slack is that it doesn't have threads; that's annoying. It doesn't have emoji reactions, which sometimes would be nice. IRC v3 does. Yes, v3, but no one uses it, and you cannot edit your messages. Yeah. For the rest, it works perfectly fine. So, how do you communicate without emojis? Well, that's why I said it's for old people. Old people, all right. And we do emojis with, you know, colons, dashes, and parentheses, right, so... Old school. Old school, yeah. So anyway, you communicate on IRC. What were we even talking about? Yeah, we were talking about death threats, but also about having people thanking you. Sometimes I get people who send me a message or email, "Oh, thank you for VLC," and I always answer because I want to validate the fact that you need to thank the open-source community. Yeah, please, everybody listening to this, celebrate, celebrate FFmpeg, celebrate VLC, celebrate all the incredible open-source projects, Linux, everything. There are so many... and, you know what I mean, even outside of open-source, just celebrate companies that create software that you use a lot and love. Celebrate human endeavor. Celebrate the human effort to not just build something that's okay, but to build something that is damn good. Yes, this is important, right? Because, as we said, we do something very complex for normal people. We want our excellence in tech to be useful for everyone, and this is why we work, right? This is why I wake up in the morning: because I want people to use our stuff, because it's making everyone's life easier. We want to solve hard problems, work on something interesting, and work on interesting technical challenges. We're engineers; we love to build things, right? When I was young, very early on, I knew I wanted to be an engineer. I wanted to build cars, right? Maybe at some point I will go back to cars. But this is it—we want to build things that are cool and useful, and they need to be challenging, because you want your brain to turn on. When did the two of you first fall in love with programming, with building, with engineering? When was the first time you programmed, Kieran? Microsoft QBasic. That was on Windows 3.1 and Windows 95: Microsoft QBasic. Wow, what'd you build? Like, a multiplication, just counting loops like 10, 20, 30, 40. Nice. And then I thought I could do everything after that. I jumped from doing that to wanting to create a football soccer video game. I drew everything out, like how I wanted to do it, and I didn't quite grasp that it's actually a massive piece of work to jump from BASIC and drawing some pictures to a video game, but there we go. I think I did BASIC also, and then Turbo Pascal when I was at the end of elementary school. But mostly, the first time I actually did some serious programming was the first year of what you call middle school, when you're 11. I lived in Italy for a year, in Florence, and it was an amazing year. The math teacher told us to work in a programming language called LOGO, where you had a turtle that was designing things on the screen, and you turn left and right. In the end, we used that to do very complex programming because, of course, you could do recursive things, and this changed everything. I knew I wanted to do things with computers and program. I don't think we quite talked about x264 properly. We talked about dav1d. Can we return and backtrack a little bit to x264, this thing that powers basically all of the video on the internet? Can you tell me the story of x264? And Kieran, you're actually a contributor to x264. So x264 is a video encoder for the H.264 video standard. It dominates internet video but also other areas, such as Blu-ray discs. And Blu-ray discs are interesting because the people that make them really want the highest quality.
