There are some really cool high-end films that have been encoded, as well as broadcasting and other areas. x264 was a big step change because it kind of happened at the right time as well. All the development took place when HD video was coming out and Intel Core 2 and Nehalem CPUs were getting fast. You could do real-time video, but the most important thing was a key focus on visual metrics. Before that, industry and academia for 20 years had been obsessed with mathematical metrics like peak signal-to-noise ratio, mean squared error, and logarithm of mean squared error. That led to tons of issues because mean squared error leads to blurring; you actually want to minimize the overall number, so you add a little bit of error to everything to reduce the mean squared error, as opposed to having a big localized error. This led to loads and loads of blurring. So, hobbyists bucked that trend. It was for their own personal videos, mostly anime. There were two things they did differently, and there was a big iterative feedback loop with the community. They used psychovisual rate distortion, using block energy trying to compensate for human perception when making decisions. So, psychovisual distortion—that's the critical thing. That's the thing. I mean, that's kind of revolutionary, that we can rethink it, not make it this theoretic thing of compression, but make it all about being pleasing visually to the eye. Yeah, compressing in a way that loses the least amount of information for the stuff that matters for us humans. Yes, exactly, as opposed to what some parts of the industry are still obsessed with, which is mathematical numbers that don't look good in reality. And then adaptive quantization was the other big one, where it was biasing bits against complex areas and redistributing them to less complex areas, like grass. Grass has some high frequencies, but it's less complex overall compared to more complicated things. And this came around with "parkrun"—or parkjoy. Parkjoy was really the canonical sample that was... Is that the running around in the park one? Yeah, so this was created by Swedish television in the beginning of HD. It was shot on film, no expense spared in terms of production quality, and it was given away for free. This was really the sample that sorted the men from the boys, in terms of having so many challenges with the trees, the water, the grass, and the motion. I don't think there has been any public test sequence as good as that since. For people who are just listening, we're looking at a bunch of humans running along a river. You have the reflection of light, a lot of really high-information textures everywhere, the leaves, and the lighting playing with the leaves and all of this. You could show clearly that encoders with high PSNR will blur everything. You could see actually that if I turned on psychovisual stuff and adaptive quantization, it would just look so much better. But your metrics—and these metrics at the time were considered so holy—yeah, these are the holy metrics that are untouchable, where PSNR is the most important thing. Can you speak to how you measure psychovisual stuff? How do you turn how pleasing a compression is for a human eye into a number? Is that even possible? Well, that's what Netflix has been trying to do with VMAF; they say they've used a machine learning model. That's a more recent thing, but back when x264 was being developed, it was by eye. It was developers on their laptops, so it wasn't even like big companies with professional screens or anything. That was actually one of the goals, which was—the developers at the time, Loren Merritt in particular, said—"I don't want to test this on a $30,000 screen. I want this to look good on someone's laptop at home." Yeah, brilliant. And there is another sample, a sample from Planet Earth, the bird sample, that I absolutely love, and you're going to see why. It's a ton of birds, right, flying, and the further it goes, the more birds there are. At the end, it's almost like you have millions of birds—it's the most complex thing ever to encode, right? And, well, you watch it on YouTube and you see how bad the YouTube encoding actually is. This is phenomenal to optimize and get perfect quality at a constant bitrate. There was also a lot of optimization, mostly by Loren, on anime for a long time. Anime was very badly encoded because there was a ton of banding, and so you saw those issues. x264 is still the reference for any new encoder—AV1, AV2, VVC, HEVC—everyone compares to x264. One of my favorite films is Cinema Paradiso. I know the engineer who created the Blu-ray, and he showed me the comparisons of x264 versus others; it's completely different. A bunch of guys in the Blu-ray world started using x264. I think the big one was Chris Anderson from Warner Bros. He did the whole Fringe box set with that—quite a thing that a person on the street actually watches and wants to look good. They kind of took a risk in their jobs doing that because they were in a big company that could buy whatever they wanted, and they said, "No, no, no, I want to use this free and open-source thing so that things look good for my customers and we build the best." To this day, I personally still try and avoid watching the most cinematic films on streaming services and buy the physical discs because they look good without even having to buy an expensive TV. I think that's the key thing. And x264 is yet another example of an open-source project. It was started by Laurent Aimar when he was at the École Centrale Paris, where VLC was born. Then you got a generation of people like Loren, Jason, Mans, and so many others—Henrik, Anton—and this is where the assembly code that we use now on FFmpeg, dav1d, and so on, was born, right? So, x264 is an amazing project with people all over the world, and I think most of them never met each other. But all of them, according to Kieran, or a large percentage, love anime. There are several things I've never gotten into, and one of them is anime. I watch anime so much. Especially at that time, because a lot of anime content didn't exist commercially. We were before Crunchyroll, right? So, what happened was usually people who loved anime took some DVDs from Japan and ripped them because there was no commercial offering. Some of the people who were what we call fansubbers were basically translating it themselves to make subtitles, right? At that time, you downloaded completely illegally; it was the only way to do that. So all of that was handcrafted, and it fit the open-source community, right, because they needed tools to encode and to do fansubbing. One of the most amazing open-source projects for subtitles is called Aegisub, and it's done for anime, for South Asian and Japanese languages. There are weird textures in anime that I don't think you get in real-life content. I think that was a key one: optimizing these weird textures that you get because anime is not made in a normal fashion. Yeah, the way it's produced is mostly on screens since a bit of time, and you have all those gradients in colors because they are very easy to produce digitally but very complex to produce in real life. The subtitles also are very complex because you need to have the Japanese, and then you need to have the diacritics—what we call the ruby, which is the hiragana and the katakana for the kanji—along with the English or French subtitles because you want to learn, right? There are so many crazy things in subtitling, and we had crazy samples on subtitles. Subtitles that we've seen all around. So this is an important part of the culture, but also because there was no official offering, there was no other way of doing that. Can you speak to the difference between H.264 and AV1, and then x264 and dav1d? This is a big step. Can you help people understand? Are some of the streaming sites moving more towards the direction of AV1? Let's be honest: all of those codecs since MPEG-2 video use the same concepts. They use the same concepts of inverse transform, intra prediction, motion compensation, and entropy coding. All of them. However, each generation gives you a bump of between 25% and 50% more compression for the same quality. And so you had MPEG-2, the DivX era, and then H.264, which was a game changer; H.264 improved things so much. Then you had HEVC and VP9 at the same time. VP9 is a bit similar to HEVC in terms of quality compression, but it's royalty-free. Because in multimedia, there are tons of patents, and the licensing after H.264 got out of hand, costing hundreds of millions of dollars per year. So it made no sense. Google did VP9, and the Alliance for Open Media did this new codec called AV1. You can imagine that AV1 saves between 40% and 60% bandwidth compared to H.264 for the same visual quality at a given bitrate. That's how you increase the quality: either you set the bitrate and increase the quality, or you set the quality and decrease your bitrate. But because we've moved from SD to HD, and HD to 4K, and 4K to 4K HDR, we are increasing the size by a factor of two, three, or four, right? So you need better compression to keep it manageable. AV1 has more coding tools, bigger blocks, and lots more sub-partitions in each block. It's just exponentially more complex. It's more complex because the encoder needs to search many more possibilities. For example, one of the things that is easy to understand is predicting a block from another color block. You have directions, right? You can go left, right, bottom, up, and then into the other quadrants like north-east, north-west, and so on. But that's eight directions. Then you can do more directions—you can do 16, or 69, or 128. Every time, your encoder is going to spend more time to see, "Oh, well, this block is exactly this one." The encoder needs to check which of those tools are going to compress the block better. So, I guess that AV1 encoding is two orders of magnitude more complex than H.264 in terms of CPU cycles. It is orders of magnitude, right? Yeah, and as we discussed, CPUs are not getting faster; you're just throwing more cores at the problem. But also, it's a fact that you encode once, and you have hundreds of millions of users, right? For example, YouTube is a very good case. YouTube encodes almost everything in H.264, but the popular videos get re-encoded in AV1 because, of course, it costs more to encode, but you encode once and send that to millions. So it's a trade-off between encoding time and complexity on the server side versus CPU usage on the client side. Because in the end, if you're distributing a video to hundreds of thousands of people and the size is half of what it would be, then it's better. It's better for your battery, it's better for your modem, etc. So, we can lay out, let's say, the top five codec-container combos: H.264 inside MP4 containers, AV1 inside MP4/WebM containers, and ProRes for non-linear editing inside MOV containers. For people who don't know, ProRes is Apple's codec for editing, originally for Final Cut Pro. It's designed for fast decoding and fast seeking because an editor needs to move very quickly, so it's a different use case than distribution. There is no, or very minimal, temporal compression. There's none in ProRes, so you can make clean cuts. This is what we call an intra-only codec, right? So I'm going to explain quickly what I, P, and B frames are. I-frames, often called keyframes, are complete frames. It's like an image, like a JPEG, right? You start, and you see everything. Then, the next image can be a P-frame, which is a predicted frame. You take some part of the previous image—saying, "Well, I need block five, seven, and 42"—and you replace it, and then you just give the extra residual information, right? But that means that in order to decode this P-frame, you need to have access to the previous I-frame, right? And then, of course, you have the more complex ones, which are B-frames: bi-predicted frames, which can depend on different types of frames, some in the past, some in the future. ProRes is an intra-only codec, meaning it only uses I-frames. For H.264, the default GOP, group of pictures, in FFmpeg is something like 250 frames. And to me, this is like magic—that you can predict and have a complete frame only every several seconds, and then still have this chain of predictions. The fact that somebody like me can use FFmpeg to compress something and not notice, and the result still plays back smoothly, is like magic. You can even have—and we use this a ton on Kyber—what we call "intra-refresh," where basically there are no I-frames after the first one at the beginning. You never send another I-frame. How does that work? You build up an I-frame gradually across the stream as it continues, refreshing certain parts of the image, so you never have a full I-frame. This is intra-refresh, right? But for me, the biggest mind-blowing thing when I started was B-frames. B-frames, meaning bi-predicted frames, can depend on frames that are coming in the future. That means that in order to decode this B-frame, you need to wait for the next frame it depends on, buffer that, and decode that one first so that you can decode the B-frame, right? So the way you decode the frames—the decoding order—is not the same as the display order. That means the encoder needs to be very clever and decide, "Well, you know, I'm going to depend on things in the future." This is mind-blowing. The fact that it works so smoothly every day is kind of miraculous in some ways. It works so well that you can have a stream that works across the world on a decoder in Europe versus one in the US, made by different manufacturers, and they produce bit-for-bit exactly the same material. That's quite remarkable. And to do such complex things, getting more and more complex, and still be bit-exact—there's a lot of work that goes into that. There are a lot of knobs you can control in this whole process. There's a lot of really fascinating parameters that I've gotten to know more and more over the years that FFmpeg gives you complete access to. Maybe you can speak to some of them. First of all, obviously, we can lower the resolution, we can lower the frame rate, and we can use different kinds of codecs, from H.264 to AV1. There are ways to tune the trade-off between bitrate and quality, as we've spoken about. You could do constant bitrate, you can do constant quality, CRF, CQP, or QP. We can do a longer or shorter group of pictures, GOP, and control the number of B-frames. I mean, all that kind of stuff. It's crazy. What is crazy is that a ton of people's job is to optimize those parameters, right? A ton of people that you see at YouTube, Netflix, Meta, and so on, they are not writing codecs; they're just finding the right parameters for the file they have, for the format they have. Because something that is for a movie versus user-generated content from your phone, or a screen recording, or something you're going to video edit—you don't want the same things. There are thousands of people whose job is just to optimize all that. Yeah, they're wizards. Hats off to them. All the streaming sites actually deliver at scale, and YouTube is really magical because it's not just doing what Netflix does, which is one-way, broadcasting-type delivery. They also have to upload videos from everywhere, so they are doing encoding at scale for videos that are going to be watched by maybe five people, and it still has to deliver them at a moment's notice with very minimal latency. They also serve them in all different resolutions. Like, YouTube is basically the web version of VLC. Well, actually, that's funny because Google Video—which was something they did before they acquired YouTube—was actually using the VLC plugin so that you could run VLC inside the web browser using the ActiveX plugin. It worked in Internet Explorer, and you were actually running VLC inside your browser. This is funny because today we have the opposite, where we have VLC WebAssembly, where we compile all of VLC and FFmpeg to run VLC inside the JavaScript virtual machine. Okay, there's this legendary story that you pointed me to, that was discovered via a WikiLeaks release of Vault 7 documents: the CIA was using a modified version of VLC to basically try and trick people to steal their data? Yes, exactly. Can you explain what the heck happened? This was a surprise, right? Because at some point, WikiLeaks mentioned some documents—there were a few related to Blu-rays and VLC—but the most interesting one was the CIA Vault 7, which, if I understand correctly, showed the CIA had a custom version of VLC where they had a specific plugin. Yeah, exactly. We had to write a press release on that. VideoLAN wrote a press release saying, "The only safe source for getting VLC media player is the official VideoLAN website." I mean, I suppose that's a security vulnerability for basically any piece of software; somebody can trick you into downloading it from a fake website, or via a targeted advertisement. That was a targeted attack: to watch a specific file, they told you you needed to watch it with this custom version of VLC. It used the normal binaries of VLC, except they added one DLL—I think it was `psapi.dll`—which basically read your document folder, encrypted it, and sent it. This is very clever, to be honest, because once you're watching a movie, you're going to do that for two hours and you're not going to touch your computer. It's normal, because it's HD, that your fans go up and there is a ton of CPU usage because you're using VLC. But what you don't see is that it's actually a powered-up version of VLC being used by the CIA. We had exactly the same problem with Chinese hackers targeting Indian people, which got VLC banned in India until I had to fight in court against the Indian government to unban VLC. They didn't modify VLC directly; they just took one DLL because we signed our DLLs correctly, and they used that DLL to load another program. So you had a legitimate `vlc.exe` calling `libvlc`, but it was loading a fake payload DLL, and they used that to target people. There is not much we can do actually to block those types of hacks. Yeah, and I think people should, for all open-source software and all software in general, pay attention to where they download the thing. Yes, because that means they were not downloading it from our website. Do the search engines help you prevent threats from people manipulating SEO to get up there? Absolutely not. We have had a big issue for more than 10 years with a fake version of VLC in Germany. It was reported 12 years ago, and Google basically decided not to act. They know what's in it, but the binary is too big for their virus analyzer to analyze, and so, if you're in Germany, you can end up on a website with a fake version of VLC with a custom installer. It's very popular in Germany because the website is in German, and Google lists it before the official VideoLAN site. And the weird thing is that it doesn't do anything on your machine for three weeks, because that's how they evade detection. After three weeks, there is a small service installed at the same time that wakes up and starts downloading spyware and adware. Google knows about it, and they decided not to do anything. The guys use dark SEO in Germany to do that. This is very damaging, because one of the things they're downloading is actually something that replaces the ads inside your browser. It's actually quite surprisingly effective. Whoever is doing it—just like with spam on Twitter or X, where you get emails saying, "Your X account has been hacked," and however they phrase it, it gets you to at least click on the email. Whatever they are doing with the psychology to try to trick you, they are quite good. You receive an email saying, "Hey, there is a security update for VLC, think about updating right now because it can hack your computer." You visit a website that looks decent, you download it, and it's a new version of VLC. Great. You don't know that a month later you're hacked, with no idea that you are now part of a botnet. Yeah, so make sure wherever you're downloading stuff, it is legitimate. Speaking of sandboxing, you've mentioned that VLC sandboxing is something you're working on, and it's actually quite challenging. Why is it important, and why is it hard? Well, VLC is a core engine with around 500 plugins. One of them is FFmpeg, but we support so many other formats, new protocols, new filters, and weird architectures. In any release of VLC, you have modules that are going to call your drivers—mostly the hardware decoders, which call your Intel, NVIDIA, or AMD driver—or call FFmpeg. There might be a security issue in a shader, in VLC, or in FFmpeg that is going to cause a crash. The issue is that you are running VLC like any other program, like Adobe; you're running it on your machine, and it has access to all your documents. So, the idea is to build a sandbox so that we can protect ourselves from ourselves. Inside the VLC process, code is running that is not even ours—either it's open-source projects we integrate, or it's your GPU driver, or something provided by someone else. When we crash, we want to make sure people can't do bad things. One of the common ways of hacking people is to crash a program, which is very often done with web browsers or PDF files, and less often with multimedia, but it can happen. When you crash, you can launch something on the user's machine, like ransomware or a botnet. So, security of desktop applications is very important. On mobile, it's a bit different because most mobile applications are already running inside their own sandbox. For VLC on desktop, we could run it inside a sandbox, but the problem is that we need access to so many things that we would basically have to request every permission. And so, if you have a sandbox but you put holes everywhere, it defeats the purpose. What we are trying to do, and are actually doing, is splitting VLC into several processes: one is decoding, one is demuxing, and one is filters, and all of them run in their own sandbox. That way, if a part of VLC crashes, it's like Chrome crashing on some tab; that tab crashes, but it does not crash the whole program. This is what we're trying to do. It is difficult because it's a sandbox that needs to sustain gigabits per second of memory copies. It's not like a website handling 5 or 10 megabits; we are talking about hundreds of megabits per second. This is why it is quite challenging, and this is a research topic that we are working on in order to have a multimedia player that is secure. This is the kind of stuff you have to think about when millions of people are using your software. You've mentioned something similar where, with so many people using VLC, someone is going to use every single feature, and they will tell you about it. The best feature in VLC is called the puzzle filter, right? You click the puzzle filter, and it transforms your video into a jigsaw puzzle, and you can click and move the pieces around. It's very useful when you're watching a French movie and you're bored, because it's a very long movie about a love triangle—we've seen that so many times, right? But you need to watch it because your wife or your boyfriend told you to, so you're sitting there, and you can click and move the puzzle pieces around. It's absolutely useless, right? Who cares about that? Originally, it was written by a math teacher in a high school in the South of France to teach his students about Bezier curves, which is something everyone should know about. It's very useful, and the code was clean, so it got merged into VLC back in 2010. Five years later, I received an email saying, "Hello JB, I have a problem with VLC: the puzzle is too simple." And I was just like, "What?" Yes, the puzzle in the UI was limited to a maximum of 16 by 16, which is only 256 pieces. He said, "I'm sorry, but I love puzzles, and during a movie, this is too simple." So there is a commit of mine that you can check online where I changed the dimensions to allow up to 256 by 256. Brilliant. My point is, so many seemingly useless features are used by at least a few people. There is a way to watch VLC movies in the command line without any UI, using ASCII art. Is it useful? Very useful. Imagine you're debugging a multicast network with a very complex networking stack. You can SSH to all of the routers and run VLC on them with no UI, and you can see whether the output is black or not. You can see if it's all green or not, and debug it that way. It's amazing. People don't realize there are so many things in VLC that are useful, and they have users because once you have hundreds of millions of users, someone is going to use every single feature. I would love to zoom in and talk a little bit more about the distinction between downloading a file to watch it offline versus streaming. What are the complexities and challenges of streaming? Is there something we can say about what it takes to stream files? Because we've been talking about codecs, and I think a lot of that implies encoding and decoding without having to communicate over the network. Can you elaborate on what is required to do this over-the-network stuff? Yeah, but it is less complex than it seems compared to everything else we've talked about. The most complex thing is actually not about consumer streaming services, but what was historically done to broadcast through satellites. In most modern broadcasting services, you can pause and resume. But when you're sending live streams, whether it's satellite broadcast or live streaming services, it is much more difficult because you need to encode in real time. When you broadcast via satellite, you have a fixed bandwidth link; you cannot have a burst of bandwidth, even for a few seconds, because you don't have the space for that in your channel. There are different types of challenges there, which are interesting, but I think they are less complex than the ones we saw in the late '90s and early 2000s with broadcasting and streaming over satellite. They're different; they're control systems challenges, whereas some of the other things are more mathematical. I think that's the difference. In the streaming world, what you have is what we call adaptive streaming. The difficulty—and it's not really a video problem, it's mostly a CDN problem—is that you might have too many people watching the same thing at the same time, causing network congestion. So, your player has difficulty downloading things fast enough to play them. What happens is that, locally, the player is going to read a lower resolution of it. There are some very clever algorithms to do that, but most of it is quite basic, to be honest. "Even on the buffering side, it's pretty basic?" "Yeah, you start, you download a segment—what we call a segment—and then you time it. If it takes more than 50% of the time to download the segment, you go down. The difficulty is more about when do you go up in bandwidth and quality, but this is not very complex to do. When you encode, you're going to encode seven resolutions and you're going to give the bitrate. The difficulty is to have your encoder give the same bitrate, but it's not as strict as it used to be." "Yeah, probably YouTube has to figure out the human psychology side of that. Like, how pissed off do you get when it's a very low bitrate? And how long should it wait before it increases the bitrate even though the connection is better? Because maybe the changes in the bitrate are what affect you psychologically." "All right, I think actually, the interesting one is the audio." "That's true. You can kind of notice when they move from full fat AAC to the compressed versions of AAC that use spectral band replication. You can kind of hear it go a bit tinny, and that up and down is very jarring. The video side is a lot smoother and there's less noticed. It's really the audio—you can definitely feel it when it's moved from a different audio profile to one of the other." "I don't know, we're surprisingly tolerant of skipping audio glitches." "I'm surprised how tolerant people I know who are not video engineers are to watching sports at 30 FPS, for example, whereas it should really be 60. The world is a lot more tolerant of that, but for audio, people have an immediate feedback mechanism of, 'Oh, there's a glitch.' If you have an audio glitch, you realize it directly." "Yeah, I get to fully realize that, I suppose. One of the things I'm afraid of when I listen to audio more and more is that I get to notice every single tiny detail, and that you can over-obsess. People in general are able to kind of blur their consumption; they can look past certain imperfections." But then when you combine an event that is, for example, a sports event, it is probably going through satellite or somewhere else to a central place for encoding. Then you need to encode all these resolutions in real time; you don't have time for QA. You need to push that to CDNs, you probably need to add DRM or protection, and you need to deliver that over a ton of different devices. Then, yes, it is complex. And also, you're in the web browser or on very different devices than what you use for television, where you had a defined set-top box or cable box that you controlled end-to-end. So, it's a challenge. But the networking part, while you agree to have 10 to 20 seconds of latency, I don't think is very difficult. "Speaking of networking and latency, your new effort, as we mentioned, is Kyber, which is aimed at ultra-low latency. As you say, every millisecond counts, and you're applying that to remote-controlled machines like robots, drones, and computers. Can you tell me about it?" "Sure. If you start from where we used to be, you used to use FFmpeg to encode files, right? And then we used FFmpeg and VLC to encode in streaming services, right? And then you need to go lower and lower, and the question was: up to where can we go? This question is very important because there are many use cases where you need to be fast, and that is when you have feedback interaction. You are not just listening to something; you're actually controlling it." "That's the biggest difference compared to what we've done so far. I need video to have feedback on something that is happening live. Whether it's a drone flying, controlling humanoid robots from a distance, controlling a rover, or playing a video game in cloud gaming—which is what I did in a previous job, right? I was CTO of a cloud gaming startup, and this is a very interesting topic because you push the network to the limit." "You need to care not about the quality, like we've done on video and talked about with x264, but you care about latency, because a millisecond is meaningful when you're controlling a car. You've used Waymos, right? When Waymos don't work, even if it's only 1% of the time, there is someone who is basically remote-controlling it. This is exactly the stuff that we are building. We are building an SDK platform to do end-to-end control of machines." "So, this comes up quite a lot in a lot of different contexts in robotics. Teleoperation—teleop—is becoming more and more important, including for training robots via machine learning." "Yes. And what we do is a bit different from everyone else. We take only one socket, one connection, which is the QUIC protocol based on UDP. This is interesting because it's designed for low latency. It doesn't have the TCP head-of-line blocking problem or the HTTP head-of-line blocking problem; it's safer by default." "On the same wire, we send multiple streams, like multiple tracks. We send audio, we send video, but we also send the commands—mouse, keyboard, gamepad, and so on. And we do that while maintaining coherence and synchronization." "What people don't realize is that all clocks actually drift. When you're controlling a robot, that robot is going to have two cameras, five cameras, ten cameras, a ton of sensors, GPS, and so on. If you want to train your robotic AI model correctly, you need to have all of those in sync and coherent." "What we've done—utilizing all the things we learned on VLC in real-time broadcast, and MPEG-TS that Kiran knows well—is account for clock drift. When we record a Kyber stream from a robot, I am sure that it is going to be predictive in the way it plays back. When you record and train your AI model, you need to be sure that every time you retrain based on the data, the data is going to stay coherent." "Clocks actually drift. The existing solutions work with one camera. Once you have five or six, it becomes much more complex." "So, you want to make sure that the visual snapshot perfectly matches the time it actually happened." "Exactly. Also, if you're going to control a robot, when I do something, I need to be sure that it is actually happening at that precise time. On the server side—which would be the robot—we have a re-timestamping mechanism accounting for clock drift. That is one of the use cases of Kyber: to control robots." "I also see use cases like remote drones, remote cars, remote submarines—whether for defense or non-defense—and many places in industry, or remote surgery, where experts cannot go everywhere the machine is because it is either too dangerous or too costly. So, you allow people to have machines next to them." "The goal of Kyber is to make distance disappear, because it is either the projection of skills or the projection of power." "Imagine we are all using devices like the Meta Ray-Bans. You need to stream to them because you're not going to run anything heavy locally. You need GPU power that is on the cloud or on a phone to stream that." "All of these use cases need real-time latency for video, not just extremely low latency. That means we are toying with the encoders so that they encode a frame in 4 milliseconds. Kiran, with his company, also targets those ultra-low latencies because you need to optimize local latency to the absolute maximum. The decoder time, the encoder time, and so on, are all added to your networking time." "And it's not just about low latency; it's also about reliability. We do clever things like forward error correction. Forward error correction means you over-transmit a bit of data, maybe a few percent. While over-transmitting, you're allowed to lose some packets." "This is very difficult over an internet network when you are doing things from very far away. If you check that all packets are delivered, you add a ton of latency. If you want to avoid latency, we over-transmit some data so you can reconstruct it on the client side when things are broken." "A few weeks ago, we were doing a demo around Las Vegas for CES. We had a rover that was fully 3D printed. It's very simple—just a small car with a telescopic arm—and it was actually controlled from France. The video was captured with a webcam and run on a very small PCB server, sending the stream to someone on the other side of the planet." "There are so many use cases. You can also think about having AI that controls many drones and so on. Technically, we need to be amazing in video, we need to be amazing at networking, and we need to care about every single millisecond in networking, encoding time, and decoding time. You also need to integrate at a very low level." "So, syncing everything together well, but what kind of latency can you get to? When you say milliseconds, what is the goal?" "My goal is 4 milliseconds glass-to-glass latency." "What does glass-to-glass mean?" "It's simple. You have a computer running a program, probably a video game, or it could be a robot. You have the replication done through the network. If you take a 1000 Hz camera and take a picture, you want that to be at 4 milliseconds. Four milliseconds means 240 Hz." "Yeah, it's nuts." "So far, we have achieved 7 milliseconds from Windows to Windows, or Windows to Mac. If you look at the timing, there is around 3.5 milliseconds inside the NVIDIA hardware encoder, and around 2 milliseconds on the Intel decoder. The encoder plus the decoder is already close to 6 milliseconds. In order to go down, we will need either other types of codecs or faster encoders, but 4 milliseconds would be the holy grail." "It's pretty nuts, but I love it. I don't think anyone has ever achieved that over the internet." "You can achieve that with custom hardware, like SDI or professional hardware. But I want that to work over the internet. I want that to work with any robot, even if you just have a small Jetson Nano or an N150 in it. There are going to be millions of robots—drones are just rolling, flying, or swimming robots, right? It's just a machine you control." "To do that, you either need to teleoperate them, or, when everything is fully autonomous, you need to teleobserve them and check what is happening." "Yeah." "In my view, in the future, all those remote cars will be teleobserved by an AI model that is just going to say, 'Well, everything is good.' When it's not good, it will say, 'Hey, there is a problem,' and then you have a human operator. This is going to be about safety." "When you have a humanoid robot taking care of your grandma or my grandma, I want to be sure that everything goes well. I don't want to end up in one of those horrible scenarios where the robot is dangerous. Or when driving, I want the car to stop when it should stop, and if needed, someone can take over. There are so many scenarios around real-time control, and the goal of Kyber is to make distance disappear." "It's incredible. Some of the same technology and ideas we are talking about are connected to what you are doing." "And for me, it's amazingly challenging. I would say on video I'm doing okay, but on networking, I have so much more to learn. It's about congestion protocols and real-time bitrate adaptation, but it's quite fun. I created this project and we are fundraising in the US, of course. But it's open source—that is important. Everything on Kyber is open source." "How do you make money?" "It's a dual license: commercial and AGPL. You remember what we said about licenses. Basically, if you want to use Kyber in your product, you must make your full product open source. If you want to use this amazing technology but keep your product closed source, you pay for the commercial license." "The hobbyists and the very small guys who want to use it can use the technology to build something open source. That's awesome. If you're a large company, you get support, all the IP, the right to modification, and so on." "So, yeah, it's really cool. I am also building robots, and I love that. The rover we have is 3D printed. We are finishing a demo with an actual wing, like a drone wing, that is also fully 3D printed. We are trying to do a sailboat that is 3D printed, and we will work on some humanoid robots next. Of course, they are not going to be amazing robots—that's not our job—but we are here to make cool tools for everyone to build robots." "You're talking to the right guy; I love robots. There is a bunch of them upstairs. Teleoperation is going to be really important, especially as the number of robots scales across the world." "One hundred percent. Let's talk about the future of multimedia: FFmpeg, VLC, and some of the codecs. We didn't really mention AV2 yet. Can we lay out what AV2 is, what the hope for it is, and what H.265 and H.266 are?" "AV1 is a codec created by the Alliance for Open Media, which includes Google, Netflix, Amazon, Apple, and VideoLAN. We tried to make a very good, royalty-free codec, and now it is finally being deployed. The codec was actually finished in 2018, but a codec takes years to be used in wide scenarios." "AV2 is the next generation of this codec, and it is 30% better. If you keep the same quality, you get a 30% bandwidth reduction compared to AV1." "What's the connection between dav1d and AV2?" "We are going to make a dav2d, which I call 'devid' because 'deux' is 'two' in French." "Well done." "You have to know that dav1d is an actual recursive acronym. It stands for 'dav1d is an AV1 decoder'." "Oh, nice! I didn't even think about that. And people should know dav1d is spelled with a '1'." "Yes, and so dav2d is going to be spelled with a '2', please, please. It is going to be d-a-v-2-d. I don't know how you pronounce that, but we did a demo at CES of VLC running the first demo of AV2." "Can you clarify the specification of AV2 versus the encoding and decoding?" "For sure." "The specification is the document that explains how the codec is supposed to work. That is AV2, just like H.264. Then you have an encoder. The current reference encoder is called AVM, and there will probably be other encoders, like one called SVT-AV2." "It is the same way x264 is an encoder for H.264, and x265 is an encoder for the H.265 codec. The decoder for AV1 is dav1d, and the decoder for AV2 is dav2d. The decoder for H.264 is ffh264 inside FFmpeg, and the decoder for HEVC is ffhevc inside FFmpeg." "There is also a next-generation codec from the MPEG world. After H.264 and H.265, there is one called H.266, also known as VVC. So, HEVC is H.265, and VVC is H.266." "Why is H.266 super sexy and so much better?" "The question we often get is: why are there two names? Most of the time, it is a conjoint work from the ISO world and the ITU, which is the International Telecommunication Union. These are two regulatory bodies—one is a private entity, and one is part of the United Nations." "Which one is private?" "ISO is private. In theory, H.264 is MPEG-4 Part 10 H.264 / AVC." "And this is the full name." "Yes, it's the concatenation of the ISO name and the ITU name, even though they work together. This is historical politics, you know." "Yeah." "And for HEVC, it's MPEG-H H.265 HEVC." "Got it." "Then there is H.266, which is also named VVC." "Is there a high-level thing to say about the improvement?" "Thirty percent each generation is the best summary." "This is true both for the AV codecs and H.264, H.265, and H.266. The professionals listening to us are going to kill us because they will say, 'No, it's 35%, 25%, 50%,' or whatever. But globally, you need to know that HEVC is 30% better than H.264, and H.266 is 30% better than H.265." "There are so many different cases and scenarios. For example, in screen recording, the gains are humongous because you have a tool designed specifically for that. For a specific video, a new generation might give you a 70% or 80% gain." "There used to be a ton more codecs, but now the two main families for transmission are H.264/H.265/H.266 and AV1/AV2. I guess the major difference is the cost of encoding..." "Yes." "...and the royalty of the patents." "...YouTube, whereas patents are not even discussed in the MPEG world. Patents are off-topic completely." "Can you educate me on the patent side?" "So, usually MPEG defines a format. Then everyone comes around and says, 'Well, I have all these patents for the format.' They usually form a union called the MPEG LA—MPEG Licensing Association. You put all your patents in, and then you ask everyone who uses this format to pay for it." "Can you elaborate on what it means to have a patent on a codec? Why are there so many patents?" "Imagine I'm doing something where, instead of using square blocks, I use rectangles." "Oh, so every little idea, somebody patents it?" "Yes." "Yes." "Oh, man." "Yes." "Oh, man, how many lawyers are...? I mean, it pays for a lot of lawyers, right?" "The biggest issue is this: at the time of H.264, the patent landscape was relatively sane. But there was so much money in it that, for HEVC, a ton of things were pushed into the specification that are not useful 99.9% of the time, just so someone could add a patent to it." "It became so complex that for HEVC licensing, you had MPEG LA plus another patent pool called HEVC Advance, plus Nokia was outside the patent pool, and a few others were outside as well. It was impossible to license. A few months ago, HP decided to remove HEVC support from their Windows laptops because the patent costs were increasing." "It reached a point where patents were uncapped. For YouTube or Netflix, you could be talking about hundreds of millions of dollars in licensing fees per year. They said, 'You know what? For a hundred million a year, we could create our own codec.' And that is exactly what they did." "That is why we have the Alliance for Open Media, which we are part of, which created AV1 and is now creating AV2. We also create audio codecs. Because you have to work around existing patents, a lot of things are different. The basic things done in MPEG-2 thirty years ago are out of patent, but other things—like golden frames, S-frames, or different prediction types—are patented." "Yeah, it's like, 'I Can't Believe It's Not Butter!' 'I Can't Believe It's Not a B-frame!'" "It is kind of like that. It's just a slightly different variant of a B-frame to try and sidestep patents. You need double creativity: creativity to be more efficient, and creativity to be sure you don't infringe existing patents. For example, VVC has all the patents of HEVC plus new ones, while AV2 tries to be as royalty-free as possible." "To what degree do FFmpeg and VLC have to think about this kind of stuff?" "We don't. One of the reasons VLC was based in France is that France rejects software patents. Most of those patents are illegal there. I once calculated that if we had to pay all the licensing fees for VLC, it would cost more than €200 per user. Most of those patents are invalid in Europe because they are considered mathematical or abstract idea patents, which are not valid in Europe." "Let me ask you, just out of curiosity. The meme online—on X, Twitter, and so on—and from my own friends in Europe is that Europe is not friendly to entrepreneurship. They over-regulate, there is too much bureaucracy, and so on. Is there anything positive to say? Is there hope for entrepreneurship in the future of Europe, or is Europe over from a tech perspective?" "Just look at the two of us! It's notable that there are two people from the European continent on this podcast talking about video. Safe to say the community is heavily weighted..." "What you probably don't see yet is that there is a new generation of entrepreneurs in Europe, especially in France. The UK has done it for a long time because they have a more Anglo-Saxon approach to business. But in France, today, most young people entering the market want to create startups. Fifteen years ago, that wasn't the case; everyone wanted to work for big companies." "Twenty years ago, if you failed and your startup folded, you were basically not allowed to create a new company. There was a lot of stigma. That stigma is gone. There are so many things happening with AI in France right now. Sure, there is over-regulation—I know that firsthand as an entrepreneur—but there are good things as well." "But are there paralyzing aspects? If I look at the case of someone I've become close with, Pavel Durov, he was blamed directly by the French government for the kind of things his platform was hosting. I could see the same kind of stuff happening to VLC, being blamed for the kinds of videos people are watching." "They tried, right? We had issues..." "I mean, is that the pressure people worry about? If you have to think about that kind of stuff when you're just trying to focus on technology..." "No, you don't think about it, and that's okay. But there is no physical office. VideoLAN doesn't have an office." "But that is what happened with Pavel—they arrested him. They arrested him for content shared on the platform." "Sure, but I don't run a platform. Everything is client-side." "Yeah, but they can still arrest you." "On what ground? I'm not sharing anything. The content doesn't go through my servers." "For sure, but there are still lawyer fees. That is the problem. It's paperwork. If you had trillions of dollars, you would easily win because you are on the right side, but they can suffocate you with paperwork and bureaucracy. It's a Kafkaesque process." "Yes, but you have to realize that one of the good things in France, and most of Europe, is that answering a court order does not bankrupt you. It's not like in the US, where legal fees can easily bankrupt you. I receive lawyer letters every week, and the total cost of legal fees for VideoLAN is less than $10,000 per year. So, it's not really scary." "Similar to Pavel, have intelligence agencies tried to ask you to put a backdoor in VLC?" "Yes, two of them." "What did you say?" "No. Well, I was a lot less polite than that." "You're basically saying, 'Hell no.' If you had to compromise your software, you would shut it down. This is clear. What is your definition of compromise?" "Allowing a government to put in a backdoor. There is no code that gets into VLC that we do not control. The way we compile VLC, you would call me completely paranoid. We compile on offline machines. We start by compiling the compiler itself, and we do everything offline on systems that have never been connected to the internet." "We also use a double-signature process for signing binaries. This is especially important because we have seen—and we believe it was a non-Western governmental agency—attempts to push a fake binary onto our servers, which scared us a lot." "But VideoLAN is open source. How can you kill it? If they try, I can just move to Malta or the Cayman Islands, change the domain name, and start again. VLC is a tool that helps people do things; we are not a platform. And as for patents, you shouldn't be able to patent math and matrices. That is just wrong." "Does VLC ever censor the kind of videos it can play based on the content?" "No, never. We never do that. VLC is completely offline and doesn't talk to any server, so we have no idea what you are using the software for." "So, a government couldn't come in and say, 'We think anime is destructive to society, so you can't allow anime to play'?" "No, they cannot do that. They also tried to ask us if we could tell them whether a specific person watched a certain video, and our answer was simply, 'We have no idea.' So, no to surveillance as well." "There is no telemetry in VLC. It would be very difficult to incorporate because of the international nature of our project. We have developers in the UK, Germany, and the US who would see that code. The only thing we do is help with technical support." "For example, we once had a case where US police reached out because they had a murder case, and the video file was corrupted and wouldn't play in a certain version of VLC. They asked if we could help. We never had access to the video itself; we just helped them get the file to play." "I also remember during the Afghan War, I received an email from someone in the military saying they had a big issue with the latest version of VLC. It wasn't playing files correctly from their RTSP server where they kept all their movies. He said VLC was very important for troop morale on the ground, because it gets boring at night." "I had recently pushed an update that broke RTSP support, so I quickly built a custom version just for them. Because VLC is completely open source, the US military was able to audit the code and allow it on their laptops. So, the only way we work with authorities is to help them with technical support. We don't track how people use VLC, and we stand strong on that." "Do you feel the stress of this? You have millions of people using it, the military using it, and pressure from governments. That's a lot for a small team." "Yeah, the core team is only six to eight people. But legally, everything falls on me. Everything legal is only me." "Are you not stressed about this?" "I used to stress about it a lot. But we are doing what we can for the greater good. We make extremely complex technology easy for everyone. We are just a tool, and any tool can be used for good or bad. You cannot blame the tool. This is very important to us. I am not stressed anymore." "What's the secret to your zen? In all our conversations, you seem very zen." "I always think about the absolute worst-case scenario. Like a chess player, I ask: 'In the end, am I dead? Yes or no?' I do that constantly. That is also how I approach my startups. What is the worst case? The company goes bankrupt. That's life—companies live and die." "My moral compass is always: 'Am I dying? Am I hurting someone?' If the answer is no, then too bad if some lawyers are unhappy. What are they going to do, take all of VideoLAN's money? They'll get 50 grand. The source code is out there; it cannot be stopped. What we do is good, and it is for everyone." "That's beautiful." "Kiran, you mentioned there is an active archiving and preservation community. I think that's super fascinating. They are stretched on budget, but they see FFmpeg as a Rosetta Stone so that multimedia can be played a thousand years from now. That is a beautiful way to look at FFmpeg and VLC." "Yes, one of the coolest communities in open-source multimedia is the archiving community, largely led by Dave Rice from the City University of New York—shout out to him. They value open source because of limited budgets, but also because they recognize that archiving video is vital for the world, though playing it back is a huge challenge." "Famously in the UK, there was the 'New Domesday Book' project. They archived a lot of material on BBC Microcomputers, but within 15 to 20 years, no one had the software to play it. Someone had to reverse engineer it. If that happens in just 20 years, imagine the challenge in a thousand years." "One of the great things about FFmpeg is that it is written in C. C is about as close to mathematics and pure logic as you can get." "Do you think we will still have C compilers in a thousand years?" "Yes. We have mathematical notation that hasn't changed. C will be like Latin—a classical language you learn from the past, but still completely usable in certain contexts." "The archiving community is practically fantastic. Despite limited funds, they funded the development of the FFV1 codec, which is a lossless format. They are terrified of compression losing details, and they have a fair point." "If you compress too hard, it can subtly alter the historical record. They want files to be compressed losslessly and fast. They worked with FFmpeg to develop a brand new codec designed for fast, software-based encoding." "They are also very concerned about resilience. If they store data on tapes or hard drives and a few bits rot, they need to recover quickly. They can't afford to lose an entire Group of Pictures (GOP) just because of one bad bit. They even funded GPU encoding in FFmpeg to make FFV1 encode faster, aiming to preserve the world's multimedia heritage in a usable way." "Many archival groups worldwide have chosen FFmpeg and FFV1 as their standard. They provide us with super-specialized advice, explaining details like 1950s colorimetry on specific tapes that we would never learn anywhere else. They know things about video history that we don't." "Yes. Every time I talk to Dave Rice or people from the British Film Institute, I learn something new, and I've been doing video for 20 years. They have deep knowledge, especially on colorimetry, storage, and preservation." "They have a deep appreciation of the content itself. When thinking about lossless formats, they are terrified of losing something essential, which forces them to deeply understand the material. It's a different perspective than just focusing purely on the technology of encoding." "When you enter the rabbit hole of film scanners used for digitization, it is a massive topic that could easily span another five-hour podcast. A lot of old film is actively degrading because it isn't stored in the right environments." "Because our tools are open source, they share their workflows with countries that can't afford formal archiving institutions, where preservation is done by volunteers. They go and teach FFmpeg commands to children in India. They are the model community, embodying the ethos of what we want to achieve. They know their work will speak to people a thousand years from now." "In a thousand years, we may be drowning in AI slop, so archiving what life was actually like is incredibly important." "Yes, capturing the 20th and 21st centuries is essential. It represents a transition point where we went from a scarcity of data to oceans of digital slop." "People don't realize we are losing a ton of films today. There are films from the '30s, '40s, and '50s that are considered to have no commercial value and are left to rot. For tapes from the '70s and '80s, there simply aren't enough working tape heads left in the world to read them all. Archivers have to make tough decisions about what to save and what to throw away, which is a massive moral hazard." "But there is digital stewardship—if that's a real phrase—to make sure the world's information remains playable by everyone, rather than locked on obsolete devices." "Realistically, it's like finding a needle in a haystack. There is so much value in archiving this footage because over time we find hidden gems. Something compressed away might have contained a vital detail. By keeping it lossless, they can prove it mathematically. They can manage bit rot so that a single bit flip only ruins a tiny portion of a frame, and perform advanced error recovery." "Do you think VLC and FFmpeg will still be here a hundred years from now?" "FFmpeg, yes." "Yep, FFmpeg, yes." "VLC, maybe." "Where are FFmpeg and VLC going in the next 5, 10, or 20 years?" "The next 5 to 10 years are easy. The real question is what happens after that. Do we transition to something like holograms?" "Will VLC and FFmpeg expand to whatever multimedia becomes? Multimedia might eventually mean brain-computer interfaces like Neuralink, where we directly consume data streams. We could have VLC for Neuralink, and run FFmpeg with an input format of the human brain." "Will there be codecs for the brain to compress neural information?" "Yes, absolutely. Today we already have codecs for things like point clouds and volumetric video. There is ongoing research into RGB-D—depth codecs useful for robotics and 3D modeling—as well as compression for 3D elements and astronomy." "We already have VR and XR versions of VLC. We also support streaming XR content on KaiOS for low-power smart glasses and headsets like the Apple Vision Pro or Meta Quest. We are already working on 3D, XR, interactive, and ultra-low latency streams. It's not stopping, and we will eventually manage full 3D data inside VLC and FFmpeg. That is where it is going." "Not everyone in the community sees that, but since Kiran and I are entrepreneurs, we see where it's going. I suppose there is some natural tension inside FFmpeg where people say, 'Hey, we are really good at audio and video, why expand?'" "To answer that, we have to define what multimedia is. Multimedia is the digital representation of sensory streams for human perception. If technology allows us to capture smell with an odor sensor and output it through a diffuser, that will eventually get into FFmpeg." "So, your demuxers would have a new track type for odors?" "Yes, absolutely. It's just like audio; you might have a left and right nose track." "Stereo smell, yeah." "In VLC, we already have a plugin for haptics, mostly used for 4D cinema experiences where chairs move on hydraulic arms. The synchronized physical movement data is transported as a feed." "Is there a standard for that yet?" "There are actually several standards. That makes me so happy." "Yes, we have a custom plugin—not in the mainstream VLC distribution—that transports physical, haptic movements. It targets a human sense, so it belongs in the player." "That's an exciting future. But how do you pull that off with such a small developer community? It must be stressful to support all these different platforms and operating systems." "Look at it from the other direction. We aren't the only ones writing the code; we are the maintainers. Every year, about 150 people contribute to VLC, and 300 to FFmpeg. Our job as a core team is to integrate those contributions." "If there is high usage, people will write the modules and formats. We focus on the core architecture. For example, we recently introduced spatial audio in VLC, which required architectural changes. Now that the framework is ready, adding second or third-generation modules is easy. The same will apply to smell or haptics." "We are a multimedia framework. That means anything that is timed and sensory belongs here—including brainwaves eventually." "I love that, because FFmpeg and VLC push the industry to standardize. I hope companies like Neuralink standardize brain-computer data, or robotics companies standardize haptics." "In our experience, it always follows the same pattern. A new technology starts with five different proprietary standards. Then the hype dies down. The secondary players get together to create an open standard, we implement it, and it gains popularity. Eventually, the market leaders are pressured into adopting it. Look at 3D audio: Google Cardboard and others had competing formats that died, but now it's coming back with mature, open standards. We try to avoid closed systems." "I saw you didn't have nice things to say about Dolby." "No, I don't." "Why? What did they do that made you mad?" "They used to be an amazing company with incredible engineers who defined what sound was. Now they are mostly run by lawyers and licensing departments. They don't innovate the way they used to. It's a bit like HP." "Since we've talked about Twitter, do you have a favorite or most embarrassing tweet from the VideoLAN or FFmpeg accounts?" "My two favorites are: 'Talk is cheap. Send patches.' That embodies our ethos. Stuff doesn't just build itself or appear from the ether; someone has to write the code." "The other one I like is: 'FFmpeg: Nothing is beyond our reach.' I think that was inspired by a US military satellite patch for a global monitoring system." "Wasn't FFmpeg running on a Mars rover as well?" "Yes, the Mars 2020 rover uses FFmpeg to compress pictures. NASA wanted to use as much commercial-off-the-shelf technology as possible. So, FFmpeg runs on Mars—we are a multi-planetary open-source library." "Nice." "We often see tweets of people using VLC in unexpected places. Formula 1 teams use VLC in the paddocks to play live telemetry feeds. We have seen SpaceX and the European Space Agency monitoring rocket launches with VLC. It fills you with joy." "I've heard it's used in particle accelerators too." "Yes, one of the most amazing trips I took was to CERN. They were using VLC to monitor sensors on the 27-kilometer Large Hadron Collider ring. They had analog cameras connected to capture cards, which streamed over their multicast network using VLC so the whole campus could watch." "I visited CERN in 2010 with Laurent. We fixed their technical issue in about an hour—it was just some undocumented parameters. Afterward, they asked what we wanted to see, and they gave us a full tour of the antimatter colliders. It was one of the best days of my physics background. It's used everywhere." "Are there any tweets you regret, Kiran?" "No tweets of regret." "How does the French song go? 'Regret nothing'?" "'Je ne regrette rien', yes. That is very important to me: do not regret anything. Regret is just a tax on your brain. Learn from your mistakes, but don't hold onto regrets. Unless you have a time machine, there is no point; it just drains your mental energy." "That's beautiful—'a tax on your brain.' It reminds me of a Johnny Depp quote where he said, 'Hate is a very expensive emotion.'" "Are you comparing me to Johnny Depp? Because that is a first!" "Well, gentlemen, I am eternally grateful for the software that you and the wider open-source community have built with FFmpeg, VLC, and everything else. I am also grateful for the spicy tweets—never stop. And thank you for this amazing hat. I feel like a wizard!" "It's special to celebrate the software that has brought so much value to the world. Thank you both for your time today." "Thank you for having us." "Thank you so much." "Thanks for listening to this conversation with Jean-Baptiste Kempf and Kiran Kunnya. To support this podcast, please check out our sponsors in the description, where you can also find links to contact me, ask questions, and leave feedback." "Now, let me leave you with some words from the legendary Linus Torvalds: 'Most good programmers do programming, not because they expect to get paid or get adulation by the public, but because it is fun to program.'" "Thank you for listening, and hope to see you next time."

Inside Alts: Warburg Pincus' Perlman on the evolution of private equity

CNBC Television(3.4M) ·21:58 ·685 views · 2026-05-06
Leslie Picker (Senior Finance and Banking Reporter, CNBC) Jeffrey Perlman (CEO, Warburg Pincus)
Warburg Pincus CEO Jeffrey Perlman advocates for a return to "diversification by design" and a performance-first private partnership model to navigate a "third wave" of private equity characterized by retail wealth participation and structurally higher interest rates.
TL;DR Warburg Pincus CEO Jeffrey Perlman advocates for a return to "diversification by design" and a performance-first private partnership model to navigate a "third wave" of private equity characterized by retail wealth participation and structurally higher interest rates. The...

TL;DR

Warburg Pincus CEO Jeffrey Perlman advocates for a return to "diversification by design" and a performance-first private partnership model to navigate a "third wave" of private equity characterized by retail wealth participation and structurally higher interest rates.

Strategic Growth and the Partnership Model

  • The "Private Partnership" Advantage: Perlman attributes the firm’s 60-year staying power to its status as a private partnership, which creates better alignment because the firm is the single largest investor in its own flagship funds.
  • Performance vs. Assets Under Management (AUM):
      • Unlike listed peers, Warburg Pincus prioritizes "performance-related earnings" over "fee-related earnings."
      • The firm avoids "asset gathering" for the sake of scale, specifically choosing not to enter large-scale private credit because it is viewed more as a "beta" opportunity rather than a high-performance engine.
  • Adjacency Expansion: The firm is leveraging its existing "deal engine" to move into performance-based adjacencies:
      • Capital Solutions: Hybrid capital offerings.
      • Partnership Solutions: GP-led secondaries, which now represent roughly 15% of the total exit market.
      • Real Estate: An emerging vertical born out of their core private equity business.

Navigating the "Distribution Drought"

  • Industry Outlier Status: While the broader PE industry struggles with a lack of exits, Warburg Pincus has distributed $54 billion and invested $34 billion since 2020.
  • Critique of the 2021 Vintage: Perlman notes the industry over-invested in 2021 at peak valuations (particularly in tech/software) and many firms are now waiting for companies to "grow into" those valuations.
  • Diversification as a Liquidity Tool:
      • By being diversified by sector and geography, the firm can find "open doors" for exits even when specific sectors are closed.
      • Example: When industrial sales slowed due to tariff uncertainty, the firm pivoted to selling defensive healthcare and software assets.

Macroeconomic Outlook and AI Integration

  • Structurally Higher Rates: Warburg is underwriting all new investments under the assumption of "structurally higher rates" and a potential moderate recession, rather than hoping for a return to post-GFC low rates.
  • AI Portfolio Management: The firm conducted a portfolio-wide audit to categorize companies as AI "beneficiaries" or "threats."
      • Exit Strategy: The firm is moving to exit AI-exposed businesses earlier, even at lower valuations, to avoid deeper losses later.
      • Holding Strategy: They are providing extra capital to "beneficiaries" to allow them to compound over the next several years.
  • Public vs. Private Markets: Perlman observes that public markets have outperformed their 100-year average by 50% over the last decade; he expects a reversal that will make private markets increasingly attractive.

The Global Pivot to Asia

  • Rebalancing U.S. Exposure: Global investors are shifting from "hyper-exceptional" U.S. exposure (over 70% of global equity markets) back toward "exceptional" levels, reallocating the difference to growth markets.
  • The Case for Asia:
      • Asia is projected to provide 50–60% of global growth over the next decade.
      • Perlman argues that in a high-rate environment, growth is the essential lever for returns.
  • Competitive Footprint: While peers have pulled back from China or focused heavily on the U.S., Warburg Pincus intends to leverage its 30-year footprint in Asia to capture these shifting capital flows.
"Investors forgot about the core principle of being diversified... They wonder why they’ve seen a lack of distributions. The benefit of being diversified... is when one door closes, others tend to open." — Jeffrey Perlman
"Hope is not a strategy. A lot of firms have been waiting to try to grow into those [2021] valuations, or there was maybe some hope that rates were going to go back down... We should all expect that we’re going to live in a world of structurally higher rates." — Jeffrey Perlman
"I laugh a little bit if you listen to every single public company earnings call—I have yet to hear a CEO say that AI will not be great for their business. The reality is technology rarely is a 100% positive for everyone." — Jeffrey Perlman
Long seen as the oldest private equity firm in the world, Warburg Pincus is celebrating its 60th anniversary this year. And it's doing so under the stewardship of a relatively new CEO, among the youngest in the industry. We sat down with Jeffrey Perlman for his first-ever U.S. TV interview since taking the top job at the firm. Perlman spoke about the staying power of Warburg Pincus, the evolution of the PE industry, and where he wants to take the firm in the future. This is Inside Alts. Well, thank you so much for taking the time for doing this. I'm very excited. Warburg Pincus, 60th year this year. It's the oldest private equity firm in the world. In this cutthroat, evolving industry, what do you think has given Warburg the staying power? Well, it's great to be here, first of all. Look, I think when you take a step back, you know, 60 years is a pretty remarkable milestone, and I think, you know, two things have stood out above all else. I think, first, the investor-first private partnership model that we've maintained for 60 years. It's created amazing alignment, you know, with our investors, with our portfolio companies and management teams. And obviously, you know, we're also the single largest investor in our own flagship funds. So alignment's an incredibly powerful thing, and I think that structure remaining a private partnership has, I think, been a big driver of the long-term success. And certainly Lionel Pincus and the architecture of the firm, we owe him a deep debt of gratitude for creating that single global partnership. I think the second has been our diversification-by-design model. I think diversification fell out of favor over the last kind of handful of years, and now I think investors are now starting to remember it as the core principle of investing that it is. But what it has allowed us to have is consistent, persistent performance over many cycles, many decades, and investors reward that. You're only the third person to be CEO across those six decades. How are you thinking about making your own mark strategically? Yeah, so, you know, fortunately, I inherited a great firm. The firm's in a great position, great support from our investors. But at the same time, the industry's going to change and evolve, I think, dramatically over the next 10, 15, 20 years. You know, we saw the first wave, which was when, and I think Lionel Pincus had a hand in it, state pension funds in the U.S. were allowed to start to invest in the asset class. Then you had sovereign wealth funds that, you know, were able to start investing in the asset class. And now it's really wealth, and obviously retirement could be on the horizon here as well. So we're now seeing kind of this next, you know, third period for the asset class. And for us, you know, it's an opportunity to, I think, leverage the fact that I think we have a great model also to capture the wealth audience, you know, this global diversified model, the ability to deliver persistence over the long term. And so for us, we're really looking as we think about the growth of the firm, how do we leverage this amazing deal engine that we've built, right? We have nearly 300 investment professionals, 64 investing partners, huge value creation team. And, you know, we've had our core private equity business, but we've now started to expand into other performance-based adjacencies, capital solutions, think about it as kind of hybrid capital, what we call partnership solutions, which is our GP-led secondaries business. As you've seen, this has now become, I think, a permanent feature of the exit market going forward. It's now about 15% of total exits. And then we've had a real estate business that's emerged from our private equity business. So we think all of these areas are performance-based products. It taps into this great deal engine that we've built over 60 years and all the relationships and the ecosystems that come with it. And I think from there, we want to continue evolving in that direction. But we're not here trying to have 35 different products. We want to do a couple things, do them really, really well. And I think investors are looking for that, and they're going to continue to reward that over the long term. I want to follow up on what you said about wealth, because that has been an increasingly popular area. It's also one that's been met with a lot of headlines lately that may not have transpired had it been kind of relegated just to the institutional client base. Why do you see it as a net positive for Warburg Pincus? And I ask this in the construct of, you know, the redemptions that we've seen, some semi-liquid products and how going into wealth and going into retail requires different structures, different types of communication and potential regulatory scrutiny as well. Look, it's a great point, you know, a number of great points buried in that. And look, what I'd say is you as a firm, you always want to have a diversified set of capital flows. And so obviously we have, you know, our institutional investors, whether it's sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, insurance companies. We've always had a component of wealth who've invested with us. I think increasingly through many of their advisors, you know, they've been really pushed more into these more evergreen semi-liquid vehicles. And for us, you know, of course, you want to make sure you can provide the right structure that those investors are looking for. So I do think it's going to continue to be an important feature. Certainly regulation is important. You know, as a firm, we've, you know, you have institutional investors, you have to account for that. And I think in wealth, as an industry, it's important that we try and make sure we get that right. Because with retirement on the horizon, there's obviously, you know, employers have, you know, liability associated with that. So we want to make sure as an industry that we get that right. Because if you do, obviously the amount of capital that can continue to flow into the asset class is significant. Do you think it's right at this point in time or do you think there's some lessons learned that, you know, have been highlighted by the levels of redemptions and the structures that we've seen so far? Look, I think redemptions are always going to be a function of, you know, we have even our institutional investors also, you know, through the secondary market obviously trade those positions. There's obviously at various points in time everyone has liquidity needs, that's fair. And we always want to make sure we make that available on the institutional side. And I think you have to be prepared to do it, you know, on the wealth side as well. So I don't think anybody should be surprised that there are going to be redemptions, both in good times and also, you know, in tougher periods. And sometimes that may be the place that investors look to to get liquidity because, you know, maybe the marks are tighter than maybe some of their other positions may be. So I think we have to structure the product collectively as an industry that can still consistently facilitate that. But when you also communicate to your investors as a part of that product that we will, you know, cap the amount of redemptions, that sometimes it gets reported that that's a negative, that it's being like that's, those are folks who are following exactly as what had been communicated to those investors. You don't want to disadvantage the longer tail of folks that are still in the product by trying to accommodate that. So I do think there just there needs to be more education on it. But as an industry, we do have to make sure that we hold up our end of the bargain and make sure we deliver transparency and, you know, follow through with, you know, what it stipulates under the agreements. More of a feature as opposed to a bug. You're not in private credit. Is that by design? Is that an area that you plan to stay out of, you know, forever? Is there a reason behind not getting into it when a lot of your peers have? You know, it's a great question. You know, we looked at it for our side and we said, you know, what do we do really well as a firm? And I think again, being performance-based with our set of investment professionals, obviously being on the equity side and now kind of on the hybrid side, we think those are more performance-based products. I think, you know, indirect lending in private credit that's been really more, especially if you're trying to play at scale, has been more of a beta opportunity. And it's not to say like we're big users of private credit for our private equity, you know, portfolio. It's not to say kind of never, but at the same time for us, we want to be in areas that are again very performance-driven. And I think that's why our investors want to continue to invest with us. And I think if you go down that path, you need a whole separate team, there's other elements. And I think what you're seeing in the industry between the listed asset managers and folks like us who are, you know, private partnerships are is is again, it goes back to alignment, you know, fee-related earnings versus, in our view, performance-related earnings. And that aligns with us also in the case of our, you know, global flagship fund being the largest investor in our own fund. So for us, we of course want to be, you know, have an unwavering focus on value creation in the portfolio as opposed to obviously looking more for scale for the benefit of of AUM. Asset gathering as the cynics would call it. You said it, not me. He said it, my mouth. You mentioned listed, some of your listed peers. Is that something you would ever consider doing an IPO of your own? You know, it's a fair question.
