  • The Iranian Extortion Scheme: Iran has attempted to weaponize a critical global choke point—which handles approximately one-fifth of global oil consumption—by shooting at civilian tankers, seizing container ships, and attempting to enforce an illegal tolling system.
      • Since the ceasefire was announced, Iran has fired at commercial vessels nine times and seized two container ships.
      • The blockade has left 22,500 mariners onboard more than 1,550 commercial vessels trapped in the Arabian Gulf.
  • Operational Successes: U.S. Navy destroyers successfully escorted the first two U.S.-flagged merchant vessels safely through the strait. Concurrently, six Iranian ships attempting to run the blockade out of Iranian ports were intercepted and turned around.
  • International Transition Plan: Secretary Pete Hegseth emphasized that the U.S. expects global partners (including South Korea, Japan, Australia, and Europe) to step up and assume responsibility for the waterway, noting that "the world needs this waterway a lot more than we do."

U.S. Combat Capabilities and Regional Defense

  • Localized Sea and Air Control: The U.S. military has established an enhanced security area on the southern side of the strait, backed by over 15,000 American service members.
  • Combat Power Deployment:
      • Surface Fleet: Guided-missile destroyers are actively detecting and defeating Iranian fast-attack boats and one-way drones.
      • Air Overwatch: More than 100 fighters, attack aircraft, and unmanned systems provide 24/7 defensive coverage.
      • Tactical Command: The Army's 82nd Airborne Division is coordinating land, air, sea, space, and cyber operations using AI-enabled, next-generation tactical networks and Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2).
  • Recent Kinetic Clashes:
      • Within the last 24 to 48 hours, U.S. Navy MH-60 and Army AH-64 Apache helicopters successfully neutralized Iranian cruise missiles, drones, and small boats targeting commercial shipping.
      • U.S. forces successfully defeated adjacent Iranian strikes, including one attack on Oman and three on the UAE (including the Fujairah Oil Terminal).
  • Honoring the Fallen: General Caine paid tribute to Sergeant William Rivers, Specialist Brianna Moffett, and Specialist Kennedy Sanders of the 718th Engineering Company, who were killed on January 28, 2024, by an Iranian-backed proxy attack at Tower 22 in Jordan.

Ceasefire Status and Diplomatic Posture

  • Status of the Ceasefire: Secretary Hegseth confirmed that the ceasefire with Iran is *not* over. Current Iranian operations are characterized as "low-level harassing fire" that remains below the threshold of restarting major combat operations.
  • War Powers Act and Next Steps: Following President Trump's notification to Congress that the 60-day War Powers hostilities had terminated, the military "clock" has paused. However, if Iran violates its agreements, the President retains the authority and improved military capacity to restart major combat operations instantly.
  • Conventional vs. Nuclear Deterrence: Hegseth defended the administration's strategic sequencing, noting that Operation Midnight Hammer successfully dismantled Iran's nuclear facilities. He stated that the subsequent campaigns focused on neutralizing Iran's "conventional umbrella" of missiles and fast boats, preventing them from adopting a "North Korea-style" defense strategy.
  • U.S.-Israel Alignment: Responding to aggressive statements from Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and the head of Mossad regarding the collapse of the Iranian regime, Hegseth reiterated that while Israel is a vital partner, "only one hand is on the wheel" directing U.S. policy, and that is President Trump.

Notable Quotes

"Project Freedom is defensive in nature, focused in scope, and temporary in duration with one mission: protecting innocent commercial shipping from Iranian aggression." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"No adversary should mistake our current restraint with a lack of resolve." — General Caine
"We prefer this to be a peaceful operation, but are locked and loaded to defend our people, our ships, our aircraft, and this mission without hesitation." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
Well as you know, President Trump has directed US Central Command to restart the free flow of commerce through the Strait of Hormuz under the umbrella of Project Freedom. To be clear, this operation is separate and distinct from Operation Epic Fury. Project Freedom is defensive in nature, focused in scope, and temporary in duration with one mission: protecting innocent commercial shipping from Iranian aggression. American forces won't need to enter Iranian waters or airspace. It's not necessary. We're not looking for a fight, but Iran also cannot be allowed to block innocent countries and their goods from an international waterway. Iran is the clear aggressor, harassing civilian vessels, threatening mariners from every nation indiscriminately, and weaponizing a critical choke point for its own financial benefit—at least trying to. For too long, Iran has been harassing ships, shooting at civilian tankers from all nations, and trying to impose a tolling system. Iran's plan, a form of international extortion, is unacceptable. That ends with Project Freedom. Two US commercial ships, along with American destroyers, have already safely transited the strait, showing the lane is clear. We know the Iranians are embarrassed by this fact. They said they control the strait; they do not. So American ships led the way, commercial and military, shouldering the initial risk from the front as Americans always do. And right now, hundreds more ships from nations around the world are lining up to transit. Sentcom, along with partner nations, is in active communication with hundreds of ships, shipping companies, and insurers. All of these ships from all around the world want to get out of the Iranian trap that they have been stuck inside. As a direct gift from the United States to the world, we have established a powerful red, white, and blue dome over the strait. American destroyers are on station, supported by hundreds of fighter jets, helicopters, drones, and surveillance aircraft providing 24/7 overwatch for peaceful commercial vessels—except Iran's of course, which is why our ironclad blockade remains in full effect as well. In fact, six ships tried to run the blockade out of Iranian ports as Project Freedom commenced, and they were all turned around. This is more than strategy; it's also humanitarian. By breaking Iran's illegal stranglehold, we're protecting the lives and livelihoods of sailors from dozens of countries, securing global energy routes, and preventing shortages that hit the world's poorest people the hardest. Once again, America is using its strength to lift up others. Iran is trying to subjugate the world. To what remains of Iran's forces: if you attack American troops or innocent commercial shipping, you will face overwhelming and devastating American firepower. The President has been very clear about this. We prefer this to be a peaceful operation, but are locked and loaded to defend our people, our ships, our aircraft, and this mission without hesitation. To Iran: let innocent ships pass freely. These international waters belong to all nations, not to Iran to tax, toll, or control. To our partners, allies, and the rest of the world: this is a temporary mission for us. As I've said before, the world needs this waterway a lot more than we do. We're stabilizing the situation so commerce can flow again, but we expect the world to step up. At the appropriate time and soon, we will hand responsibility back to you. To the incredible Americans executing this mission right now: simply, thank you. Your courage, your readiness, your unmatched professionalism and precision are why the world can breathe easier today and every day. You are and always will be the greatest fighting force in human history. Unmatched always. Project Freedom is underway, commerce will be flowing, and America is once again leading with strength, clarity, and purpose for the benefit of the entire world. Our will is unshakable. God bless our troops and God bless the mighty United States Navy as well. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone. Thanks for being here. On Sunday, at the direction of the President, Sentcom initiated Project Freedom with the objective of facilitating the safe passage of international commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Over the past seven weeks, Iran has repeatedly threatened and attacked commercial shipping in the strait in order to effectively cut off commercial traffic and damage the global economy. By obstructing one of the world's most critical maritime checkpoints—a vital transit route for approximately one-fifth of the global oil consumption—Iran is weaponizing the global supply chain. This action disregards established navigational rights in the Law of the Sea, and the regime led by the IRGC is effectively attempting to hold the entire global economy hostage. Let me give you an update of the last few days of activities. And if you take a look at the map here, as the Secretary said, the United States has established Project Freedom to open up the Strait of Hormuz. This graphic highlights the kinetic engagements that the Iranians have attempted to make or have taken against the region. The green dots and blue dots show the groupings of commercial cargo and tanker vessels, and the United States has established a significant presence over the strait here in order to open up the free flow of commerce, noting the blockade continues along the eastern flank. The map shows the examples and samples of Iranian attacks against commercial vessels in the strait as well as the Gulf of Oman and up in the Arabian Gulf. Since the ceasefire was announced, Iran has fired at commercial vessels nine times and seized two container ships. And they've attacked US forces more than ten times, all below the threshold of restarting major combat operations at this point. You can also see the grouping of tankers and cargo vessels in the US blockade line as I mentioned. As a result of Iran's indiscriminate attacks across the region, there are currently 22,500 mariners embarked on more than 1,550 commercial vessels trapped in the Arabian Gulf, unable to transit. In addition to shipping, Iran has continued to deliberately attack its neighbors. Just yesterday, Iran attacked Oman once and the UAE three times, including an attack on Fujairah Oil Terminal, which was successfully defeated. They also launched cruise missiles, drones, and small boats at US forces defending commercial shipping in the straits, and United States Navy MH-60 helicopters and Army AH-64 Apache helicopters successfully defeated those threats. Thus far, today is quieter. Let me describe this defensive operation a few clicks down for you. As the Secretary said, Project Freedom is designed to protect commercial shipping, help restore the flow of commerce through the strait, and sustain freedom of navigation. Sentcom has established an enhanced security area on the southern side of the strait that is now protected by US land, naval, and air assets to help defeat further Iranian aggression against commercial shipping. I'd like to share a little bit more about US capabilities, but for operational security purposes, I'm not going to get into specific details out there. On the surface, guided-missile destroyers and other warships are detecting and defeating Iranian threats. This includes fast boats and one-way attack drones. In the air, more than 100 fighters, attack aircraft, and other manned and unmanned aircraft synchronized by the 82nd Airborne Division are in the air 24 hours a day providing defensive overwatch for the enhanced security area and its approaches that I discussed. These forces are comprised of over 15,000 American service members and have established localized air and sea control to protect against Iranian threats. Commercial vessels that transit through the area will see, hear, and frankly feel US combat power around them: on the sea, in the skies, and on the radio. And as of this morning, as was mentioned prior, two US-flagged merchant vessels have transited and we anticipate more to transit over the coming days. Further up the gulf, US Naval Forces Central Command continues to work to identify and mitigate any threats. Beyond Project Freedom, Sentcom and the rest of the joint force remain ready to resume major combat operations against Iran if ordered to do so. No adversary should mistake our current restraint with a lack of resolve. And the President has said, as the Secretary has, that now is the time for those with an equity stake in the strait to come assist. Before I turn it back to the Secretary, I want to take a moment to highlight part of our joint force today. And today I want to talk about the paratroopers of the United States Army's 82nd Airborne Division. When the President or the Secretary need immediate, scalable, and lethal combat power in Sentcom or elsewhere, the All-American Division answers the call. The 82nd is the core of our nation's Immediate Response Force and ready to deploy within hours of notice. These paratroopers are capable of accomplishing any mission in any environment, constantly training, constantly ready to jump from Air Force aircraft into ground combat and seize key terrain if ordered to do so, just like their predecessors did in Sicily and Normandy in World War II, or to secure or enable the follow-on forces to flow into theater as they did in Grenada or Panama. They can do the range of missions, from counterinsurgency to counterterrorism operations to power projection, as they have many times over the course of the history of this great division. They can do all these things because they're incredibly well-trained, well-equipped, and well-led, but also because of the indomitable spirit of the All-American paratrooper. Every single one of them volunteered for this role, volunteered to go to airborne training, and volunteered to serve. But this division is now more than just a formation that we use for forcible entries. For example, just today, they are out there coordinating, integrating, and synchronizing land, air, space, sea, and cyber forces using Joint All-Domain Command and Control. They use next-generation tactical networks that are AI-enabled to augment intelligence and operations, and they seamlessly synchronize all of these effects and are doing so in support of Project Freedom as we speak. The relentless focus on technical innovation allows the commanders out there in the field to see, sense, and understand the opportunities that are in front of them along with the associated risks and make smart decisions out on the objective. With incredible courage, tenacity, and grit, they are going all the way every day, and whenever the nation calls on them, they are there to answer the call. We are grateful for their service and their leadership. Lastly, before I close, as I often do, I want to remember some of our fallen. Today we remember Sergeant William Rivers, Specialist Brianna Moffett, and Specialist Kennedy Sanders of the 718th Engineering Company, US Army Reserves out of Fort Benning, Georgia. They were tragically killed on January 28, 2024, when attacked by an Iranian-backed proxy force at Tower 22 in Jordan. We continue to carry on their memories. We remember their names. We remember their families. And the Secretary and I are deeply grateful for all of our deployed forces who are out there doing our nation's work. And, sir, with that, I'll kick it back over to you. Very good. We'll take a few questions. Go ahead. Thank you. Alexandra Ingersol, One America News. Do you assess, Mr. Secretary, do you assess what's left of this small boat fleet to be a significant threat or a nuisance to be managed? And then, secondly, what is your message to critics who say that the US doesn't have a strategy in this mission? Well, any threat needs to be taken seriously, and just like those six attack boats that the Chairman mentioned were taken seriously and dealt with before they were any real threat to the American military vessels that they were approaching. And that is it—small craft like that are all that Iran has left. Their ability to get close with a destroyer of the capabilities that we have is limited, but we're going to take it very seriously and kinetically, quickly. We're not going to allow a threat to gather. And I can tell you, even from just this morning, the strategy remains laser-focused. In fact, what I think you see from Project Freedom is that we're not allowing ourselves to get distracted. This is a separate and distinct effort, temporary in nature, that we plan to hand over. The world has expressed a desire to be a part of this. As is often the case, or maybe almost always the case, American leadership is required. So the President was willing to undertake this: send commercial ships through, send destroyers in, provide this red, white, and blue bubble of protection, and encourage the world to step up, because ultimately this waterway is, as I said, far more crucial to the rest of the world and they need to have the ability to defend it. So I think this is a reflection of a laser-focused strategy, actually. Right here. Morning, Mr. Secretary, Mr. Chairman. David Zere, Real America's Voice. I wanted to ask you if any of the fast boats that have been either sunk or identified, are there any armaments larger than say small, you know, AK-47 type stuff? Any anti-ship cruise missiles identified? And my second question is, if there's a divide between the IRGC and Tehran, is there a way to get Tehran to agree to let us go after the IRGC that is doing these attacks, without violating the ceasefire? Well, right now, anything coming at our crafts, regardless of what's on it, has to be defended against and that's the case. I don't know what the exact armaments were on those fast boats. Small arms, nothing significant on the fast boats—rifles or small machine guns. There were some small coastal cruise missiles shot down as well. So there's been, I think, some frothiness in the beginning aspects of this that were dealt with very directly by our destroyers, and we're communicating both overtly and quietly to the Iranians to allow this defensive operation to happen on behalf of the world. There are some actions the IRGC takes sometimes that are outside the bounds of what maybe Iranian negotiators would like. That's their job to rein that in and ultimately create a condition for a deal, right? That's not something I talked about in these remarks, but that's happening in real time. Iran has an ability to make that deal, but what we're demonstrating with Project Freedom is they don't control the strait. I mean, we know Iran is embarrassed by the fact that our blockade is holding and we can run ships through, and we're going to help the world run ships through. And if they hold it at issue, the whole world knows who's doing it. It's not us. We're willing to open it up; it's Iran, and that creates a dilemma for them, and we hope they make a deal. Thank you, sir. My first question's for General Caine. Mary Margaret with the Daily Wire. You said that the attacks by Iran so far are below the threshold of restarting major combat operations. What is that threshold? And then for Secretary Hegseth: are there still concerns about mines in the strait, and can you kind of clarify these reports of Kamikaze dolphins that we've heard about? Hmm, haven't heard the Kamikaze dolphin thing. It's like sharks with laser beams, right? The threshold of restarting is a political decision above my pay grade. What I'll say is it's more low harassing fire right now. It feels like Iran is grasping at straws to try to do something across the southern flank. To your question, David: their command and control structure remains very fractured, and I think they're struggling to maintain control down echelon at the edge, but we're still—it's still pretty low-level kinetics at this point in time. And I can't confirm or deny whether we have Kamikaze dolphins, but I can confirm they don't. Ultimately, any follow-on effort if there are mines identified would be something that some of our units could undertake or the world could undertake, but right now we know we have a lane of safe passage that commercial shipping can flow through. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. Last 24 hours or so, Iran's fired at us, we fired at Iran. Just going to ask you more directly: is the ceasefire over? No, the ceasefire is not over. Ultimately this is a separate and distinct project, and we expected there would be some churn at the beginning, which happened. And we said we would defend, and defend aggressively, and we absolutely have. Iran knows that. And ultimately the President's going to make a decision whether anything were to escalate into a violation of a ceasefire, but certainly we would urge Iran to be prudent in the actions that they take to keep that underneath this threshold. This is about the straits, this is about freedom of navigation, this is about international waterways, this is about free flow of commerce—all the things that happened before, and only Iran is contesting. So right now the ceasefire certainly holds, but we're going to be watching very, very closely. Mr. Secretary, thank you. General, thank you. I want to first express my gratitude and admiration for the work you do and for everyone involved in our armed forces, and also for the accomplishments of Operation Epic Fury, which I think are too often dismissed too lightly. But those accomplishments don't obscure, I think, a central default that has occurred here, and I would like you both to address it. On the first day of this conflict, President Trump addressed the Iranian people directly and said, "When we're finished, take over your government. It'll be yours to take." And then on the seventh day of the conflict, in a Truth Social post, the President said, quote, "There will be no deal with Iran except," all caps exclamation mark, "UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER." What happens to that pledge to the Iranians? And when did the President decide to capitulate on his demand for unconditional surrender? Well, James, I wouldn't—you started out nicely, but you ended exactly where we knew you would end. The President hasn't capitulated on anything. He holds the cards, we maintain the upper hand, and Project Freedom only strengthens that hand. And so he—we will ensure that whatever deal is made or whatever end state is reached creates—ensuring that Iran never has a nuclear weapon, which is A number one, and he's been focused on that, and the deal and discussions are centered on that. And what the Iranian people take advantage of after the fact is up to them, and he's been very clear about that. And maybe you do it now, maybe it happens later, but ultimately he has also been clear we're not going to entangle this into some nation-building project. Our objectives are clear; they've been pursued from day one. Hopefully the Iranian people take advantage of that because they're being taken advantage of by this regime. As you know, 45,000 Iranians—innocent Iranians—killed before the outset of this, that's what this government does: kills their own innocent civilians. Getting out from underneath that is going to be a challenge for the Iranian people, and we certainly hope they take advantage of that. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Can you share any information regarding the South Korean ship that was reportedly hit by Iran? Is Seoul in contact with the US military about it? And is there any indication that they would heed Trump's call made on social media to join Project Freedom? We hope so. We're in contact with that ship, Sentcom I should say, and the maritime coordination forces are in touch. And I think targeting like that is a reflection of the indiscriminate nature of what Iran is doing. We hope South Korea would step up, just like we hope Japan would step up, just like we hope Australia would step up, just like we hope Europe steps up. But we're not waiting for them to do so; we're looking to set the conditions to hand to them. But the President was clear in his Truth message that, "Hey, this is your ship, you should take part in helping to defend it." We very much hope they do. Mr. Secretary, in terms of new US intelligence reports suggest that the timeline for Iran to get a nuclear weapon has not changed since last summer, still at nine to 12 months. Is that intelligence wrong? How can this be after so much bombing? And for General Caine: going back on what you're talking about in the Strait of Hormuz, the President recently said that the blockade is even more effective than bombing. So why didn't we start the campaign with a blockade? And initially, how many planned ships do you plan to escort or take through commercial ships to the Strait of Hormuz today or in the coming days? Well, we don't discuss the specifics about intel, and anybody that does shouldn't be, and I can't confirm or deny whether that is indeed correct—speculation as far as I'm concerned coming from you. But ultimately, what Operation Midnight Hammer did in the obliteration of those facilities was set back their program. And then because of the efforts of the 12-Day War, the President was able to recognize that this threat, while they continued to seek nuclear capabilities—their will was still there to seek a nuclear bomb—ultimately they were creating a conventional umbrella of so many missiles and so many capabilities that no one would want to challenge them. Think of the North Korea—this is the North Korea strategy. We have so many conventional capabilities, you won't do something about it, and underneath that we're going to develop more nuclear capability. So the will was still there. President Trump had the courage to say we have to dismantle and take on those conventional capabilities while the nuclear facilities are watched 24/7 and over-watched. And we will ensure, one way or another—hopefully Iran chooses a deal—that they give up those ambitions, give up those capabilities, and we're able to confirm that every step of the way. So President Trump has been focused on it. He's set them back like no other President. He ripped up the terrible deal that would have given them a bomb, he took care of it in Midnight Hammer, we're watching it right now, and ultimately feel good about the fact that the end state will ensure that Iran never has a nuclear weapon. Sir, I'll let Sentcom talk to the number of ships they're going to take through because they're the nearest ones to talking to the commercial shippers, and I don't want to get out in front of them. On the order and sequence around the military objectives, that's a decision the President and the Secretary go get us at, and at the time we were focused on the primary objectives that were in front of us. Thank you. Rama Morgan with The Epoch Times. On Friday, President Trump notified Congress that the hostilities that began on February 28 had terminated. That came on the deadline of the 60-day War Powers deadline. Will this administration be seeking Congressional approval for any further military operations if the ceasefire breaks down? Our view is the one you described, that ultimately, with the ceasefire, the clock stops. If it were to restart, that would be the President's decision. That option is always there, and Iran knows that. And that's why, you know, their choices in Project Freedom are important. The President retains the opportunity and the capabilities, more capabilities than we had at the start of this, to restart major combat operations if necessary. If Iran is not willing to follow through on its side of the bargain or make a deal, then the War Department is postured, locked, loaded, and ready to go. We hope it doesn't have to go in that direction, but Admiral Cooper and our forces are in a three-point stance and ready to go. Yeah, last question. Thank you. Liam Cosgrove with Zero Hedge. In recent weeks, both Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Israel's head of Mossad have stated their intent not to cease combat with the Iranians. Netanyahu put this on Twitter. Israel's head of Mossad said Israel's goals are not finished until the Iranian government collapses. And so they're referring to the government that President Trump has brokered a ceasefire with and is working on a lasting peace in the region with. And so that's, you know, something many Americans, especially American farmers, are hoping he does get to a lasting peace. And so, if President Trump is successful in that and the Israelis are explicitly stating their intent to continue fighting at a later date, how can you ensure America doesn't get roped back into a war if that comes? And secondly, with Netanyahu comfortable tweeting something like that out, and also Netanyahu has continued bombing Lebanon despite President Trump explicitly telling him not to. I would say your question is based on the false premise that somehow President Trump is being pulled in by Prime Minister Netanyahu to any of these actions. And that's where you're going. Just to be clear, President Trump has led at every step of this based on his view of American interests and America First. And we're grateful that Israelis have been very capable partners at many steps of this, and they may have some objectives at times that are slightly different than ours, but there's only one hand on the wheel ultimately directing this, whether it's Project Freedom or previously Operation Epic Fury, and it's President Trump. So we're grateful for their input, their insight, the existential nature of the threat they face from an Iranian bomb, the capabilities that they can bring to that, but ultimately the coordination will happen with the leadership of President Trump. Thank you very much. I appreciate it. What is your message to critics who say that the US doesn't have a strategy in this mission? Well, any threat needs to be taken seriously, and just like those six attack boats that the Chairman mentioned were taken seriously and dealt with before they were any real threat to the American military vessels that they were approaching. And that is it; small craft like that are all that Iran has left. Their ability to get close with a destroyer of the capabilities we have is limited, but we're going to take it very seriously and kinetically, quickly. We're not going to allow a threat to gather. And I can tell you, even from just this morning, the strategy remains laser-focused. In fact, what I think you see from Project Freedom is that we're not allowing ourselves to get distracted. This is a separate and distinct effort, temporary in nature, that we plan to hand over to the world. The world has expressed a desire to be a part of this. As is often the case, or maybe always the case, American leadership is required, so the President was willing to undertake this, send commercial ships through, send destroyers in, provide this red, white, and blue bubble of protection, and encourage the world to step up, because ultimately, this waterway is, as I said, far more crucial to the rest of the world, and they need to have the ability to defend it. So I think this is a reflection of a laser-focused strategy, actually. Right here. Morning, Mr. Secretary, Mr. Chairman, David Sanger, Real America's Voice. I wanted to ask you, of any of the fast boats that have been either sunk or identified, are there any armaments larger than say small, you know, AK-47 type stuff or any anti-ship cruise missiles identified? And my second question is, if there's a divide between the IRGC and Tehran, is there a way to get Tehran to agree to let us go after the IRGC that is doing these attacks without violating the ceasefire? Well, right now, anything coming at our crafts, regardless of what's on it, has to be defended against, and that's the case. I don't know what the exact armaments were on those fast boats. Small arms, nothing significant on the fast boats—rifles or small machine guns. There were some small coastal cruise missiles shot down as well, so there's been, I think, some frothiness in the beginning aspects of this that were dealt with very directly by our destroyers, and we're communicating both overtly and quietly to the Iranians to allow this defensive operation to happen on behalf of the world. There are some actions the IRGC takes sometimes that are outside the bounds of what maybe Iranian negotiators would like. That's their job to rein that in, and ultimately create a condition for a deal, right? That's not something I talked about in these remarks, but that's happening in real time. Iran has an ability to make that deal, but what we're demonstrating with Project Freedom is they don't control the strait. I mean, we know Iran is embarrassed by the fact that our blockade is holding, and we can run ships through, and we're going to help the world run ships through. And if they hold it at issue, the whole world knows who's doing it. It's not us; we're willing to open it up, it's Iran, and that creates a dilemma for them. We hope they make a deal. Yes. Thank you, sir. My first question is for General Caine, Mary Margaret with the Daily Wire. You said that the attacks by Iran so far are below the threshold of restarting major combat operations. What is that threshold? And then, for Secretary Hegseth, are there still concerns about mines in the strait, and can you kind of clarify these reports of Kamikaze dolphins that we've heard about? Hmm, I haven't heard the Kamikaze dolphin thing. It's like sharks with laser beams, right? The threshold of restarting is a political decision above my pay grade. What I'll say is it's more low harassing fire right now. It feels like Iran is grasping at straws to try to do something across this southern flank. To your question, David, their command and control structure remains very fractured, and I think they're struggling to maintain control down-echelon at the edge, but we're still—it's still pretty low-level kinetics at this point in time. And I can't confirm or deny whether we have Kamikaze dolphins, but I can confirm they don't. Ultimately, any follow-on effort, if there are mines identified, would be something that some of our units could undertake or the world could undertake, but right now we know we have a lane of safe passage that commercial shipping can flow through. James, go ahead. Mr. Secretary, thank you. General, thank you. I want to first express my gratitude and admiration for the work you do and for everyone involved in our armed forces, and also for the accomplishments of Operation Epic Fury, which I think are too often dismissed too lightly. But those accomplishments don't obscure, I think, a central default that has occurred here, and I would like you both to address it. On the first day of this conflict, President Trump addressed the Iranian people directly and said, "When we're finished, take over your government. It'll be yours to take." And then on the seventh day of the conflict, in a Truth Social post, the President said, quote, "There will be no deal with Iran except," all caps, exclamation mark, "UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER." What happens to that pledge to the Iranians? And when did the President decide to capitulate on his demand for unconditional surrender? Well James, I wouldn't, I wouldn't... you started out nicely, but you ended exactly where we knew you'd end. The President hasn't capitulated on anything. He holds the cards, we maintain the upper hand, and Project Freedom only strengthens that hand. And so he will ensure that whatever deal is made or whatever end state is reached, creates ensuring that Iran never has a nuclear weapon, which is A number one, and he's been focused on that and the deal and discussions are centered on that. And what the Iranian people take advantage of after the fact is up to them, and he's been very clear about that. And maybe you do it now, maybe it happens later, but ultimately, he's also been clear we're not going to entangle this into some nation-building project. Our objectives are clear; they've been pursued from day one. Hopefully the Iranian people take advantage of that because they're being taken advantage of by this regime, as you know, 45,000 Iranians—innocent Iranians—killed before the outset of this. That's what this government does: kills their own innocent civilians. Getting out from underneath that is going to be a challenge for the Iranian people, and we certainly hope they take advantage of that. Thank you. Right there. Secretary, can I get one follow, please? If I may, one follow. Go ahead. Everybody else, everybody else... Follows direct. Go ahead. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Can you share any information regarding the South Korean ship that was reportedly hit by Iran? Is Seoul in contact with the US military about it, and is there any indication that they would heed Trump's call made on social media to join Project Freedom? We hope so. We're in contact with that ship—Sentcom, I should say—and the maritime coordination forces are in touch, and I think targeting like that is a reflection of the indiscriminate nature of what Iran is doing. We hope South Korea would step up, just like we hope Japan would step up, just like we hope Australia would step up, just like we hope Europe steps up, but we're not waiting for them to do so. We're looking to set the conditions to hand to them. But the President was clear in his Truth message that, "Hey, this is your ship, you should take part in helping to defend it." We very much hope they do. Go ahead. Mr. Secretary, in terms of new US intelligence reports suggest that the timeline for the war in Iran to get a nuclear weapon has not changed since last summer, still at nine to twelve months. Is that intelligence wrong? How can this be after so much bombing? And for General Caine, going back on to what you were talking about the Strait of Hormuz, the President recently said that the blockade is even more effective than bombing, so why didn't we start the campaign with a blockade initially? How many planned ships do you plan to escort or take through commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz today or in the coming days? Well, we don't discuss the specifics about intel, and anybody that does shouldn't be, and I can't confirm or deny whether that is indeed correct—speculation as far as I'm concerned coming from you. But ultimately, what Operation Midnight Hammer did in the obliteration of those facilities was set back their program. And then because of the efforts of the Twelve-Day War, the President was able to recognize that this threat, while they continued to seek nuclear capabilities, their will was still there to seek a nuclear bomb, ultimately they were creating a conventional umbrella of so many missiles and so many capabilities that no one would want to challenge them. Think of the North Korea... this is the North Korea strategy. We have so many conventional capabilities, you won't do something about it, and underneath that we're going to develop more nuclear capabilities. So the will was still there. President Trump had the courage to say we have to dismantle and take on those conventional capabilities while the nuclear facilities are watched 24/7 and over-watched. And we will ensure, one way or another, hopefully Iran chooses a deal, that they give up those ambitions, give up those capabilities, and we're able to confirm that every step of the way. So President Trump has been focused on it; he's set them back like no other President. He ripped up the terrible deal that would have given them a bomb, he took care of it in Midnight Hammer, we're watching it right now, and ultimately feel good about the fact that the end state will ensure that Iran never has a nuclear weapon. Go ahead. So I'll let Sentcom talk to the number of ships they're going to take through because they're the nearest ones to talking to the commercial shippers, and I don't want to get out in front of them. On the order and sequence around the military objectives, that's a decision the President and the Secretary go get us at, and at the time we were focused on the primary objectives that were in front of us. Thank you. Ryan Morgan with Epoch Times. On Friday, President Trump notified Congress that the hostilities that began on February 28th had terminated. That came on the deadline of the 60-day War Powers deadline. Will this administration be seeking congressional approval for any further military operations if the ceasefire breaks down? Our view is the one you described, that ultimately, with the ceasefire, the clock stops. If it were to restart, that would be the President's decision. That option is always there, and Iran knows that, and that's why, you know, their choices in Project Freedom are important. The President retains the opportunity and the capabilities, more capabilities than we had at the start of this, to restart major combat operations if necessary. If Iran is not willing to follow through on its side of the bargain or make a deal, then the War Department is postured, locked, loaded, and ready to go. We hope it doesn't have to go in that direction, but Admiral Cooper and our forces are in a three-point stance and ready to go. Yeah, last question. Thank you. Liam Cosgrove with Zero Hedge. In recent weeks, both Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Israel's head of Mossad have stated their intent not to cease combat with the Iranians. Netanyahu put this on Twitter. Israel's head of Mossad said Israel's goals are not finished until the Iranian government collapses. And so, they're referring to the government that President Trump has brokered a ceasefire with and is working on a lasting peace in the region with, and so...

Franco Delle Donne alerta sobre Palantir, Peter Thiel y el avance del tecnofascismo

El Mostrador(72.8K) ·25:53 ·1.6K views · 2026-05-06
Franco Delle Donne (Author and Doctor of Communication)
The "Palantir Manifesto" reflects a shift toward "technofascism," an ideology spearheaded by Peter Thiel that views democracy as an inefficient obstacle to innovation and seeks to replace traditional states with corporate entities governed by "CEO-monarchs.
TL;DR The "Palantir Manifesto" reflects a shift toward "technofascism," an ideology spearheaded by Peter Thiel that views democracy as an inefficient obstacle to innovation and seeks to replace traditional states with corporate entities governed by "CEO-monarchs." Democracy vs....

TL;DR

The "Palantir Manifesto" reflects a shift toward "technofascism," an ideology spearheaded by Peter Thiel that views democracy as an inefficient obstacle to innovation and seeks to replace traditional states with corporate entities governed by "CEO-monarchs."

The Ideology of Peter Thiel and Palantir

  • Democracy vs. Capitalism: Franco Delle Donne explains that Peter Thiel, founder of Palantir, holds the fundamental premise that democracy and capitalism are incompatible. In Thiel’s view, universal suffrage and the democratic process hinder economic freedom and technological progress.
  • The "Palantir Manifesto": Recently published on X, this document argues that the development of AI-driven weaponry is inevitable; the only relevant question is who will build them and for what purpose.
      • End of Diplomacy: The manifesto signals a move away from "soft power" and post-WWII diplomacy toward a "hard power" doctrine where force and technological superiority dictate the global order.
  • Transition to Global Actor: Palantir is moving from a "shadow" provider of surveillance and data mining for governments to an overt political actor attempting to define international policy.

The "Corporate State" and Technofascism

  • Citizens as Customers: The "Dark Enlightenment" or "neoreactionary" vision proposes dismantling the nation-state. In its place, the state would function like a corporation where:
      • CEO-Monarch: Instead of elected leaders, a "most capable" individual (proven through professional success) governs as a manager or king.
      • Customer Status: Inhabitants lose their status as citizens with rights and become "clients" or "shareholders" who can "exit" to a different state-corporation if dissatisfied.
  • Social Darwinism: This model follows a Darwinist logic where the "most fit" lead and innovate, while the "weak" are left behind.
  • Proxy Models: Proponents point to city-states like Singapore, Dubai, or Hong Kong as successful examples of high-efficiency, low-democracy governance.

Silicon Valley’s Influence on State Power

  • Political Financing: Peter Thiel’s influence is tangible in U.S. politics; he was a primary financier for Vice President-elect J.D. Vance (contributing $15 million) and an early, consistent supporter of Donald Trump.
  • The "Barons of Silicon Valley": Delle Donne notes the symbolic and practical importance of tech leaders (from Amazon, Apple, OpenAI, and Tesla) appearing alongside political leadership, indicating they are now the primary architects of government lines.
  • Monopoly as Innovation: Contrary to traditional liberalism, Thiel argues that competition is harmful. He believes monopolies are superior because they can focus entirely on innovation rather than fighting competitors, a logic Palantir applies to its dominance in state data and defense contracts.

The Crisis of Democracy as a "Tool"

  • The "Broken Promise": Disillusionment with democracy arises from its failure to solve modern economic inequalities. Delle Donne argues that citizens working 50 hours a week who still cannot afford basic living costs become receptive to "science fiction" political programs.
  • Instrumentalization of Democracy: A primary danger today is viewing democracy merely as a "tool" or instrument to solve problems.
      • The Risk: When an instrument stops working, people seek to replace it. This leads to the erosion of democracy from within, as it is no longer seen as a fundamental way of life or a peaceful conflict-resolution mechanism.
  • Technofascist Emergence: This environment allows technofascism—the marriage of authoritarian leadership with total technological control—to gain mainstream legitimacy as a viable alternative to "failing" democratic systems.

Notable Quotes

"For [Peter Thiel], democracy and capitalism are not compatible, just as freedom and democracy are not compatible. He has a vision in which democracy, as we know it, only makes our quality of life worse." — Franco Delle Donne
"The State would stop being a national state governed by politicians... it would be a state more like a corporation. Instead of an elected government, it would have a sort of CEO, a manager to solve the problems of its inhabitants, who would abandon their condition as citizens and become simply clients." — Franco Delle Donne
"The issue is not whether weapons will be manufactured with artificial intelligence; it is who will manufacture them and for what purpose. Our adversaries will not stop to engage in theatrical debates." — Palantir Manifesto (Contextualized by Mirna Schindler)
"Democracy is being eroded from within... we are hearing [anti-democratic] rhetoric not just from classic authoritarian leaders, but from leaders who were elected through a democratic system." — Franco Delle Donne
En el mostrador podcast continúa Al Pan-Pan con Mirna Schindler. La conversación que buscas para comenzar tu mañana. Ya estamos de regreso. Les decía que hace algunos días la empresa Palantir Technologies emitió un comunicado que se llamaba "El Manifiesto de Palantir" a través de la red social X, donde plantea varios puntos. Entre los más controversiales es que la cuestión, dice, no es si se fabricarán armas con inteligencia artificial, sino quién las fabricará y con qué propósito. Nuestros adversarios no se detendrán a enzarzarse en debates teatrales. Son varios temas que, más allá de lo que uno esperaría de una compañía, reabren la conversación respecto de lo que se ha llamado la ilustración oscura, por ejemplo, o derechamente el tecnofascismo. Queremos conversar a esta hora con el doctor en comunicación, autor del libro "Epidemia Ultra del fascismo europeo a Silicon Valley", también fundador y director del proyecto Epidemia Ultra, Franco Delle Donne. Franco, muchas gracias por estar con nosotros esta mañana desde Alemania, ¿no? "Sí, ¿qué tal? ¿Cómo estás? Un placer. Muchas gracias." A ver, vamos a hablar de tu libro de todas maneras, pero quiero preguntarte antes lo primero. ¿Te sorprendió este manifiesto de Palantir Technologies? Y también entremos ahí, ¿qué es Palantir Technologies? Bueno, no me sorprendió en absoluto. Es una declaración de principios que coincide perfectamente con la ideología que está detrás de sus dueños, de sus fundadores. Entre otras cosas está Peter Thiel, que se hizo bastante conocido en los últimos meses justamente por ser uno de los dueños de Palantir. Pero también él es alguien que, por ejemplo, fundó PayPal en su momento, luego la vendió, ayudó a construir Facebook y una serie de startups y de iniciativas, algunas más conocidas con mayor o menor éxito. Pero lo más importante es que en su ideología hay una premisa que es fundamental, que él ya ha dejado por escrito y después podemos profundizar sobre eso. Hay una premisa y es que para él la democracia y el capitalismo no son compatibles, así como tampoco lo son la libertad y la democracia. Es decir, él tiene una visión en la cual la democracia tal como nosotros la conocemos solamente hace que empeore nuestra calidad de vida. Él plantea que estemos peor, que no podamos solucionar problemas, que seamos ineficientes. En ese sentido, el planteo que hacen tiene que ver con esa visión de implementar la solución de los problemas mediante la fuerza, mediante llevar adelante una política que no tenga nada que ver con lo que pueda desear o querer la mayoría, sino más bien con quién tiene la capacidad de imponerse. En ese sentido, lo que plantea Palantir en ese comunicado no se aleja para nada de esa ideología. Con respecto a Palantir en concreto, es una empresa que se ha dedicado durante muchos años a proveer de tecnología, más que nada para cuestiones de vigilancia y control, a diferentes gobiernos y a diferentes agencias en diferentes países del mundo. Ha desarrollado una capacidad para llevar adelante sus objetivos como ninguna otra y ha conseguido también construir una red de contactos que la ubican en posiciones clave a nivel global. Ahora dieron ese salto extra de salir, si se quiere, de las sombras y convertirse en un actor global. Claro, ese punto que tú decías es muy interesante porque lo que señala justamente el pensamiento de Peter Thiel es que el sufragio universal masivo es incompatible con la libertad económica y con la innovación. De alguna manera, como tú bien describías, la democracia es casi responsable de esto de que tengamos que votar cada ciertos años. Esto de la alternancia en el poder es visto, al revés, como justamente el fondo de todos los males. Ahora, la pregunta es: ¿qué implica esto? ¿Ellos aspiran a un gobierno autoritario donde el ciudadano se convierte en un cliente como también se ha señalado? Mira, la pregunta en sí está muy bien porque nos ayuda a entender tal vez las diferencias con otro tipo de ultraderecha o de derecha radical que estamos viendo en la actualidad. De hecho, te lo muestro, este es el librito en el cual hay un capítulo dedicado a él; le llamo el caballero de la ilustración oscura. Y justamente esto que preguntás es clave porque sí, es verdad, lo que plantea es una visión autoritaria. Pero no tal vez como la que conocemos con Viktor Orbán, o la que apunta a construir poco a poco Giorgia Meloni en Italia, o la que podemos ver en Rusia de Putin o en Turquía de Erdoğan. Lo que él plantea es una visión en la cual el Estado deje de ser el Estado tal como lo conocemos, un Estado nacional gobernado por políticos elegidos de manera más o menos democrática o competitiva a través de elecciones. Lo que plantea es la construcción de un Estado más parecido a una corporación. Un Estado que sea casi una empresa y que, en lugar de tener un presidente o un gobierno elegido por el pueblo, tenga una suerte de CEO, de gerente, que se ocupe de solucionar los problemas de lo que serían sus habitantes. Estos abandonarían su condición de ciudadanas y ciudadanos y pasarían a ser simplemente clientes, o accionistas incluso. Es decir, parte de esa empresa que sería el Estado se ocuparía constantemente de satisfacer las necesidades de esos clientes. Entonces es una visión, te diría, distópica en la cual se plantea la presencia de lo que también se le llama CEO-monarca, una especie de rey que no termina de quedar claro en los escritos cómo sería la definición de esa persona. Lo podemos deducir, pero no está bien claro. No hay un sistema como puede ser el sistema democrático donde vos decís "se vota la mayoría por tal sistema, un parlamento o directamente el presidente". Es una visión mucho más abstracta donde lo que se desprende de los diferentes escritos, porque no es que hay una ideología cerrada sino que hay varias subcorrientes, es que el que llega ahí es el más capaz. El que lo logra. El que es capaz de demostrar mediante sus acciones que está capacitado para... mira que digo tantas veces "capaz", pero es un adjetivo muy importante en ese sentido. El que demuestra que puede hacerlo. ¿Y cómo lo demostrás? Bueno, a través de tus acciones en tu vida profesional, en construir una empresa, en llevarla adelante, etc. Sería como aquel que es el más apto. De ahí se desprende otro elemento que es muy importante para entender esto, que es esta visión más darwinista. Pareciera que los débiles quedarían atrás y los más aptos, los que mejor se adaptan y los que más se pueden desarrollar, serían los que se sostienen. El último elemento que es clave de este Estado corporativo es que el cliente, si no está satisfecho, simplemente lo que tiene que hacer es irse y cambiar a otro Estado vecino. Nunca dicen que el verdadero nombre para irse a buscar estar mejor en otro lugar en realidad es migración, porque obviamente están en contra de ese concepto y de los migrantes en sí. Pero es interesante cómo en el fondo terminan por construir esa distopía que en realidad no deja en claro cómo funcionaría ni cómo solucionaría todos esos problemas. Tampoco termina de dejar en claro cuál sería el rol de esos ciudadanos más allá del hecho de consumir y estar satisfecho. Claro, es que aquí quiero entrar, Franco, porque muchos de los que nos escuchan a esta hora dirán: "pero esto es una cosa excéntrica de un grupo de chalados". Pero la verdad, y aquí es donde quiero entrar, Peter Thiel estuvo hace poco acá en nuestro país, también en Argentina. Se reunió en el caso de Chile con el diputado Johannes Kaiser, diputado libertario, y se reunió con José Piñera, el creador de las AFP en nuestro país. Es un personaje, entonces, que tiene todo y que ha sido financista además del vicepresidente de Estados Unidos, J.D. Vance. Quiero entrar ahí porque esto no es algo tan extraño. Esto tiene una injerencia en Silicon Valley. Es decir, ¿este pensamiento está penetrando allá? Cuéntanos un poquito de eso. Bueno, hay en esa lógica lo que se intenta obviamente es buscar ejemplos y modelos. Decir: "bueno, esto en realidad no es una distopía, esto ya existe y se puede aplicar". Y lo que encuentran son ejemplos aproximados a eso y hablan de ciudades-estado como puede ser Singapur, como puede ser Hong Kong, como puede ser Dubái. Lo que pasa es que no te cuentan toda la visión. Desde su punto de vista, esa visión sería la que más se acerca a lo que plantean, donde el ciudadano es libre de hacer lo que quiere y no hay restricciones. Y sobre todo, y esta es la clave, no tiene que ocuparse de pensar en la política. No tiene que ocuparse de pensar si apoya a un partido político o a otro porque ese tema estaría solucionado por esa empresa absolutamente eficiente que reemplazaría al Estado. Entonces no te cuentan tampoco la otra parte, donde esa supuesta libertad está coartada porque ese gobierno termina siendo autoritario en el fondo, porque te impone las reglas y cómo tenés que actuar en esa vida social. Te termina quitando también la libertad. Y ahí es donde no termina de quedar claro cómo definen la libertad este grupo libertario, valga la redundancia. ¿Dónde empieza y dónde termina lo que nosotros entendemos como un concepto que proviene de las máximas de la Revolución Francesa, donde la libertad es ese valor relacionado con los derechos humanos donde todos somos iguales ante la ley? En Silicon Valley se piensa esto, y de hecho esto nace de Silicon Valley. En el libro lo describo. Es una idea que nace de un blog en el año 2007 que escribe una persona llamada Curtis Yarvin. Él escribía bajo un seudónimo en aquel momento y escribe estas ideas de la ilustración oscura sobre cómo tendría que funcionar un Estado que no sea político, que sea absolutamente una empresa. Es algo que nace de un blog de alguien que estaba cansado de sentirse decepcionado por el sistema por diferentes circunstancias. Y esto tampoco lo justifica, pero creo que ahí es clave también para entender por qué empieza a prender esta idea loca. ¿Por qué llama la atención? ¿Por qué encaja? ¿Por qué algunas personas empiezan a pensar que tal vez sirve? Porque justamente hay una herida ahí, una promesa rota. Es una situación en la cual empezamos a ver que aquella caída del Muro de Berlín, que teóricamente nos llevaba al fin de la historia y que condenaba a todos los Estados democráticos a ser exitosos por toda la eternidad, en realidad no es tan así. En realidad hay desigualdades, hay problemas, hay partidos políticos y otros actores importantes en nuestras sociedades que no pudieron cumplir con esa promesa. Mucha gente se levanta, trabaja 40 o 50 horas por semana, llega a fin de mes y no le alcanza la plata. Y entonces dice: "¿bueno, y entonces esto para qué sirve?". Estos planteos, que parecen muy fuera del consenso o de lo que nos parece normal porque fuimos criados con valores democráticos, encaja en personas que están buscando otra cosa. Creo que ahí podemos llegar a entender por qué estas ideas que otrora serían parte de ciencia ficción ahora parecen parte de un programa político. Claro, es que por eso te decía que no hay que verlo de manera tan excéntrica, como de un grupo de fanáticos. Hay que entender que esta empresa, Palantir Technologies, tiene importantes contratos con el Estado de Estados Unidos. Es una empresa a nivel global de minería de datos que tiene acceso a nuestra información. Entonces aquí estamos hablando de un modelo de negocios donde no solo ganas plata, sino que estableces vínculos profundos con el Estado. Esto con mayor razón se ve en un gobierno como el de Donald Trump. Ahí hay una injerencia muy importante en los asuntos de Estado. Volvamos al tema de lo que señala este manifiesto: que la cuestión no es si se fabricarán armas de IA, sino quién las fabricará y con qué propósito. Habla sobre la idea de que la era atómica está terminando y que se abre una nueva era basada en la inteligencia artificial. ¿Qué es eso realmente? Bueno, me pareció muy importante esto que mencionaste sobre cómo se relaciona esto con el segundo gobierno de Donald Trump. Cuando uno mira la jura de Donald Trump en esa sala donde está lleno de gente, en general todos los políticos que han jurado como presidente de Estados Unidos han tenido ahí a las personas de confianza de su partido apoyándolo. Cuando vemos lo que pasó en esa jura de Trump, lo que vemos es que ahí estaban sentadas unas personas muy especiales. Les llamamos los varones de Silicon Valley: el jefe de Amazon, el propio Elon Musk que en ese momento todavía no se había peleado con Trump, el CEO de Apple y estaba sentado también el CEO de ChatGPT, de OpenAI. ¿Qué hace esta gente ahí? Es una declaración de parte de Trump de decir "ellos están de mi lado", o en realidad estábamos viendo que el gobierno de Estados Unidos empezaba a tener a estas figuras como relevantes para, de a poco, ir penetrando y decidiendo lo que serían las líneas de gobierno. Ni te hablo de las políticas públicas, te hablo de las líneas de gobierno. Ahí aparece algo que también mencionaste: Peter Thiel es un financista no solamente del vicepresidente J.D. Vance, quien recibió 15 millones de dólares de su parte, sino también del propio Donald Trump. De hecho, Thiel es uno de los pocos que apoya a Trump en la primera presidencia allá por 2016 cuando nadie creía que iba a llegar ahí. Ahí lo que se ve es un proyecto más de largo plazo que hoy en día llega a la Casa Blanca. Ellos se arrogan este derecho y esta capacidad para producir este manifiesto y decir que ahora, con estas ideas, están determinando cuáles son los lineamientos de la política internacional y, en particular, lo que tendría que ser la doctrina de la política exterior de Estados Unidos. Lo está haciendo una empresa privada sin ningún filtro. No digo que antes no se haya hecho; tal vez sí, pero funcionaba a través de lobbies o de otro tipo de relaciones con los distintos gobiernos. En este caso es la mismísima empresa que, con el poder que ha construido, puede plantear esos lineamientos y esas definiciones de lo que es el orden mundial actual. Eso es algo que tenemos que tratar de asimilar y de comprender. Más allá de si estamos o dejamos de estar de acuerdo con Palantir, es cierto que hay otro orden mundial que funciona con otras reglas. Ha dejado de lado lo que sería el poder blando o la diplomacia que ha caracterizado a los años de posguerra, después de la Segunda Guerra Mundial, y empieza a activarse de vuelta una visión de poder duro para imponer a partir de la fuerza lo que sería la lógica de la política internacional. Ya pues, y esa fuerza que tú señalas, la lógica de la fuerza y el fin del poder blando, ¿qué relación tiene con la inteligencia artificial? Porque además este es un grupo de personas que sostiene que la regulación hay que dejarla de lado; o sea, la menos regulación posible, ¿no? Tal cual. Bueno, eso es parte también de la lógica ideológica de ese grupo. Tiene que ver con la visión libertaria de la desregulación cero, pero al mismo tiempo tiene que ver con la capacidad de esta empresa de poder atribuirse el control de ciertas herramientas tecnológicas que queden solamente bajo su cuidado, que teóricamente sería el indicado. Es muy interesante saber que hace un tiempo Peter Thiel escribió un libro que se llama "De cero a uno". En ese libro él desarrolla una idea que a cualquier persona liberal le generaría mucho ruido, y es que él sostiene que la competencia entre empresas es algo negativo. Él cree que lo mejor que puede pasar en cada una de las áreas es que se formen monopolios. Él dice que un monopolio lo que hace es poner todo su esfuerzo en mejorar su producto y en generar innovación, es decir, en superarse. En cambio, si hay competencia, las empresas ponen el esfuerzo en ganarle a la competencia y no en generar innovación o dar saltos de calidad. En ese mundo tecnológico, Palantir lo que está apuntando es a construir ese monopolio en hacerse cargo de ser los que dominan a través de esa tecnología. Por ejemplo, dominan la construcción de esas armas que seguramente ni siquiera nos imaginamos de qué se trata. Estamos hablando de armas que tal vez no son físicas en el sentido clásico, como una bomba, sino tal vez armas de otro tipo que pueden funcionar en un mundo digital y que pueden generar aún más daño que enviar un avión y hacer un bombardeo. Claro, no, brutal lo que hablamos ahora. Hay ciertos analistas, por ejemplo Cas Mudde, que es un neerlandés especializado en ultraderecha, que dijo que todo esto no es más que tecnofascismo. ¿Existe realmente desde tu punto de vista y lo que tú has investigado el tecnofascismo? Sí, es una forma de definir esto que estamos hablando o de definir la ilustración oscura. Se le ha llamado también grupo neorreaccionario, que sería como un equivalente a esa fase posfascista. Tiene que ver justamente con la combinación de las herramientas que te da la tecnología para poder aplicarlas en la construcción de un Estado fascista. Sería un Estado en el sentido en el que no hay democracia, en el sentido de que hay un líder que guía y que lleva adelante el país porque es el indicado por aclamación popular. A partir de ahí construye el poder desde una lógica corporativa, con una sociedad que simplemente obedece esa voluntad del líder porque teóricamente en ese líder se encarnan los intereses de la nación. Tal vez estarían estas diferencias ya cuando afinamos el lápiz. Pero yo coincido con Cas Mudde; creo que la idea de él lo que nos cuenta es que estamos saliendo de una visión democrática. Hace un rato te mencionaba lo de esa promesa rota y el otro día discutía con algunas personas sobre ese tema hablando también de este libro "Epidemia Ultra". Creo que vivimos en un momento en el cual se instauró la idea de que la democracia es un instrumento. Un instrumento que soluciona problemas, y eso es efectivamente un problema. Pensar que la democracia es un instrumento es peligroso porque cuando un instrumento no cumple más su función por la razón que sea, hay que cambiarlo por otro. Eso significa dejar de pensar a la democracia como una forma de vida, como una forma de solucionar los conflictos sin violencia y de la manera más inclusiva posible, y pasar a convertirla en un "solucionador de problemas". Como si fuera una herramienta. Lo que nos está llevando es que, cuando sentimos que nuestras necesidades no se están satisfaciendo, el problema es la democracia. Y eso es lo que estamos escuchando cada vez más seguido. No lo escuchamos solamente de los líderes autoritarios clásicos; lo estamos escuchando de líderes que fueron elegidos a través de un sistema democrático. Es decir, la democracia se está erosionando desde dentro y ese es el mayor problema que tenemos hoy en día. Ese es el riesgo: que volvamos a situaciones que ya habían pasado en la historia y que creíamos pasadas después de la Segunda Guerra Mundial. Uff, tremendo tema que nos dejas también para conversar y para seguir meditando y reflexionando. Franco Delle Donne, doctor en comunicación, autor del libro "Epidemia Ultra del fascismo europeo a Silicon Valley", te queremos dar las gracias por haber estado con nosotros esta mañana. "No, gracias a ustedes por invitarme. Que estés muy bien. Gracias." Bien, pues nos deja hartas reflexiones nuestro entrevistado. Ya saben que nos pueden ver después en YouTube y de más está decirles que se suscriban a nuestro canal @elmostradorpodcast. Decirles también que con la fuerza de la región más austral del mundo llega "Aquí Magallanes", el nuevo newsletter regional de El Mostrador. Te contamos lo que pasa, lo que se conversa y lo que importa en la región del Estrecho de Magallanes, la Patagonia, Puerto Williams, Torres del Paine, Punta Arenas y la Antártica Chilena. Todo directo a tu correo cada miércoles porque Santiago no es Chile. Inscríbete gratis en "Aquí Magallanes", el nuevo newsletter regional de El Mostrador. Me despido, nos reencontramos mañana a las 8 en punto. Que tengan un muy buen día. Cerramos por hoy Al Pan-Pan con Mirna Schindler. De lunes a viernes de 8 a 9 de la mañana. Entrevistas para partir el día. El Mostrador Podcast. El poder de la independencia.

Comer Defends 'Very Transparent' Lutnick After Epstein Interview, Accuses Dems Of 'Cover Up'

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·13:10 ·178 views · 2026-05-06
James Comer (Representative (R-KY)) Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
> "The only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was wasn't 100% truthful on the brief visit to the island with his family.
TL;DR: Chairman James Comer defended Howard Lutnick’s "very transparent" voluntary testimony regarding his minimal history with Jeffrey Epstein, while accusing House Democrats of lying to the media and prioritizing social media optics over the substance of the investigation. ...

TL;DR: Chairman James Comer defended Howard Lutnick’s "very transparent" voluntary testimony regarding his minimal history with Jeffrey Epstein, while accusing House Democrats of lying to the media and prioritizing social media optics over the substance of the investigation.

Howard Lutnick’s Testimony and Epstein Connections

  • Interaction Frequency: Lutnick testified to having only three interactions with Jeffrey Epstein over a ten-year period.
  • The Island Visit: Lutnick corrected his previous record, admitting he visited Epstein’s island for a two-hour lunch.
      • The visit included 12 people (Lutnick’s family of six and another family of six).
      • Comer noted that Lutnick did not enter the house and that the visit occurred before Epstein’s 2008 prosecution when "bad vibes" were the only red flag.
  • Voluntary Correction: Comer emphasized that Lutnick corrected his timeline regarding the island visit in his opening statement and argued that any misstatements were not intentional lies.

Critique of Democratic Committee Members

  • Accusations of Dishonesty: Comer repeatedly accused Democratic members of misrepresenting what happens behind closed doors to the media.
      • Rep. Ro Khanna: Accused of previously making false criminal allegations against individuals in the Epstein case and having to recant.
      • Rep. Walkenshaw: Criticized for allegedly lying to "pander to outraged people" for primary election purposes.
  • Motivation for Video: Comer argued Democrats are obsessed with video recordings not for the investigation, but to create clips for "MSNBC" and "low IQ viewers" on social media.
  • Historical Allegations: Comer claimed the real "cover-ups" on the committee involve Democrats hiding facts regarding Metro Police crime statistics, Minnesota fraud (referencing Tim Walz), and California hospice fraud.

Procedural Disputes and Transparency

  • Recording Policy: Comer defended the decision not to videotape the interview, noting that depositions for the Minnesota fraud and Metro Police whistleblowers were also not taped.
      • He stated that 99.9% of depositions are handled via transcript and that video is a rarity (citing the January 6th hearings as an exception).
  • Witness Cooperation: The Chairman explained that securing voluntary testimony from "the richest and most powerful people in the world" is difficult and requires significant staff negotiation; forcing video can make witnesses less likely to cooperate.
  • Transcript Release: Comer promised to release the transcripts of the interviews once lawyers for both the majority and minority agree on the text.

Scope of the Epstein Investigation

  • Legal Actions: The committee has subpoenaed the Epstein estate and the Department of Justice.
  • Future Witnesses: Comer indicated that the investigation will bring in at least six more "quality witnesses" in the coming weeks who possess pertinent information.
  • Bipartisan Accountability: Comer asserted that the committee’s goal is "justice for the survivors" and that they will question anyone regardless of wealth or political affiliation, including those linked to the Clintons or Donald Trump.
"The only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was wasn't 100% truthful on the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that in his opening statement... if we find that there were any misstatements by Lutnick, it’s a felony to lie to Congress and he’ll be held accountable."

— Rep. James Comer

"The Democrats want video so that they can mislead their base... into trying to say something happened that didn't happen. The transcript, any Democrat that wants to be here can be here."

— Rep. James Comer

"It’s always about Donald Trump. It’s never about the victims, it’s never about justice for the survivors, it’s never about holding people accountable that should have prosecuted Epstein years ago."

— Rep. James Comer

I feel compelled to have to come out and and clean up and correct some of the statements that the Democrats made. I couldn't believe as I was getting updates what they were saying. And I hope that when you get the transcripts you fact-check them on what they say. This is a habit of the Democrats on this committee, coming out telling you all stuff that was said that was not said. The only cover-up that I've seen on the oversight committee is the cover-up of the Democrats trying to cover up our investigations of the Metro Police Department lying about crime statistics. There is also the cover-up of the Minnesota fraud by Tim Walz and Keith Ellison and the cover-up of the hospice fraud in California. So what we asked some very substantive questions the first hour, the Democrats during their hour repeated the exact same questions because there's only so many questions you can ask Howard Lutnick. He live had a property next door to Epstein in New York but talked to him three times over a decade—three interactions over a decade. He was on the island with his family with his wife and kids, six people in his family and six people in his friend's family for lunch for two hours, and he has admitted that. So the Democrats are going to go another hour; I'm sure they're going to try to filibuster and do whatever. They had a narrative to come in; they were hoping Lutnick wouldn't come in, they were hoping Pam Bondi wouldn't come in. Remember Pam Bondi came in a month ago to answer questions; the Democrats didn't ask him a single question. The first 20 minutes of the Democrats' questioning here, they complained about not having video and and not having things to be able to put on MSNBC for their angry purple-haired viewers to to be outraged over. This is a serious investigation. I've subpoenaed the estate, we've subpoenaed the Department of Justice, we've brought in two—one cabinet secretary fixing to be two cabinet secretaries. We've brought in, we're bringing in, some of the richest and most powerful people in the world. We're doing everything in our ability to get answers. And it's so frustrating when someone like Ro Khanna, who I I can't believe when when Ro Khanna comes in here and he's already gone on the floor and accuse accused men of being criminals in the Epstein investigation who weren't even criminal who weren't even involved, and he had to go recant his story. And then Walkenshaw, my gosh, I mean I know he's in a tough primary against a a unique individual and I guess you have to lie and and pander to to outrage people over to try to win a Democrat primary. I don't know, but the the the times that Ro Khanna—I'm not going to say Ro Khanna lied—the mistakes that Ro Khanna's made and come out here and delivered to you all I can't keep up with. And the times that Walkenshaw has walked out here, who has just been here a few weeks—he's been in Congress a few weeks—but he thinks he's the smartest person here and the number of lies that he's said. So I'm hoping that when you get the transcript, you will fact-check these members. It's a disservice to you, it's a disservice to your readers, to your viewers when the Democrats come out here and just lie. So we're going to go back in, we'll see if the Democrats ask any different questions than what we asked over the first hour. Again, there's only so many questions you can ask a man that had three interactions with Epstein over a over his entire life. So did anybody have any questions? About yes. Mr. Chairman, Democrats had zeroed in in their discussions out here with us about Lutnick's testimony regarding when he said "I would no longer see Jeffrey Epstein." They said that he meant him personally one-on-one and not in a group setting. Can you describe to us how you interpreted Secretary Lutnick's answer on those ques on that line of questioning and if you were satisfied? Which one now? Which line of? He said that the reason why, you know, in 2005 he said that he would no longer he cut he would cut ties with Jeffrey Epstein and then he met with him later on. Democrats had said that Lutnick essentially said that he didn't personally want to be one-on-one with him in a room but was okay suggested he was okay in a group setting. Yeah, if I remember from recollection and we'll see with the with the transcripts, but I believe he was saying that when he he met him, he just had bad vibes but didn't know anything. You know, it's hard to hard to imagine some of the things. And by all accounts, whether it was the Clintons or or President Trump or whomsoever they had interaction with Epstein, I don't think anybody knew about the crimes until he was prosecuted in Palm Beach in in 2008 I believe, yes. Are you concerned—one of the things the Democrats said but has come out—is that Secretary Lutnick has changed some of what he said whether it's the timeline or whether it's going to the island? So do you feel like you got direct answers or are you still concerned that he's changing the facts? And he, right off the bat, he corrected his statement on the time that he he came to the island. And he said, you know, that if I remember correctly and and you can look back on the on the transcript, he said that somehow Epstein found out that his family and friends were going to be vacationing in the Caribbean and around the Virgin Islands. They they—he invited them to lunch and they they docked there for he thinks no more than two hours. It was his wife and four other family members and then six six other, so there were twelve in a group. They didn't go in the house or or anything there. Did he explain why that was okay when you know back in 2005 he seemed to suggest that he didn't want any further involvement with him? Did he explain that discrepancy in detail and if so, what did he say? I mean I I feel like that Lutnick's been very transparent. He came in voluntarily. I said before this thing started the only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was wasn't 100% truthful on the the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that in his opening statement and you know we'll see. We'll continue our investigation and if if we find that there were any misstatements by by Lutnick, it's a it's a felony to lie to Congress and he'll be held accountable. It's too bad it's not a felony for a member of Congress to lie to the media or we would be we would have a lot of Democrats that would be having to lawyer up right now. So yeah, Max. The Democrats made a a big point about this not being videotaped. Can you respond to why this is not a video? We had Ted Wade in last week; it wasn't videotaped. They didn't say a thing about that. We had the Metro Police whistleblowers come in on that investigation that they said was was phony, that we proven they they lied about the crime statistics—which I think is happening in other cities in the United States and we'll we'll continue our investigation in other cities. They didn't say anything about that. We we've had the Minnesota fraud depositions with the with the whistleblowers in Minnesota; those weren't videotaped. We're trying to get people in as quickly as we can. You see the list continues to grow of people we bring in for interviews and depositions with respect to this investigation or any other investigation. And let me tell you, it's very hard to get people in. I don't think the average person realizes how many hours of phone time is involved with our staff communicating with their lawyers to try to get people to come in to do these interviews. I mean everything has to be perfect.
