They may say a day that that we have another transcribed interview or maybe a day that they have a medical appointment or or a child's wedding or things like that. So we're doing as the best we can to bring people in if they'll come in voluntarily, you know we we try to work with them and and do whatever we can. But the truth of the matter is the Democrats want video so that they can mislead their their base or whatever into into trying to say something happened that that didn't happen or or whatever. The transcript—any Democrat that wants to be here can be here. I know that I've missed one deposition I think in the Epstein when I had oral surgery and the Democrats trashed me on it. This is the second Epstein interview we've had in a row and Garcia wasn't here. I know he was reading comic books I think during the Ted Wade video. I don't know where he is today. But again that's another thing; the members don't have to be here. This is done by staff. We have professional staff, the Democrats have professional staff, Republicans have professional staff. We have attorneys who are professional depositors, that's what they do. But at the end of the day, if you'll come in voluntarily we'll work with you, we'll try to get get this thing in and out. And the videos—to my knowledge there was never any videos of depositions until the January 6th hearings, yes. Democrats suggested that the reason Lutnick may have been changing his story is because they have talked to the administration prior to coming. Of course they said that. Of course they said that. It's always about Trump. It's never about the victims, it's never about justice for the survivors, it's never about holding people accountable that that should have prosecuted Epstein years ago. It's always about Donald Trump. It's always about the midterm elections and trying to flip the House and trying to motivate their their base. From my standpoint, from most of the Republicans on this committee's standpoint, our purpose is to get the truth to the American people. We don't care if they're Republicans or Democrats, we don't care if they're rich or poor, we're going to bring them in, we're going to ask them questions, we're going to be transparent with you all. I'm not going to sit up here and lie and say somebody said something that they didn't, like Walkenshaw does time and time again. I'm hope I don't make mistakes like Ro Khanna does time and time again. So one more question. Mr. Chairman, the Democrats have also been pushing for for when Pam Bondi comes in to have that on video, her situation. Why? Why? They they had her in. They just had her in. They had an opportunity to have Pam Bondi and Todd Blanch. They they were going to be here all day and they didn't ask a single question. They didn't ask a single question because they want video. They want video and they want that ACY or whatever that thing is so he can always miss miss misinform the low IQ viewers who follow him on Twitter as to what was said. And you read the transcript, that's way 99.9% of depositions are done, and again this isn't about the administration. This is about the victims and hopefully hopefully we'll have witnesses that'll be coming in over the next the next six witnesses that will have pertinent information. I think you know this this guy Lutnick had three interactions with with Epstein over ten years. He's been very forthcoming with those interactions. And again we asked I think every question you all would ask that first hour. The Democrats after their outrage, they asked the exact same questions. I'm going to make a prediction and you can look at the transcript: they're asking the exact same questions that we asked the first hour and they asked the second hour. So just clear you don't believe the secretary is lying; are you are you ruling out then any videos for future depositions? Well, it just depends on how hard it is for people to come in. It just just depends. If you know the Clintons took seven months to come in and that's something that you know most I don't think it's any secret why nobody wants to be videoed. If you'll come in, you work with us, then then you know you might not have to be videoed; that's you know. So you're not ruling it out? I'm not ruling it out. It would just it just depends on how easy it is to get these people in because we're on a timeline, the clock's ticking, we're dealing with some of the most powerful attorneys in the world. We're dealing with the O.J. Simpson legal team with a lot of these guys and it's very difficult to get these people in. But I think we've had great success in that. I hope we continue to do that. It's just real frustrating when people like Walkenshaw, who's been here two months and is losing in a Democrat primary, has to come out here and lie to try to save his political butt. And it's just very frustrating but we'll continue to be transparent. As soon as the lawyers for both sides agree on the transcript, we'll release this one. Hopefully we'll get the Wade transcript released in a few days and I think we've got some some more quality witnesses coming in in the next few weeks that'll hopefully have more information that I think will be relevant to the investigation. Thank you all. What would be the Democrats' punishment?

Oversight Democrats call on Sec. Lutnick to resign over Epstein testimony

LiveNOW from FOX(5.6M) ·20:21 ·10.4K views · 2026-05-06
Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s resignation following a transcribed interview, alleging he provided evasive and contradictory testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and his 2012 visit to Epstein's private island.
TL;DR House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s resignation following a transcribed interview, alleging he provided evasive and contradictory testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and his 2012 visit to Epstein's...

TL;DR

House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s resignation following a transcribed interview, alleging he provided evasive and contradictory testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and his 2012 visit to Epstein's private island.

Discrepancies in Testimony and "Pathological Lying"

  • Contradictory Timelines: Democratic members highlighted that while Lutnick previously claimed on a podcast that he never saw Epstein again after a 2005 meeting, he admitted during the interview to visiting Epstein’s island in 2012 with his wife, children, and staff.
  • Selective Memory: Rep. Yassmin Ansari (D-AZ) noted that Lutnick could remember specific details from 2005—such as being uncomfortable seeing massage tables in Epstein’s home—yet claimed he could not remember the reason for his 2012 visit to the island.
  • Lack of Credibility: Members described Lutnick as a "pathological liar," noting he failed to explain how he remained unaware of Epstein’s 2008 solicitation of a minor conviction despite being a neighbor and acquaintance.

Semantic Contortions and Evasive Tactics

  • Redefining "I": Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA) and Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-TX) criticized Lutnick’s attempt to redefine his past statements. Lutnick reportedly argued that when he said "I" would never see Epstein again, he meant he would not see him *alone*, but felt it was acceptable to visit him with his family.
  • Social vs. Professional: Despite previously stating he had no social relationship with Epstein, Lutnick admitted during the interview that his visit to the island for lunch constituted a social interaction.
  • Characterization of Visits: Lutnick repeatedly used terms like "meaningless," "inconsequential," and "unsettling" to describe his meetings, which Democrats dismissed as an attempt to downplay his ties to the disgraced financier.

Allegations of a Federal Cover-Up

  • Retracted Statements on Blackmail: Lutnick reportedly walked back previous public assertions that Epstein engaged in blackmail and used videotapes to record guests, now claiming he was "just speculating."
  • Administration Influence: Rep. Khanna suggested that Lutnick’s change in story regarding blackmail and recordings followed conversations with members of the Trump administration.
      • Lutnick denied speaking to Donald Trump specifically about the testimony but refused to answer if he had spoken to the President about the matter more generally.
  • Video Recording Ban: Democrats expressed frustration that the interview was not videotaped, arguing that the public deserves to see Lutnick’s demeanor and "sweat on his brow" while answering questions.

The Defense and Republican Response

  • Chairman Comer’s Stance: House Oversight Chairman James Comer (R-KY) stated prior to the interview that he had seen no evidence of wrongdoing by Secretary Lutnick.
  • Lutnick’s Defense: According to reports from the room, Lutnick maintains he only had three brief interactions with Epstein:
      • A 10-15 minute coffee/tour in 2005.
      • A meeting to discuss scaffolding in a foyer.
      • A "short lunch" in the U.S. Virgin Islands in 2012, which he claims was an "unsettling" invitation arranged by an assistant.
  • Business Ties: Lutnick admitted that a subsidiary of his company was invested in a firm where Epstein also held an investment, though he characterized this as a standard business occurrence.

Notable Quotes

"If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing... he made a farce of the English language." — Rep. Ro Khanna (D-CA)
"Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history... It just demonstrates the culture of enabling these crimes and allowing rich and powerful people like Jeffrey Epstein... to continue." — Rep. Yassmin Ansari (D-AZ)
"They deserve to see the sweat on the Secretary's brow as he struggles to answer basic questions about his lies to the American people. Secretary Lutnick is not going to take any responsibility for lying today." — Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-TX)
You're seeing a massive media stakeout outside the room where Howard Lutnick, the Commerce Secretary, is sitting for a transcribed interview with members of the House Oversight Committee. This is part of their ongoing investigation into Jeffrey Epstein and anybody who had a connection to him. So, oh, here we go. We got Democrats coming in here. Let's see if they talk. So, let's go ahead and pull the audio up and listen live. Well, now we know why that interview was not videotaped. If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing. He was asked very straightforward questions about whether he regretted misleading the American people. I mean, he said that he would never see Epstein again in 2005, and everyone knows that he took his wife and kids to see Epstein in 2012. And yet, it was just contortions and lies and no acknowledgment that he misled the American public. And if you saw the exchanges that my colleagues had with him, you would see he made a farce of the English language. I mean, he was trying to define "I" as if saying that what he really meant is that he would not see Epstein alone but would be totally fine with having his wife and kids see Epstein. It made no sense. And then, he's raised even more serious concerns about the investigation. He originally had said that Epstein engaged in blackmail and recorded videotapes. Now he's saying, well, he was wrong, he was just speculating, and Epstein actually didn't engage in blackmailing. This raises the question of what the cover-up is. Did someone tell him to say that? I've not heard that Epstein never engaged in blackmail. Why is Howard Lutnick changing his testimony, changing his story? He's lost all credibility, and really it's a shame that the American people don't get to see what he did there. Total lack of truth and lack of honesty. Yeah, I I agree. Howard Lutnick should resign. That was absolutely mind-boggling what we just heard in the room. He was evasive, nervous, and he was dishonest. He would not admit to lying, which he clearly did in the podcast. He said he was never in the room with Jeffrey Epstein ever again after the first time he met him. Yet, he then admitted that he was in the room with Jeffrey Epstein. I had to ask him whether he and I were in the same room just now because I couldn't understand his meaning of "we were in the same room together." And so, and then he said that Jeffrey Epstein was a sick man that he would never want to be in the room with him again, yet he was in the room with him again when he visited his island where Jeffrey Epstein raped women, underage women. He brought his kids and his friends with him to that island when he met with Jeffrey Epstein. He said he never met with Jeffrey Epstein again socially, yet he admits that that was a social visit to his island. And so, he was clearly being dishonest. He was clearly being evasive. And finally, he doesn't remember why he went to Jeffrey Epstein's island, which is also mind-boggling. He somehow remembers going to the island after pictures surfaced of him being at the island, but he doesn't remember why he went to the island. And we had the emails. That's why we found out he was at the island. This is absolutely absurd and he should resign. Good afternoon. Congresswoman Ansari from Arizona's third district. After what we have seen so far in this transcribed interview, I feel very comfortable saying that Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. We have now gone through the timeline of events with him in in detail, in excruciating detail, from when they met in 2005. This is Howard Lutnick and his wife meeting Jeffrey Epstein. He described it himself in great detail on a podcast last year where he said that they went to his home and they took a tour. Allegedly, they were so uncomfortable after seeing the massage tables in Jeffrey Epstein's home that Howard Lutnick went on this podcast to tell the American people that he himself would never socially or professionally be involved or be in a room with Jeffrey Epstein again. My colleagues referenced the definitions he tried to change of what he meant there. Then in 2012, he took his family and his staff to Jeffrey Epstein's island. I focused my questions today on how he could have done that if him and his wife were truly so uncomfortable, especially when in 2008, we know that Jeffrey Epstein was accused and the sweetheart deal took place for soliciting a minor. Howard Lutnick tried to tell us in that room that he essentially didn't know any of that. I asked him how did I, as a child at the time, see this all over the media about Jeffrey Epstein and you, an adult who was this person's neighbor who had engaged with him before, how could you possibly not have known? The level of the lies that are taking place inside that room without video is unbelievable and part of this egregious cover-up. I recommend that every single American, when this interview is released, read the document in full. You will see Howard Lutnick use phrases like how it's "inexplicable" and "unsettling" that he went to the island. That's how he defines it himself. It's inexplicable how this happened. He also tries to define all of their interactions as quote unquote "meaningless" and "inconsequential." I think he's used those terms about 20 times so far and continues to use them. So, I will just end by saying that there are real victims here. There are potentially over a thousand women who were abused and raped when they were children. And even at the bare minimum, Howard Lutnick was aware of these things. He knew. It just demonstrates the culture of enabling these crimes and allowing rich and powerful people like Jeffrey Epstein and all of the other disgusting rich and powerful men who committed these crimes to continue. People like Howard Lutnick knew these things were happening because they were all over the news and continued to engage and legitimize somebody like Jeffrey Epstein. Earlier today, Chairman Comer said that he hadn't seen any evidence of wrongdoing by Secretary Lutnick. And that's actually the problem with the Trump administration and this Republican majority in Congress, because lying to the American people is wrongdoing. And Howard Lutnick lied to the American people. I hope today that we would have heard some remorse, some contrition from Secretary Lutnick for flat-out lying to the American people. Instead, what we've heard thus far is hours of testimony where he's attempting to redefine the meaning of the word "I." He claims that when he said "I would never be in a room again with Jeffrey Epstein," he meant only him and Jeffrey Epstein. Epstein was so gross to him, so disgusting that he wasn't willing to be in a room with him, but he was perfectly okay with his wife and family being in a room with Epstein. He's lying. And today's transcribed interview is part of the ongoing cover-up because the American people deserve to see the video of what's taking place in there. They deserve to see the sweat on the Secretary's brow as he struggles to answer basic questions about his lies to the American people. So, we're going to continue to listen and ask tough questions, but unfortunately, it appears that Secretary Lutnick is not going to take any responsibility for lying to the American people today. Can you explain for just a moment when you talked about the trip on the boat? And you're saying that he's saying "Oh, we were okay doing that," but he didn't want to be in the same room with him. Characterize what his remarks were, his response when you said "Wait a minute, isn't this inconsistent with his travel if you're uncomfortable with being with this person?" We asked him over and over again why did you go to the island. And he says he doesn't remember, that it was "inexplicable," and he simply didn't know how to answer the question at that point. On the island, did he say that he saw anything from his time there that now in retrospect raises alarm about Epstein's activities? I mean, you mentioned in his 2005 visit to Epstein's apartment that he saw massage tables that made him uncomfortable. Did he see anything on his time on the island? Those questions were specifically asked by the majority during the first hour and he said no to all of it. And I also think it's important to note, he could remember nothing about the visit to the island. He couldn't remember why he was there, couldn't remember anything he saw. Apparently, he could remember in great detail the 2005 visit to the townhouse and the massage tables that were there. Now, 2005 is seven years before 2012. So, his memory was sharp with respect to 2005 but dull with respect to 2012 and the visit to the island. And this is why this is such a cover-up. I mean, he refused to say there was any blackmail even though he thought that happened in the past. He refused to say there was any video recording even though he said in the past that Epstein was video recording these young girls. He refused to say anything abnormal about Epstein's island. So, someone obviously got to him from the administration. In fact, he said he talked to people in the administration and that's why he's changed his tune. The question is not just why he's lying, the question is why he's covering up. Why he's no longer willing to share what he knows about Epstein's abhorrent behavior. Did the Secretary indicate—did he talk to President Trump ahead of this interview? Did he talk to people in the Trump administration? He said he did not talk to President Trump. Why do you think Secretary Lutnick agreed to appear voluntarily? Did he address that at all? It is significant that he's the only cabinet secretary that has appeared for this. He didn't want to be subpoenaed. I mean, he knew that we have the Republican votes to subpoena him just like we did with Pam Bondi. And it's the first time that a cabinet member has been subpoenaed. What I expected, honestly, I expected him to say "Well, you know, I regret what I said on the podcast. I should have been transparent about this incident with the family. Nothing happened that was terrible but, you know what, I should have had better judgment. And here are the things I know about Epstein, he was a disgusting person, let me try to help in the investigation." He did none of that. Can I ask two questions? A follow-up on what you just said, Congressman Khanna. You mentioned that he spoke to members of the administration and then he changed his tune. Did he say who exactly he spoke to and when he spoke to them? And then secondly, the Secretary had told you guys that he found the invitation to the island to be unsettling, how his assistant knew that he was in the US Virgin Islands. Did he talk about why he accepted an unsettling invitation? He could not recall who he spoke to in the administration. It's also important to note, he was asked by a number of us if he spoke to President Trump about today's testimony and he refused to answer that question. He did say he didn't speak to President Trump or Donald Trump when he was not president, but he would not answer the question as to whether he spoke to President Trump about today's testimony. This is what I was asking during my line of questioning. Why did you go when you were so clear on this podcast and you described in great detail that you and your wife were so alarmed by this person and did not want to ever be in a room with him? And to the point of the statement that he had made, I mean, they were very clear statements that "I would never in a social professional setting be in a room with this person." And then we later got him to admit that visiting someone's island and having lunch with them is in fact considered a social situation. And so no, he would not. And I find that extremely unbelievable. I mean, I think of all of the depositions that we have done so far, I find Howard Lutnick to be the least credible with the way that he just refuses to acknowledge how misleading his statements were. I mean, quite frankly, he lied in the podcast but he refuses to acknowledge that. He still is trying to stand by the fact that he was truthful in those statements. Did the Secretary indicate whether or not he had had any business dealings with Jeffrey Epstein and more specifically whether they invested in the same company? Yeah, the Republicans asked that in the first hour. He did admit that a subsidiary of his company was invested in a company Jeffrey Epstein was invested in as well. And they did have an email exchange about that. And then as Democrats, are you all planning to do anything to compel the Secretary's testimony on camera going further? That may be coming but stay tuned. Meaning that you might ask for a public hearing? I didn't say that. I said generally that we're going to make sure that these types of interviews are on video and that the American people can see them. So to be clear, you're going to try to have him come in again and try to maybe do this under a subpoena which would compel—I'm not—I didn't say him again. Just in the future. I'm not ruling out him again. I'm just saying generally. Can we get your reaction on something else? On the other investigation dealing with the DC and the investigation with the police department. Nine officers have now been put on leave or in investigation. Just want to get your thoughts on that. That's a great question for another day. I think that was the last question. Thank you. Thank you. All right, as you see there, listening in to some of those Democratic lawmakers taking questions about the testimony that Howard Lutnick, the Commerce Secretary, made today in a behind-the-closed-door seating. We wanted to make sure we were able to bring that to you here on LiveNOW from FOX, continuing to watch this stakeout camera there as several other members who are inside of that meeting are likely to come outside and speak as well. And so once again, this is one of the major updates, things that we're following here on LiveNOW from FOX today. In the meantime, I want to put this up on your screen from Chad Pergram there with FOX News as he's also following this, saying this: "From the closed-door interview with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein from a person familiar: 'Jeffrey Epstein and Secretary Lutnick were neighbors from 2005 to 2019. He purchased the property in 1997 but did not move in until renovations were complete in 2005. They had no personal or professional relationship.' 'Secretary Lutnick only had three meetings with Epstein: Lutnick and his wife met Epstein at his home for coffee when they became neighbors. It lasted about 10 to 15 minutes. Lutnick stated that Epstein provided a short tour of his home, and that's where they saw the massage table.' 'At that time, Lutnick decided that he did not want a personal or professional relationship with Epstein. While in the US Virgin Islands with his family, Lutnick and his family were invited to Epstein's home for lunch. Lutnick did not know how Epstein's assistant knew he was in the USVI and found that to be unsettling.' 'He had a short lunch with family and friends. Epstein and Lutnick met one time to discuss scaffolding in Epstein's foyer. He never saw Epstein with any young women and never witnessed anything inappropriate with young women.'" And again, that's from Chad Pergram. Of course, we heard just a bit of the opposite from some of the Democrats who were also inside of that meeting calling for Lutnick to step down from his position due to some of these ties that he had at the time with Jeffrey Epstein. And so again, this is the House Oversight Committee that started this closed-door interview with him a few hours ago as a part of the panel's inquiry into Epstein and the federal government's handling of its investigation into the disgraced financier. I do want to put this back up on your screen, another live vantage point that we have there of that stakeout camera in case any other members of Congress decide to step up and walk there. We do know that Representative Comer, the Republican from Kentucky and committee's chairman, said that before the session, he had expected those lawmakers and staff to ask Lutnick about the timeline of his ties to Epstein, including a visit to Epstein's private island. We just got some insight from Chad Pergram into how that conversation went. And so we did want to bring that to you here on LiveNOW from FOX. There's a lot more to uncover. And again, if any other details come out, we'll bring them to you on air.

Greer on EU Complying With Trade Deal, Trump to China

Bloomberg Television(3.1M) ·10:48 ·2.4K views · 2026-05-06
Jamieson Greer (US Trade Representative)
US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer warns that the European Union is "past due" on fulfilling its commitments under the Turnberry trade agreement, signaling potential tariff escalations if the EU fails to remove industrial tariffs and provide regulatory flexibility.
TL;DR US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer warns that the European Union is "past due" on fulfilling its commitments under the Turnberry trade agreement, signaling potential tariff escalations if the EU fails to remove industrial tariffs and provide regulatory flexibility....

TL;DR

US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer warns that the European Union is "past due" on fulfilling its commitments under the Turnberry trade agreement, signaling potential tariff escalations if the EU fails to remove industrial tariffs and provide regulatory flexibility. Concurrently, the U.S. is preparing for high-level talks in China to address Beijing’s non-compliance with Iranian oil sanctions and the "weaponization" of critical minerals.

EU Trade Compliance and Potential Escalation

  • The Turnberry Agreement Lag: Greer reports that while the U.S. modified its tariff rates months ago to meet the framework established last summer, the EU has yet to comply with its side of the deal.
      • Specific U.S. Demands: The U.S. is calling for the removal of EU tariffs on industrial goods, duty-free quotas for key agricultural products, and regulatory flexibility regarding methane content in LNG, deforestation regulations, and the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM).
  • Legislative Hurdles: A tariff bill currently in the European Parliament contains "amendments that we didn't agree to," which could limit the relief and benefits for U.S. exports.
  • Tariff Threats: While Greer declined to set a hard deadline, he noted the U.S. is currently in compliance while the EU is not.
      • Auto Tariffs: The U.S. currently maintains a 25% tariff on global autos, but has applied only 10% generally; failure by the EU to move "relatively soon" (potentially before July) puts further presidential action on the table.

Confronting China on Sanctions and Trade

  • Iranian Oil Sanctions: A primary agenda item for the upcoming presidential visit to Beijing is China’s refusal to comply with U.S. sanctions on Iranian crude.
      • Greer emphasized that buying Iranian oil contributes to the "world's largest state sponsor of terrorism."
      • Stability vs. Accountability: While the U.S. seeks relationship stability, Greer noted that China directing firms to ignore sanctions must be addressed at the highest levels.
  • Economic Weaponization: Greer highlighted a G7-aligned stance against China "weaponizing" critical minerals.
      • Collaborative Response: The U.S. is pursuing a plurilateral trade agreement with Europe and Japan to secure downstream processing and manufacturing of critical minerals to reduce reliance on Chinese supply chains.

Reindustrialization and Shifting Alliances

  • U.S. Economic Priorities: Greer defined the current U.S. trade posture as a necessity for "reindustrialization," aimed at reducing the trade deficit and increasing real wages and manufacturing’s share of the GDP.
  • The EU Trade Surplus: In a significant shift, Greer noted that the European Union—not China—now holds the largest trade surplus with the United States as of last year.
  • Siloing Security and Trade: Greer argued that hard economic conversations with allies are necessary and should be "siloed off" from military and security cooperation.
      • He cited the history of U.S.-France relations (notably de Gaulle’s exit from NATO's military structure) as evidence that alliances can survive deep policy disagreements.

Strategic De-risking and Diversification

  • European Sentiment: Greer acknowledged that European countries and companies are becoming more "clear-eyed" about the risks of doing business in China.
  • Decoupling vs. Diversification: Greer clarified that the U.S. is not seeking a "massive decoupling" but rather a diversification of the economy to reduce vulnerability.
      • The goal remains a "constructive trade relationship" with China, provided it can become reliable and predictable.
"We have to reindustrialize, we have to reduce our trade deficit, we have to get real wages up... That necessarily means that we need to fix the terms of trade with other trading partners, friend or foe." — Jamieson Greer
"The Europeans... have the largest trade surplus in the world with the US right now. It's no longer China as of last year." — Jamieson Greer
"China's the country that's been cutting off and limiting access to critical minerals and that's why we're cooperating with Europe on projects for critical minerals... as we enter into a period of geo-economic uncertainty." — Jamieson Greer
Ambassador Greer, welcome back to this program, sir. Can we just start off by getting your assessment of the current characterization of talks between the EU and the US? What is the current status of things? So I've spent a lot of time over the weekend and here while I've been in Paris speaking to my counterparts at the European Commission and my counterparts in member states about this issue. Just to go back in time a little bit to last summer when we concluded the Turnberry agreement which was an initial framework, the United States immediately set to work modifying its tariff rates to meet the agreement that we made. The EU has had a lot of time, many, many months to comply with their tariff commitments and their commitments on non-tariff barriers and that hasn't materialized yet. I think that the minds are focused in the EU and in Europe, I think is probably the best way to describe it right now. They're focused on what next steps are and how they might come into compliance. Well what-what are the next steps? Of course the Europeans have a process; it's guided by their parliament. What's the exact ask of the United States? So the ask, if you go back to August, we have a written piece of paper, a joint statement with the Europeans, and our ask is that Europe fulfill those obligations they committed to. The United States complied with its tariff obligations for many, many months. The Europeans have agreed to remove their tariffs on industrial goods; they've agreed to give duty-free quotas to key agricultural products coming from the United States. They've agreed to provide flexibilities for the United States on many of the regulatory matters that the European Union seems to specialize in: things related to methane content in-in LNG, things about deforestation regulations, things about the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism. So we expect the European Union to keep its part of the deal, which includes flexibilities on those types of things as well as the tariff things. And with the US, our part of the deal is to align our-our new tariff structure with what we committed to. And if they don't come into an agreement by when is the question, then would the President potentially escalate to the 25% auto level? Right now the United States has a 10% tariff on the world, generally speaking, and it has 25% on autos from around the world. Europe has had it at only 15% this whole time. We have tariffs on steel and aluminum. We have other investigations ongoing which may or may not result in tariffs depending on the outcome. The idea would be, can-can Europe come into compliance? I'm not setting a hard date on this. My view is it's already past due, frankly. This is part of the reason why the President has come out and said if the-if the Europeans aren't going to be complying with this deal right now, you know, why should we be complying with all of it? All that being said, the autos is just one element. There are other elements to the deal where the United States remains in full compliance, in contrast to where the Europeans have been for many months. It's just that your colleague, the ambassador to Europe, told us earlier they would need to see movement relatively soon and basically said that July would be too late. We're now in May, so basically do you need to see something between the next few weeks for the President not to act? Well, what I would say is right now this afternoon I was meeting with my colleague Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič, the trade commissioner in the European Union. He-he left the G7 at an appropriate time to go have the—what they call the trilogue process—between the European Parliament, Council, and Commission where they go over the tariff bill that's being debated. That tariff bill in the European Parliament, it also has a lot of amendments attached to it that we didn't agree to with the European Union. These are amendments that could limit the relief, limit the benefit of the agreement, or place limits on US exports to-to Europe under a preferential trade agreement. So some of those amendments need to be resolved as well; it's not just about passing the bill, it's about making sure the bill reflects what we agreed last summer with the Europeans. Ambassador Greer, I know for you the focus is going to change to what's going on in China because the President will be going there next week. Beijing is basically refusing to comply with US sanctions. I'm sure you've seen the reporting that they're telling some firms, some refiners, that they don't need to comply when it comes to imports of Iranian crude. How big of-of an agenda item is this going to be when the President sits down with Xi Jinping? Obviously it's something we're going to have to discuss. Iran is the world's largest state sponsor of terrorism and anyone who's buying oil from Iran is contributing to that. And so when the Treasury takes steps similar to steps it's taken many times over decades with respect to oil refiners who are dealing with Iran and you have a country that refuses to comply or directs non-compliance, that's going to have to be a discussion item. We certainly have had it raised at the—you know—staff level between China and the United States. I would say we're looking for stability with China. We don't want this to be something that derails the broader—the broader relationship or any agreements that might come out of our meeting in Beijing, but it's certainly something that may come up. What more could be done besides secondary sanctions imposed potentially on Chinese banks? I mean yesterday the President also said even though China is doing this, the President said Xi Jinping has been very respectful of him. This doesn't seem respectful of the United States and our rules. Remember, China and every country, you know, they-they-they're going to undertake domestically the laws and regulations they think they need to do. Sometimes they'll be in conflict with ours and we're going to expect some kind of resolution. At the same time, we have the European Union—European Union for years has had a blocking statute against complying with our sanctions on Cuba. So what China's doing is-is serious, we take it very seriously, but again for us it's not something where we're going to let that become the only thing we talk about in the relationship. We have a lot of things that we have to cover. That will be addressed, but then we'll address other issues related to trade and geopolitics and foreign policy and everything that comes with a relationship between two of the most important countries in the world. Ambassador Greer, there's a real question about alliances here and the way that the United States and Europe have been discussing and the tensions that have been emerging versus the rhetoric that's been surrounding the US and the China meeting that is planned for next week. Do you see the US alliances shifting as some of these negotiations take place or are these negotiations independent of any kind of traditional alliance that the United States has had? So I-I think that's a-that's a good question because it puts into context everything that we're doing. And I think you have to look a little bit at history. People think about our alliance system as something that is set in stone, never had issues, have always been perfect and never been problems. That's just not true. I'm sitting here in Paris in a place where, you know, decades ago Charles de Gaulle essentially pulled France out of the military structure of NATO and sought to have its own independent nuclear program. Which-which in retrospect, you know, maybe de Gaulle was right. So, you know, we have a long history of having a back and forth between the US and its closest partners as we try to find the right balance between international cooperation and alliances and what we need for our sovereign right. When it comes to trade, the United States, we have to reindustrialize, we have to reduce our trade deficit, we have to get real wages up, we have to have manufacturing as a bigger portion of our economy. That necessarily means that we need to fix the terms of trade with other trading partners, friend or foe. And that means we have to have hard conversations with the Europeans who, by the way, have the largest trade surplus in the world with the US right now. It's no longer China as of last year. With the United States, I don't think we're going to let that get in the way of whatever military cooperation we want to have with the Europeans. If-if other countries want trade to get in the way of that kind of cooperation, that's up to them, but there's a long history of having economic differences and challenges and resolutions and disputes, etc., and siloing that off from military and security questions. Sometimes they get mixed, sometimes they stay apart. Ambassador Greer, it seems like European representatives feel a little bit differently. The French trade minister said that it seems like there's a weaponization of mutual dependencies and that type of action is causing some real rethinking on their side. How do you address things like that—commentary with some of your counterparts that we're seeing and hearing? So, you know, I-I saw that language today, but it was with respect to—and-and maybe-maybe we have a different understanding. We put out a G7 statement that talks about that, but it's really about China weaponizing critical minerals. This is an area where France, the rest of Europe, and the United States are quite aligned on the way forward and how we shouldn't allow basic commodities to be weaponized. You know, the United States is not in the business of cutting off commodities. We've been exporting oil during this whole-Iran operation. You know, China's the country that's been cutting off and limiting access to critical minerals and that's why we're cooperating with Europe on projects for critical minerals, on downstream processing, on manufacturing. That's why we're talking about having a plurilateral trade agreement on trade in critical minerals with Europe, with Japan and others. So there are areas of a lot of collaboration and cooperation as we enter into a period of geo-economic uncertainty. Ambassador, that's an interesting point because for a long time the Europeans have just sat on the fence. They've wanted the security umbrella of the United States, but also wanted the economic benefits of having a close relationship with the Chinese. Do you think that's changed? I think that every—I think that every country is going to pursue their trading relationships in the way they-they think is best. China's a great example. We're always going to trade with China; the Europeans are always going to trade with China. Europeans, Germany in particular, they've had very close economic relations. I do think that a lot of European countries and companies are rethinking that. I think they're being more clear-eyed about the risks and the challenges of doing business in and with China. At the same time that's a big market; they do a lot of manufacturing there, they have a lot of consumers there, and so it's-it's a place where people want to be able to do business, but they want to be able to do it in a reliable and predictable way. And that's not the case right now and hasn't been for some years. So while I think that the European view of China is changing and developing, it doesn't mean there's going to be some kind of massive decoupling in the same way that even in the United States, we haven't had a massive decoupling from China. We've reduced our reliance on them, we've diversified from their economy, but we still want to have a constructive trade relationship if we can.

Watch: Hegseth and Gen. Caine Full Press Conference on Iran War | WSJ

WSJ News(637.0K) ·34:40 ·907 views · 2026-05-06
Pete Hegseth (Defense Secretary) Dan Caine (Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff)
The U.S.
TL;DR The U.S. has launched "Project Freedom," a defensive military operation to restore the free flow of commerce through the Strait of Hormuz by providing 24/7 armed overwatch for international shipping while maintaining a strict blockade on Iranian ports. Mission...

TL;DR

The U.S. has launched "Project Freedom," a defensive military operation to restore the free flow of commerce through the Strait of Hormuz by providing 24/7 armed overwatch for international shipping while maintaining a strict blockade on Iranian ports.

Project Freedom: Mission and Objectives

  • Mission Definition: A defensive, focused, and temporary operation directed by President Trump to protect innocent commercial shipping from Iranian aggression.
  • Strategic Distinction: Secretary Pete Hegseth clarified that Project Freedom is "separate and distinct" from previous offensive operations like EPIC FURY.
  • The "Red, White, and Blue Dome": The U.S. has established a persistent security umbrella over the strait to prevent Iran from imposing illegal "tolls" or extortion on international vessels.
  • Operational Scope:
      • U.S. forces will not enter Iranian waters or airspace.
      • The mission aims to secure global energy routes and prevent economic shortages for the world's poorest populations.

Current Maritime Crisis and Blockade Status

  • Trapped Assets: General Dan Caine reported that approximately 1,550 commercial vessels and 22,500 mariners are currently trapped in the Arabian Gulf due to Iranian threats.
  • Iranian Aggression: Since the ceasefire, Iran has fired at commercial vessels nine times, seized two container ships, and attacked U.S. forces more than 10 times with "low-level harassing fire."
  • Blockade Enforcement:
      • The U.S. "ironclad blockade" of Iranian ports remains in effect.
      • Six Iranian ships recently attempted to run the blockade and were forced to turn around.
  • Proven Transit: Two U.S. commercial ships, escorted by destroyers, have already successfully transited the strait to demonstrate the lane is open.

Military Capabilities and Tactical Innovation

  • Force Composition: The operation involves 15,000 service members, 100+ fighter jets/drones, and multiple guided-missile destroyers.
  • 82nd Airborne Division: The "All-American" division is coordinating the mission, utilizing:
      • JADC2: Integrating land, air, space, sea, and cyber forces.
      • AI Integration: Using next-generation tactical networks and AI to augment intelligence and decision-making for field commanders.
  • Defensive Successes: U.S. Navy MH-60 and Army AH-64 Apache helicopters recently defeated Iranian cruise missiles, drones, and small boats targeting shipping.

Diplomatic Outlook and Strategy

  • Call for International Support: Hegseth emphasized that the U.S. expects the "world to step up," specifically naming South Korea, Japan, Australia, and Europe as nations that must eventually take over the mission.
  • Nuclear Goal: The administration remains "laser-focused" on ensuring Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon, asserting that Operation MIDNIGHT HAMMER significantly set back Iranian capabilities.
  • Ceasefire Status: Despite recent skirmishes, Hegseth stated the ceasefire is not over, but warned that U.S. forces are "locked and loaded" to respond to further attacks.

Notable Quotes

"Iran is the clear aggressor, harassing civilian vessels, threatening mariners from every nation indiscriminately, and weaponizing a critical choke point for its own financial benefit... That ends with Project Freedom." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"No adversary should mistake our current restraint with a lack of resolve. CENTCOM and the rest of the Joint Force remain ready to resume major combat operations against Iran if ordered to do so." — General Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
"The world needs this waterway a lot more than we do. We're stabilizing the situation so commerce can flow again, but we expect the world to step up. At the appropriate time and soon, we will hand responsibility back to you." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
Well, as you know, President Trump has directed U.S. Central Command to restart the free flow of commerce through the Strait of Hormuz under the umbrella of Project Freedom. To be clear, this operation is separate and distinct from Operation EPIC FURY. Project Freedom is defensive in nature, focused in scope, and temporary in duration, with one mission: protecting innocent commercial shipping from Iranian aggression. American forces won't need to enter Iranian waters or airspace. It's not necessary. We're not looking for a fight, but Iran also cannot be allowed to block innocent countries and their goods from an international waterway. Iran is the clear aggressor, harassing civilian vessels, threatening mariners from every nation indiscriminately, and weaponizing a critical choke point for its own financial benefit—or at least trying to. For too long, Iran has been harassing ships, shooting at civilian tankers from all nations, and trying to impose a tolling system. Iran's plan, a form of international extortion, is unacceptable. That ends with Project Freedom. Two U.S. commercial ships, along with American destroyers, have already safely transited the strait, showing the lane is clear. We know the Iranians are embarrassed by this fact. They said they control the strait; they do not. So American ships led the way, commercial and military, shouldering the initial risk from the front, as Americans always do. And right now, hundreds more ships from nations around the world are lining up to transit. CENTCOM, along with partner nations, is in active communication with hundreds of ships, shipping companies, and insurers. All of these ships from all around the world want to get out of the Iranian trap that they have been stuck inside. As a direct gift from the United States to the world, we have established a powerful red, white, and blue dome over the strait. American destroyers are on station supported by hundreds of fighter jets, helicopters, drones, and surveillance aircraft providing 24/7 overwatch for peaceful commercial vessels. Except Iran's, of course, which is why our ironclad blockade remains in full effect as well. In fact, six ships tried to run the blockade out of Iranian ports as Project Freedom commenced, and they were all turned around. This is more than strategy; it's also humanitarian. By breaking Iran's illegal stranglehold, we're protecting the lives and livelihoods of sailors from dozens of countries, securing global energy routes, and preventing shortages that hit the world's poorest people the hardest. Once again, America is using its strength to lift up others. Iran is trying to subjugate the world. To what remains of Iran's forces: if you attack American troops or innocent commercial shipping, you will face overwhelming and devastating American firepower. The President has been very clear about this. We prefer this to be a peaceful operation, but we are locked and loaded to defend our people, our ships, our aircraft, and this mission without hesitation. To Iran, let innocent ships pass freely. These international waters belong to all nations, not to Iran to tax, toll, or control. To our partners, allies, and the rest of the world: this is a temporary mission for us. As I've said before, the world needs this waterway a lot more than we do. We're stabilizing the situation so commerce can flow again, but we expect the world to step up. At the appropriate time and soon, we will hand responsibility back to you. To the incredible Americans executing this mission right now, simply thank you. Your courage, your readiness, your unmatched professionalism, and precision are why the world can breathe easier today and every day. You are and always will be the greatest fighting force in human history—unmatched, always. Project Freedom is underway. Commerce will be flowing, and America is once again leading with strength, clarity, and purpose for the benefit of the entire world. Our will is unshakable. God bless our troops, and God bless the mighty United States Navy as well. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning, everyone. Thanks for being here. On Sunday, at the direction of the President, CENTCOM initiated Project Freedom with the objective of facilitating the safe passage of international commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Over the past seven weeks, Iran has repeatedly threatened and attacked commercial shipping in the strait in order to effectively cut off commercial traffic and damage the global economy. By obstructing one of the world's most critical maritime checkpoints—a vital transit route for approximately one-fifth of the global oil consumption—Iran is weaponizing the global supply chain. This action disregards established navigational rights and the Law of the Sea, and the regime led by the IRGC is effectively attempting to hold the entire global economy hostage. Let me give you an update of the last few days of activities. If you take a look at the map here, as the Secretary said, the United States has established Project Freedom to open up the Strait of Hormuz. This graphic highlights the kinetic engagements that the Iranians have attempted to make or have taken against the region. The green dots and blue dots show the groupings of commercial cargo and tanker vessels. The United States has established a significant presence over the strait here in order to open up the free flow of commerce, noting the blockade still continues along the eastern flank. The map shows the examples and samples of Iranian attacks against commercial vessels in the strait as well as the Gulf of Oman and up in the Arabian Gulf. Since the ceasefire was announced, Iran has fired at commercial vessels nine times and seized two container ships. They've attacked U.S. forces more than 10 times, all below the threshold of restarting major combat operations at this point. You can also see the group of tanker and cargo vessels in the U.S. blockade line as I mentioned. As a result of Iran's indiscriminate attacks across the region, there are currently 22,500 mariners embarked on more than 1,550 commercial vessels trapped in the Arabian Gulf, unable to transit. In addition to shipping, Iran has continued to deliberately attack its neighbors. Just yesterday, Iran attacked Oman once and the UAE three times, including an attack on the Fujairah oil terminal which was successfully defeated. They also launched cruise missiles, drones, and small boats at U.S. forces defending commercial shipping in the straits. United States Navy MH-60 helicopters and Army AH-64 Apache helicopters successfully defeated those threats. Thus far, today is quieter. Let me describe this defensive operation just a few clicks down for you. As the Secretary said, Project Freedom is designed to protect commercial shipping and help restore the flow of commerce through the strait and sustain freedom of navigation. CENTCOM has established an enhanced security area on the southern side of the strait that is now protected by U.S. land, naval, and air assets to help defeat further Iranian aggression against commercial shipping. I'd like to share a little bit more about U.S. capabilities, but for operational security purposes, I'm not going to get into specific details out there. On the surface, guided-missile destroyers and other warships are detecting and defeating Iranian threats. This includes fast boats and one-way attack drones. In the air, more than 100 fighters, attack aircraft, and other manned and unmanned aircraft, synchronized by the 82nd Airborne Division, are in the air 24 hours a day providing defensive overwatch for the enhanced security area and its approaches that I discussed. These forces are comprised of over 15,000 American service members and have established localized air and sea control to protect against Iranian threats. Commercial vessels that transit through the area will see, hear, and frankly feel U.S. combat power around them: on the sea, in the skies, and on the radio. As of this morning, as was mentioned prior, two U.S.-flag merchant vessels have transited, and we anticipate more to transit over the coming days. Further up the Gulf, U.S. Naval Forces Central Command continues to work to identify and mitigate any threats. Beyond Project Freedom, CENTCOM and the rest of the Joint Force remain ready to resume major combat operations against Iran if ordered to do so. No adversary should mistake our current restraint with a lack of resolve. The President has said, as the Secretary has, that now is the time for those with an equity stake in the strait to come assist. Before I turn it back to the Secretary, I want to take a moment to highlight part of our joint force today. Today, I want to talk about the paratroopers of the United States Army 82nd Airborne Division. When the President or the Secretary need immediate, scalable, and lethal combat power in CENTCOM or elsewhere, the All-American Division answers the call. The 82nd is the core of our nation's immediate response force and ready to deploy within hours of notice. These paratroopers are capable of accomplishing any mission in any environment. They are constantly training, constantly ready to jump from Air Force aircraft into ground combat and seize key terrain in order to do so, just like their predecessors did in Sicily and Normandy in World War II, or to secure or enable the follow-on forces to flow into theater as they did in Grenada or Panama. They can do the range of missions, from counterinsurgency to counterterrorism operations to power projection, as they have many times over the course of the history of this great division. They can do all these things because they're incredibly well trained, well equipped, and well led, but also because of the indomitable spirit of the All-American paratrooper. Every single one of them volunteered for this role, volunteered to go to airborne training, and volunteered to serve. But this division is now more than just a formation that we use for forcible entries. For example, just today they are out there coordinating, integrating, and synchronizing land, air, space, sea, and cyber forces, fusing all-joint all-domain command and control. They use next-generation tactical networks that are AI-enabled to augment intelligence and operations, and they seamlessly synchronize all of these effects and are doing so in support of Project Freedom as we speak. The relentless focus on technical innovation allows the commanders out there in the field to see, sense, and understand the opportunities that are in front of them, along with the associated risks, and make smart decisions out on the objective. With incredible courage, tenacity, and grit, they are going all the way every day, and whenever the nation calls on them, they are there to answer the call. We are grateful for their service and their leadership. Lastly, before I close, as I often do, I want to remember some of our fallen. Today we remember Sergeant William Rivers, Specialist Brianna Moffatt, and Specialist Kennedy Sanders of the 718th Engineering Company, U.S. Army Reserves out of Fort Benning, Georgia. They were tragically killed on January 28th, 2024, when attacked by an Iranian-backed proxy force at Tower 22 in Jordan. We continue to carry on their memories; we remember their names; we remember their families. The Secretary and I are deeply grateful for all of our deployed forces who are out there doing our nation's work. And sir, with that, I'll kick it back over to you. Very good. We'll take a few questions. Go ahead. Thank you. Alexandra Ingersall, One America News. Do you assess, Mr. Secretary, do you assess what's left of this small boat fleet to be a significant threat or a nuisance to be managed? And then, secondly, what is your message to critics who say that the U.S. doesn't have a strategy in this mission? Well, any threat needs to be taken seriously. Just like those six attack boats that the Chairman mentioned were taken seriously and dealt with before they were any real threat to the American military vessels that they were approaching. And that is, small craft like that are all that Iran has left. Their ability to get close with a destroyer of the capabilities that we have is limited, but we're going to take it very seriously and kinetically, quickly. We're not going to allow a threat to gather. And I can tell you, even from just this morning, the strategy remains laser-focused. In fact, what you see from Project Freedom is that we're not allowing ourselves to get distracted. This is a separate and distinct effort, temporary in nature, that we plan to hand over to the world. The world has expressed a desire to be a part of this. As is often the case, or maybe almost always the case, American leadership is required. So the President was willing to undertake this: send commercial ships through, send destroyers in, provide this red, white, and blue bubble of protection, and encourage the world to step up because ultimately this waterway is, as I said, far more crucial to the rest of the world and they need to have the ability to defend it. So I think this is a reflection of a laser-focused strategy, actually. Right here. Morning, Mr. Secretary, Mr. Chairman. David Zeer, Real America's Voice. I wanted to ask you, of any of the fast boats that have been either sunk or identified, are there any armaments larger than say small, you know, AK-47 type stuff or any anti-ship cruise missiles identified? And my second question is if there's a divide between the IRGC and Tehran, is there a way to get Tehran to agree to let us go after the IRGC that is doing these attacks without violating the ceasefire? Well, right now, anything coming at our crafts, regardless of what's on it, has to be defended against, and that's the case. I don't know what the exact armaments were on those fast boats... Small arms, nothing significant on the fast boats. Rifles or small machine guns. There were some small coastal cruise missiles shot down as well. So there's been some frothiness in the beginning aspects of this that were dealt with very directly by our destroyers. And we're communicating both overtly and quietly to the Iranians to allow this defensive operation to happen on behalf of the world. There are some actions the IRGC takes sometimes that are outside the bounds of what maybe Iranian negotiators would like. That's their job to rein that in and ultimately create a condition for a deal. That's not something I talked about in these remarks, but that's happening in real time. Iran has an ability to make that deal. But what we're demonstrating with Project Freedom is they don't control the straits. We know Iran is embarrassed by the fact that our blockade is holding and we can run ships through, and we're going to help the world run ships through. And if they hold it at issue, the whole world knows who's doing it. It's not us. We're willing to open it up; it's Iran. And that creates a dilemma for them, and we hope they make a deal. Thank you, sir. My first question's for General Caine. Mary Margaret with The Daily Wire. You said that the attacks by Iran so far are below the threshold of restarting major combat operations. What is that threshold? And then for Secretary Hegseth, are there still concerns about mines in the strait and can you kind of clarify these reports of kamikaze dolphins that we've heard about? Hmm, I haven't heard the kamikaze dolphin thing. It's like sharks with laser beams, right? The threshold of restarting is a political decision above my pay grade. What I'll say is it's more low harassing fire right now. It feels like Iran is grasping at straws to try to do something across the southern flank. To your question, David, their command and control structure remains very fractured, and I think they're struggling to maintain control down echelon at the edge. But we're still—it's still pretty low-level kinetics at this point in time. And I can't confirm or deny whether we have kamikaze dolphins, but I can confirm they don't. Ultimately, any follow-on effort if there are mines identified would be something that some of our units could undertake or the world could undertake. But right now we know we have a lane of safe passage that commercial shipping can flow through. Go ahead. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. Last 24 hours or so, Iran's fired at us, we fired at Iran. I'm just going to ask you more directly: is the ceasefire over? No, the ceasefire is not over. Ultimately, this is a separate and distinct project, and we expected there would be some churn at the beginning, which happened. And we said we would defend and defend aggressively, and we absolutely have. Iran knows that. And ultimately the President's going to make a decision whether anything were to escalate into a violation of a ceasefire, but certainly we would urge Iran to be prudent in the actions that they take to keep that underneath this threshold. This is about the straits, this is about freedom of navigation, this is about international waterways, this is about free flow of commerce—all the things that happened before and only Iran is contesting. So right now the ceasefire certainly holds, but we're going to be watching very, very closely. Absolutely. James, go ahead. Mr. Secretary, thank you. General, thank you. I want to first express my gratitude and admiration for the work you do and for everyone involved in our armed forces and also for the accomplishments of Operation EPIC FURY, which I think are too often dismissed too lightly. But those accomplishments don't obscure, I think, a central default that has occurred here, and I would like you both to address it. On the first day of this conflict, President Trump addressed the Iranian people directly and said, "When we're finished, take over your government, it'll be yours to take." And then on the seventh day of the conflict, in a Truth Social post, the President said, quote, "There will be no deal with Iran except," all caps, exclamation mark, "UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER." What happens to that pledge to the Iranians? And when did the President decide to capitulate on his demand for unconditional surrender? Well, James, I wouldn't—you started out nicely, but you ended exactly where we knew you would end. The President hasn't capitulated on anything. He holds the cards, we maintain the upper hand, and Project Freedom only strengthens that hand. And so he will ensure that whatever deal is made or whatever end state is reached ensures that Iran never has a nuclear weapon, which is A number one and he's been focused on that, and the deal and discussions are centered on that. And what the Iranian people take advantage of after the fact is up to them. And he's been very clear about that. And maybe you do it now, maybe it happens later. But ultimately he's also been clear we're not going to entangle this into some nation-building project. Our objectives are clear; they've been pursued from day one. Hopefully the Iranian people take advantage of that because they're being taken advantage of by this regime. As you know, 45,000 Iranians—innocent Iranians—were killed before the outset of this. That's what this government does, kills their own innocent civilians. Getting out from underneath that is going to be a challenge for the Iranian people and we certainly hope they take advantage of that. Thank you. Thank you. Actually, right there. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Can you share any information regarding the South Korean ship that was reportedly hit by Iran? Is Seoul in contact with the U.S. military about it, and is there any indication that they would heed Trump's call made on social media to join Project Freedom? We hope so. We're in contact with that ship—CENTCOM I should say—and the maritime coordination forces are in touch. And I think targeting like that is a reflection of the indiscriminate nature of what Iran is doing. We hope South Korea would step up just like we hope Japan would step up, just like we hope Australia would step up, just like we hope Europe steps up. But we're not waiting for them to do so; we're looking to set the conditions to hand to them. But the President was clear in his Truth message that, "Hey, this is your ship, you should take part in helping to defend it." And we very much hope they do. Go ahead. Mr. Secretary, in terms of new U.S. intelligence reports that suggest that the timeline for Iran to get a nuclear weapon has not changed since last summer, still at 9 to 12 months. Is that intelligence wrong? How can this be after so much bombing? And for General Caine, going back on what you're talking about in the Strait of Hormuz: the President recently said the blockade is even more effective than bombing. So why didn't we start the campaign with the blockade? And initially, how many planned ships do you plan to escort or take through commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz today or in the coming days? Well, we don't discuss the specifics about intel and anybody that does shouldn't be, and I can't confirm or deny whether that is indeed correct speculation as far as I'm concerned coming from you. But ultimately what Operation MIDNIGHT HAMMER did in the obliteration of those facilities was set back their program. And then because of the efforts of that 12-day war, the President was able to recognize that this threat—while they continued to seek nuclear capabilities, their will was still there to seek a nuclear bomb. Ultimately they were creating a conventional umbrella of so many missiles and so many capabilities that no one would want to challenge them. Think of the North Korea—this is the North Korea strategy. We have so many conventional capabilities you won't do something about it, and underneath that we're going to develop more nuclear capabilities. So the will was still there. President Trump had the courage to say we have to dismantle and take on those conventional capabilities while the nuclear facilities are watched 24/7 and overwatched. And we will ensure one way or another—hopefully Iran chooses a deal—that they give up those ambitions, give up those capabilities, and we're able to confirm that every step of the way. So that has been President Trump's focus on it. He's set them back like no other president. He ripped up the terrible deal that would have given them a bomb. He took care of it in MIDNIGHT HAMMER, we're watching it right now, and ultimately feel good about the fact that the end state will ensure that Iran never has a nuclear weapon. Go ahead. Sir, I'll let CENTCOM talk to the number of ships they're going to take through because they're the nearest ones to talking to the commercial shippers and I don't want to get out in front of them. On the order and sequence around the military objectives, that's a decision the President and the Secretary gave us, and at the time we were focused on the primary objectives that were in front of us. Thank you. Liam Cosgrave with Zero Hedge. In recent weeks both Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Israel's head of Mossad have stated their intent not to cease combat with the Iranians. Netanyahu put this on Twitter. Israel's head of Mossad said Israel's goals are not finished until the Iranian government collapses. And so they're referring to the government that President Trump has brokered a ceasefire with and is working on a lasting peace in the region with. And so that's, you know, something many Americans, especially American farmers, are hoping he does get to a lasting peace. And so if President Trump is successful in that and the Israelis are explicitly stating their intent to continue fighting at a later day, how can you ensure America doesn't get roped back into a war if that comes? And secondly, with Netanyahu comfortable tweeting something like that out, and also Netanyahu has continued bombing Lebanon despite President Trump explicitly stating his intent for peace... I think your question is based on the false premise that somehow President Trump is being pulled in by Prime Minister Netanyahu to any of these actions. And just to be clear, President Trump has led at every step of this based on his view of American interest and America first. And we're grateful that the Israelis have been very capable partners at many steps of this and they may have some objectives at times that are slightly different than ours. But there's only one hand on the wheel ultimately directing this, whether it's Project Freedom or previously Operation EPIC FURY, and it's President Trump. So we're grateful for their input, their insight, the existential nature of the threat they face from an Iranian bomb, and the capabilities that they can bring to that, but ultimately the coordination will happen with the leadership of President Trump. Thank you very much, I appreciate it. Thank you.

BREAKING NEWS: Vivek Ramaswamy Speaks To Supporters After Winning Ohio GOP Gubernatorial Primary

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·15:39 ·6.7K views · 2026-05-06
Vivek Ramaswamy (Winner, Ohio GOP Gubernatorial Primary)
Vivek Ramaswamy has secured the Ohio Republican gubernatorial nomination, framing the upcoming general election as a choice between "celebrating success" and "socialist" policies while pledging to transform Ohio into the nation’s leading state for economic growth and education.
TL;DR Vivek Ramaswamy has secured the Ohio Republican gubernatorial nomination, framing the upcoming general election as a choice between "celebrating success" and "socialist" policies while pledging to transform Ohio into the nation’s leading state for economic growth and...

TL;DR

Vivek Ramaswamy has secured the Ohio Republican gubernatorial nomination, framing the upcoming general election as a choice between "celebrating success" and "socialist" policies while pledging to transform Ohio into the nation’s leading state for economic growth and education.

The General Election Matchup: Ramaswamy vs. Acton

  • Contrast in Philosophy: Ramaswamy characterized the race against Democrat Amy Acton as a choice between two diametrically opposed visions for Ohio.
      • Celebrating Success: Ramaswamy positioned himself as a "pro-free enterprise, pro-liberty conservative" who views individual and economic success as a state asset.
      • Critique of Opponent: He labeled Acton a "socialist" who "villainizes success" and highlighted her background as a billionaire critic despite his own "heir to nothing" upbringing.
  • Unified GOP Front: Ramaswamy emphasized that the Republican party is entering the general election cycle more united than ever, supported by endorsements from President Donald Trump and current Governor Mike DeWine.
  • Running Mate Selection: He officially introduced Ohio Senate President Rob McColley as his Lieutenant Governor nominee, citing their complementary skills as essential for leading the state.

Economic Prosperity and Fiscal Reform

  • Tax Reduction: Ramaswamy proposed a path toward a zero-income tax state to compete with high-growth states like Texas, Florida, and Tennessee.
      • He pledged to reduce property taxes, asserting that land belongs to the citizens, not the government.
  • Energy and Housing: A core pillar of the platform includes lowering electric bills by increasing in-state energy production and reducing "red tape" to lower housing prices and encourage homeownership for the next generation.
  • Government Efficiency:
      • Medicaid Reform: Plans to "wring out the waste, fraud, and abuse" from the Medicaid system to reduce health insurance premiums.
      • Regulatory Cuts: A commitment to making Ohio the most "pro-capitalist" state by fostering an environment where businesses no longer flee to other regions.

Education and the "American Dream"

  • Educational Standards: Ramaswamy advocated for a "world-class education" focused on math, reading, and writing rather than "victimhood psychology" or "indoctrination."
  • Personal Narrative: He cited his own life—as the son of immigrants who attended public schools and built successful businesses—as proof that the American Dream is still achievable in Ohio.
  • National Ambition: The goal is for Ohio to lead the nation in education and family-centric policies, moving beyond being "the best in the Midwest" to becoming the "national standard."

Critique of Pandemic Management and Leadership

  • The Lockdown Record: Ramaswamy leveled a "damning indictment" against Amy Acton’s tenure as Health Director, specifically citing the two-year closure of public schools.
      • He highlighted the disparity between public school closures and private schools (like his alma mater, Saint X), which remained open.
  • Scientific Accountability: He criticized Acton for failing to distinguish between high-risk seniors and low-risk students, and for ignoring natural immunity.
  • Leadership Fitness:
      • Responsibility: Ramaswamy argued that Acton’s decision to "quit" when challenged by legislators proved she was unfit for the "hard work" of leadership.
      • Pledge of Persistence: He vowed to "never quit on Ohio" and to make decisions based on nuanced data rather than "lazy" or "scientific authority" signaling.

Historical Context and Path to November

  • Heritage of Innovation: Ramaswamy invoked Ohio’s history as a leader in the industrial revolution, citing innovators like Thomas Edison and the Wright Brothers to inspire a "new dawn" for the state.
  • Voter Mobilization: He issued a "1-to-10" challenge to supporters, asking each voter to bring nine others to the polls in November, specifically targeting Independents, Libertarians, and "orphaned Reagan Democrats."
"I will remind you that I was not born a billionaire, I was not born a millionaire... I was born an heir to nothing. But my story is possible in this great country and in our great state because we embrace free enterprise." — Vivek Ramaswamy
"By the time we’re done, maybe even by the end of next year, it will be Ohio that sets the national standard that Texas and Tennessee and Florida look up to rather than the other way around." — Vivek Ramaswamy
This is how legends are made. This is how legends are made. Well, thank you guys. It is an honor and I am proud to officially say that today it is our pleasure to become the Republican nominee for the Governor of Ohio. So thank you guys for getting us to this point. Now, the real destination is in November, but I do want to take a moment to thank the people who got us to where we are, including the folks in this room who have day in and day out worked the phones, who have knocked doors, who have put up signs, and who have shown up with us in all of the 88 counties. I want to say thank you to our grassroots volunteers who made this possible. I want to say thank you to our US President, President Trump, for his endorsement in this campaign since day one. I want to thank the current Governor of Ohio, Mike DeWine, for his endorsement as well. I want to thank our current Senate President, Rob McColley, for accepting my invitation to be the next Lieutenant Governor. You're going to do a great job, Rob. And Denise, thank you for... we had some conversations at the very end. I remember around my kitchen table with Rob and you and he were talking, and there was no decision for Rob without making sure that it was right for the family. So thank you, Denise, as well for entrusting us with this journey. I believe we have a historic opportunity to lead Ohio not just to be the best state in the Midwest, because we are already the best in the Midwest. We have a historic opportunity to lead Ohio to be the top state in the country to raise a young family, to give our kids a world-class education, and to be the state where we revive this quaint idea that we call the American dream. And tonight, that is very personal to me. In any other country on planet Earth, I would not be standing here tonight.
