  • The "Human Touch" Philosophy:
      • Instead of hiding, the team called every single merchant to explain the situation personally and listen to their frustrations.
      • Trust over Efficiency: Razorpay avoids using AI to replace customer support. They believe that in B2B financial services, the primary role of support is not just solving a ticket, but establishing human trust during a crisis.
      • Impact: Many merchants who "abused" the team during that shutdown remain Razorpay customers today because of the transparency shown during the crisis.

Strategic Bets and Capital Efficiency

  • Betting on UPI: In 2016, most large banks and payment gateways were skeptical of UPI. Razorpay bet early, becoming the first gateway to go live on the system.
      • When demonetization hit and the market shifted to UPI, Razorpay was the only player ready. This allowed them to land major clients like Zomato and Swiggy in just two weeks.
  • Logical B2B Growth: Razorpay maintained extreme capital efficiency, even putting their $11M Series A into fixed deposits because their burn was so low.
      • Mathur argues that B2B is inherently logical: if you add value, the business pays. Burning money to "acquire" B2B customers is ineffective if the core product value isn't there.

Scaling and "Founder Mode"

  • The Manager Mode Trap: Mathur admits to a period where he hired great executives and stepped back into "manager mode," which he now views as a mistake.
  • Returning to the Weeds: He emphasizes that while delegating execution is necessary, a founder must never delegate the core vision or product direction. No leader will care about the company as much as the founder.
  • The AI Shift: Mathur is currently using AI to reinvent Razorpay’s platform from scratch to avoid "incumbent fallacy."
      • Execution as the Only Moat: As AI makes the "build" process faster and cheaper, the only remaining moats are deciding *what* to build and how fast a team can execute.
"When a digital system is harder than a physical system, it does the reverse of democratization; it creates silos. And that felt like a completely incorrect, wrong thing for us." — Harshil Mathur
"The regulation can seem unfair to you at times but the best part is it's actually very fair. Every company, no matter how big or small they are, they have to clear the same lot of guidelines... that itself creates a very deep moat." — Harshil Mathur
"In customer support, in our case, support is not about solving the problem; it is the channel to establish trust. We’re using AI in everything except replacing customer support." — Harshil Mathur
"The only way to survive is to figure out what the market is going to be and move today. Any company who takes the call that 'we're going to respond to the market' is already dead." — Harshil Mathur
B2B is a business of trust. And the trust, at the end of the day, nothing replaces the human touchpoint of trust. Right? You pick up the phone, you call. Even today, just having somebody, a human on the other side saying that yes, I am with you, I'm managing it, gives a lot of trust. It's so exciting to sit here and chat with Harshil. I've followed along and been a fan for a long time. Now, to give some context, because I would say that Razorpay really needs no introduction, but just to set some context: Razorpay went through YC in the winter of 2015. It was the first Indian company that Y Combinator ever invested in. From then till now, it's the largest payments platform in India, and it's grown at breakneck pace. And what I think is really interesting about the story is I'd love to learn more about sort of the early struggles. You know, many of the folks in the audience are probably kind of where you were in the beginning, right? And there's a lot of lessons and interesting parts of your journey that I think everyone could benefit from. So maybe what we do is rewind the clock a little bit. Kind of growing up and in your undergraduate studies, was it always the plan for you to start a company? Like, what was your mindset at the time? Yeah, no, definitely not. So I think I was a techie by background. Loved to code during college, from school days, loved to code, loved to build stuff. And when I was graduating college, even before I started Razorpay, I had no idea of finance or financial systems and so on. I just loved to code. So getting into Razorpay and getting into building payments in a regulated space was definitely not on the cards completely. But when I graduated from college, I went into a job. I got placed into an oil company, like you get into IIT placements, and went to work in an oil company in the Middle East. And I never really enjoyed it. Oil was not really my calling. So I used to spend my weekends and evenings—unlike a lot of people, I didn't really have a lot of life outside work—so I used to spend those days, evenings and weekends coding and building stuff. And for one of those things, I had to accept payments. I was building a social crowdfunding platform, mostly like a side project, and wanted to accept payments for it. So I went through the standard channels, standard approach, approached banks and all of that, and realized that it was fairly complicated to accept payments in India. First, it was very hard to just get started. And even if you get started, the experience was very poor because it was not built for techies to start accepting payments; it was built for large companies to start accepting payments. And that seemed like a typical techie mentality of "how hard could this be." So I thought that maybe it's easier and, in hindsight, it was a very dumb question. But now I can say that at that point it felt like this seems like just a simple problem, why is nobody solving it? And I started spending my time figuring out what other people are doing. Facebook was very popular then. So I would go to Bangalore startups, Pune startups, Facebook groups, and see what other people are doing. And I saw almost everyone had the same problem when they were starting up, that payments is extremely hard in India. In fact, it was easier to accept cash in India than to accept digital payments. Which, as a techie, made no sense to me. Like the point of technology, the point of internet, everything has been democratization. Right? So social media gives everyone a voice, internet gives everyone a channel, e-commerce gives everyone a selling point. It democratizes. Right? So when a digital system is harder than a physical system, it does the reverse of democratization; it creates silos. And that felt like a completely incorrect, wrong thing for us. So I started spending a lot of time figuring that out and that's how I got into payments. But as I say now, that it was probably a very hard business to get into, and if I knew what I knew today, maybe I wouldn't have started. Yeah, I think this is actually an interesting pattern for B2B companies because I think I'd love to understand—because it wasn't what I find really interesting is that it wasn't your actual idea to begin with. And so what kinds of, you know, as you sort of developed that first version of the product, was there a moment when you said, "look, this is clearly not going to work and maybe I should just go build something else"? Or did you, as you said, it sort of naturally led to some insight? How deep did you have to get into the weeds in order to get those insights? Yeah, so we were very clear we wanted to build online payments. But we had to choose a GTM strategy. So at that point of time, we thought we'll start by selling to educational institutes and payments for education, because it seemed e-commerce was a small and harder market to get into. We thought education seems large because there's a lot of fees payments in India. Most of those fees payments were done through physical channels or non-digital channels. We thought if we can convert the fees payments in India into digital, it could be a very large opportunity. And it sounds logically right. Like massive amount of fees is collected by schools and universities and if we can digitize that and make some small percentage on that, it could be a really good opportunity. So that's how we created a plan. In fact, we applied to YC by saying that we are going to go into education institutes. What happened is once we got into YC, we started selling to education institutes and I would go—I remember like I was in Jaipur and I would go into small universities and so on and try to convince them that "hey, why don't you accept your fees digitally." And the guy would look at me and say, "if they pay digitally, they're going to pay you 1% extra?" I said yes. So he said, then why don't I charge just 1% more fees? Right? And that's when you realize that your customer doesn't really care about digital collections. Like an education institute didn't really care. They knew that if they want, whatever way they charge the fees, the guy is going to come and pay it. Because education in India is that kind of a business. If you're in a college and the guy says "I don't collect digitally, I'll only collect cash," you will go and give cash. He says "I want to collect check, you have to go to this counter and pay," you will go to that counter and pay. So nobody really cared about smooth, easy collections in education. So on the parallel we started selling to startups because we were working in a co-working setup and a lot of startups were our friends who wanted digital payments. So they said "why don't you give it to us" and we started getting traction there. So we quickly pivoted our way and said that this is a sector that is going, even though it's small, it's growing for us. This is a sector that's definitely not working for us. So we completely pivoted away into startups and I think it was a really good decision in hindsight. Interesting. I love how because you were kind of in the weeds with the customers and in front of them and like watching them and understanding their problem, you kind of got those insights. I'm curious sort of as you look at the fintech space—and you mentioned that if you knew how complicated or difficult the problem was, maybe it would have been a different way that you would have started. But as you look at regulated industries, as you started to continue to build the product, was there a moment when you said, "actually this is going to give me more energy" or "this is actually the hardest problem that gives you more energy or perhaps more moat over time"? Yeah, so let me talk about how our journey started. So we decided to start building into payments. We applied to YC, we got into YC. We spent the three months of YC without doing a single live transaction because we didn't get an approval from the partners. So we got an in-principle approval before YC. It took us a year after that to do our first live transaction. Now, very few businesses in the tech world have that kind of a gestation period. Most tech businesses you can start out of your bedroom, start selling, you can build an e-commerce store, start selling, and typically YC also recommends: go and start selling first. We were in a business where we couldn't start selling. We needed to wait for approvals, we needed to wait for things. Even after getting into YC, it took us almost a year to do our first live transaction because that's how complicated regulated business is. We had to wait for a license; we first had to get a lot of certifications, approvals, then the final approval, then we could do the first live transaction. So while all of that sounds fairly complex, and our teams used to discuss that "hey, it's taking us a year to do our first live transaction, like how do we have that patience." One thing that I kept telling us is that yes, there are a lot of hurdles, but everyone coming after us will also face the same hurdles. So the hurdles become a moat over time because unlike most spaces in India which get funded, we are still in a space where there are still much lesser number of companies in competition. Because it's not very easy to just build a payment gateway, get an approval, and start it. You don't have 100 companies coming up in our space in a month because it is not easy to build in that. So the regulation can seem unfair to you at times, but the best part is it's actually very fair. No company, no matter how big or small they are, they have to clear the same lot of guidelines, same set of rules, and follow the same one-year process that we followed to get an approval and go live. And very few companies will have the patience or the energy to go through that effort. So that itself creates a very deep moat that it's not easy to get into that space. So I'll say regulated businesses are hard in the sense that you have to—there are certain set of rules you have to comply with—but it also allows you to build a very deep moat once you comply with them because everyone coming after you also has to do the same job and that allows you to create a lot of differentiation on top. Yeah, and is that what gave you ultimately the conviction? Because I think these types of cold-start problems are very, very difficult. And you had to wait a year, you had to go well beyond YC to sort of land that first, solve that first cold-start problem. Was there a moment when you said actually "you know this is going to take too long" or did you get conviction as you were building? No, I think it's easy to say today that yeah we had conviction; it wasn't really the case. Like every single month I would sit with my co-founder and say, "why are we building in this space? Like why don't we do something else?" Like, there were companies getting funded in e-commerce, companies getting funded in food delivery and so many spaces, and we said why don't we just do that? Like, we are techies at the end of the day, we can spend our time coding something else, we can spend our time building something else. But we connected with the problem so much because we spoke to so many founders, so many people who could be our customers, and I think that gave the highest form of energy. Every single customer we started meeting, everyone said, "yes I have a problem, yes nobody's solving it." And that gave us the conviction that yeah it is hard, but if it is hard to sell to customers it's a problem. If it's hard for other reasons, it's not really a problem; it's actually a moat. Because if it's hard for other reasons, then nobody else will get into that problem. And if your customers really care about it once you build it for them, they'll really love you. So once we started getting customers, when we were actually able to start onboarding customers, and customers loved that—hey, finally you are there, finally there's something who solves for us the way we wanted to be solved. And I think that really created a deep moat and we had a lot of loss of conviction through this process. The only thing that kept us going is like there were enough customers. As YC says, "make something people want." So as long as there were enough number of people who wanted what we were building, I think we were in a good shape. Yeah, it's interesting. It's a very common theme among really, really generational founders and building generational companies is that ultimately the customer kind of gives you energy, right? And that's really what you focus on. Not investors, not sort of regulatory bodies, but really the customers that give you that energy. One thing that is often quoted is that points of crisis or when you kind of have these near-death experiences, you can either survive them or you can actually get out of them stronger. And in our conversations, there's some near-death moments or crisis moments. Would love to hear a moment when you thought maybe things were going to end and how you kind of tackled it and how the company got stronger because of it. Yeah, it keeps happening in a business we are in. I can tell you one of the early days, right? We got a bank approval, we got a license—I was telling you throughout the YC time we were not live. Just a week before demo day, we got our final approval and we actually went live and launched. In fact, before demo day, we were considering that YC has an option that you can delay and do the next demo day. So we were discussing not to launch on demo day because we were not ready. But we got an approval a week before demo day, we did the TechCrunch launch, we went live, all great, we did the demo day, a lot of investors were interested. About two weeks after demo day, the bank that had enabled us pulled the plug. They said like there were some issues, one customer did a complaint, and they said we can't support you anymore and they completely stopped our platform. We had at least 50 customers live, merchants live, who were using us for payments and one day all of them were shut because the bank pulled the plug. Now this is like the hardest place you can be in a payments business because your customers want to collect money. Somebody has finally agreed that they want to use a small payment gateway like you to accept their money and now you have to tell them that, "hey your business is shut because our bank pulled the plug." And I think that was probably the hardest time of the company because a lot of us were in the room, we tried begging and pleading the bank that "hey, give us some more time" and they said "no we can't" and it's done. And they didn't care. So I think we had to take some very—we had to discuss what we do because in a payments business trust and reliability is so critical. Once these guys go out and say, "hey Razorpay duped us" or whatever, then our business is impacted because of them. And we had just done a YC launch. Like it could be its own set of things and then we'll never be able to establish trust. I think the one fundamental principle we set is that we'll call every single customer and tell what is happening, exactly why it's happening, and what we are doing about it. I think a lot of times when these crises happen, people stop picking up calls, people stop responding because you don't have much to say. Like what do you tell your customers? They're just gonna pick up the phone and scold you. We said we'll get all the scolding, we'll hear all the abuses, but we'll never stop picking the phone, we'll never stop calling these people and telling what's happening. So we had six or seven of us sat in a room and started calling each and every customer explaining what's happening. A lot of them understood, a lot of them didn't understand, a lot of them abused us. And I can tell you like when Indians abuse, they abuse a lot. So there were like full-on Hindi abuses that our team had to—and me, I personally had to—listen to. But we said it's okay, we'll listen through it, we'll hear them out, we'll let them vent their frustration, we'll tell them what's happening. And we kept doing that. And it took us about four or five days before which we could get another partner and we took it live. And then we were able to get a lot of these merchants live. I think even some of those merchants who even abused us are still live with Razorpay even today. Because at the end of the day, like B2B is—unlike B2C—B2B is a business of trust. Right? That's and particularly in financial institutions it's a business of trust. And the trust at the end of the day, nothing replaces the human touchpoint of trust. Right? You pick up the phone, you call. Even today, like when customers raise a support query or questions, we have a rule that if it crosses two or three or four exchanges, you pick up the phone and call the customer. Because in B2B, just having somebody, a human on the other side saying that yes I am with you and I'm managing it, gives a lot of trust. Even though the efficiency might be weaker, the trust coming out of that is stronger. So for example, a lot of companies are using AI to replace support today and we've taken a call that we are using AI in everything except replacing customer support. Because in our case, customer support is not about solving the problem; it is the channel to establish trust that hey, we are here, there's somebody as a human spending his time on solving a problem and we can't take that away. So I think those fundamentals got set for us because of that crisis because we learned that even when we had the worst of worst happening to us, just because we were picking the phone and saying that "hey I'm on top of it and I'm spending my time just figuring this out" gave a lot of trust to these people. That's dedication. That's very inspiring. I think that's a hugely important lesson for building any company but certainly very much for a trust-based company. One of the interesting things about our conversation was this notion of conviction that you guys had very early on. And you know this was a time—and I think folks probably realize, maybe some of the folks here are a little bit too young to remember—you know the UPI systems wasn't there, demonetization has not happened yet. And so early days there are many competitors, particularly competitors from overseas, competitors locally. And you know they would say things like, "oh you know we're in this space already, you don't know anything, you don't have the background, we're going to crush you, how about we just buy you?" Or you know, and they're certainly motivated by some amount of fear associated with that, right? So what was your mindset at the time? Like what gave you conviction to keep going when in some of those cases I think some founders probably would have said, "well you know maybe we pack it up and join one of these companies" or "maybe we're not going to win." Yeah, and I think that that happened very early. Like in our space it actually happened very early. When we were in YC, even before we launched, we started getting acquisition offers from some of the largest payment companies in the world. They started saying that "hey, because of YC we got noticed." So some of the large payment companies or founders of them reached out and said, "why don't you join us, why don't you build India for us" and so on. I think some of those were actually exciting because the companies were really good. I think what kept us going is a) India's very, very hard and very different. So when we met a lot of these companies, global companies, I felt they didn't understand how complex India is. And the worry from that perspective was not what will happen today, but our worry was two years, three years out, the amount of investment India needs. So India is a massive opportunity today, but in 2014, 2015, if you looked at India, the payment volume was 60 billion dollars. Right? The total GMV. That's what I put in my business plan in my YC application; I put the payments market of India is 60 billion dollars. Today Razorpay alone does 180 billion dollars. Right? So that is the way the market has grown in the last 10 years. Right? So it's so hard to plan for India. Now if I told a company that "hey, for a 60 billion dollar market we need 500 million dollars to build," it's going to be very hard to convince them. But if somebody understood that its 60 billion dollar market is going to scale up massively and there is a lot of opportunity that you're going to have. Even today I feel 180 is very small. India is going to be a trillion-plus market at least in payments. But it's very hard to explain that to a global player who's not seen that because most other markets in the world are not growing at that pace. So I think for us it was very clear that if we have to solve India, we have to think of a very long-term perspective. And if we were ever to consider that we need a partner, it would have to be someone who's going to be able to understand that that is a long-term play of India, and we didn't find that. So I think for us the answer became very clear that unless we feel that what we want to achieve we can achieve faster with somebody else, there's no reason to work with somebody else. And we never found that. Yeah, your insights were pretty unique in that regard, I think. So it did work and it did start to take off and you know there was a period I think in sort of 2017 to 2020 even before the pandemic where you guys had grown some insane multiple, like 40x. What was amazing about that wasn't just the growth; it was that you guys did it with record capital efficiency. What was your mindset in that? Because you know I think we get a lot of questions obviously about "how much money do I need to raise, I need to do this thing and therefore I need to raise a lot of capital to do it." But you guys were able to grow at a breakneck pace but stay very, very capital efficient and very lean. What was that mindset? Yeah it is, in fact, it's again a great thing in hindsight. But if you know the 2015 to 2020 time period, it was not really considered a good thing for a company to burn less money. Like there was a time when investors would expect you to burn a lot of money. So when we raised our Series A funding, we raised like about 9-11 million dollars. We didn't really have that much burn. Our burn was less than 200,000 dollars a month. So we took that money and we put it in bank FDs, fixed deposits in India. The interesting part was the interest on the deposits was more than our burn, so we became profitable. And our investor was very unhappy with that because he was like, "we have given you this money to burn and grow, why are you making profits out of it?" And we had to explain that because they were investing in a lot of other consumer companies which were burning massive amount of money and here we were with 11 million dollars and we are growing their money just because of interest rates. And that made them very unhappy. So it was a very hard conversation in hindsight. But I think for us it was very clear we are in a B2B business. In my belief, because I'm a B2B founder, I find it very logical, because in B2C you have to first get the customer, spend a lot of money to grow the customer, spend a lot of money to build engagement, and then at some point through some other channel you monetize that customer. In B2B it's very simple. You add value to a business; the business pays you for it. It's a simple business where you go to a business and you say "hey I'm adding this value to you, this is what I need to charge for it." Either the business likes it or dislikes it, but that's how it is. There's no point in burning money for it; there's no point in spending money to engage and all of that, because the business is going to be logical. He uses you today because you add value; if you tomorrow stop adding value, he uses somebody else. There are moats and there is depth in product but at the end of the day it's about the value you add to their life. So I think for us it was very clear. We told the investors that like I can burn a lot more money and grow a lot faster, it's not going to do anything because it's not a business where I can have that and I can say "hey tomorrow this is going to be worth this much value." It has as much value as the value I am adding to him today. If I stop adding that value, he's going to move out tomorrow. And that makes the business far more logical but also there's a lot of intensity to that business. Because every day you have to fight for your share. The business decision maker on the other side is every day evaluating: are you still adding more value than you're charging me for? Because if you're not, then he's going to find something else. And it is easy to say but it's harder to constantly compound, especially as an org gets larger. So to give an example like you said, UPI was launched in 2016. April 2016 actually—we just celebrated 10 years of UPI. April 2016 is when UPI was launched. There were a lot of payment gateways at that point of time and I don't know if people here would remember but in 2016 when UPI was launched there were a lot of skeptics. A lot of people didn't believe that UPI will take off. The two largest banks of the country hadn't integrated on UPI till demonetization happened, till November 2016. So most payment gateways in India actually didn't integrate UPI at all because they felt with the—without the two largest banks of the country being on UPI, who will use UPI? Right? So and that's where the value of being small is so high because we had nothing to lose. We had 10,000 merchants, we were very small, we had launched in 2015, this is 2016. In September or October 2016, we became the first payment gateway in the country to go live on UPI much before the largest banks of the country had gone live. And then November is when the largest banks went live and then demonetization happened. And no other payment gateway was ready to support UPI at the scale at which we were. So all the largest companies at that point of time—Zomato, Swiggy, BookMyShow—all of them went live on Razorpay in a period of a couple of weeks because suddenly everyone wanted UPI and we were the only payment gateway offering that. Now that's the advantage of being a small player, that you can make these bets early on. We discussed with our team: the worst that will happen is that UPI doesn't take off, it's fine, we are still fighting the battle anyway. But if it does take off, it creates a unique wedge for us and we can go out. So that early bet played out in a massive way for us because it took six months for any other payment gateway to go live on UPI after we went live, and in that six months we were able to enter spaces that we couldn't have entered if there was no differentiator for us to play on. And that is how it come out for us. That's such a classic, I think, lesson. I mean we talk a lot about you know being able to be faster, right? Your rate of building product, your rate of learning, your ability to adapt to markets. And those advantages accrue to startups. This is such a classic story and such an amazing indication that that works. I'm curious you know as we look at the age of AI, there's obviously multiple things that it's fundamentally shifted our thinking on product, on how we run companies, right? Both of those things. And I'm curious how you think about the product and I think you guys recently launched core AI functionality layering in the fundamental sort of AI backbone for running a business. How do you guys think about integrating AI into the product? Yeah, I think AI is such a fundamental shift so first we have to calibrate ourselves a lot, right? So I think it starts at the top. I am spending so much time on AI; I am addicted to Claude Code so much right now. As I was meeting the Anthropic team yesterday and saying that you need to start having de-addiction programs because I'm personally spending so much time on Claude Code, on Open Claude, and so on. It's so much fun and exciting and it allows—I think as most founders like me start building companies because we love building, we love coding, but over time the layers get built in, the company gets large and you end up becoming a people manager. And honestly no founder gets into building a startup to become a people manager. So I think AI is such an amazing tool because it allows you to get back to building in some shape and form and I think I personally have been loving that. But the advantage of that is that when you spend your time doing that, you understand the power of the tool, you understand the power the technology brings in, and that is allowing us to make some very fundamental decisions. So like a couple of months back we just sat down and said, "if I were to start Razorpay today, how would I build it?" And we just started putting down on the drawing board how would our integration look like, how would our onboarding look like, how would our support look like, how would our platform look like, how would people interact with us, how would people use us. And we just put it all down on a paper and we said "okay like let's make that happen." Because that's the incumbent fallacy, right? Like you start believing that hey we have everything, we are well set, and I mean Razorpay disrupted a lot of incumbents to get where we are. And it is because of the like UPI example was the same reason. Everybody felt that "we are the largest payment player, we are doing all the cards and everything, if something changes we'll respond to it." But the market changes faster than you can respond. So if you're going to respond when the market is starting to change, you're already too late. So we said that we're going to do the same thing with AI and we're not going to act like an incumbent; we're going to act like a startup. We're going to decide what changes are happening and we are going to bring these changes before a new startup brings those in. So we completely reinvented the platform end-to-end; we launched it a couple of months back and the traction has been massive because fundamentally it allows you—and some of these are not perfect yet because AI is still evolving, the tech is there but not fully there, but you have to go with that belief that it's going to get there. It's improving every day and so we had to spend a lot of time building this, rewiring our teams, rewiring. And it's not easy because when you have large pools of capital sitting, large pools of teams sitting doing something that they're doing every day, asking them that "hey we'll hurt ourselves in short-term but we're going to pivot ourselves completely to what is going to make sense long-term," it is hard. And every team has that incumbent fallacy. But it's the only way to survive in the world that we are heading towards. AI is going to bring down time to build so rapidly that the only differentiation is going to be how fast can you move, how fast can you really decide what to build because the build itself is a very short process. I think that's the only moat. I think we've seen that tech itself was the only moat in tech—it became how fast can you execute because a lot of other things came down. The capital moat came down, the infrastructure moat came down. I think AI is going to bring down the building moat as well because building itself is going to be very easy. The only thing you have to spend time on is what to build, how to build, how should it look like, but just the build itself is going to compress massively and every single company will have to go through that transformation. Now, some of them are going to take that time and say "hey I'll respond to the market." I think any company who takes that call that we're going to respond to the market is already dead. The only way to survive is to figure out what the market is going to be and move today, and that's the call we had to take. This is true founder mode right here. That's amazing. As as you look at your journey, you've grown from a few people maybe in an apartment in Jaipur when you started to now sort of one of the generational companies. How have you think you've changed? As you scale, the business changes, the organization changes, but one of the things that we often hear is that founders that choose to build and have this intensity, they change; they become a better version of themselves or a more formidable version of themselves. As you reflect on your journey, what has changed about you? Yeah, no, I think there are a lot of learnings in the 10 years and it's not that we did everything right. I did a lot of mistakes. Let me speak about some of the mistakes like the "founder mode" that you spoke about. I loved that essay when that came out because we went through that same journey. What happens as a founder is you typically start a company, you are into it every day, and the first two-three years you're ramping up, you're figuring out, you're in the trenches. Then the company starts taking off, then you start hiring a bunch of executives, you start hiring a lot of leaders and you start feeling that "hey now I have good leaders that can take care of what I used to do and I don't need to spend my time on that, I can just manage the leaders." That's what the essay says—manager mode—and a lot of founders shift to manager mode. I myself shifted to manager mode for a couple of years where I said, "I have the best leaders, the leaders know what they are doing, why do I need to get into everything? Let me just manage the leaders." And that's the worst mistake you can do. Now there's a fine line between micromanagement and being in founder mode, right? So you don't want to be micromanaging everything, but the things that really matter to you, the founder has to get into it. For example, as a product company the product vision, the product direction, the way a founder is going to think about it no leader can ever think about it. I think and we had to—and I've spoken to a lot of founders who had to go through that journey where you start acting in manager mode, you start getting leaders, and we had some amazing leaders. But at the end of the day what differentiates a company is the founder's conviction on something that your day-to-day execution people can't take, and those decisions have to come from the founder. So I think the fundamental point is that nobody's going to care about your company as much as you do and that that is never going to change. It's not going to happen on day one, it's not going to happen on year 10, it's not going to happen on year 20. And I think every company, whether it's Facebook or whether it's any large company that's run by the founder, the founder slowly and quickly realizes that nobody, no good leader can care about your company as much as you do and I think we had to go through that journey. So I think that journey took almost 10 years for me to personally see that "hey I need to be into the core things that matter to the company" is something that I need to be there and I need to spend my time on it and everything else I can delegate. And I think that learning took a lot of time. One last quick question for me. So as as we look at the over 2,000 people that are here, that maybe reflect where you were when you started, what advice would you have for folks to take actionable steps to get building and to get started on a company? Yeah, I think the the core premise of entrepreneurship—AI is making a lot of stuff easier, so people think "maybe entrepreneurship will get easier." I don't think entrepreneurship is going to get easier because of AI. I think the fundamental tools required to build a company are getting easier. But the core aspect of entrepreneur is the ability to connect with the problem deeply and spend all of your time and effort in solving that problem. And as AI—my worry is that because it's easier to build, it's easier for people to latch onto problems that they don't really care about. Because it's easier to say "hey I can write code, I can build something and deploy this and I can start going live." I think what every founder really needs to think is: is this something that you can spend your next 10 years of your life building? Because that's what it's going to take. It can be easy to build and deploy a software on AI today, it can be easy to deploy a website on AI today, but is there a problem statement that you can spend your 10 years of your life building? Because that's what it's going to take if you're going to build a large successful company. AI is going to make the build process easier; the company building part is still going to take 10 years of your life and every day spending that hour. So you need to find a problem statement that you're closely connected with, not just because something is cool or because AI allowed me to build that code that white project. The only thing that's going to work is: is that a problem statement that you can spend all of your time and effort for the next 10 years solving for, because that's what it's gonna take at the end of the day. Inspiring. We're going to leave it there. Everyone give it up for Harshil Mathur from Razorpay. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you.

Greg Brockman’s “Rough Day” in Court, Amazon’s Hybrid AI Search, SpaceX’s $60B Cursor Deal

The Information(13.2K) ·43:54 ·1.7K views · 2026-05-06
Catherine Perloff (Amazon Reporter) Akash Pasricha (Host, TITV) Greg Brockman (President, OpenAI)
Amazon is testing a hybrid AI search experience to capture "top-of-funnel" discovery, while internal reports reveal that SpaceX and xAI’s "low-cost" data centers rely on improvised, accident-prone infrastructure with low compute efficiency.
TL;DR Amazon is testing a hybrid AI search experience to capture "top-of-funnel" discovery, while internal reports reveal that SpaceX and xAI’s "low-cost" data centers rely on improvised, accident-prone infrastructure with low compute efficiency. Hybrid Search Experience:...

TL;DR

Amazon is testing a hybrid AI search experience to capture "top-of-funnel" discovery, while internal reports reveal that SpaceX and xAI’s "low-cost" data centers rely on improvised, accident-prone infrastructure with low compute efficiency.

Amazon’s AI Search Pivot

  • Hybrid Search Experience: Amazon is exploring a search layout similar to Google’s "AI Overviews." This combines conversational summaries from its Rufus chatbot with traditional product listings.
      • Strategic Shift: The goal is to move "up the funnel" into product discovery, a role historically held by Google, rather than just being a destination for specific purchases.
  • Selective Implementation: Amanda Dorr (VP of Core Shopping) indicated that AI summaries won't be used for routine items like milk, but rather for complex research tasks like finding the best hiking boots.
  • Integrated Discovery: New features like "Buy For Me" allow Amazon to scrape the web for items not sold on their platform, positioning the site as a comprehensive first stop for internet shopping.

OpenAI Trial: The $30 Billion Testimony

  • Greg Brockman’s Stake: In the Elon Musk v. OpenAI trial, OpenAI President Greg Brockman confirmed his personal stake in the company is worth approximately $30 billion.
  • Unjust Enrichment Argument: Musk’s legal team is using Brockman’s diary entries—which mused about paths to becoming a billionaire—to argue that the company breached its charitable trust by prioritizing private benefit over its non-profit mission.
  • Defense Positioning: OpenAI’s lawyers argue the non-profit foundation remains well-funded and that Brockman’s equity was determined without his direct input.

Coinbase Layoffs and Crypto Trends

  • Workforce Reduction: Coinbase is cutting 14% of its staff (approx. 700 people) to manage costs.
  • The "AI" Justification: While CEO Brian Armstrong cited AI-driven efficiency gains, analysts suggest the cuts are a reaction to a "quiet bear market" where retail interest has stalled and revenue has dipped 20%.
  • Structural Overhaul: Coinbase is moving toward a "no pure manager" model where every leader must be an individual contributor (IC).
  • Venture Sentiment: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) raised a new $2 billion crypto fund—notably less than half the size of its previous $4.5 billion fund, signaling a more disciplined investment climate.

The Hidden Costs of xAI’s Data Centers

  • Aggressive Cost Cutting: SpaceX/xAI claims a construction cost of $2.7M per megawatt, significantly lower than the $12.3M industry average.
  • Improvised Infrastructure: Reporting reveals xAI uses "crafty" but temporary solutions that lead to reliability issues.
      • Power & Cooling: Reliance on mobile gas generators and semi-truck trailers for cooling has caused more frequent outages than standard data centers.
      • Low Utilization: Internal memos from SpaceX executive Michael Nichols describe xAI’s chip utilization metrics as "embarrassingly low."
  • Safety Concerns: Rapid buildouts have been linked to workplace accidents, including an amputated finger and incidents of electrocution and illness from generator fumes.

The Future of AI Coding

  • Consolidation: The potential SpaceX acquisition of Cursor suggests that model labs are increasingly looking to own the "interface" where code is written.
  • From "Vibe Coding" to "Agentic Engineering": Zach Lloyd (CEO of Warp) argues that the era of "vibe coding" (blindly trusting AI) is ending.
      • New Standard: Professional development is shifting toward "agentic engineering," which integrates AI agents with centralized governance, security audits, and human oversight.
  • Open Source Strategy: Warp recently open-sourced its front-end to build a neutral ecosystem, allowing developers to swap between models (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI) without being locked into a single provider.
"If you can go to Amazon to learn about products, you can spend more time on Amazon, maybe they can sell you more ads... it helps Amazon go up the funnel."

— Catherine Perloff, Amazon Reporter

"Musk's lawyer compared [OpenAI's defense] to a bank robber taking a million dollars from a bank but then saying, 'well, you have millions of dollars left.'"

— Rocket Drew, Reporter

"I don't think there's going to be a pullback to manually writing code by hand... but [vibe coding] is not a good strategy either. You're going to see a middle ground called agentic engineering."

— Zach Lloyd, CEO of Warp

Welcome everyone to The Information's TITV. My name is Akash Pasricha. It is Tuesday, May 5th. On today's show, The Information has exclusive reporting about some big product changes at Amazon. Our Amazon reporter will join the show to share with us what she knows. We'll then turn to our reporter at the courthouse, Rocket Drew, who is covering the Elon Musk OpenAI trial for a recap on Greg Brockman's testimony. We'll also unpack Coinbase's layoffs and we'll then get into our exclusive reporting on the hidden costs behind xAI's fast and cheap data center buildout. And we'll wrap with a look at AI coding, how startups are differentiating themselves in an increasingly competitive landscape. It's going to be a fun show, so let's get right on into it. Amazon is planning some big product changes for its core e-commerce website, some of which take a page from Google's playbook in search. My colleague Catherine Perloff wrote about that in a story out today. I want to bring her on to talk all about it. Catherine, welcome to the show. It's great to have you here. "Hi, Akash." "So what product changes is Amazon planning at their company?" "Well, it's, you know, it's a little, it's still hypothetical. But you know, I spoke to the VP of core shopping at Amazon, a key executive who kind of controls partly how the website looks. And one idea she said they've been sort of tossing around is creating sort of a more hybrid search experience. Up until now, there has been the Rufus chatbot, which Amazon formally introduced in 2024, where users could have kind of a more chatbot-like experience with their shopping for products. And then the more traditional search engine where, you know, a shopper could just put in terms and then they get sort of a list of items instead of a more conversational-like response. And what Amazon is thinking about is, could there be a more combined experience where there is a paragraph describing, you know, some of the product features, and then below that the result? So you know, this might remind you a little bit of Google AI Overviews, which they rolled out a few years ago, where they kind of provided this AI summary and then links to the site below that. This is just one experience Amazon might offer. It's not necessarily going to be that way all the time. You know, Amazon has a different business than Google where they're selling—they want people to buy things and not just people to buy ads. But yeah, it is interesting sort of how the Amazon website might evolve with AI." "So, so let me understand this. So when I go to search for products on Amazon, I know I have the search bar there and it takes me to all the results. And so is the idea here that what's under consideration is if I search for, you know, household items or shoes or whatever, then I will get an AI overview type of thing with links to results and then the listings?" "Yeah. So maybe there would be like a, you know, a summary. Say you're maybe you're searching like household products—this is an example I'm coming up with—like to get stains off the counter. And maybe then there would be a Rufus-generated summary of sort of like, you know, what are different chemicals or what the market looks like for these types of products. And then below that summary, there might be actual product listings for, you know, different types of wipes or counter cleaner sprays, etc. So you know, and that's different where basically kind of that summarized answer that was more like, you know, conversational, someone talking to you was sort of bifurcated in the Rufus chat window, which a shopper had to sort of toggle over and choose to use. And then there was a separate kind of main shopping experience. And this is sort of, you know, could maybe be opting in by default in certain instances, shoppers to a more conversational experience while not fully taking away the ability to browse specific items." "So I gotta be honest, like, I don't know if I'm gonna like this. I mean, you know, I kind of like when I search for the thing and I just want to see the options right away. Now they're introducing or possibly going to introduce this AI overview thing. I mean, why do you think they're doing this? And is the only reason because Google did it? I mean, we don't even know how successful the AI overview thing has been, right?" "Well, I think Amazon is aware, and you know, their business is different than Google. So you know, one thing Amanda Dorr, who I talked to mentioned, was like, for searching for something like..." "And her role, Amanda's role is what?" "VP of core shopping at Amazon." "Okay." "That you know, you, you know, when you're searching for milk, you don't really want a paragraph about like the difference between 2% and whole. You know the difference. So they wouldn't, you know, there's definitely a lot of types of searches where the kind of standard format where you just see a list of products is the best option. There are other types of searches where you're like, you know, what are the best hiking boots is an example she gave, for the, for the season. And in those cases, you know, maybe you want some more information about like what makes a good hiking boot. So I think Amazon is trying to figure out what are the types of products where more information is helpful. And I think there certainly are some of those types of products. Like I know when I'm looking for say like new earphones, I usually have to like go search on Wirecutter or some other site and try to figure out like what people like about, you know, AirPods. So I do think that there are like items where people do need more information and then there're items like milk where people really don't. And I think Amazon is trying to kind of thread that needle and figure out like..." "Like a little bit of like a Wirecutter-esque. Like where there are recommendations that can be useful, that's where it might lean into these types of searches and stuff like that." "Exactly, exactly. Where, and you know, it kind of helps Amazon in a way go up the funnel, which is kind of marketing jargon, but you know, kind of that's sort of been almost Google's historical role, helping you with the product discovery process and then you go to Amazon when you know what you want. But if you can go to Amazon to learn about products, you can spend more time on Amazon, maybe they can sell you more ads, it's probably lots of good things that can happen there for them, so." "Right, right. Now you mentioned your conversation with Amanda Dorr, the VP of core shopping, I think you said was her role. What questions did you come out of the conversation with in terms of how you think this could be rolled out or you know, the company's strategy at large?" "Yeah, I mean, I am still sort of yeah, curious sort of again, how they thread the needle. She also mentioned that like, and we've reported on this in the past, they've been tweaking the way that like the regular search engine works to sort of be more responsive for natural language. And like they've you know now I believe like 60% of searches use autocomplete and they've been trying to use LLMs to make autocomplete smarter. So it is sort of like where, like are the lines going to blur? So that is definitely a question I have, like you know, as regular search becomes more natural language-y, like you know, how does it become different from a chatbot? And maybe it's in the response, but then you know, does everyone start asking questions to the Amazon search bar? How does that sort of change the experience? Another you know question I have is like they have been really interested in this new Buy For Me feature, which is helping shoppers find items that are not on Amazon's own site, but they you know will scrape the web or use other features to find out about these items and then you can buy that item that isn't on Amazon's site. They'll direct you to the other site and you can you know find it and buy it there. And I'm also very curious like to what degree does Amazon want to be sort of like the first stop on the internet. Kind of again taking that role that Google's often had and being the place where you learn everything and you do product discovery even if that means buying a product that's not on Amazon and like how important that is for them and you know where that kind of puts them yeah in the whole e-commerce process." "Right. And last question for you Catherine, so we don't know exactly if or when they are planning to roll out this type of feature, we just know that they are considering it at this point, right?" "Yes, we don't know the exact timeline, though I will say you know yesterday actually there was some observers who saw that like when they use the regular Amazon search bar it would instead of just populating with a list of results, it would go into a more like chatbot conversational Rufus-like experience. So that was you know and and we asked Amazon about it, they're like you know we're testing things whatever. So I think it shows they're they're kind of already thinking of more hybrid modes, like you know people have been seeing them in the wild. You know so it seems like things are underway, an exact product roadmap we don't exactly know, but yes." "Right, great. Well Catherine, I want to thank you for coming on. That is Catherine Perloff, our Amazon reporter here at The Information." The Information's Rocket Drew is covering the Elon Musk OpenAI trial from the courtroom all week long. Here is his latest update for us on OpenAI president Greg Brockman's testimony in court on Monday. "Greg Brockman had a pretty rough day in court. A lawyer for Elon Musk had the chance to ask Brockman, who was a co-founder of OpenAI and its current president, some tough questions about his personal finances, including his stake in OpenAI itself but also his stakes in companies that have done business deals with OpenAI. That includes Stripe, Cerebras, Coreweave, and Helion Energy. As far as his stake in OpenAI, Musk's lawyer asked him right out of the gate and he confirmed that his personal stake in OpenAI is worth in the neighborhood of $30 billion. That puts Brockman in kind of an uncomfortable position in the lawsuit because Musk is arguing that Brockman has been unjustly enriched by his involvement with OpenAI and that OpenAI has breached a charitable trust. It's sort of broken some charitable obligations or commitments by operating more as a for-profit than as a nonprofit. In its founding documents, OpenAI says that it's not organized for the private benefit of any individual, which raises questions about Brockman's $30 billion stake. Now, in response, OpenAI's lawyers pointed out that most OpenAI employees do have equity, that Brockman's equity stake was decided without Brockman's input, and that OpenAI's charitable wing, the OpenAI Foundation, is very well funded. Now, Elon Musk's lawyer did not find that argument very compelling. He compared it to a bank robber taking a million dollars from a bank but then saying, well, you have millions of dollars left. So, Musk's lawyer in particular had a field day with records of an old diary that Brockman kept. And in this diary, Brockman is contemplating different paths forward for an OpenAI for-profit subsidiary, and he muses about which path, a path with Elon or without, will more quickly take him to a billion dollars. And in court, Brockman suggested that a million dollars would sort be sufficient for him. So Musk's lawyer countered, well, if a billion dollars is sufficient, why not take the difference, call it $29 billion, and return it to the nonprofit? That turned into a pretty uncomfortable line of questioning for Brockman. It didn't seem like he really had an answer to that prepared, but he did fall back on saying that the OpenAI nonprofit is very well-resourced. So it was a pretty tough day. I think both sides, you know, the OpenAI side and Elon's side, have had a pretty have come out unscathed by questioning from the other side's lawyers. So we'll see if OpenAI's lawyers questioning Brockman tomorrow are able to restore and rebuild his case and see if he comes out looking any better after that questioning. So that's what's up next in the Musk v. Altman trial." Coinbase said that it plans to cut 14% of its workforce in order to manage costs amid the market downturn and improve efficiency using AI. The announcement comes ahead of its first quarter earnings on Thursday. Our crypto reporter Yueqi Yang joins me now for her reaction. Yueqi, welcome back to the show. So, Coinbase is doing layoffs. We've seen layoffs across the crypto sector. What was your reaction to this one? "Yeah, so Coinbase today said they are laying off 14% of jobs and that's affecting about 700 people at the company. It is a big, sizeable layoff, but as a percentage of the total number of employees, it's actually smaller than the layoffs that Coinbase did in the prior bear market in 2022 and 2023. And at that time, Coinbase did two major rounds of layoffs. The first one was 18%, followed by another one that was 20%. And then when CEO Brian Armstrong announced the second layoff back then, he said that in hindsight, Coinbase should have cut further in the first round. So, we are waiting to see if this layoff is it for the rest of the year or if there might be even more layoffs coming out depending on market conditions." "Okay, so this is kind of interesting because we have these two crypto bear markets that have come sort of a couple I don't know, what, a couple years apart maybe. Was that bear market worse than this bear market?" "Yeah, that's a great question. I think that bear market in 2022 was characterized by dramatic blow-up of big companies like FTX and a series of crypto lending platforms. And I think in the current market, it's more like a quiet, down market. You don't see these dramatic collapses, but then at the same time, retail investors are just losing interest in crypto, and a big part of the crypto economy is down. When you look at the token prices for a lot of these tokens that are not Bitcoin, not Ethereum, but the smaller ones, they just really haven't recovered from even the last bear market. So I think the crypto industry is going through this fundamental change. It's unclear whether some of these tokens will ever make a comeback, and so that's also going to affect Coinbase's business model as well." "Well, and the reason I'm asking that question is because I think it really sets us up to answer the next question really well, which is whether or not these cuts are bear market cuts or if they are AI cuts, which is the reason that Brian Armstrong cited in his note to employees. I mean, we live in a world where CEOs can say whatever they want in terms of why they're making cuts, but what's your assessment as to whether or not this is actually a true AI-related cut or if it's just an excuse for the crypto bear market?" "It's a bear market cut. And let me show it." "Okay, alright, that's it. See you later." "The numbers show it because, in the last quarter, the fourth quarter of 2025, Coinbase's revenue was down about 20%. It posted a net loss of about $670 million. So Coinbase hasn't reported the first quarter results yet, as you mentioned, it's going to report earnings on Thursday. But some of its peers like Robinhood has already reported, and Robinhood said its crypto revenue was down by almost half in the first quarter. So we know that the numbers are not going to look good. And some of the other crypto exchanges like Gemini, like crypto.com, have already started layoffs. So the market condition is definitely the most important factor for the layoff that we are seeing here. AI is also an issue. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong is very vocal about encouraging employees to use AIs. In today's tweet announcement, he said that there will no longer be any pure manager, meaning that every leader in the company will also be an individual contributor. And he also envisioned this future where there will be one-person teams at Coinbase where people will act multiple functions, including being an engineer, being a designer, being a product person, with the help of AI." "Do you have any sense from people at the company, around the company, whether or not they are in favor of this structural change? I'm not talking about the layoffs. I'm talking about this change from, you know, having pure managers to everyone being an IC, an individual contributor, everyone having a little more ownership. I'm sort of thinking about this. And I'm asking this question because it's not just Coinbase, right? I mean, this is something that we will see across tech companies. I wonder if you've talked to any people at all about whether or not employees like this or if they kind of liked the, you know, being part of a team, maybe?" "Yeah, I think Coinbase CEO himself started to talk about how he even he as a CEO has returned to the codebase of Coinbase last year and started to do some coding himself again using AI tools. So it is a top-down, top-down approach. Everyone needs to get their hands dirty. And I think from employees' perspective, this layoff probably isn't too much of a surprise. Everyone knows the market has been in a poor condition." "Do you, do they think there might be even more because last time you just said the bear market they did two rounds of cuts and then they said we could have cut even more? Is that a concern? Do you expect maybe there to be more cuts?" "I think it's definitely going to be a concern, and it depends on market conditions in part, which we don't know how it will play out. And I think this is something that Coinbase will be asked about when they do report earnings to whether they will commit to a certain level of profitability or to be able to make sure that the company can outlast a the number of cycles that we are seeing." "I want to pivot to talk a little bit about another piece of crypto news this week show. Andreessen Horowitz has raised its latest crypto fund. It is just over $2 billion. What was your reaction to this fund that they raised?" "I think the size stood out for me. It is much smaller than the prior fund they raised in 2022. It's about half the size. So back then they raised a $4.5 billion fund. That was a record size and it happened right before the crypto crashes occurred in 2022. I think even in the blog post that they announced the new fund today, they alluded to the fact that the market condition, the market cycle is currently on a quieter part, and they mentioned that they wanted to invest in founders that are building products for everyday uses even though right now they don't get as much attention. So I think they are pretty aware of the fact that a lot of the interest from investors have moved on from crypto to AI, but they seem to suggest that that's not necessarily a bad thing because it allows them to stay focused and to also capture and identify good founders at the time when they don't get attention." "And and it strikes me that that perhaps is a little bit of a contrast to the way that Haun Ventures positioned their latest fund that they also announced this week I believe. I mean, they still said that they're focused on crypto, they said they're sort of open the aperture a little bit to take advantage of where AI and agents could intersect with crypto. But it sounds like Andreessen is saying we're we're going much deeper into crypto, we're finding the best crypto founders first. That that's what the goal is here." "I think for Andreessen, the intersection between crypto and AI is also an important focus. In their blog, they did allude to the fact that agentic payments will become more of a thing and the internet will be run by agents in the future. They didn't really spell out specific examples of how this intersection between crypto and AI will look like. It's kind of like a high-level big picture thought. So, we look forward to see how exactly that will pan out." "Great. Well Yueqi, I want to thank you for coming on. That is Yueqi Yang, our crypto reporter here at The Information." SpaceX has been highlighting xAI's speed and cost advantage in its data center buildout compared to rival AI labs. But exclusive reporting from The Information found those advantages come with some hidden costs. My colleagues Theo Wayt and Velita Pau published the story today. Theo joins me now to share with us what he knows. Theo, welcome back to the show. It's great to have you here. "Hey, Akash." "So tell us what we know about xAI's data center buildout efforts." "So, you know, xAI is this company that, you know, pitches themselves as doing things very differently from other AI companies and other data center builders. And you know, the first aspect of that is that they even chose to build their own data centers in the first place. Like if you look at OpenAI, Anthropic, other large AI labs, they're largely letting other companies do the building. xAI's building their own. And in SpaceX's S1—sorry, after acquiring xAI—they talk quite a bit about this metric called price per megawatt in data center construction. The draft of the S1 that we've seen says that xAI's cost per megawatt is $2.7 million per megawatt compared to an industry standard of $12.3 million." "So cheaper access to compute. Like 25% of the cost of what other people pay to build data centers." "But xAI didn't define that term precisely. It's unclear exactly how they measured it. There could be some you know creative decisions being made about what to include in that number. But the point is xAI is saying we know how to do this a lot cheaper than our competitors because we're crafty. And that you know comes out in a lot of ways." "Okay, so crafty. So expand on that a bit more. What is behind xAI's craftiness here?" "So they, they're using a lot of kind of like temporary and improvised measures you could say to get their data centers online over the past couple of years. That includes you know using these mobile gas generators to power the data centers, which has been quite controversial because they produce air pollution and are meant for temporary use in remote areas, and they're running them close to a city. The other kind of thing that they've been doing is relying on these mobile chillers which basically look like semi-truck trailers and they just have like over a hundred of them kind of parked outside the data center to cool the servers in there. And those are also a temporary measure. And overall you know you have like all of these temporary unusual setups that kind of add up to a lot more potential places where things can go wrong. And my understanding is that's contributed to the data center sites having a lot more outages than would be standard at a data center that took a lot more time to stand up." "So, so they've put together these improvised solutions to get these data centers up and running faster. And you've also—I mean, you've covered a number of these—what, there's Colossus 1 and then Colossus 2, I think is—is it being built at this point now? Do I have that right or is it built?" "There's one building of it that's like mostly done and then there's another building of it that is not done at all." "Okay, okay. So, so but the point is, I mean these are the xAI data centers and so what they're touting here in the SpaceX IPO is that we can get these up and running faster because we have these improvised solutions, we've got generators, we've got trucks. And what your reporting shows is that sometimes these improvised solutions don't actually—aren't actually as reliable, understandably, as if they were to have taken a little more of a traditional approach to build." "Well, to be clear, they're not touting the temporary measures in the IPO. That's based more on my reporting. The IPO pitch is that everything is amazing and perfect, obviously." "Right. So okay, so they get the data centers up and running, they get access to the compute. You know another part of your story that was interesting though is you talked a little bit about the utilization of compute and you know how well they are using all the compute that they've sort of built capacity for. Talk a little bit about that for us." "Yeah, so credit goes to to my colleague Anisa actually for for getting this memo, but there was a SpaceX executive who's been installed at xAI now, Michael Nichols, who kind of sent this memo about this metric, this internal metric basically showing how efficiently xAI is using the chips that it has spent a ton of money to get in its data centers. He said that the this metric is embarrassingly low, and xAI needs to figure out how to use it better, and in the process a ton of the leadership on the data center team and the computing team has left the company since they made that push." "The other part of your story that was interesting is—are there any—well you spelled it out in the story and I'm hoping you can explain to us here, I mean what are the safety implications behind doing things the way they have?" "Yeah, so you know in the process of reporting on the story I found out about a few different incidents where xAI employees have either been involved in accidents or made other complaints about working conditions. Employees of xAI or of contractors working on the site. In January of this year there was a worker who was using this device called a fusion machine that is basically this really powerful industrial machine that fuses pipes together. And he got his finger stuck in the machine and it crushed it and it was amputated. And following that incident the contractor that works for xAI said that they implemented additional safety measures including basically having multiple people verify that nobody's hand is in this device when it's operational. And then last month there were also some complaints at the site about like generators being used inside that made employees sick. And then last fall there was an incident where a couple of people got electrocuted. So I mean with this kind of thing it can be hard to say like what is just a typical accident in the course of construction because construction is inherently a dangerous field. But with some of the—with both the finger loss thing and the air quality issue—xAI or its contractors have committed to doing things differently following that which would indicate something is going on that they had to address. So data centers are very large ambitious projects and trying to do them really quickly can have downsides." "Right. Big picture for you Theo, before we let you go, I mean as you think about questions that you have to push this story ahead, what are sort of the next topics that you're interested in pursuing on your beat?" "Yeah, I mean I think that like with xAI right there is still this question of how on earth they're going to make money from these data centers. Like xAI's revenue is just not significant and it's a gigantic source of burn for the combined company now. Right. They had this deal they announced with Cursor where Cursor is going to use some of the compute from xAI's data centers but there's a ton of ambiguities around that. I think it's just still going to be a gigantic question for investors in the SpaceX IPO that you know is not real—like the revenue side of all this. I mean you can bring down the costs as much as you want but if nobody's buying the products then that's another issue." "Exactly. Well Theo I want to thank you for coming on. That is Theo Wayt, our Elon Musk reporter here at The Information." The SpaceX Cursor deal for a potential acquisition surprised a lot of people, and in a world where AI coding is fiercely competitive, the big question is how do startups differentiate themselves in this environment as the big are getting bigger? I want to bring on Zach Lloyd, founder and CEO at AI coding company Warp, for his point of view on this. Zach, welcome to the show. It's great to have you here. "Thanks for having me Akash, great to be here." "So I want to talk about Warp, but tell me very quickly, what was your reaction to the to the SpaceX Cursor deal announcement, partnership, whatever we want to call it at this point?" "I mean a couple things. I guess one it really speaks to just like how AI coding has taken off as a market. That's an incredible price for an AI coding company and I mean Cursor makes sense, very, very big. From my perspective, it's interesting to see another one of these AI coding companies sort of partner or maybe be acquired by a model lab. So you see sort of consolidation amongst the model labs as as the big players in the space right now." "And so explain to us a little bit about how Warp fits into the AI coding ecosystem. Are you a direct competitor of Cursor, do you guys use each other's technology, how does it all work?" "So we are in a sense a competitor, but what Warp is doing at this point is trying to position itself as like a great place to run any coding agent. So we're complementary in a lot of ways. So you can use you can use Cursor, you can use Codex, you can use Claude Code, you can use Warp's own agent within Warp. And the way that I think about our company and our positioning is like a you know we're trying to sit one level above any particular coding agent and be a great place for people to run any of them. Which matters because every I don't know month, week, the what is the state of the art here is changing and people really want flexibility for how they do that." "So this is a tool where people can can use this platform and they can use both Codex and Claude and Cursor, I should say Claude Code, they can use all these tools inside the Warp platform." "They can use all of them. And where this really—it's nice for individual developers, it really matters for companies as they are trying to figure out like how do you set up systems for deploying especially cloud agents at scale. You don't necessarily want to be locked in to one model or one provider. And so what we're trying to do is provide optionality to companies where they can adopt sort of one piece of infrastructure that lets them sort of switch between those different providers and optimize things like costs. Which are an increasingly big concern for companies, get governance around like how those agents are working, audit trails, secure them all. And so we think that the best position for us is to be something that cuts across them. Which is one reason so last week we open sourced Warp, and it's a big part of this strategy of trying to be like a neutral ecosystem player in what is a very, very competitive market where you know we're going against xAI and you know Anthropic and OpenAI. We would rather not compete with them, we would rather be something that enables them." "Why did you make the change to open source?" "Yeah, the the general philosophy here is like we thought we could actually move faster and build a better product if the community was participating. The other really, really big thing to the why now of it is that the agents—and agents are doing most of the heavy lifting in our repo now. They're actually like writing the code and doing the planning and verifying the changes. Agents have become so powerful and so good that we felt like we could actually sort of accelerate the product development meaningfully by bringing the community in and having them do a bunch of the sort of like provide like what's the right features to build and are they working right? So it's it's kind of a new way of working where there's humans but if you look at the repo it's all being built by agents, it's very cool." "You know, I want to ask you about the vibe coding craze at large and the current state of it. I mean, on one hand we have companies like Apple that are sending their coders back to bootcamp, you know, to relearn the latest and greatest ways in which software engineers should be doing their job. You also have the whole token maxing movement, right, where companies will encourage their employees to basically use AI as much as they can, and I imagine vibe coding is at the center of that. But on the flip side of that, you know, you have these security flaws and you know, I'm sure token maxing now, we saw that tide turn as well. You know, I think it was the Uber's CTO that said you know we've kind of gone through our our budget for it, and you know now we we don't have any budget left for our AI endeavors in certain pockets. Anyway, the details are what they are. Do you think vibe coding, you know it's had its moment, do you think there might be a pullback at all at these bigger tech companies where they say well maybe we should actually do things the old fashioned way because it's a little safer in some cases?" "So I don't think there's going to be a pullback to manually like writing code by hand. I think that those days have come and gone. I think vibe coding, the way that I think of it is like you're just sort of like you know driving in the back seat while the agent does all of the coding, you don't know what's going on. I don't think that is a good strategy either. So I would say those are the two extremes. And what you're going to see at companies is something in the middle, which I would call like agentic engineering, where you know you really have to reimagine what is the right way to build software in this world of new agentic capabilities. And it's not by hand and it's not by like totally like closing your eyes and letting the agent do it, it's by adopting a new set of best practices and controls and security and securing it. And so I think you're going to see a middle ground. And I do think actually costs, security, centralized audit and governance, this is like how you're going to do this at scale. And we're moving past the point where real companies are going to just be like go nuts on some coding tool on your laptop. They don't—like that era is going to end pretty rapidly." "Now I want to ask you very quickly about open source models actually given that you are now firmly in the open source ecosystem. One of the questions I've had is we've seen for example Meta—traditionally their models have been open source—and we've seen a lot of success with open source models broadly speaking, especially I'm thinking about the models from China.
