But to your point, the models and like a lot of use cases will use a model as just a small part of their stack, and that's where we spend a lot of time—going beyond the research on the product side of how you understand a user's problem and the workflow that they need. In voice agents, it's combining the audio models with knowledge and bringing that inside of the system, how you bring it outside with telephony systems so you can interact across channels, and how you evaluate, test, and monitor. And then as you create, whether that's in the agent space or the creative space, that same understanding, you build the ecosystem. And that's what we hope to build across ElevenLabs: a place where whether that's distribution and brand that people can trust, the platform where you have a pre-existing set of work that you can start off, whether it's a template for creating an agent, a template for creating a workflow in a creative space, or whether that's a voice. We had the pleasure now of having over 20,000 voices that people created and contributed that you can use across language styles and voices. And I think that will be an increasingly important layer of how you are able to cater to that diversity, make it easy for people to start and really understand that workflow. All right, I'm going to hand it back to Constantine. Mati, thank you. Andrew, thanks for being a partner. Great job guys. Amazing. Thank you guys.

Novo Nordisk CEO: Open to deals, we need the broadest pipeline in the world

CNBC Television(3.4M) ·10:52 ·501 views · 2026-05-06
Angelica Peebles (Reporter, CNBC) Mike Doustdar (CEO, Novo Nordisk)
Novo Nordisk is leveraging a successful "peptide-in-a-pill" Wegovy launch and strategic rebranding of Rybelsus to maintain a 65% share of new prescriptions, while aggressively pursuing M&A to build the world’s broadest metabolic pipeline.
TL;DR Novo Nordisk is leveraging a successful "peptide-in-a-pill" Wegovy launch and strategic rebranding of Rybelsus to maintain a 65% share of new prescriptions, while aggressively pursuing M&A to build the world’s broadest metabolic pipeline. Clinical Efficacy: The Wegovy...

TL;DR

Novo Nordisk is leveraging a successful "peptide-in-a-pill" Wegovy launch and strategic rebranding of Rybelsus to maintain a 65% share of new prescriptions, while aggressively pursuing M&A to build the world’s broadest metabolic pipeline.

Wegovy Pill Launch and Market Performance

  • Clinical Efficacy: The Wegovy pill demonstrates up to 17% weight loss with daily use, which CEO Mike Doustdar notes is approximately 30% higher efficacy than current oral competitors.
  • The "Peptide" Advantage: While competitors often use small molecules for oral drugs, Novo Nordisk intentionally chose a peptide formulation.
      • Potency: Peptides are characterized as more powerful and potent than small molecules.
      • Safety Profile: The pill is backed by 15 million patient years of safety data collected via the Wegovy injectable franchise.
  • Commercial Strategy: Currently, 90% of sales are through the cash-pay channel.
      • Affordability: The pill was priced to be accessible to a large group of patients without immediate reimbursement.
      • Expansion: International launches are scheduled to begin in the second half of the year as production scales.

Competitive Positioning and Demographics

  • Market Share: Novo Nordisk currently captures 65% of new prescriptions in the anti-obesity segment, compared to 35% for its primary competitor (Eli Lilly).
  • Patient Demographics: 60–65% of the patient base is female.
      • Drug Interactions: Doustdar highlights a competitive edge in safety, noting that Novo’s pill does not require patients to stop oral birth control, a restriction seen in some competitor clinical trials.
      • Male Adoption: The pill format is successfully reaching more male patients than the injectable versions previously did.
  • The "1-0" Lead: Acknowledging Eli Lilly CEO Dave Ricks’ comment that the "pill war" will take quarters to play out, Doustdar stated he is happy to be "1-0" after the first quarter of the launch.

Portfolio Strategy and Rebranding

  • Leveraging Brand Equity: Novo Nordisk plans to rebrand the oral GLP-1 Rybelsus as the "Ozempic pill."
      • Rationale: The goal is to capitalize on the massive global brand recognition of Ozempic to drive sales for both the pill and the injectable formats.
  • Franchise Growth: The Wegovy franchise is being bolstered by the introduction of Wegovy HD (High Dose), which has further strengthened brand recognition alongside the oral launch.

Pipeline Innovation and M&A Outlook

  • Near-Term Assets: The company is preparing for the launch of CagriSema (an amylin/GLP-1 combination therapy) early next year, following an expected approval at the end of this year.
  • Early-Stage Assets:
      • Zegalatide: A follow-on product currently in development in both injectable and pill formats.
      • UBT251: A triple agonist that has shown superior early Phase 2 results compared to competitors.
  • Aggressive Deal-Making: Doustdar emphasized that the company is "looking more than ever before" at external acquisitions.
      • Mindset: The company is actively fighting internal complacency or "not invented here" syndrome to ensure they possess the broadest pipeline globally.
  • Diversification: Following successful Phase 3 data, Novo Nordisk is seeking approval for a Sickle Cell treatment, an asset originally acquired through a business development deal.
"I’m happy that you know right now it’s 1-0 for the first quarter and then we will of course see you know where it goes in the following quarters." — Mike Doustdar
"We should not get too complacent or arrogant that if it’s not invented in our own laboratories then we would not like to look at it... we need not just the best but the broadest pipeline in the world." — Mike Doustdar
"Majority of our customers are females... We don’t have any restrictions on [having] to stop the oral birth control pills if you want to take this drug." — Mike Doustdar
Good morning. This morning we have Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar with us. Mike, thanks so much for joining us today. Really excited to talk about this quarter and also the Wegovy pill launch. So I want to start there. Talk to me about what has made this launch so successful right out the gate and where you see it going from here. I think to have a successful launch you need two things: a very good product and of course good people that push through it, and and we've had both of those. You have a situation where clearly we believe we have the superior product here with the highest efficacy. If you take the pill on a daily basis, you can lose up to 17% of your weight loss. It is a Wegovy pill, so it comes on a back of 15 million patient years of safety that we have collected over number of years on on on Wegovy. So people like the safety profile of this and at the same time, you know, we say it's the only peptide in a pill and again the word peptide is synonymous with being more powerful and potent. So all of this has meant of course that we have a really good product at hand. On the other hand of course we've told the story with our great people using partnerships and on our own, going where the patients are and have had a good launch. So we are very excited about this. It's funny because now you're saying that peptide is a strength and I feel like a year ago everyone was saying actually you wanted a small molecule here, you didn't want a peptide and part of the reason being the production, but it sounds like you are able to produce enough of it and now you're going to launch in some more international markets. So, you know, what has made it possible for you to produce more than maybe people thought you'd be able to? Correct. I I would say that it's not us who had said peptide is not the right way forward. We chose to go this direction. It was more difficult, I have to say. It's a you know it's it's a situation where you see none of our competitors currently and in the near future have gone in that direction. I believe they often go towards small molecules because it is easier to play around with small molecules. This is quite difficult science I would say, but once you do it, if you know how to scale it like we have, then then it's probably the right way forward. So from a safety point of view we believe this is the right way forward. We have had a situation where now we are able to also prove to people that besides just having this great launch in US we're ready to go international with it and you will see in the second half of the year we'll start launching this product in more market. So it's an exciting time for us. Mhm. And here in the US you know obviously cash pay has become a really important channel. So you know talk to us about how important the cash pay business has been, especially to seeing the success right out the gate and not having to wait necessarily for reimbursement for the pill. It's been very important. So right now some 90% of our sales are coming through the cash payment. It is possible because we made the pill quite affordable I would say, so a larger group of people can come and and pick up a pill without having too much worried about about the financials part of it. But we do hope that of course as we go forward we will increase the share of the reimbursed channels and the commercial channel, even though we will continue of course pushing strong with our you know partners in the cash payments. But cash payments is is definitely been a big part of the success. And at the same time your competitor we had Dave Ricks on last week from Lilly of course and he was saying that this pill competition is going to play out over quarters not weeks. So I want to get your response of you know how long you think it'll take to be able to declare a winner in this segment. I think he's very much right. We have to take it quarter by quarter. I'm happy that you know right now it's 1-0 for the first quarter and then we will of course see you know where it goes in the following quarters. Once again what gives me confidence with regards to my own pill is that when people are choosing a anti-obesity medication they're after a few things. They want an efficacious drug, they want a safe drug, a tolerable drug and they want a drug that of course they can basically trust. I believe we have all of those. We have a situation where we are by all means the most efficacious right now pill in the market. 17% if you take the pill. That's 30% or so higher than the efficacy we have seen with my competitor. We have as I mentioned a fantastic tolerability, we have seen that in our clinical trials against what they have shown in their own clinical trials and the safety is on a back of Wegovy and semaglutide safety that has been proven over and over and over again. On the other hand we have a situation where we don't have some of the drug to drug interaction restrictions that we have seen with some of our competitors. Majority of our of our customers are females for example, many of these females are on anti- on anti-birth control pills. We don't have any restrictions on you have to stop the oral anti- anti-birth control pills if you want to take this drug. So all of those leads me to think that as we go forward in the future quarters we'll be able to retain our leadership. You talked really quick about you know the fact that the majority of your patients are women and you know I'd heard some people say that maybe this could expand these pills could expand the market for GLP-1s particularly men. I noticed your Super Bowl ad you have a few men in there. So how do you see the pill reaching new patients who maybe were reluctant to take a shot? Well if you compare it to the injectables I think the percentage of the men are higher on the pill than than than on the than on the injectables, but I still see that some 60-65% of the overall patients are right now female. We don't discriminate you know different genders. We believe we have the best product out there that many people can use. We will advertise it to both genders and to anyone basically in the hope that sooner or later everyone who needs to reduce weight and get some of the extra benefit that this pill provides will be able to do so at an affordable price might I say. Mhm. And what about the you know the rebrand of Rybelsus to the Ozempic pill? Why do that now and what are your expectations on how that rebrand will it could reinvigorate sales there? It's a very good question. One of the things that we have seen and gets a little bit lost in translation during this quarter's results is not only Wegovy pill did well, Wegovy injection also did well on the back of two elements. One I think the Wegovy pill really helped the brand recognition once- once again and then we introduced something called Wegovy HD, Wegovy High Dose. Both of those elements have helped the Wegovy franchises to to do better. Right now if you think about anti-obesity medication in US in terms of new prescriptions 65% of the new prescriptions are coming to Novo Nordisk and 35% is going to my competitor during this last quarter. We are hoping that we can once again remind people that Ozempic is one of the most recognizable brand names out there. By rebranding Rybelsus into Ozempic pill and give it one extra push, we believe that it will have a spillover effect not just to the Ozempic pill but also to the injectable. Okay. And on the pipeline I think that's another area that people you know I was talking to some analysts the other day and they were saying that even if you can find success here with the pill it doesn't fix some of the the issues that investors perceive to be with your pipeline. So what are some of the areas where you feel like you have holes that you need to fill and how are you going to fill those holes? So I I would disagree I would say with that notion. We have one of the best pipelines in the industry. We are very excited to bring the first amylin GLP-1 combination therapy hopefully towards the beginning of next year after getting that approved at the at the end of this year. That's called CagriSema. We have of course accelerated our follow-on product to that Zegalatide is going to be coming up soon both in injectable as well as the pill format. We have an amylin on its own Cagrilintide that is coming in. We just announced phase 2 results of a product called UBT251, a triple agonist which has had you know some of the best early results than anyone else has been able to show. Of course there's a lot of things in my pipeline that right now I have the privy to look into and get excited but not have shared it yet with the world. So I am incredibly excited about our pipeline and I would just say to the investors who are a little bit skeptical wait and see. Do you feel like you need to do more deals though? I I think that we are looking more than ever before. We have created a bit of a a mindset here in the company that we should not get too complacent or arrogant that if it's not invented in our own laboratories then we would not like to look at it or just because we have a fantastic pipeline we should not look for more. If our ambition is to help hundreds of millions of patients out there then we need not just the best but the broadest pipeline in the world. So let's go and see who else basically has you know assets that are complementary to what we have and we are quite active with with those BD talks and acquisitions and you will see more of those as well going forward. And just the last one I want to ask you. I know it doesn't get as much airtime as obesity but you know sickle cell right you have this you had successful phase 3 data you're going to seek approval for that. How big of an opportunity do you think that sickle cell will be for Novo? And also as you look at sort of the slow the slow uptake of some of the gene therapies like what does that tell you about what you need to do to actually you know find success in sickle cell? First of all I would say we you know referring back to your previous question on the BD this is a great example of a fantastic business development deal we m- we did bringing this asset in. It's going to make a huge difference for thousands of patients that are suffering from this really difficult condition around different parts of the world. So I'm on behalf of the patient super excited and I also think this will be a very exciting business for us as we go forward. So again wait a little bit longer to for us to launch the product hopefully soon, but it's a very exciting moment for a society that for a long time has been a little bit left alone.

Why the Brain Computes 1,000,000x More Efficiently Than A GPU: Unconventional AI's Naveen Rao

Sequoia Capital(180.0K) ·14:13 ·12.1K views · 2026-05-06
Naveen Rao (founder and CEO, Unconventional AI)
Current AI scaling is hitting a physical "energy wall" that conventional GPU architectures cannot overcome; achieving Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) requires a fundamental shift from digital matrix math to nonlinear dynamical systems modeled after the 1,000,000x more efficient human brain.
TL;DR Current AI scaling is hitting a physical "energy wall" that conventional GPU architectures cannot overcome; achieving Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) requires a fundamental shift from digital matrix math to nonlinear dynamical systems modeled after the 1,000,000x more...

TL;DR

Current AI scaling is hitting a physical "energy wall" that conventional GPU architectures cannot overcome; achieving Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) requires a fundamental shift from digital matrix math to nonlinear dynamical systems modeled after the 1,000,000x more efficient human brain.

The Impending Energy Wall

  • Scaling Limitations: Naveen Rao argues that ASI is impossible on our current trajectory because we are hitting the limits of the physical world.
  • The 2-4 Year Window: Global energy capacity for AI will be exhausted within the next few years. While the world has ~9,000 gigawatts of total capacity, AI training and inference are already consuming multiple gigawatts at a rate that cannot be sustained by current infrastructure.
  • The Biological Benchmark:
      • Humanity’s Footprint: The entire human race (8 billion people) runs on only 160 gigawatts of power.
      • Individual Efficiency: A human brain operates on ~20 watts. In contrast, a squirrel performs complex navigation on less than 10 milliwatts (1/100th of a smartphone’s power).
      • The Efficiency Gap: Current digital chips are roughly three orders of magnitude (1,000x) away from the thermodynamic limit of "intelligence per watt."

Moving Beyond the 80-Year-Old Digital Abstraction

  • Obsolescence of the Substrate: Our current computing paradigm (floating point numbers and von Neumann architecture) was designed 80 years ago for entirely different purposes.
  • The Von Neumann Bottleneck: Traditional systems waste the majority of their energy moving state in and out of memory (writing, retrieving, and operating).
  • Physics-First Computing: Unconventional AI is moving toward "Non-von Neumann" architectures where there is no separation between state and computation.
      • Nonlinear Dynamics: Instead of matrix math, the system uses time-varying interactions between "neurons" to process information.
      • Computing in the Time Domain: Rather than explicit digital steps, the system uses the "time axis" of physics to perform computation. You set an initial state, "kick it," and let the physical dynamics converge on an answer.

Technical Strategy and Prototyping

  • Unconventional AI Prototype: The company went from a standing start in January to a chip prototype (taping out this summer) in just six months.
      • AI-Assisted Design: This rapid development was made possible by using existing AI to design the next generation of AI hardware.
  • Stochastic Computing: Unlike digital computers that fail if a single bit is wrong, these new systems are stochastic and robust, similar to biological brains.
  • Generative Proof of Concept: Rao demonstrated a model where image generation occurs through "converging dynamical systems." The system starts with randomness and naturally "clumps" into meaningful representations (e.g., images of cats or horses) based on trained physical couplings.

The Future of the Compute Stack

  • Evolution of Hardware:
      • CPUs: Best for fast, single-threaded operations.
      • GPUs: Best for massive parallel matrix operations (moving many operands at once).
      • Dynamical Systems: The next frontier where state and function overlap within the physics of the circuit, maximizing "galaxy brain" efficiency.
  • Landauer’s Principle: The company’s ultimate goal is pushing toward the physical asymptote of the Landauer limit—the theoretical minimum energy required to change one bit of information.
"The entirety of humanity is 160 gigawatts... our current paradigm of compute is just vastly more inefficient. We're going to get to a point within the next couple of years where we just don't have any more energy in the world for AI." — Naveen Rao
"We use the time axis of physics to do computing, and existing computing constructs do not... with something with non-linear dynamics, I actually just say 'here is the initial state, kick it, and let it run.'" — Naveen Rao
"The assumptions we made 80 years ago are actually not quite valid anymore. We just choose to keep building on them because I can build a product in two years... but we're taking a different tack: let's go back to those first principles." — Naveen Rao
We're going to jump into our next set of frontier talks. The first is from Naveen Rao. Naveen is a pioneer in the AI space. He did his PhD in neuroscience, then he started one of the first ever AI chip companies way before it was cool. He's actually the person who ran MosaicML, one of the first AI training companies, and then built all of Databricks AI. He left that amazing role to do something new and redefine the future of computing, bringing cool back to neuroscience. Naveen, come on up. All right. Good afternoon everyone. Yeah, super excited to be here. I'm Naveen Rao and CEO of Unconventional AI. So we are unconventional because maybe it's actually the wrong word to use, because I think we're going to have to change the name to conventional. It's actually a great time to be a startup as Boris alluded to, right? Having no baggage actually, I think, is a true competitive advantage. We can do things so much faster than traditional chip companies and full-stack companies can do, and I think that's what's very exciting. We can get to tape outs in months instead of years, things like this. So anyway, let's get started. ASI won't happen. I put that up there. I can guarantee you some of you are like trying to prove me wrong. All of a sudden you're like, "Oh my god, what's..." you know, I can hear the gears turning like that can't be true. Well, let me explain my logic a little bit and maybe the definition of what I call ASI. So really, I think we need to get to a much greater amount of compute efficiency. And when I say compute efficiency, I don't mean algorithmic compute efficiency or data efficiency. There's lots of people working on these problems. I actually mean the fundamental substrate—actually how I do information processing at the physics level. We chose a path of making computers work the way they did for a lot of reasons about 80 years ago. If you think about it like in the tech industry, how many things have existed for 80 years? Not a lot. The digital abstraction, sort of floating point numbers, those were around from the 1940s and from a machine that was built from a completely different substrate and for a machine that was built for a completely different purpose. And now we're building machines for intelligence. So let's think through this a little bit. Part of this is that AI will make us more efficient. I mean we've talked about coding and you know, running a thousand agents on your phone, all this kind of stuff, right? It does make us more efficient. But at some point, we start to get to a place where what does efficiency really mean? If you think about energy, actual energy, maybe we're not more efficient. And we're kind of butting up against those limitations of the physical world now. Already today we're using many gigawatts for AI inference and training. And we're going to get to a point within the next couple of years—this is not 10 years away; this is two, three, four years—where we just don't have any more energy in the world for AI. Then this topic starts to become very, very important. Right now you can kind of look at it like, well, electrical energy versus food. These are two energy sources for intelligence. And you know, there's no real limitation on the electrical side. But that's going to hit a pretty solid wall very soon. And you know, we're talking about going to space. We're talking about building fusion reactors. Great. Let's do all those things. But still, these fundamental physics apply. So if we think through this a little bit, we have about 8 billion people in the world. Our brains use about 20 watts each. That's only 160 gigawatts. So the entirety of humanity is 160 gigawatts. So just to put that into comparison, we have about 9,000 gigawatts of capacity in the world today. The US has about a thousand. And you know, this runs everything. This is like heating in your home and all the things, electric cars, all that kind of stuff. But if we said we maybe got 50% more of this and we say, okay, great, now we've got like 4,000 plus gigawatts. But the problem is our current paradigm of compute is just vastly more inefficient. So a computer, I'm making some numbers up here—I mean I could say like if I'm running inference per token I can come up with numbers—but fully loaded, like the amount of energy that goes into inference, building the model, running the model, all of that, let's call it a gigawatt something like that, or definitely in the megawatt range. But humans are on the order of 20 watts. And you could also argue that evolution over 4 billion years has created what we are. But the reality is today our constraint becomes how quickly can we get learning to happen, how quickly can we build intelligence on a given amount of energy. So if we want to build this future where we have lots of intelligence in the world and we're automating all kinds of things and we want to really be more efficient from an energy standpoint, we're going to need a lot more—we're going to need a lot more watts. Or we can think about building a vastly more power efficient computer. And that's where we come in. I love this curve here and I think most people really haven't thought about this because you just sort of assume a computer is a computer. And we haven't really questioned that. Again, this is the unconventional part is like, let's break that apart. The assumptions we made 80 years ago are actually not quite valid anymore. We just choose to keep building on them because I can build a product in two years. I can make something that I can sell in two years. But we're kind of taking a different tack, like let's go back to those first principles and see if we can build something much, much better. So there is a thermodynamic limit to intelligence per watt. Okay, that means you just can't do any better. There's something called the Landauer principle which some of you may know, which basically suggests how much compute could happen within a certain amount of energy. So there is a physical reality that we can't get past and that's sort of this asymptote here. Now biology is somewhere up here. It's actually pretty darn efficient. 4 billion years of evolution have created something that is actually very efficient. However, it's not at the asymptote yet. There's probably an order of two of magnitude between those two. We're actually here by the way. We're down here, and I think limits of 2D lithography—that's what chips are built on today—is call it somewhere down here. And I think with focused effort we can get to the point where we're pushing the limit of that. And so we'll have something that's... this, by the way, is something like three orders of magnitude from where we are. It is very far away from what we could be in terms of energy efficiency and so really that's what we're focused on today. How do we do it? I mean, yeah, this is great, make something more power efficient. Wonderful, right? But the reality is it's not so simple. We can't be thinking about the computer in exactly the same way as we have been. It's not about a machine that runs matrix math. That's been the simple way to move forward. NVIDIA of course has owned that market and continued to push the envelope. But if you look at the power efficiency numbers, actual power efficiency of delivering an FP8 flop for instance, it's not that much better. Costs have gotten better because manufacturing's gotten better, our ability to package has gotten better, but actual energy per flop with memory access has not gotten better. It's very, very incremental now. So I am a neuroscientist. I was a computer architect for 10 years before that. So I've been thinking about this problem for a long time. Actually on the order of 30 years. So it's a very exciting time for me personally. And you know, biology really does provide an existence proof. I mean, you can argue that okay, the tokens per second out of a human are lower than a machine. But the intelligence is higher. We still haven't gotten to the point with these gigawatts that we're throwing at it that we're rivaling a human's intelligence in terms of discovery. We'll get there. We're going to get there in a very short amount of time but it's going to come at the cost of a lot of energy. What I think is most interesting here is actually not just that human brains are 20 watts, but that the wattage kind of scales with the weight. A macaque monkey's brain is probably less than a watt. And actually you see this all through the mammalian world and also the insect world. Like you have very complex behavior for milliwatts. Just for reference, your phone in your pocket is about one watt. So you know, a squirrel jumping from branch to branch is running on less than 10 milliwatts. That's one one-hundredth of your phone. We can't actually do this perfectly—like a squirrel's jumping 10 feet across from branch to branch within wind and all that stuff. We can't do that with a much, much larger computer. So biology still created something quite amazing and I just don't feel like there's been an appreciation for that. So I'm just, you know, just a little reminder there. So now, great, we see this kind of phenomenologically—like biology is efficient, can do amazing things. But how does it actually work? We don't really know. I'll be honest as a computer scientist and a neuroscientist, but there are some ideas that we can harvest from neuroscience. And one of them is that the brain is dynamic. It does not use matrix math to do compute. It uses what's called non-linear dynamics to do compute. What this means is that there's a time varying interaction between neurons and that's actually where the compute lies. Can we extract that and actually apply it to synthetic circuits? Maybe. They don't do floating-point math. They don't do matrix math. They do something that can be characterized as such, but it's actually much richer than that because of these non-linear dynamics. And they're stochastic. Brains' compute is not a strict one and zero. In a digital computer if we're off by a one or a zero the whole system falls apart. So really not computers. So I'm going to try to go through this quickly. This is a thing called Kuramoto synchronization. So if you look at a bunch of oscillators here and they're kind of rigidly coupled to each other on this plank, you'll see over time that they start off in any state and then they actually synchronize. This is an example of a contracting or converging dynamical system. So no matter how you start it, it converges and it's only based upon the coupling between them. Well, you can generalize this to something that has kind of a flexible coupling. Call it a trainable coupling between those things. And then it can have all kinds of interesting dynamics. It can move through this state space of dynamics in many, many different ways. So if you generalize this, you can actually think about it in electronics as an electronic circuit. You can say, "I have a bunch of oscillators and they have a fabric on which they're coupled." And now when I make this fabric trainable, I can actually see something that starts to look a little bit more like the dynamics of the brain. It actually has non-linearities and they interact with each other in very interesting ways. It's actually very rich and can represent a lot of information. This is an actual chip that we're going to be building this summer. So we went from basically no team in January to a full prototype in six months. And that's because of AI. So this is what's pretty cool about not having baggage is you can do things in completely different ways. And the way you compute with something like this instead of the traditional way would be you basically loop over some sort of linearized time. This is how we do things in a von Neumann machine. We write state out, we retrieve it, we operate on it, we write it back, so we keep going back and forth. Turns out that's what burns most of the energy in an existing computing system. With something with non-linear dynamics, I actually just say, "Here's the initial state, kick it, and let it run." So the physics themselves basically do this computation. And it does it sort of... the state is implicit. It's not an explicit write. So in some ways you can think about, if you take anything from this talk, that we use the time axis of the physics to do computing and existing computing constructs do not. And so the question then is can I train this? And the answer is yes. I can actually steer the system into multiple, multiple different things. In fact we're sort of traced out in state space an unconventional logo, that's the idea here, and we can train it in a few different ways. So yes, we can train these systems and steer them into basically any arbitrary set of trajectories. And can we compute? Can we connect it to AI problems like image generation? So actually I have a better version of this I can go to in just a really quick demo here. Let's go to the next one. Can we put the demo up? Yeah, so basically what you see here is something that's running on a model of dynamics that was trained on these different images. So basically I can say okay, I have to use cats—I think Andrew Ng is here, so homage to him—but we can do anything. But basically this is a pretty simple generative model. And I can basically say like okay, at time t equals one, I'm going to backprop an error from randomness to a particular image class. And after that point we let the system just run naturally. What you'll find is that it actually has clumped its representation into places that are meaningful. They're no longer just random pixels but they're actually pixels that make different kind of machines or different kind of animals or whatever. So for horses I start off as random, and at t equals one you should see it kind of converge into horse-like things. And then over time you'll actually see it sort of morph between those. So it's already learned in the state space that it can move between these different things. So let's go ahead and move out of this. So this is really the emergence of something new. CPUs actually do very fast single-threaded things the best. Even today it's faster than a GPU. And really what you're doing is this kind of von Neumann machine where you're moving in and out of memory and cache and doing operations. GPU basically did this with multiple operands at once. So we move a bunch of operands from memory, do some stuff to it, write it back. Compute in memory, like Groq sort of did the same thing, but just did it on chip. It's kind of a more fine-grained version of this. Now what we're talking about is doing something in a dynamical system. The state and the function are overlapped with the physics themselves. So you now have no separation between state and computation. And you know, compute efficiency goes up, of course galaxy brainness goes up. And this is truly non-von Neumann. So with that, I'm just going to leave you with this quote. It's been something I've guided my entire life by and I'm really excited about this time because I've been thinking about this problem for 30 years and we're at this point where I think we can actually start to understand how brains work because now we can build them. Thank you.

Marco Rubio Drops Hammer on Iran: 'Total Defeat' or Prosperity — Choose | 5/6/26

Pat Gray Unleashed(69.8K) ·96:04 ·3.8K views · 2026-05-06
Marco Rubio (Secretary of State) Pat Gray (Host, Pat Gray Unleashed)
Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the conclusion of Operation Epic Fury and the launch of "Project Freedom" to counter Iran's blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, as skyrocketing domestic gas prices and dueling speeches by Rubio and Vice Presiden...
TL;DR Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the conclusion of Operation Epic Fury and the launch of "Project Freedom" to counter Iran's blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, as skyrocketing domestic gas prices and dueling speeches by Rubio and Vice President JD Vance heat up...

TL;DR

Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the conclusion of Operation Epic Fury and the launch of "Project Freedom" to counter Iran's blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, as skyrocketing domestic gas prices and dueling speeches by Rubio and Vice President JD Vance heat up early speculation for the 2028 GOP presidential ticket.

***

U.S. Military Operations and the Strait of Hormuz Crisis

  • Operation Epic Fury Concluded: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, acting as White House press secretary, confirmed that the tactical objectives of Operation Epic Fury have been met. The administration prefers a diplomatic resolution, specifically a memorandum of understanding to open the shipping lanes, but Iran has refused to negotiate.
  • Project Freedom Launched: Following the end of the previous operation, the administration initiated "Project Freedom" to address the humanitarian crisis caused by the Iranian blockade.
      • Stranded Civilians: The mission aims to rescue approximately 23,000 commercial sailors and civilian crew members from 87 countries who have been trapped in the Gulf for over two months.
      • Economic Threats to Iran: Rubio warned that Iran faces "generational destruction to the wealth of their country" if they continue blockading international shipping.
  • Defensive Counter-Blockade: Rubio outlined the U.S. strategy to mirror Iran's actions.
      • Reciprocity Policy: If international vessels are blocked from passing, the U.S. will blockade Iranian ships. Rubio argued this is a defensive measure rather than an act of war, contrasting it with Iran's illegal placement of sea mines.
      • The Nuclear Threat: Rubio cautioned that if Iran were allowed to acquire nuclear weapons, they could make an $8 or $9 per gallon fuel price permanent, and the U.S. would lose its leverage to intervene.

Early 2028 GOP Presidential Maneuvering

  • The Frontrunners Emerge: Observers highlight Secretary Rubio and Vice President JD Vance as the leading torchbearers for the administration's platform, setting up a highly competitive 2028 primary landscape.
  • Rubio's Vision for America: During his briefing, Rubio delivered a highly praised, spontaneous response regarding his faith in the nation.
      • Perpetual Improvement: He defined the American story as one of "perpetual and continuous improvement," where each generation leaves the next freer, safer, and more prosperous.
  • Vance Rallies the Working Class: Speaking at a campaign event in Iowa, Vice President JD Vance targeted congressional Democrats for prioritizing illegal immigration over the needs of American citizens.
      • Populist Message: Vance asserted that the opposition party has "gone off the rails," focusing on special interests and foreign priorities rather than the domestic working class.
  • The DeSantis Factor: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis is also actively laying the groundwork for a 2028 presidential bid. Commentators noted that unlike previous election cycles, the GOP has an embarrassment of riches with three highly viable candidates in Rubio, Vance, and DeSantis.

Domestic Fuel Inflation and Economic Strain

  • Surging Gas Prices: Despite administration messaging, retail fuel prices are rising sharply, placing immediate financial pressure on middle- and lower-income families.
      • Inflation-Adjusted Real Cost: While the current national average sits at $4.53 per gallon (down from a nominal high of $5.01 under the previous administration), AI calculations indicate that when adjusted for cumulative inflation, the real-world cost is equivalent to $5.64 per gallon.
      • Regional Disparities: In Texas, prices have jumped roughly 50 cents in a matter of days, reaching up to $4.29 per gallon in Dallas. Meanwhile, California drivers are facing a painful average of $6.16 per gallon.
  • Diminishing Wage Gains: Financial hosts noted that the rapid escalation in transportation costs is entirely wiping out any economic relief voters felt from recent tax cuts or policy implementations like "no tax on tips."

California’s Sanctuary Clashes and Local Elections

  • Los Angeles Anti-ICE Campaign: A growing conflict has erupted between Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass and federal authorities over immigration enforcement.
      • Public Property Bans: The city of LA has installed more than 450 signs at public parks, transit hubs, and libraries declaring city property off-limits as staging or processing areas for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).
      • Taxpayer Expense: While city officials have not disclosed the exact cost, industry estimates place the production and installation of these signs at roughly $250,000.
  • Statewide Progressive Policies:
      • Healthcare for Undocumented Immigrants: Former congressional representative and candidate Katie Porter defended proposals to fund statewide healthcare for illegal immigrants, referring to them broadly as "Californians."
      • Maebe A. Girl's Senate Bid: Progressive LGBTQ activist and drag queen Maebe A. Girl is running for State Senate in Los Angeles, campaigning on tuition-free college, housing as a human right, and the immediate expulsion of ICE from the state.

***

Notable Quotes

"They are facing real catastrophic destruction to their economy, generational destruction to their economy, generational destruction to the wealth of their country, imposed on themselves by the actions they're taking. They should check themselves before they wreck themselves in the direction they're going." — Marco Rubio, Secretary of State
"The world is a victim of Iran because they're terrorists, because of what they're doing now in the straits. But the people of Iran are daily victims of the regime... [They] just want a normal life, a regular life." — Marco Rubio, Secretary of State
"When the President of the United States asked the entire Congress to stand for the simple principle that the people's government ought to fight for the American people first, what did we see? We saw every single Congressional Democrat sit on their hands." — JD Vance, Vice President of the United States

You asked for it, and he delivered. Really good. And here he is as Tucker. Let's talk about your relationship with the President and where it stands currently. When was the last time you were in the Oval Office? A few months ago, I went to the Oval Office. I had gone several times, actually, like a lot, like a lot, like a lot. And what did you see when you were there? You know, everybody knows this, but I'll just confirm it. Like Bebe had been in there before me, and he probably had a gun to Trump's head. Really? Like an actual gun? Like a loaded weapon. You're saying Benjamin Netanyahu had a gun to his head? And he had Donald Trump in a chokehold, and I know that Donald Trump had a wrestling background, he was involved at one point in the WWE, but clearly that didn't matter, like at all, and Bebe had a gun to his head and said, "We're going to Iran, we're doing this now." And Trump was like, "No." And you saw this with your own eyes? I mean, Washington insiders know this, they know this. Wait, so this happened or didn't happen? I mean, I wasn't in the room, but I imagine it was something like that that was going on. I mean, you don't go to Washington multiple times without trying to influence the President for your agenda. But you went... I'm sorry, I'm confused. Okay, well what is your assessment of President Trump currently? Well, I attempted to communicate with the President, and I went outside into the forest, and I screamed, and I screamed, "Don't do it!" I don't know if that was effective, I don't know if he heard, I don't know if he could hear me, but I certainly tried to do that, I did. I think it's possible Donald Trump might be a wizard, but I do believe that, I do. What do you mean by that? Well, the last time I was at the Oval Office, I walked in and at one point the chief of staff looked at me, and Donald Trump himself looked at me, he said, "You shall not pass." And I thought, that's not our President, that's Gandalf. Gandalf the White. And there's nothing wrong with being white, by the way. I say that as a white person and as a man, as a Christian. But Trump might be a wizard. And so I understand you correctly. What you're saying is you literally believe President Donald Trump might be Gandalf from Lord of the Rings? I don't know. But we should be able to ask those questions, I think. Okay, moving on. What is your current relationship with Turning Point USA? Well, like I've always loved TPUSA, I've loved the organization, I've headlined many times. They didn't ask me to do it this year, and so that is what it is. But I've always participated in their events, I've never charged anything, by the way, I've always done it for free. And they always paid me a lot, like a lot, like a lot, like a lot. So, I... And do you think TPUSA as an organization is in decline? I haven't the faintest idea, but yes, I just have questions, like legitimate questions. And the moment you ask questions, "Oh, socialist! Communist! Antisemite!" No, no. I just have legitimate questions. Like, what happened to Charlie Kirk? Should capital be taxed at half the rate of labor? What's Bibi Netanyahu's father's middle name? Or if a demon rapes you without protection, should you get checked for STDs? Because it's itchy. Okay, tell me more about J.D. Vance. Who is J.D. Vance? J.D. Vance, the vice president? I mean, I know J.D. are letters, I mean they're letters, J and D. I mean, J, people are going to say, "Oh, Nazi! J!" But those are just letters to me, those are just, those are just letters of the alphabet to me. You're telling me you don't know who J.D. Vance is? I literally don't know who that is, those words have never come out of my lips, actually, never uttered those words ever and ever. Tucker, look, I've watched you for years, okay? Way back during the cable news days when you had a show on... On what show? Your television show. I've never had a show, like at all. You had one of the highest-rated television news programs. I've literally never uttered words out of my lips on television. Like, ever like that, that I can recall. I'm talking about the years when you were on Fox. What do you mean by Fox? Like, the animal? No, Fox News. I'm sorry, I only know Fox, the animal. I mean, I don't know what it sounds like, actually, like at all. What does the fox say? Mimi mimi mimi mimi. Tucker, are you okay? I've never been on TV. I don't, I don't know what this is. Where are we? Who are you? Who are you? Who am I? What is this? What is this? What is this? These are a lot of questions. Who am I? I don't know. I will do it. Where are we? We should take a break. Hi, I'm Jean Valjean. Oh, what is this? Why are you all wet, baby? What is this? Yes, he's here. How are the hallucinations? Get out of here, Tucky. These people are all Christian Zionists. You've been here? A patient of this institution. I was molested by a demon in my sleep. The dreams you have every night... There were claw marks, there were... And you've uncovered a conspiracy so that anything we tell you about who you are, what you've done, you can dismiss as lies. Okay, shut up, Nazi! I'm friends with J.D. Vance. I've been having this fantasy for two years now, I know every detail. I love that. I love to see the most dangerous patient we have. Your crime is terrible, one you can't forgive yourself for, so you invented another self. I totally agree. Do you understand? So good. Oh, man. Wow. I think we're there. He does Tucker so good. He does, he does it so well, I would say, but... No, so good. Maybe that's just me. It is. Being a stickler, you know. No, it's not just you. It's not just me? No, it's not. He does it well. Can't do it, can't do it. You did it, you did it. Alright, we got overtime coming up. See you back here tomorrow. This is Pat Gray Unleashed.

Washington (5-6-26): Iran says it is reviewing latest U.S. peace plan; Ted Turner dies

C-SPAN(1.8M) ·57:38 ·3.8K views · 2026-05-06
Donald Trump (President) Melania Trump (First Lady) Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
Iran is currently reviewing a U.S.
TL;DR Iran is currently reviewing a U.S. peace proposal to end the ongoing conflict, amid a "wall of steel" naval blockade and President Trump’s ultimatum of escalated bombing; meanwhile, the U.S. national debt has officially surpassed 100% of GDP for the first time since World...

TL;DR

Iran is currently reviewing a U.S. peace proposal to end the ongoing conflict, amid a "wall of steel" naval blockade and President Trump’s ultimatum of escalated bombing; meanwhile, the U.S. national debt has officially surpassed 100% of GDP for the first time since World War II.

Geopolitics and the Iran Conflict

  • Diplomatic Negotiations: Iran’s Foreign Ministry confirmed it is reviewing an American peace plan conveyed via Pakistani mediators.
      • The Trump Ultimatum: President Trump issued a public warning on Truth Social, stating that if Iran does not agree to the deal, U.S. bombing will resume at "a much higher level and intensity than ever before."
      • The "Skirmish" Progress: At a White House Mother’s Day event, the President described the conflict as a "skirmish" that is going "smoothly," comparing it to a rapid intervention in Venezuela.
  • Military Blockade: The U.S. Navy continues a "wall of steel" blockade in the Strait of Hormuz.
      • Tanker Interception: U.S. Central Command intercepted and incapacitated an Iranian-flagged oil tanker on Wednesday for violating the blockade.
  • Strategic Critique: Former Secretary of State Anthony Blinken argued that while the U.S. has damaged Iranian infrastructure, the regime now has "new normal" leverage over the Strait of Hormuz that previous administrations avoided provoking.

National Debt and Economic Indicators

  • Historic Debt Levels: The national debt reached 100% of the U.S. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) this week, with public debt hitting $31.27 trillion.
      • Fiscal Outlook: Analysts from the Concord Coalition and Brookings Institution warned that without a plan to address the $2 trillion annual deficit, debt could reach 240% of GDP within 30 years.
      • The "Squeeze": Interest payments on the debt now exceed the cost of national defense and Medicare.
  • Market Response: Wall Street reacted positively to news of a potential peace deal, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average rising 612 points.

Domestic Politics and 2026 Elections

  • Ohio Senate Race: The general election matchup is finalized between incumbent Republican Senator Jon Husted and former Democratic Senator Sherrod Brown.
      • Primary Results: Brown won his primary with 82% of the vote. He is centering his campaign on Husted's alleged ties to Jeffrey Epstein co-conspirators and corporate interests.
      • Husted Platform: Senator Husted is running on an "Ohio First" agenda, criticizing Brown’s 32-year tenure in Washington for contributing to high energy and healthcare costs.
  • Indiana State Primaries: President Trump successfully ousted five of the seven Republican state senators he targeted for opposing his redistricting plans.
  • Presidential Speculation: Former CNN anchor Don Lemon confirmed in London that he is considering a run for the presidency, advocating for a "meritocracy" in administration appointments.

Federal Investigations and Immigration

  • Epstein Investigation: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick testified in a closed-door House Oversight Committee session regarding his past relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.
      • Contradictory Statements: Rep. Yasmine Ansari (D-AZ) labeled Lutnick a "pathological liar" for visiting Epstein’s private island in 2012 after previously claiming he had severed ties in 2005.
      • Defense: Committee Chair James Comer (R-KY) stated Lutnick was "transparent" and corrected his timeline during his opening statement.
  • Sanctuary Law Standoff: Border Czar Tom Homan threatened to "flood the zone" with ICE agents in New York if Governor Kathy Hochul signs the "Local Cops, Local Crimes Act."
      • State Resistance: Governor Hochul rejected the threat, stating she would not request federal assistance and that the law is intended to focus local police on community protection rather than civil immigration violations.

Notable Quotes

"If they don't agree, the bombing starts at a much higher level and intensity." — President Donald Trump (via Truth Social)
"At the very heart of America’s strength lies the boundless love and quiet power of mothers. We are the most devoted teachers... guiding our children towards goodness." — First Lady Melania Trump
"We are already borrowing to repay the Social Security trust fund... it makes a debt crisis so much more likely because it is difficult to imagine the bond market absorbing that much demand." — Jessica Riedel, Brookings Institution Fellow
"What's going to happen to places like New York... we're going to flood the zone. You're going to see more ICE agents you've ever seen before." — Tom Homan, U.S. Border Czar
Welcome to Washington Today on C-SPAN Radio for Wednesday, May 6, 2026. Iran says it is looking over the latest US plan to end the war, while President Donald Trump issues a new ultimatum to Iran: accept a deal or face US bombing, quote, "at a much higher level and intensity than ever before." The President and First Lady Melania Trump pay tribute to mothers of military service members ahead of Mother's Day, with the First Lady saying, "at the very heart of America's strength lies the boundless love and quiet power of mothers." Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is interviewed in closed session by the House Oversight Committee in the investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, answering questions about why he previously testified that he had cut off ties with Epstein when he had later visited Epstein's island with his family. The major party candidates are set in Ohio's US Senate race after Tuesday's primaries; incumbent Republican John Husted will face former Democratic Senator Sherrod Brown. Plus, the national debt reaches 100 percent of GDP, its highest since World War Two. Border Czar Tom Homan threatens a federal immigration enforcement surge in New York if that state passes a sanctuary law. And Ted Turner has died at the age of 87; he founded CNN, owned the Atlanta Braves Major League Baseball team, and later in life was a billionaire philanthropist. All that and more ahead on Washington Today. The New York Times is writing that Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Ismail Baghaei said Wednesday that his government was reviewing an American plan to end the war, hours after President Trump put pressure on Iran to agree to a deal and renewed threats, fueling uncertainty around negotiations. Mr. Baghaei said that Iran had yet to respond to that proposal. After finalizing its considerations, Iran will convey its views to the Pakistani side, he said in an interview with the semi-official agency ISNA earlier in the day. Another Iranian official had dismissed a reported proposal to end the war as a list of American wishes. The mixed messages came a day after President Trump abruptly paused the US military operation to escort commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz, citing what he said was great progress in talks. In a Truth Social post on Wednesday, Mr. Trump said he would end the war and offer safe passage for vessels through the Strait if Iran agrees to give what it has agreed, without elaborating. He also wrote, "If they don't agree, the bombing starts at a much higher level and intensity." That was from the New York Times. The President spoke about Iran at a White House event today that honored military mothers ahead of Mother's Day this coming weekend. We begin with First Lady Melania Trump talking about the war with Iran and military moms before introducing the President. "This is a time of military conflict for America, and whereby we honor our selfless and valiant service members regularly. Today is the day we hold their mothers in the highest regard. It is these military mothers who find themselves alone, awake in their thoughts in the darkest hours of the night. Of course, we must also remember the mothers with a spouse overseas, praying for a safe return so their family can become whole once again. At the very heart of America's strength lies the boundless love and quiet power of mothers. We are the most devoted teachers, gently nurturing empathy, inspiring dreams, and guiding our children towards goodness. We help them rise with courage when life grows difficult. In every hug, every story read at bedtime, and every sacrifice made without complaint, mothers build the moral foundation of our families. In doing so, America's mothers help build the soul of our nation. I pray you find enduring strength as your loved one serves in the defense of our freedom. Most know my husband as the strong commander-in-chief, but his empathy transcends the role and shapes a caring leader who constantly remembers each and every American soldier is someone's child. Distinguished guests, please join me with a warm welcome for our President, Donald J. Trump." "So I want to thank everybody for being here. We have so many things to talk about, but there's nothing more important than military moms that I can tell you, because without you, I don't think it would be in this house. I don't think this house probably ever would have been built a long time ago. So important. I want to start off by saying we were in—our great vice president's right here—and we're in a, I call it a skirmish because that's what it is, it's a skirmish. And we're doing unbelievably well as we did in Venezuela, where it was rapid, over in one day. And we're doing pretty much equally as well, I would say, larger, but we're doing very well in Iran. It's going very smoothly and we'll see what happens. They want to make a deal, they want to negotiate, and I think that military mothers would want to hear that. Do you agree? I think it's appropriate. Somebody would say, 'oh that's not appropriate,' but I think military mothers want to hear about this stuff. And we have the greatest, and we have the greatest, we have the greatest military that we've ever had. We have the greatest military in the world by far, there's nobody even close, and I think we've displayed that." That was President Donald Trump and First Lady Melania Trump at the White House at an event to honor mothers of military service members ahead of Mother's Day. CNN is reporting the United States and Iran are moving closer to an agreement on a short memorandum to end the Iran war, according to a regional source familiar with the negotiations. Although Trump administration officials caution that talks had previously fallen apart at the last minute, the White House received positive feedback from Pakistani mediators on Tuesday that the Iranians were progressing toward a compromise. Two administration officials told CNN this while offering some skepticism about Pakistan's optimism. Back to the White House event, later in his remarks, President Trump reiterated the goal of denying Iran the ability to obtain a nuclear weapon. "We're not going to let Iran have a nuclear weapon. We're not going to let that happen, and we won't let that happen. And so we're dealing with people that want to make a deal very much and we'll see whether or not they can make a deal that's satisfactory to us. We have it very much under control. The blockade is unbelievable. The Navy's been incredible; the job they did, it's like a wall of steel, nobody goes through. In particular, the Iranians—they're not getting anything through one way or the other, so they're out of business. And we'll see whether or not they are agreeing, and if they don't agree, they'll end up agreeing shortly thereafter. So that's the way it is." That was President Donald Trump at the White House. On Wall Street, word of a possible agreement to end the Iran war sent stocks higher. The Dow up 612, Nasdaq up 512, S&P up 105. The Navy Times is writing that US military forces intercepted and incapacitated an Iranian-flagged unladen oil tanker Wednesday that was sailing through an Iranian port in violation of the US Navy blockade, the service announced. US Central Command military forces observed the ship transiting in the direction of the Gulf of Oman and issued multiple warnings before taking action, according to a US Central Command post reporting from the Navy Times. Anthony Blinken, former Secretary of State in the Biden administration, gave his take on the US conflict with Iran during an interview today at the Economic Club of New York. "I think you have to recognize this about the Iranians. Have we done tremendous damage to them? Yes. Removing one ayatollah, significantly damaging their supply of missiles, rockets, etc., infrastructure, there's no doubt about it. But what is the actual strategic effect? Has it changed the fundamental equation? And here, I think from my perspective at least, the President may have misread the situation. He was enthusiastic after the, at least on its surface, successful venture in Venezuela. He took into account when there was this initial effort by Israel and the United States to go after the nuclear program, a pretty tepid Iranian response, and thought that we can actually do more. And in this instance, because what was done was perceived as an existential threat to the regime, they started to do things that they'd held back from doing, notably the Strait of Hormuz. So now we have a new normal that didn't exist before where the Iranians have leverage that had never been exercised over the Strait. They've also launched more rockets, more munitions at the Gulf countries than they did at Israel in this. And the infrastructure's taken a beating. Just in fact yesterday they launched at the UAE; there's a critical pipeline that takes oil and is kind of a backstop for the Strait of Hormuz to get oil out through a different way, demonstrating that they can hold that at risk. So both parties have shown that they can do damage to the other. There's a certain amount of mutual assured destruction but there's an asymmetry in it because for relatively little, the Iranians even diminished can hold things at risk. And they've demonstrated now they can hold the Strait of Hormuz at risk in ways that we all knew before was a real problem. It's why administration after administration chose not to push things this far: the first Bush administration, Obama administration, Biden, even the first Trump administration. I think there's a deal in the making, at least I hope there's one, but the President's going to have to acknowledge—well, he won't acknowledge it, he'll claim victory, okay fine—but he's going to have to give something to the Iranians in this process. And this gets me to the last point. If you look at the history of Iran and the clerical regime, the only two times where there's actually been a deal as in something where the regime has agreed to make a compromise: one was ending the war with Iraq after eight years, and there had to be an off-ramp, and the off-ramp was the United Nations negotiating something, the off-ramp was getting Iraqi troops out of Iranian territory. And then the 2015 nuclear deal, where Iran got not the right to enrich but was allowed to continue enriching at a very, very low level so they could say 'we sustained our right.' They are going to insist on getting something for this." That was former Secretary of State in the Biden administration Anthony Blinken today at the Economic Club of New York. The Associated Press is writing an article about an anti-war protest in Washington; a protester who camped out atop one of Washington's bridges for five days came down Wednesday morning, ending his stand against AI and the war in Iran. Guido Reichstadter, a Florida-based activist, was seen coming down from one of the arches of the Frederick Douglass Memorial Bridge where he had been camped since May 1st. His presence caused periodic traffic jams as Washington police closed lanes and negotiated with him. The AP article says he has been arrested and does face charges. This is Washington Today. In a story from The Hill, House Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chair James Comer, Republican of Kentucky, told reporters ahead of Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's testimony before the panel that Lutnick had not been 100 percent truthful about whether he had been on the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein's island. Lutnick is appearing before the Oversight panel on Wednesday for a closed-door transcribed interview as part of its investigation into Epstein. Lutnick has come under increasing scrutiny after he admitted in February to visiting Epstein's private island despite previously telling senators that he had cut off all contact with the disgraced financier after first meeting him in 2005. He noted his wife and children were with him during the visit. That's the article from The Hill. Congressman Comer spoke to reporters outside the closed-door interview. "And one of the things that the Democrats said, but has come out, is that Secretary Lutnick has changed some of what he said, whether it's the timeline or whether it's going to the island. So do you feel like you got direct answers, or are you still concerned that he's changing the facts?" "Right off the bat he corrected his statement on the time that he came to the island and he said, you know, that if I remember correctly—and you can look back on the transcript—he said that somehow Epstein found out that his family and friends were going to be vacationing in the Caribbean around the Virgin Islands and he invited them to lunch and they docked there for, he thinks, no more than two hours. It was his wife and four other family members and then six other, so there were 12 in a group. They didn't go in the house or anything there." "Mr. Chairman, can he explain why that was okay when back in 2005 he seemed to suggest that he didn't want any further involvement with him? Did he explain that discrepancy in detail and if so what did he say?" "I mean, I feel like Lutnick's been very transparent. He came in voluntarily. I said before this thing started the only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was he wasn't 100 percent truthful on the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that in his opening statement and, you know, we'll see. We'll continue our investigation and if we find that there were any misstatements by Lutnick, it's a felony to lie to Congress and he'll be held accountable. It's too bad it's not a felony for a member of Congress to lie to the media or we would be having a lot of Democrats that would be having to lawyer up right now." That was Congressman James Comer, Republican from Kentucky, chair of the Oversight and Government Reform Committee, speaking to reporters in the hallway outside the room where the committee members questioned Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick in the Jeffrey Epstein investigation. The committee Democrats also came out. Here is Yasmine Ansari from Arizona. "After what we have seen so far in this transcribed interview, I feel very comfortable saying that Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. We have now gone through the timeline of events with him in detail, in excruciating detail, from when they met in 2005—this is Howard Lutnick and his wife meeting Jeffrey Epstein. He described it himself in great detail on a podcast last year where he said that they went to his home, they took a tour, and allegedly were so uncomfortable after seeing the massage tables in Jeffrey Epstein's home that Howard Lutnick went on this podcast to tell the American people that he himself—and my colleagues reference the definitions he tried to change of what he meant there—but he said that he would never socially or professionally be involved or be in a room with Jeffrey Epstein again. Then in 2012 he took his family and his staff to Jeffrey Epstein's island. I focused my questions today on how he could have done that if him and his wife were truly so uncomfortable, especially when in 2008 we know that Jeffrey Epstein was accused and the sweetheart deal took place for soliciting a minor. Howard Lutnick tried to tell us in that room that he essentially didn't know any of that. I asked him how did I as a child at the time see this all over the media about Jeffrey Epstein and you, an adult who is this person's neighbor, who had engaged with him before, how could you possibly not have known? I mean, the level of the lies that are taking place inside that room without video is unbelievable and part of this egregious cover-up. I recommend that every single American, when this interview is released, read the document in full. You will see Howard Lutnick use phrases like how it's 'inexplicable and unsettling' that he went to the island. That's how he defines it himself. It's inexplicable how this happened. He also tries to define all of their interactions as, quote, 'meaningless and inconsequential.' I think he's used those terms about 20 times so far and continues to use them." That was Congresswoman Yasmine Ansari, Democrat from Arizona, speaking to reporters outside the closed-door Oversight Committee interview with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick in the Jeffrey Epstein investigation, with other Democrats standing beside her. The New York Times is writing about today's testimony; the Commerce Department said in a statement on Wednesday before the session that Mr. Lutnick looked forward to, quote, "putting to rest the inaccurate and baseless claims in the media." Though the committee's investigation into Mr. Epstein and the Justice Department's handling of the case against him has sprawled to include a number of political figures, Mr. Lutnick is the first current Trump administration official to testify before the panel. That was from the New York Times. Newsweek is writing that with both parties now settled on their nominees, the stage is set for one of the most closely watched Senate races of the 2026 cycle, which is a November showdown between Democrat Sherrod Brown and Republican John Husted in Ohio's special election for the seat once held by Vice President J.D. Vance. Brown, a former three-term senator, is seeking a comeback after his 2024 loss to Republican Bernie Moreno. He will face Husted, who was appointed to the Senate seat after Vance became Vice President. That was from Newsweek. Sherrod Brown spoke to his supporters in Cleveland Tuesday night after winning the Democratic primary. "Instead of working to bring down costs and taking on corporate interests, politicians in Washington are making it worse, we know that. Just look at John Husted. Hold on, there is more, trust me. He's saying Ohioans are struggling financially; those who are struggling don't know, in his words, how to navigate the real world. Think of that. I think we know who's out of touch with the real world, and it's John Husted. We know why Ohioans can't keep up with rising costs no matter how hard they work, because politicians like John Husted give handout after handout to corporations and billionaires, the same corporations and billionaires he cashes in from, the same corporations and billionaires that fund his campaign. John Husted voted not once, not twice, John Husted voted nine times against lowering health insurance premiums, costing 120,000 Ohioans their health insurance. Why'd he do that? Well he did that because he's taken $700,000 in campaign contributions from those same insurance companies that jacked up your rates. He cast a deciding vote to gut Medicaid for 490,000 Ohioans and to cut Medicare. Why? Again, to pay for a tax cut for his billionaire friends and his buddies in corporate America. He took more than $116,000 from one of Jeffrey Epstein's co-conspirators, including a maximum donation just last year, and then weeks later John Husted voted against releasing the Epstein files. And John Husted even voted six times, six times in favor of the White House's war in Iran, that's raised the price of groceries and fertilizer and diesel for all of us and for our farmers. Six times he voted. And get this: Husted says—these are his words—says the war is going much better than anyone thought it would. And Husted wrote the law that allowed utility companies to jack up electricity prices in Ohio." That was former Senator Sherrod Brown, Democrat from Ohio, at his victory party in Cleveland Tuesday night after winning the Democratic primary for the Senate race this year. He got 82 percent of the vote. He'll be facing incumbent Senator John Husted who won the Republican primary uncontested. John Husted posted a video on Tuesday, election day, in which he criticized the policies of the person he expected to meet in the general election, Sherrod Brown. "Election Day, I just voted and my daughter Katie got to vote for the first time in her life today. And I want to thank all of you for getting out there, casting your vote, but once we win today, we're going to have a challenge over the next six months. We face a 32-year incumbent Senator Sherrod Brown. His policies have failed us over the course of that time; while he was in Washington, we saw jobs go to China, electricity prices go up, healthcare prices go up, open borders that led to crime, drug abuse, and we can do better than this. I promise you that if you elect me this fall, we will fight hard for you in Washington, we will make sure that we have an Ohio First, America First agenda. I look forward to the battle that will come over the next six months. We need you in this fight with us. We can beat Sherrod Brown after 32 years in Washington. It's time for him to go." That was Senator John Husted, Republican from Ohio, who posted that video on Tuesday. He and Sherrod Brown won their party primaries for the Senate race this year on Tuesday. More from the Newsweek article: recent polling underscores just how narrow the margin is in this election. A March survey by On Message Public Strategies of 600 likely voters found Brown narrowly leading Husted 47 percent to 45 percent with 8 percent undecided and a margin of error of plus or minus four percentage points. That was from Newsweek. The Associated Press is writing from Indianapolis that five months ago, President Donald Trump was stinging from one of the first political defeats of his second term as Republican state senators defied him on redistricting in Indiana. Now he has proved he can still punish wayward party members after he endorsed a slate of challengers who defeated almost every one of the lawmakers he wanted to dislodge. That was from AP. Five of the seven Republican state senators targeted by President Trump lost on Tuesday. One, Travis Holdman, spoke to the Indianapolis Star. "Well I have one lesson for people: revenge and retribution is not a Christian value, and that's what this was all about. And I'm not bitter about it, it was just the fact. And there's life after serving in the Indiana Senate, and I'm going to find out what it's like." "I know that you said you weren't confident either way, but does the result surprise you at the end of the day or no?" "Somewhat. I was always hopeful I'd win, but it's hard to fight the order, so." "Message I guess to your colleagues in the Senate who are going to face those tough votes, right? And you know you have faced so many tough votes over the years and some of them, you know, result in this." "Do what your constituents want you to do. Just let the chips fall where they may, because that's what I did. Over 3,000 phone calls, 90 percent of which said, 'do not vote for redistricting,' and that's what I didn't do. And we weren't privileged to see the maps until the House had the maps a week before they went into session, even though the Vice President told me we would have the maps the next day when I started asking him the question 'when will we have the maps' and he said 'you'll have them the next day.' I didn't see them until December the 1st. Vote for the maps, don't ask any questions, that's how they want to do business in the Senate? No. Do you feel like at the end of the day this was the result of voting for what your constituents wanted? Yes, yes, and dishonesty with the campaign ads ran against me." That was Indiana State Senator Travis Holdman, a Republican interviewed by the Indianapolis Star after losing his election primary Tuesday night to Blake Fitcher, who was endorsed by President Trump. It was 39 percent to 61 percent. IndyStar was at Fitcher's victory party Tuesday night. "I want to thank Travis for 18 years of hard work, but I also really want to thank Becky because I know from what my wife has gone through over the last several months. You've had 18 years of a lot of support for Travis, so I think that deserves a round of applause. Obviously I can't stand up here and say that this was not a contested or an easy race because the reality is this was a tough, gut-punching race. I know we all felt it, you all felt it with mailers and flyers and whatever outlet it was, but just know that I am Republican, I am ready to turn the page. I know that I had some people in here support me, some that obviously didn't and I'm okay with that. I am willing to represent you, I want to represent everyone from District 19 and I really am praying I don't have as hard of a general as it was a primary. So please come alongside me and support me come November. So thanks guys." That was Indiana State Senator Republican nominee Blake Fitcher at his victory party Tuesday night after defeating an incumbent targeted by President Trump for opposing congressional redistricting in Indiana. The redistricting is part of a nationwide effort to try to get more favorable districts for Republicans to win US House seats.
