NBC News reports that $12 million were spent on ads in the Indiana State Senate races this year; that compares to less than $500,000 in the 2024 election cycle. Don Lemon, former CNN anchor and now host of the online Don Lemon Show, confirmed today he's thinking of running for president. He was interviewed at the Sir Harry Evans Investigative Journalism Summit in London. Kara Swisher, another journalist, played a recent podcast viral clip in which Don Lemon said, "I could definitely run this country a lot better than Donald Trump." "Being a president is a hard job and in some ways it's an easy job because what you—the most important part of the job is hiring people who are credible, who have, you know, the experience and the expertise to be able to run whatever position that you appoint them to. And so if you want to be a good president, you get a great team around you who know more than you, and you listen to those people. At the end of the day it's your decision, but if you have really smart people around you who you trust, I think it makes being a president easier and the smartest and most effective presidents, that's what they do." "So Kash Patel wouldn't survive a Lemon administration?" "Kash Patel would not survive, nor Pete Hegseth nor a Pam Bondi." "Well you outlasted her." "None of those people would ever be in a Don Lemon administration. It would actually be on meritocracy." "All right, away from this ridiculous speculation, would you ever consider running for office?" "Yeah, I would. I would. I don't know why." "Wow." "But you're going to go right to freaking president? Well why not? Why not? I mean, I'm a man of a certain age, I've been around long enough, I've worked hard. You laugh, I know people laugh, but no I'm not. I am a completely self-made person. I did not come from a, well I did not have $400 million to start, I have become an icon, I've made it to the top of my profession, and I've also started a business and I'm a self-made millionaire from a country where my ancestors were enslaved. So I think that's a pretty big accomplishment whether people want to acknowledge it or not." "All right, Lemonheads." That was journalist Don Lemon, former CNN anchor, at the Investigative Journalism Summit in London, interviewed by another journalist Kara Swisher. Don Lemon is currently facing federal civil rights charges after reporting on a protest in a church in Minneapolis against federal immigration enforcement. Washington Today continues in a moment. CBS News is writing that America's national debt has surpassed the country's gross domestic product for the first time since World War Two, marking a stark increase in the government's fiscal burden. Debt held by the public stood at $31.27 trillion at the end of April, edging above the US GDP of $31.22 trillion between April 2025 and March 2026, according to a recent analysis by the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. C-SPAN spoke this morning to Carolyn Bourdeaux, Concord Coalition Executive Director. "The headline of a graph from Concord Coalition: the US has no plan to bring the debt under control. And this shows from 1900 to projecting out to 2050 about the debt held by the public as a percent of GDP. Again, we mentioned that it just reached last week at 100 percent. Here it is at World War Two, which probably none of us remember that having reached there, it went down a little bit. We've got the great recession and then we've got a peak here at COVID-19, but then it just keeps on going." "Right. So that's really the challenge here, is that Congress has no plan to close that $2 trillion deficit. In fact every proposal they've been putting on the table is making that gap wider and wider and is adding more and more to our almost $40 trillion in debt. And so step one when you're in a hole is to stop digging it deeper, and right now our national government—it is Democrats, it is Republicans—neither side is putting a plan on the table to start closing that gap. And eventually, as I call it, it does catch up with you. Eventually you do hit the wall. When you hit that kind of wall, it creates a very, very serious fiscal and economic crisis and we don't want to have that experience. It would be very devastating. In Argentina, one of the reasons you look at them is they have experienced that kind of devastating crisis that we will eventually hit if we keep going down this path." Carolyn Bourdeaux is the executive director of that coalition, joining C-SPAN on this morning's Washington Journal program. A follow-up question to her: what can be done to get this national debt under control? "Public officials aren't going to be calling for higher taxes and cuts to programs that people rely on; they get to lose their jobs if they do that. So where does that leave us?" "We just—we need to really open the national dialogue around getting the fiscal situation under control. We are a wealthy country, we can do this, but we are going to have to make some choices like you are describing. We are going to have to take a sip from each cup; we're going to have to cut some of our spending certainly, and we're going to have to tackle revenues. There are ways to do this in a reasonable way but we are going to have to do that and we need people in the public to start pushing our elected officials to get there, and that is one of the challenges we're trying to take on here at Concord Coalition." More from the CBS News article: federal spending soared during World War Two; by contrast, the recent debt surge has been fueled by a combination of tax cuts, increased government spending on interest payments, and the challenge of serving an aging population, which is making programs such as Medicare and Social Security more costly, according to the Peter G. Peterson Foundation. The nation's ballooning debt is translating into higher federal interest payments, with the US now spending more to service that debt than to fund national defense or Medicare. That was from CBS News. The Cato Institute in Washington DC held a discussion today about the national debt and Social Security and Medicare. Here's a portion of it, with the moderator asking Eugene Steurle, Urban Institute Fellow, and Jessica Riedel, Brookings Institution Budget and Tax Fellow. "Most of Medicare, as you mentioned Gene, is already funded based on general revenues and in large part it is through borrowing because the federal government is running massive deficits. We're already borrowing to repay the Social Security trust fund. Why not keep borrowing to continue to pay Social Security benefits in full?" "Well, the combined Social Security and Medicare cash shortfall was $157 trillion over the next 30 years when including the resulting interest on the debt. Yes, let me back up, that is $157 trillion with a T. About a third of that is Social Security and two-thirds is Medicare. It would push the debt held by the public, which was 40 percent of GDP during most of the post-war period, from 100 percent of the economy today up to 240 percent of the economy in 30 years. Yes, we can do that. We can try to just keep borrowing and running up deficits, but if the political solution is to borrow an extra $157 trillion, it makes a debt crisis so much more likely because it is difficult to imagine the bond market absorbing that much demand for Treasuries without notably raising interest rates. And then higher interest rates would turn around and raise the debt in a vicious circle. So in the short term it's easy to say just keep running deficits, but the trends are so bad long term that something's got to give. Give you one simple statistic that to me says a lot about how our system's working, is if you take the revenue increases that come with economic growth, project out 10 years you might have 15, 20 percent more revenues—or 10 years maybe more if you had good economic growth. All of those revenues, at least as projected by the Congressional Budget Office, are not enough to cover the increase in Social Security cost, plus the increase in Medicare cost, plus the increase in interest on the debt. And so everything else is getting squeezed. Even with the deficits we're running, we're really squeezing our opportunity to do everything else. And why is the squeeze so hard? Well, you've got the policy makers in a huge fiscal dilemma which is at this point the promises for this growth are so great that if you measure whether you're cutting benefits relative to current levels... well, you'll get in this fight 'oh you're cutting benefits.' A long-term solution to Social Security, Medicare, anything is to basically get the growth rate in line with what the revenues can support." That was Jessica Riedel, Brookings Institution budget and tax fellow, and Eugene Steurle, Urban Institute fellow, at a program today on the national debt and the Social Security system held by the Cato Institute. The Congressional Budget Office projected in its February report that the national debt held by the public will increase on its current pace from 101 percent of US GDP this year to 120 percent in 2036; that would top the previous high in 1946 of 106 percent. WABC-TV in New York City is writing that Mayor Zorran Mamdani and housing officials announced a big win against a landlord in the Bronx. Mayor Mamdani said the city won a $31 million settlement against the owners of Robert Fulton Terrace and Fordham Towers. City officials said tenants had complained for years about a range of issues including lack of heat and hot water while continuing to pay rent. Reporting from WABC-TV in New York City, here is Mayor Mamdani. "Today we are announcing unprecedented action against the current landlords of Robert Fulton Terrace and Fordham Towers, Karun Singh and Rajmatti Persaud. After two years of litigation, HPD has secured $31 million in penalties. The largest civil penalty won in HPD history. All on behalf of the residents of these buildings. This is a long time coming. For years, Singh and Persaud let more than a thousand violations accumulate while they collected rent month after month. They have overleveraged this building and Fordham Towers multiple times. They are on Public Advocate Jumaane Williams' list of worst landlords, and for good reason. That is why this court penalty includes the appointment of a chief restructuring officer who is in charge and has already hired a property manager to ensure that repairs are actually made. We have negotiated that at least $900,000 for the chief restructuring officer be put towards repairs immediately. These funds come directly from the landlords' accounts—money that should have otherwise been allocated towards this work long ago. And we will not only make the urgent repairs that are needed today, but also the long-term improvements that tenants deserve. We are calling on Fannie Mae to work with the city to secure a preservation buyer for these homes, one who will actually deliver on the investments and the improvements that tenants deserve. Because this is more than just a conversation around patchwork solutions to ensure that you can live from today to tomorrow. It's about shattering a cycle of abuse and neglect that has long defined not just these towers, but frankly affordable housing across our city. And as we look to tomorrow, we will ensure the end of this impunity is measured not merely in accountability for those landlords who have violated our city's housing laws, but also in better living conditions for those who were forced to live under their negligence. This win must mean something comprehensive and lasting to tenants." That was New York City Mayor Zorran Mamdani, a Democrat, at a news conference today in the city. He also said reports of problems over the years at the apartment complexes included no working elevators and rat infestations. Spectrum News is writing that the debate over US Immigration and Customs Enforcement—or ICE—operations continues in New York. Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said this week more ICE agents could be sent to New York if legislation limiting how law enforcement can work with the agency is passed. The Local Cops, Local Crimes Act, proposed by Governor Kathy Hochul earlier this year, could ban state and local law enforcement from coordinating with federal immigration enforcement for non-criminal violations, and would also prohibit local governments, police, and corrections from entering agreements to allow the federal government to use their resources for civil immigration enforcement. Reporting from Spectrum News, Tom Homan made the remarks Tuesday at a Border Security Expo in Phoenix. "What's going to happen to places like New York and these other people want to pass this ridiculous legislation not to work with us? We're going to flood the zone. You're going to see more ICE agents you've ever seen before. So congratulations. Because when we send these teams out there, we'll find the bad guy—most times we do—and when we find the bad guy he's with others. Others who might not be a priority target but they're in the country illegally. There are more people we're looking for, but we found them during these operations. Well guess what, they're coming too. You're going to see collateral arrests increase in these areas; you're going to see more agents in your neighborhood." That was border czar Tom Homan at a Border Security Expo in Phoenix on Tuesday. He also said for the people out there saying President Trump's getting weak on mass deportation, "you don't know what the hell you're talking about, you ain't seen blank yet, this year will be a good one, mass deportations are coming." New York Governor Kathy Hochul, a Democrat, responded at a news conference on Tuesday in Albany. "We just received word that Tom Homan made some comments at a speech he's giving saying that he is sending in a surge of ICE agents to the state of New York should this legislature pass what I introduced in January known as Local Cops Local Crimes. The premise behind this is simple, that local law enforcement is there for one purpose, that is to help protect our communities. I've invested over $3 billion to help local law enforcement; we can see the results—crimes are dropping dramatically across the state from Buffalo to New York City and we're proud of that. We also will continue working with ICE in cases of dangerous criminals—we always have, always will, that will not waver. All I'll say to Mr. Homan is that Donald Trump himself said he would not send a surge of ICE agents to the state of New York unless I ask. I'm not asking." That was New York Governor Kathy Hochul, a Democrat, holding a news conference in Albany, New York on Tuesday. An Associated Press article about a new AP-NORC poll says that after months of increased immigration enforcement across the country, few Americans view the United States as a great place for immigrants today, though they believe it once was. About a quarter say that concerns about immigration status have impacted daily life for them or someone they know in the past year. 61 percent say the US used to be a great place for immigrants but is not anymore, and only 27 percent feel the country is currently a great place for immigrants. 10 percent say the US was never a great place for immigrants. That was from the Associated Press. WBNS-TV Columbus, Ohio, is writing that the anticipated hotel boom tied to the upcoming FIFA World Cup may be falling short of expectations according to a new report. The American Hotel and Lodging Association released a report on Monday highlighting the challenges faced by hotels across the US in the lead-up to the international soccer tournament. Reports said that across host cities, hotels have not seen strong bookings and that domestic travelers are outpacing international visitors. Reporting from WBNS-TV in Columbus, the football or soccer tournament hosted by the US, Mexico, and Canada this year starts June 11th. On Tuesday, the president of the governing body of football worldwide, FIFA, Gianni Infantino, was interviewed at the Milken Institute Economic Summit in Los Angeles. "What is the best business outcome for the MLS and candidly the NWSL? The Women's World Cup will be here in 2031. Is it more investors? Is it higher valuations? Is it the next Landon Donovan? Like, what is the business outcome that would be the best for US soccer from this World Cup?" "Well, let me give you one other figure. The global football or soccer GDP worldwide is around 300 billion a year. So it's quite sizeable, right? Not huge, but quite sizeable. What is interesting is that 70 percent—seven-zero—of this is generated in Europe. The US counts for around 3 percent in this whole ecosystem." "Of soccer GDP?" "Yes, of soccer GDP in the world. So if the US would be doing—and this is a message to all the investors here—if the US would be doing 30 percent, not 100 percent, 30 percent of what Europe does, I think the US is capable of doing 30 percent of what Europe does in soccer. We are speaking about a hundred billion a year GDP impact in the US. Yeah. What does this mean? This means that investors in the US have to believe in US soccer, have to invest here, have to bring players... of course you have to bring players, the best play, Messi shows it, but you cannot count only on one player. You need to bring the best players and then you need to train the best players as well. One of the things that shocks me is that in the US children have to pay in order to play soccer; it's quite expensive actually. So many, many families cannot afford to send their children to play soccer. For the American sports, the schools jump in, on the men's side... on the women's side they play soccer but the men, they play football and basketball and whatever, but not or not so much soccer. So we need to change the top by creating an ecosystem that brings in the best and then revenues go up because with the viewing figures we have here... I mean, the final of the World Cup is watched by 20 times more people than the Super Bowl. 20 times more. So if you have so—there is a market, not just here, but there is a market worldwide that can start from here. So we need to work on the top, on the elite, but we need to work as well on the grassroots, making sure that we create academies, that we create clubs for youth, for children to be able to play." That was FIFA President Gianni Infantino interviewed at the Milken Institute Economic Summit in Los Angeles on Tuesday. The Guardian newspaper is writing about the interview; FIFA President Gianni Infantino has defended World Cup ticket prices, insisting that football's global governing body was obliged to take advantage of US laws that allowed tickets to be resold for thousands of dollars above face value. CNN is reporting today that Ted Turner, the media maverick and philanthropist who founded CNN—a pioneering 24-hour network that revolutionized television news—died peacefully Wednesday surrounded by his family. According to a news release from Turner Enterprises, he was 87. The Ohio-born Atlanta businessman, nicknamed "The Mouth of the South" for his outspoken nature, built a media empire that encompassed cable's first superstation and popular channels for movies and cartoons, plus professional sports teams like the Atlanta Braves. That was reporting from CNN. Walter Isaacson, a former president of CNN, now best known as a biographer, spoke about Ted Turner's death and legacy at the Investigative Journalism Summit in London. "CNN, as some of you might have seen already, we've just had the news as Walter and I were waiting offstage that Ted Turner, legendary founder of CNN, his death has been announced in the last few minutes. And he is up there with Harry in being fearless, the most fearless journalist I've ever seen." "How did you experience that?" "You know, he was fearless when it came to his corporate overlords. When Time Warner bought him, he was fearless when it came to political leaders who tried to push back on him. I can remember in 2011, when the twin towers get hit, Ted had been kind of banished upstairs at CNN Center because the corporate overlords at Time Warner didn't want him meddling with it. But when we were watching the towers go down in that newsroom I said to our news editor, 'excuse me a second, I'm going to have to go upstairs.' And there was Ted alone in his office and I said, 'Ted, you got to come back.' He said, 'I'm not.' I said, 'no I'm asking you, come to the newsroom, this is why you created this network.' And he brought a Confederate sword he had hanging on his wall and he rallied us and he said journalism is made for times like this. But it's interesting that he had respect for the boundaries, so until you went to get him he needed you to ask him to come down at that particular moment." "Yeah, it was such a sad thing. When corporations take over great media empires they sometimes need to exert control when you have a founder like Ted. He was just the heart and soul of the place and you can find it in all corporations: when the founder gets replaced by the suits, it's always a problem." "Well on that note, are you concerned about the future of CNN today?" "I haven't focused on it too much because I don't quite know where it's going to end up. I hope somebody benevolent buys it, but also the benevolent owner model is not always the perfect one." That was Walter Isaacson, biographer and former CNN president, at the Sir Harry Evans Investigative Journalism Summit in London today. Also there was Christiane Amanpour, CNN's chief international anchor, who was on a panel with other journalists talking about the state of global journalism. This was before the news broke that Ted Turner had died, but Christiane Amanpour talked about Ted Turner's influence in the Persian Gulf War. "CNN, Ted Turner 46 years ago, June 1st, created a media revolution precisely to, you know, sort of get rid of all the special interest, precisely to be the sort of man in the middle and not have an agenda and just go and tell the stories from all over the world. And I learned from him in the first Gulf War of 1990 that we actually must cover all sides of the stories. The problem with us now is we are being weaponized by leaders—weaponized by leaders in the United States, by leaders all over the world. But he said you have to be in Baghdad when the White House was saying, 'don't have your people in Baghdad Ted, we're going to, you know, kill them probably,' and he said 'no, volunteer, we have to tell all sides of the story.' And I want to say one more thing: the fact that Israel prevents us from going to Gaza is indefensible on every single level, so that's another threat to being able to tell the truth." That was CNN chief international anchor Christiane Amanpour on a panel with other journalists at the Investigative Journalism Summit in London before the news broke that Ted Turner had passed away at the age of 87. C-SPAN covered Ted Turner many times over the years; in 1999 he gave the commencement address at the University of Georgia and gave some thoughts to the graduates about how to succeed in business. "Remember that your family should be your top, your top priority, and then after that your country and your service and then, then, then your career. That doesn't mean that you neglect your career, that it's not important—it is important. But money is not everything. In fact, having had a great deal of experience both with not having it and with having it, it's better to have it than not to have it, I want you to know. There's nothing, there's nothing wrong with it, there's nothing wrong with it. But don't dedicate your life just to making money. I really did the things I did in business because I thought they'd be interesting and I was curious to see whether they would work or not. I mean, that's basically the way to really hit a home run in business is to do something that nobody's ever done before. And that requires thinking; you got to think up something new. You got to be able to see something that other people didn't see, like Bill Gates did with Microsoft and Alexander Graham Bell did with the telephone, Thomas Edison did with electricity. I mean, I did it with satellites and cable TV. I mean, I thought of something. I was just so good." And it was great, you know, but but I always kept moving too even though I was in the same business. I I wanted to keep growing so for the television business I when I got into that, then then I got into the sports business and the documentary business and the news business and the movie business and the television programming business. I just kept on, kept on moving all the time; every three or four years I'd come up with something new to do. And I didn't get everything that I wanted in life. I wanted one of the big networks, I wanted a long I had them, I had them in the palm of my hand and they just, just like we could have beaten the Yankees, you know. You're not going to get everything that you want; you just what you got to do is you got to say, "Well, didn't get that one but I'll go get something else." Ted Turner speaking to graduates at the University of Georgia in Athens, Georgia in 1999, that from C-SPAN's video library. Ted Turner has died at the age of 87. President Donald Trump writing on Truth Social: "Ted Turner, one of the greats of all time just died. He founded CNN, sold it, and was personally devastated by the deal because the new ownership took CNN, his baby, and destroyed it." "It became woke and everything that he is not all about. Maybe the new buyers, wonderful people, will be able to bring it back to its former credibility and glory. Regardless, however, one of the greats of broadcasting history and a friend of mine, whenever I needed him he was there, always willing to fight for a good cause." And a statement from Jason Carter, the board chair of the Carter Center: "Ted Turner was larger than life, a visionary in the media world, a pioneer in philanthropy, an Atlanta legend, and a devoted friend of my grandfather President Jimmy Carter. He led one of the most remarkable lives in American history, from growing up in Georgia to the America's Cup to the superstation to the creation of CNN." And before we go on C-SPAN Radio's Washington Today, one more news item: Supreme Court Justice Clarence Thomas is now the second longest-serving justice in US history, surpassing this week both Stephen Johnson Field and his former colleague on the high court John Paul Stevens with 34 years and 195 days. If Justice Thomas serves two more years he'll pass William O. Douglas, 36 years and 209 days. Justice Thomas turns 78 years old in June. Thanks for listening to Washington Today. Don't forget, sign up for C-SPAN's evening newsletter "Word for Word" to get the latest on what's going on in Washington sent to your inbox every day. Subscribe at c-span.org/connect. Have a good night.

A conversation with Dario Amodei & Daniela Amodei

Claude(244.0K) ·33:10 ·11.7K views · 2026-05-06
Dario Amodei (Co-Founder, Anthropic) Daniela Amodei (Co-Founder, Anthropic) Ami Vora (Chief Product Officer, Anthropic)
Anthropic is currently navigating "hyper-exponential" growth (80x annualized revenue/usage) that has exceeded even the founders' aggressive scaling predictions, signaling a shift toward multi-agent AI hierarchies and the potential for a one-person, billion-dollar company by 2026.
TL;DR Anthropic is currently navigating "hyper-exponential" growth (80x annualized revenue/usage) that has exceeded even the founders' aggressive scaling predictions, signaling a shift toward multi-agent AI hierarchies and the potential for a one-person, billion-dollar company...

TL;DR

Anthropic is currently navigating "hyper-exponential" growth (80x annualized revenue/usage) that has exceeded even the founders' aggressive scaling predictions, signaling a shift toward multi-agent AI hierarchies and the potential for a one-person, billion-dollar company by 2026.

Navigating Hyper-Exponential Growth

  • Extreme Scaling: While the founders predicted scaling laws over a decade ago, the actual experience is "strange and unsettling," likened to the 2,000-foot waves in the movie *Interstellar*.
      • 80x Growth: In Q1 of this year, revenue and usage grew at an annualized rate of 80x, far surpassing the "normal" 10x growth the company had planned for.
  • Compute Constraints: This unexpected surge led to compute shortages.
      • Infrastructure Solutions: Anthropic is aggressively acquiring more compute—including a recent deal with SpaceX—to pass capacity back to developers.
  • The "Claude-on-Claude" Feedback Loop: For the first time, internal productivity (measured by pull requests) has inflected upward because Anthropic is using Claude to build Claude.

The Future of Software Engineering and "Solopreneurship"

  • The $1B One-Person Company: Dario Amodei reaffirmed his 2026 prediction for the first billion-dollar company with a single employee.
      • Shift in Task Complexity: AI has evolved from writing code to helping humans think of software engineering as a task, and eventually, thinking of building an entire economic unit or business as a task.
  • Amdahl’s Law in AI: As the speed of writing code increases, other bottlenecks become more prominent.
      • New Focus Areas: Anthropic is focusing on "non-verifiable" aspects of development that cannot be solved by simple unit tests, such as security, architectural design quality, and verification.
  • Developer Renaissance: AI is enabling a renaissance across industries including medicine, financial services, and infrastructure upgrades (e.g., major migrations at Stripe).

Shift Toward Multi-Agent Hierarchies

  • Agentic Evolution: The technology is moving from single-agent interactions to "multiple agents," where various instances of Claude manage one another.
      • "Country of Geniuses": Dario described the trajectory as moving from a smart individual to a smart team in a room, eventually leading to a "country of geniuses in a data center."
  • Organizational Productivity: Future models will focus on making whole teams—rather than just individuals—more productive in a way that is "more than the sum of its parts."

Mission, Safety, and Real-World Impact

  • "Hold Light and Shade": This internal cultural value dictates Anthropic’s approach to balancing the transformative potential of AI with its risks.
      • Risk Mitigation: The company focuses on labor disruption, security vulnerabilities, and responsible release cadences.
  • High-Impact Use Cases: Daniela Amodei highlighted diverse applications of Claude:
      • Global Health: Mobile doctor interfaces for the Global South to provide medical advice in remote areas.
      • Scientific Research: Accelerating biomedical research and drug discovery.
      • Personal Utility: Using Claude to retrieve corrupted wedding photos or even chart the growth of garden tomato plants.

***

Notable Quotes

"It is a remarkable experience to write down these lines on graphs and have the predictions come true... And yet the actual experience of seeing what it's like is just so crazy that you're shocked anyway." — Dario Amodei, Co-Founder & CEO
"We have this internal cultural value called 'hold light and shade.' It is such a good encapsulation of... how we really want to get stuff out as quickly as we can, we're trying to build the best products, and we're just trying to do it responsibly." — Daniela Amodei, Co-Founder & President
"I think we've moved from the models are writing code to the models are helping us think of software engineering as a task, to the models are helping us think of 'how can I build a business or an economic unit' as a task." — Dario Amodei, Co-Founder & CEO
Let's give them a round. Woo! It is a delight to have you here at Code with Claude. We've been having a great day of sessions, demos, customer sessions, all sorts of fun stuff. But I wanted to ask you, maybe just a little bit zooming out. We talked a lot about the exponential in some of our conversations this morning and what it feels like to be on the exponential. And so as two people who are definitely on the exponential, we've talked a lot about growth and what it feels like. What does it feel like for you all? Well, first of all, it's great to be here. Thank you so much for having us. You know, at Anthropic we have this little slack emoji of the rollercoaster. You guys know the one I'm talking about? But it's like an inflected rollercoaster, so it's almost like it's like going straight up. And I think of me and Dario as riding at the front and the back of that rollercoaster. I don't know if you all have ever, maybe not recently, been on a rollercoaster. I'm not always sure which one of us is in the front and which one of us is in the back, but you get a different type of whiplash depending on which side you're on. And I think that's probably the best encapsulation of what it has felt like. It's like we're having a lot of fun, there's a ton of adrenaline. We're not totally sure that the operator of the rollercoaster isn't like a 15-year-old doing a summer job of like questionable level of sound mind. But it's been great. It's fun, it's an adventure, there's a lot going on. Yeah, you know, the way I always think about the exponential is, you know, it was me and the other co-founders who kind of, you know, first predicted it through the scaling laws, you know, over 10 years ago. And we wrote down these lines on graphs that say like, well first we're going to spend a thousand dollars in an amount and then ten thousand and then a hundred, you know, it's going to go all the way to hundreds of billions. And you know, the model is going to be this good at this task and this good at this other task and that good at coding. And it is a remarkable experience to write down these lines on graphs and have the predictions come true. So in that sense they are not surprising at all. And yet the actual experience of seeing what it's like is just, it's so crazy that you're shocked anyway, even though what you wrote down on the graph is exactly what happened. I'm always reminded of like, you know, the famous scene in the movie Interstellar where they go to this planet that's very close to a black hole. And so, you know, the planet has these waves that are like, you know, 2,000 feet high and, you know, I was a physicist, I know the math of general relativity, how much things can be sheared. But actually seeing it on human scale, like there's something deeply, you know, this kind of deeply strange and unsettling about seeing it actually happen. And you know, that's what it's felt like every year at Anthropic. And I feel like this is the first year where like the rest of the world is kind of seeing us, seeing it with us because we're so much in the spotlight. You know, it applies to the internal growth of the company, it applies to our own work within the company where for the first time we've seen the number of internal PRs inflect upwards due to the work that Claude is doing. And we've seen it externally because actually this is the first year we've grown faster than the exponential. So, you know, we tried to plan very well for a world of 10x growth per year. In the first quarter of this year we saw if you were to annualize it, 80x growth per year. Ooh. In revenue and usage. And and so, you know, as you saw today with the SpaceX compute deal, we're working as quickly as possible to provide more compute than than we have in the past. We'll continue to do so, we'll pass that compute on to you as as soon as we can, as soon as we can do so. I guess I hope the 80x growth doesn't continue because that's just crazy and it's too hard to, too hard to handle. I hope for some more normal, more normal, more normal numbers, a mere, a mere, a mere 10x. AI normal. But we will manage it, absolutely as best we can and, you know, we're every day trying to obtain even more compute that that we can, we can, we can, we can, we can pass on to you. We're sorry if sometimes it takes some time but we're going to keep going to acquire as much as we can. Awesome. Yes, yay for compute limits. You know, this is an audience of developers and builders and that's really what today is about. It is about how we're making our platform better because developers are who help us close the gap between what the models can do and what they're actually doing for real people out there. And you know, both of you talk a lot internally about the importance of developers and builders. Maybe Daniela I'd love to start with you, like how do you think about supporting this ecosystem, supporting this community? Yeah, I mean, I'll start out by saying I think in many ways developers are the most important users of Claude, I think for a variety of reasons. You know, one is Anthropic ourselves are majority developers. Right, if you think about how we develop this technology, what we're building, we learn so much from the developer community. It's like the best partnership because we, it's first of all I think it's a group that gives like honest feedback, right? And so I think that that is actually really hard to get. You know what I mean? It's like you build a product and you're like, I see some numbers, like those are nice, but like the genuineness with which the developer community I think engages with us is something that is so special. We have tried, you know, really from day one, I think Anthropic has always, you know, primarily built for developers, for businesses. I think we're a little bit unique in the AI ecosystem, you know, for that reason. And I think we have been very fortunate to be able to benefit from the feedback, from the engagement, from the community development. You know, developers are, in my experience, they're like, they're very ecosystem and community oriented, which I think we are too, right? We're like, how do we build for this sort of broader ecosystem of people who are developing by the way some of the most inspiring transformative technologies and building the most incredible companies in the entire world? Right, there has been this renaissance of things in, you know, medicine and software development and financial services, I mean it's like you pick the industry and there is an incredible developer or an incredible developer-based company that is transforming that industry, helping, you know, leveraging our tools sometimes. And I think that's such a special, that's like a, that's both a privilege and a responsibility that I think Anthropic holds to say, you know, developers are really the backbone of like how we learn, how we build better tools for all of you. And I think that's a, that's a really special relationship that that we feel like pride in and also a responsibility towards. Yeah, I mean, and this is an example of the developer community. I think part of what Daniela's saying is feedback is a gift. We hear the positive, we also hear the negative. Please keep it coming. It is part of how we know what's working and what isn't. And so we really value and I'm sure as you're talking to people around, we really value all of that. And it helps us know what to do better. Sorry Dario, back to you. Yeah, you know, one thing I would say is that technology doesn't diffuse at a kind of an even pace across the economy, right? And I think there's a spectrum where the software engineers are the ones who are fastest to kind of adopt new, kind of fastest to adopt new technology. That's, that's why there's so much kind of focus on this area and it's the beginning of it. But it's like it's a foreshadowing of how things are going to work across the economy, right? And how the economy is going to be transformed by AI. So I think, you know, getting this right and really, really making it work for for this community, it's kind like a microcosm of how we have to, of how we have to make it work across the world. And and I think one thing that, you know, a dynamic we should watch, it was about, roughly, I think it was roughly a year ago there was an event like this where Mike Krieger asked me, you know, when will there be the first billion dollar company with one person. And I said 2026. And I think we're actually on track to, which even hadn't quite happened yet. There's been like two-person companies that are one billion dollars built with AI. There's been like one person that's worth several hundred million, million dollars. So but but like, you know, we got we got seven more months. No, we do. Seven more months in 2026, or eight. And you know that's that's that's eternity on the exponential. But what I'm trying to say with this though is that there's an enormous ability for one person or a tiny set of people to do a set of things that are incredible, right? Where, you know, before if you just had an idea and you had a vision, like there's so many resources you'd have to accumulate over several years in order to make that vision happen. And I think there's a very unique opportunity for single, single individuals or very tiny teams to do things that are incredible, right? Where I think we've moved from the models are writing code to the models are helping us think of software engineering as a task, to the models are helping us think of like how can I build a business or an economic unit as a task. And so there's an extraordinary amount of opportunity for people in this room to take advantage of that. Yeah, it really feels like it's it's kind of removing barriers. You know, there were all these barriers to like creating that kind of value in the world and now I mean I guess the gauntlet has been thrown, we've got an eight-month timer and I'm excited to see what comes of it. I would love to maybe hear what's going to change for developers. So Dario you talked a little bit about what they can do now and you've talked, you know, in the past about how you expect Claude to build more with Claude. Can you just talk about how you expect things to move? Yeah, I mean I think there's several like maybe maybe trends. One is going from single agents to kind of multiple agents. So the idea that you know you have a bunch of these Claudes, it's like managing a team, right? You have a bunch of Claudes running and like you know you kind of you kind of farm a bunch of things out to your Claudes and maybe some of the Claudes farm things out to other Claudes with like different, so you have a kind of a whole hierarchy or hierarchy. We're gradually making our way to like the country of geniuses in a data center. You know we're starting with like a a team of smart people in a you know in a room or something and working our way up, to a different exponential to a country. So I think that's one trend that we're kind of already starting to see and we're already like offering tools that can help do that. I think a trend that's related to that is like, you know, what we've done so far with Claude code is like you know it it helps kind of individuals to be more productive. But I think increasingly we're going to start thinking at the level of whole teams and organizations and how can you make whole teams and whole organizations more more productive in a way that is kind of more than just the sum of its parts. And then finally you know I think in this area as with everywhere else if you want to think about what's next when something's working really well you should always think about Amdahl's law which is you speed one thing up, what are the things you're not speeding up. And and so I think there are a bunch of things like security, like verification, like just if you're living in a world where you can within an organization write three or four times as many PRs as you could previous, as you could previously, you start to understand there are all these other things that are holding you back or that will go wrong if you speed up just that and not everything else. And and so working to speed up those those kind of those kind of other things so that we can greatly increase people's productivity but we can do it smoothly and smoothly and productively and reliably, I think that's going to be very important. Does that have any impact on how you think about training new models or, you know, what the future of models looks like? Yeah, I mean, you know, that's true on several levels. I mean I've already said many times we're using Claude to speed up Claude, right? That's that's already something that's happening. But I also wonder if the things we're trying to do with the models could also influence how we build them. So when I talk about these things like verification or kind of design quality or things like that, like one of the reasons training models for code and software engineering has gone so fast is that you have this verifiability, right? Where you train the model and it's like, you know, you're able to verify it by running unit tests and so that it has a lot of properties that simplify the process of training. But what you discover is that there are these of course aspects of the job that are not verifiable, right? And you know some of the, is this thing really right? Can we find errors? Are there security issues? Not quite as verifiable. And and so training the models to be better at that, which I think will also make the models better at other things where they haven't made progress as fast as coding, like their ability to write or their ability to kind of do less objective scientific tasks. So I think it's going to have benefits in many in many other areas but, you know, I think we find even within software engineering these kind of soft or somewhat subjective skills and abilities become surprisingly important because of Amdahl's law. I would love to hear, you know we talk a lot about our mission internally. Like Daniela as we just keep growing and the stakes of this whole industry keep getting higher, what should people know about our mission and about us as a company? You know, I think when I think about what Anthropic is trying to do, there's these sort of like two maybe two pillars, right? The first is around how do we develop this transformative technology in a way that is good for everybody, right? And I think this goal of Claude is this incredible tool. It has the power to really transform, you know, what people build and how they create and the ambition level of what they can develop themselves. And I think there comes a huge amount of opportunity there and there's also some risk, right? I think that we've talked about this a lot publicly. There's some risk to just labor disruption. There's risks to ensuring that the technology is developed safely, that it's good for people. And I think Anthropic's job or what we try to do is really think about how to balance these two things in equal measure. We have this internal cultural value called hold light and shade. And I think that it is such a good encapsulation of, you know, what we see about how the technology is being leveraged today and and also just our approach to putting the technology out into the world, right? Mithos and Glasswing are great examples of this. The potential to build something incredible with a model that capable is so vast and we want to be a little bit careful about how we release it because of some of the security vulnerabilities, right? And I think that this is this kind kind of complicated dance that we do where we're like we really want to get stuff out as quickly as we can. We're trying to build the best products and release the most powerful models and we're just trying to do it responsibly. I think that is actually really the underpinning of the majority of actions that we take is sort of grounded across those two pillars. I really experience that. I think the hardest part of it is you can get really familiar with the problems because the problems don't change that fast, the problems are about humans, you know, like we're always going to have similar problems. But a thing that's hard is to learn to be fresh-eyed all the time about what the technology can do and like constantly scan for that. And then I also feel it on a personal basis where your job just changes because you hit a new bottleneck, you know, and like the way you spend your day is just a little different. Maybe I'll just ask a couple more questions. Maybe Dario very quickly you talked a lot about model capabilities and how they change so often. Is there one thing that makes you most excited in the models that are coming in the next call it six months? You know, I would say this idea of thinking thinking at the level of the organization rather than just rather than just one person. And, you know, again it ties to this one person billion dollar business which, you know, maybe that will turn out to be to be an underestimate. But but, you know, just just kind of the idea that, you know, you can both have an AI do the work of many people but that when you have a team of humans that AI is not just doing the work of many people working for one person but that it it does the work of many people many times over by operating within an organization of humans. Awesome. And maybe I'll just ask to close like Daniela one thing we talked about is some of the kind of developers or use cases we see that are just so inspiring. I talked a bit in the keynote about some of my favorites whether that's people using it for efficiency at places like Stripe doing major infra upgrades, but also people using it for these very personal and specific ways like connecting kids to foster families faster. And I would just love to hear, you know, what are some of your favorite examples of how you see people using the tools? Yeah, I mean, I think even going back to the days when I was at Stripe, something that I've just loved about the developer community in general is like for every interesting challenge or problem there is in the world, there's like an incredible dev somewhere who's like trying to make that thing better. And I think getting to watch people build things with Claude that are just bringing so much utility and value and meaning to people around the world is really inspiring. I think, you know, for me personally, a pilot that I've seen with some developers who are building these like interfaces basically for mobile doctors in the Global South. So places where it's really hard to reach an actual doctor because of access issues, right? You're down a dirt road somewhere, you know, 50 miles from the nearest city, but people there still encounter health challenges. How do you actually work with this really smart technology to build these like interfaces where people can just ask it a question, it can give you some medical advice, right? In a way that's like sanctioned. I'm also just like in general blown away by the medical research field and what is coming out of Claude being able to accelerate biomedical research across many of the developers that work with us, that build on us. There are also just some really heartwarming individual examples; we have this user happiness channel at work. Some of my favorite ones: there's a developer who used Claude to help retrieve their wedding photos that were corrupt, like on a corrupted hard drive. I thought that was so sweet and just like such a personal use case but so meaningful. And then my last one was just so random. Someone is using Claude to chart the growth of their tomato plants in their garden and I was like, never in a million years would I have thought of it, but it's so... I was like, do you have a live cam and can I subscribe to this? Because I would really love to see it, but I think just the range of things that people are able to do is just astonishing. Well Dario, Daniela, thank you both so much for spending time with us here. It's a pleasure to see you. Thank you to all of you for joining us. We've got a great rest of the day coming, but please join me in giving a round of applause to these co-founders. Thank you.

BREAKING: House Oversight Dems React To Howard Lutnick's Epstein Testimony: 'He Should Resign'

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·16:37 ·39.8K views · 2026-05-06
Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary)
House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for the resignation of Howard Lutnick following a closed-door interview, alleging he provided "pathological" and "dishonest" testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and a 2012 visit to Epstein's private island.
TL;DR House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for the resignation of Howard Lutnick following a closed-door interview, alleging he provided "pathological" and "dishonest" testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and a 2012 visit to Epstein's private...

TL;DR

House Oversight Committee Democrats are calling for the resignation of Howard Lutnick following a closed-door interview, alleging he provided "pathological" and "dishonest" testimony regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein and a 2012 visit to Epstein's private island.

Contradictions in Testimony and Personal Conduct

  • The "I" Definition: Democrats criticized Lutnick for attempting to "make a farce of the English language" by redefining his previous claims. Specifically, he argued that when he stated "I" would never see Epstein again after 2005, he meant he would not see him *alone*, effectively justifying why he brought his wife and children to see Epstein in 2012.
  • Timeline Discrepancies:
      • 2005 Visit: Lutnick recalled specific details from a 2005 visit to Epstein’s townhouse, claiming he felt "uncomfortable" after seeing massage tables and vowed to cut ties.
      • 2012 Island Visit: Despite his alleged discomfort, Lutnick took his family and staff to Epstein’s private island in 2012, four years after Epstein’s 2008 conviction for soliciting a minor.
  • Selective Memory: Members noted a sharp contrast in Lutnick's recall; while he remembered minute details from 2005, he claimed his 2012 trip to the island—where high-profile abuse occurred—was "inexplicable" and that he could not remember why he went or what he saw.

Allegations of an Administration Cover-up

  • Reversal on Blackmail: Ro Khanna highlighted that Lutnick previously claimed Epstein engaged in blackmail and recorded video tapes of guests. During the interview, Lutnick walked these claims back, calling them "speculation."
  • External Influence: Lutnick reportedly admitted to speaking with Trump administration officials before his testimony. Democrats suggested these conversations may have prompted him to change his story regarding Epstein’s illegal activities to protect others.
  • Communication with Donald Trump: When asked if he had spoken specifically with President Trump about his testimony, Lutnick reportedly refused to answer, though he denied speaking to Trump about Epstein in the years prior to the presidency.

Business and Social Ties to Epstein

  • Financial Connections: Under questioning from Republicans, Lutnick admitted that a subsidiary of his company was invested in a company where Jeffrey Epstein also held an investment, and that the two had exchanged emails regarding the matter.
  • Legitimizing a Criminal: Rep. Yassamin Ansari argued that Lutnick’s continued engagement with Epstein after the 2008 conviction helped "legitimize" a known predator, contributing to a culture that allowed Epstein to continue his crimes against minors.
  • Characterization of Visits: While Lutnick attempted to label his interactions with Epstein as "meaningless and inconsequential," he eventually conceded that a lunch visit to a private island constitutes a "social" interaction.

Demands for Transparency and Future Action

  • Lack of Video Record: Democrats expressed frustration that the interview was not videotaped, claiming the American people are being denied the ability to see the Secretary’s "evasive" demeanor and "the sweat on his brow."
  • Calls for Resignation: Multiple members, including Rep. Ro Khanna and Rep. Yassamin Ansari, explicitly stated that Lutnick has lost all credibility and should resign immediately.
  • Future Proceedings: Democrats signaled they may push for a public hearing or further subpoenas to compel on-camera testimony, vowing that if they regain the majority, all such investigations will be conducted with full transparency and video records.
"Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history... It just demonstrates the culture of enabling these crimes and allowing rich and powerful people like Jeffrey Epstein to continue." — Rep. Yassamin Ansari
"If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing... He's lost all credibility and really it's a shame that the American people don't get to see what he did there." — Rep. Ro Khanna
"He claimed that when he said 'I would never be in a room again with Jeffrey Epstein,' he meant only him... Epstein was so gross to him that he wasn't willing to be in a room with him, but he was perfectly okay with his wife and family being in a room with Epstein. He’s lying." — Democrat Committee Member
Well now we know why that interview was not videotaped. If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing. He was asked very straightforward questions about whether he regretted misleading the American people. I mean, he said that he would never see Epstein again in 2005. And everyone knows that he took his wife and kids to see Epstein in 2012. And yet, it was just contortions and lies and no acknowledgment that he misled the American public. And if you saw the exchanges that my colleagues had with him, you would see he made a farce of the English language. I mean, he was trying to define "I" as if saying that what he really meant is that he would not see Epstein alone, but he'd be totally fine with having his wife and kids see Epstein. It made no sense. And then, he's raised even more serious concerns about the investigation. He originally had said that Epstein engaged in blackmail and recorded videotapes. Now he's saying, "Well, he was wrong. He was just speculating, and Epstein actually didn't engage in blackmailing." This raises the question of what the cover-up is. Did someone tell him to say that? I've not heard that Epstein never engaged in blackmail. Why is Howard Lutnick changing his testimony, changing his story? He's lost all credibility and really it's a shame that the American people don't get to see what he did there. Total lack of truth and lack of honesty. Hey, I agree. Howard Lutnick should resign. That was absolutely mind-boggling what we just heard in the room. He was evasive, nervous, and he was dishonest. He would not admit to lying, which he clearly did in the podcast. He said he was never in the room with Jeffrey Epstein ever again after the first time he met him. Yet he then admitted that he was in the room with Jeffrey Epstein. I had to ask him whether he and I were in the same room just now because I couldn't understand his meaning of "we were in the same room together." And then he said that Jeffrey Epstein was a sick man that he would never want to be in the room with him again. Yet he was in the room with him again when he visited his island where Jeffrey Epstein raped women, underage women. And he brought his kids and his friends with him to that island when he met with Jeffrey Epstein. He said he never met with Jeffrey Epstein again socially, yet he admits that that was a social visit to his island. And so he was clearly being dishonest; he was clearly being evasive. And finally, he doesn't remember why he went to Jeffrey Epstein's island, which is also mind-boggling. He somehow remembers going to the island after pictures surfaced of him being at the island, but he doesn't remember why he went to the island. And we had the emails; that's why we found out he was at the island. This is absolutely absurd and he should resign. Good afternoon. Congresswoman Ansari from Arizona's 3rd District. After what we have seen so far in this transcribed interview, I feel very comfortable saying that Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. We have now gone through the timeline of events with him in detail, in excruciating detail, from when they met in 2005. This is Howard Lutnick and his wife meeting Jeffrey Epstein. He described it himself in great detail on a podcast last year where he said that they went to his home, they took a tour, and allegedly were so uncomfortable after seeing the massage tables in Jeffrey Epstein's home. Howard Lutnick went on this podcast to tell the American people that he himself—and my colleagues referenced the definitions he tried to change of what he meant there—but he said that he would never socially or professionally be involved or be in a room with Jeffrey Epstein again. Then in 2012, he took his family and his staff to Jeffrey Epstein's island. I focused my questions today on how he could have done that if him and his wife were truly so uncomfortable, especially when in 2008 we know that Jeffrey Epstein was accused and the sweetheart deal took place for soliciting a minor. Howard Lutnick tried to tell us in that room that he essentially didn't know any of that. I asked him, "How did I as a child at the time see this all over the media about Jeffrey Epstein, and you, an adult who was this person's neighbor, who had engaged with him before, how could you possibly not have known?" I mean, the level of the lies that are taking place inside that room without video is unbelievable and part of this egregious cover-up. I recommend that every single American, when this interview is released, read the document in full. You will see Howard Lutnick use phrases like how it's "inexplicable and unsettling" that he went to the island. That's how he defines it himself. It's inexplicable how this happened. He also tries to define all of their interactions as "meaningless and inconsequential." I think he's used those terms about 20 times so far and continues to use them. So I will just end by saying that there are real victims here. There are potentially over a thousand women who were abused and raped when they were children. And even at the bare minimum, Howard Lutnick was aware of these things. He knew. It just demonstrates the culture of enabling these crimes and allowing rich and powerful people like Jeffrey Epstein and all of the other disgusting, rich, and powerful men who committed these crimes to continue. It continues because people like Howard Lutnick knew these things were happening because they were all over the news and continued to engage and legitimize somebody like Jeffrey Epstein. Earlier today, Chairman Comer said that he hadn't seen any evidence of wrongdoing by Secretary Lutnick. And that's actually the problem with the Trump administration, this Republican majority in Congress, because lying to the American people is wrongdoing. And Howard Lutnick lied to the American people. I hope today that we would have heard some remorse, some contrition from Secretary Lutnick for flat-out lying to the American people. Instead, what we've heard thus far is hours of testimony where he's attempted to redefine the meaning of the word "I." He claims that when he said "I would never be in a room again with Jeffrey Epstein," he meant only him and Jeffrey Epstein. Epstein was so gross to him, so disgusting that he wasn't willing to be in a room with him, but he was perfectly okay with his wife and family being in a room with Epstein. He's lying. And today's transcribed interview is part of the ongoing cover-up because the American people deserve to see the video of what's taking place in there. They deserve to see the sweat on the Secretary's brow as he struggles to answer basic questions about his lies to the American people. So we're going to continue to listen and ask tough questions, but unfortunately, it appears that Secretary Lutnick is not going to take any responsibility for lying to the American people today. Can you explain for just a moment when you talked about the trip—and I'm happy to ask this to anyone—can you explain for a moment when they took the trip on the boat and you're saying that he's saying "Oh we were okay doing that" but he didn't want to be in the same room with him? Characterize what his remarks were, his response when he said "Wait a minute, isn't this inconsistent with this triumph if you're uncomfortable with being with this person?" We asked him over and over again "Why did you go to the island?" and he says he doesn't remember, that it was inexplicable and he simply didn't know how to answer the question at that point. Right. Can I follow up on the island, on the island? Did he say that he saw anything from his time there that now in retrospect raises alarm about Epstein's activities on the island? He mentioned in his 2005 visit to Epstein's apartment that he saw massage tables that made him uncomfortable. Did he see anything on his, in his time on the island, any specifics about what he saw there? Those questions were specifically asked by the majority during the first hour and he said no to all of it. And it's also important to note he could remember nothing about the visit to the island. He couldn't remember why he was there, couldn't remember anything he saw. Apparently he could remember in great detail the 2005 visit to the townhouse and the massage tables that were there. Now 2005 is seven years before 2012, so his memory was sharp with respect to 2005 but dull with respect to 2012 and the visit to the island. And this is why this is such a cover-up. I mean he refused to say there was any blackmailing even though he thought that happened in the past. He refused to say that there was any video recording even though he said in the past that people, Epstein, were video recording these young girls. He refused to say anything abnormal about Epstein's island. So someone obviously got to him from the administration. In fact he said he talked to people in the administration and that's why he's changed his tune. And the question is not just why he's lying, the question is why he's covering up, why he's no longer willing to share what he knows about Epstein's abhorrent behavior. Can we follow up on what you just said Congressman Khanna? You said he talked to President Trump ahead of this interview. You said he talked to people in the Trump administration. He said he did not talk to President Trump. Why do you think Secretary Lutnick agreed to appear voluntarily? Did he address that at all? It is significant that he's the only cabinet secretary that has appeared for us. Well, he didn't want to be subpoenaed. I mean he knew that we have the Republican votes to subpoena him just like we did with Pam Bondi, and it's the first time that a cabinet member has been subpoenaed. What I expected honestly—I expected him to say "Well you know I regret what I said on the podcast. I should have been transparent about this incident with the family." "Nothing happened that was terrible but you know what? I should have had better judgment and here are the things I know about Epstein, he was a disgusting person, let me try to help in the investigation." He did none of that. Can I ask two questions to follow up on what you just said Congressman Khanna? You mentioned that he spoke to members of the administration and then he changed his tune. Did he say who exactly he spoke to and when he spoke to them? You want to take that you guys? Yeah. He could not recall who he spoke to in the administration and it's also important to note he was asked by a number of us if he spoke to President Trump about today's testimony and he refused to answer that question. He did say he didn't speak to President Trump or Donald Trump when he was not President, but he would not answer the question as to whether he spoke to President Trump about today's testimony. And then secondly the Secretary had told you guys that he found the invitation to the island to be unsettling—how his assistant knew that he was in the U.S. Virgin Islands. Did he talk about why he accepted an unsettling invitation? This is what I was asking during my line of questioning. Why did you go when you were so clear on this podcast and you've described in great detail that you and your wife were so alarmed by this person and did not want to ever be in a room with him? To the point of the statements that he had made—I mean they were very clear statements that "I would never in a social professional setting be in a room with this person." And then we later got him to admit that visiting someone's island and having lunch with them is in fact considered a social situation. And so no he would not, and I find that extremely unbelievable. I mean I think of all of the depositions that we have done so far, I find Howard Lutnick to be the least credible with the way that he just refuses to acknowledge how misleading his statements were. I mean quite frankly they were—he lied in the podcast, but he refuses to acknowledge that. He still is trying to stand by the fact that he was truthful in those statements. Behind you, behind you. Did the Secretary—I haven't gotten a question yet sorry. Did the Secretary indicate whether or not he had had any business dealings with Jeffrey Epstein and more specifically whether they invested in the same company? Yeah, the Republicans asked that in the first hour. He did admit that a subsidiary of his company was invested in a company that Jeffrey Epstein was invested in as well and they did have an email exchange about that. And then as Democrats are you all planning to do anything to compel the Secretary's testimony on camera going further since you don't seem satisfied that this was... That may be coming but well, stay tuned. Meaning you guys might ask for a public hearing? Chairman Comer promised a public hearing. Sorry I have... if I may go thank you. You said that you believe that he's covering up. Do you have any indication or in your questioning were you able to zero in or perhaps you've seen in the documents who you believe he's covering up for and how extensive that cover-up is? Well I think he's covering up for the administration and let me give the exact exchange that Representative Walkinshauk can elaborate on because he really drilled down on it. First Secretary Lutnick said on the podcast that there was blackmail going on, right. Then he says to my inquiry "Well no no no I was just speculating." I said "Well what was your basis for speculating?" "Well I didn't have any basis I take it back." You know this seems like this is the administration's modus operandi just speculate with no basis. But here he's saying "Okay I take it back." So then I said "Well why did you take it back?" "Well I talked to administration officials." Then Representative Walkinshauk asked him "Well who did you talk to?" and he says "Well no no no I just learned about this in public. I didn't talk to the administration officials." Well Massey and I have been doing this and all of us have been doing this for months. I've never seen a single public statement that there was no blackmail. So the question is why is he changing his tune? Who is getting to him in the administration to basically have him say nothing was wrong, let's move on. That's what he was saying. Anyone else? No I think you nailed it. That's good. Representative Khanna, the Justice Department last July said that there was no blackmail in its memo. So why do you keep saying that there was no blackmail said by the administration? We have not—that is not what the survivors believe, that is not what the testimony has been of other individuals. And there has not been an investigation of people like Les Wexner or people like Leon Black and many other people. That's why we're calling for investigations. And he certainly did not reference that memorandum. If the Democrats win the midterms what changes with regard if this investigation is continuing? What do you see changes with regard to this investigation and how you get more answers should the Democrats win? Well for starters these interviews are going to be on video and we're going to make sure that the American people get to see what we got to see as legislators today. We're going to be completely transparent and honest when it comes to this investigation. So to be clear you're going to try to have him come in again and try to maybe do this under a subpoena which would compel... I didn't say that I said generally that we're going to make sure that these types of interviews are on video and that the American people get to see them. But not him again just in the future? I'm not ruling out him again I'm just saying generally. Can we get your reaction on something else? On the other investigation dealing with the DC and the investigation with the police department. Nine officers have now been put on leave or in investigation. I just want to get your thoughts on that investigation and where we're at on it. That's a great question for another day. I think that was the last question. Thank you. Thank you so much. Thank you. I just want to see whether...

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff update on war with Iran

WVLT News(56.0K) ·24:04 ·162 views · 2026-05-06
Pete Hegseth (Defense Secretary)
The United States has launched "Project Freedom," a Centcom-led military operation to secure the Strait of Hormuz and restore global maritime commerce following Iranian attacks on merchant vessels.
TL;DR The United States has launched "Project Freedom," a Centcom-led military operation to secure the Strait of Hormuz and restore global maritime commerce following Iranian attacks on merchant vessels. While the U.S. is currently providing a "bubble of protection" with...

TL;DR

The United States has launched "Project Freedom," a Centcom-led military operation to secure the Strait of Hormuz and restore global maritime commerce following Iranian attacks on merchant vessels. While the U.S. is currently providing a "bubble of protection" with significant naval and air assets, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth emphasized that this is a temporary mission and expects international partners to take over responsibility for the waterway soon.

Project Freedom: Securing Global Commerce

  • Mission Objective: To facilitate the safe passage of international commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which accounts for approximately one-fifth of global oil consumption.
  • Iranian Aggression: Since the ceasefire was announced, Iran has fired on commercial vessels nine times, seized two container ships, and attacked U.S. forces more than 10 times.
      • Current Maritime Crisis: Approximately 22,500 mariners on over 1,550 commercial vessels are currently trapped in the Arabian Gulf due to Iranian threats.
      • Weaponizing Supply Chains: General Caine stated that the IRGC is attempting to hold the global economy hostage by obstructing critical maritime checkpoints.
  • International Expectation: Secretary Hegseth signaled that U.S. leadership is intended to stabilize the situation in the short term, but "the world needs this waterway a lot more than we do," and allies are expected to "step up" and take responsibility for the mission shortly.

Military Posture and Tactical Operations

  • Force Composition: The operation involves over 15,000 American service members, 100+ fighter and attack aircraft, and guided-missile destroyers providing 24-hour defensive overwatch.
  • Strategic Command: The 82nd Airborne Division is currently coordinating all-domain command and control (land, air, space, sea, and cyber).
      • Technological Edge: The division is utilizing AI-enabled tactical networks to augment intelligence and streamline decision-making for commanders in the field.
  • Recent Kinetic Engagements: U.S. forces recently defeated several Iranian threats, including:
      • Aviation Successes: Navy MH-60 and Army AH-64 Apache helicopters successfully neutralized cruise missiles, drones, and small boats.
      • Surface Defense: Destroyers are actively detecting and defeating Iranian fast boats and one-way "kamikaze" attack drones.

Strategic Outlook and Diplomacy

  • Ceasefire Status: Secretary Hegseth confirmed the ceasefire with Iran is technically holding, despite "frothiness" and low-level kinetic skirmishes at the start of Project Freedom.
  • Nuclear Non-Proliferation: The administration’s primary strategic objective remains ensuring Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon. Hegseth asserted that previous operations (Midnight Hammer) significantly dismantled Iran's conventional and nuclear infrastructure.
  • Escalation Readiness: While the U.S. is currently exercising restraint, General Caine warned that Centcom remains "locked, loaded, and ready" to resume major combat operations if ordered by the President.
  • Regional Partners: The U.S. is in contact with South Korea regarding an attacked vessel and is encouraging nations like Japan, Australia, and European allies to contribute to the maritime security coalition.

Memorializing the Fallen

  • Tower 22 Remembrance: General Caine closed the briefing by honoring three U.S. Army Reserve soldiers killed on January 28, 2024, by an Iranian-backed proxy force in Jordan:
      • Sergeant William Rivers
      • Specialist Brianna Moffett
      • Specialist Kennedy Sanders
"To Iran, let innocent ships pass freely. These international waters belong to all nations, not to Iran to tax, toll, or control." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"Iran is weaponizing the global supply chain... the regime led by the IRGC is effectively attempting to hold the entire global economy hostage." — General Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
"We're stabilizing the situation so commerce can flow again, but we expect the world to step up. At the appropriate time and soon, we will hand responsibility back to you." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"No adversary should mistake our current restraint with a lack of resolve. Centcom and the rest of the joint force remain ready to resume major combat operations against Iran if ordered to do so." — General Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
Our aircraft and this mission without hesitation. To Iran, let innocent ships pass freely. These international waters belong to all nations, not to Iran to tax, toll, or control. To our partners, allies, and the rest of the world, this is a temporary mission for us. As I've said before, the world needs this waterway a lot more than we do. We're stabilizing the situation so commerce can flow again, but we expect the world to step up. At the appropriate time and soon, we will hand responsibility back to you. To the incredible Americans executing this mission right now, simply thank you. Your courage, your readiness, your unmatched professionalism and precision are why the world can breathe easier today and every day. You are and always will be the greatest fighting force in human history, unmatched always. Project Freedom is underway, commerce will be flowing, and America is once again leading with strength, clarity, and purpose for the benefit of the entire world. Our will is unshakable. God bless our troops, and God bless the mighty United States Navy as well. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning, everyone. Thanks for being here.
